title: Incident response procedures for unexpected stored PAN
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Incident Response Documentation: There is a clearly written procedure that explains how to handle unexpected storage of PAN outside of the CDE, including steps for secure deletion, migration, or remediation.
• Operational Records: Logs and audit trails showing that the incident response procedures were activated when unexpected PAN was detected. Additionally, change management or remediation tickets that include follow-up on investigative actions can be considered evidence.
• System Configurations and Automated Alerts: Evidence from monitoring systems that detect the presence of cleartext PAN outside authorized areas and automatically trigger the incident response process.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Documentation Without Action: Organizations may have a well-documented procedure but lack evidence that the procedure was actually used in realistic scenarios.
• Minimal Alerting: A superficial configuration of alerting tools that does not corroborate actual incident handling (e.g., testing alerts only periodically without real data).
• Incomplete Response Logs: Log records might exist but fail to capture all key steps like secure deletion or verification of whether sensitive authentication data accompanied the PAN.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the existence of documented incident response procedures addressing unexpected PAN detection.
WHY: This procedure outlines the process, ensuring that steps for secure deletion, migration, and remediation are defined.
HOW: Review the incident response policy documentation and procedural playbooks; check that the document explicitly details actions for handling PAN incidents outside the CDE.
FAIL: Absence of a documented procedure or documents that lack details on remediation steps for PAN found in unexpected locations.

CHECK: Review operational logs and incident tickets for historical activation of the incident response procedures.
WHY: Real incident records demonstrate that the documented procedures are executed in practice when unexpected PAN is encountered.
HOW: Use log analysis tools, SIEM dashboards, or ticketing system queries for records when PAN was detected outside the CDE. Confirm that the response steps (identification, secure deletion/migration, analysis of source and contributory factors) were properly followed.
FAIL: No historical record of incident response events, or incident records that do not include all mandatory response actions.

CHECK: Interview incident response personnel and IT staff to confirm familiarity and proper training on the incident response process for unexpected PAN storage.
WHY: Staff interviews validate that the process is understood and regularly executed, beyond just being documented.
HOW: Conduct targeted interviews, asking for specific examples of incidents involving unexpected PAN and their full remediation lifecycle.
FAIL: Personnel provide vague or inconsistent responses that suggest limited understanding or practical usage of the procedures.

CHECK: Assess automated monitoring tools that detect PAN outside the CDE, and verify that they are configured to alert the incident response team.
WHY: Automated detection is a key factor to ensure timely incident response; proper configuration confirms active monitoring.
HOW: Review configuration settings in monitoring solutions and check alerting logs to ensure alerts were dispatched during outlier events.
FAIL: Monitoring systems are not properly configured, lack proper alerting mechanisms, or fail to detect cleartext PAN outside expected boundaries.

CHECK: Evaluate the post-incident analysis and remediation documentation related to unexpected PAN storage incidents.
WHY: Detailed post-incident reports and remediation records prove that root cause analyses are performed, and control gaps are addressed proactively.
HOW: Obtain and review incident investigation reports that document how PAN ended up outside the CDE, what remediation actions were taken, and corrective measures to prevent recurrence.
FAIL: Absence of comprehensive post-incident analysis or a history of repeat incidents with similar root causes, indicating poor control improvement.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcut: Focus on correlating incident logs with alert system records – if an alert was generated, it should trigger a documented incident ticket.
• Critical Indicator: Look for detailed remediation steps in incident reports. Shallow reports may indicate a paper-only approach rather than a well-executed response.
• Key Detail: Ensure that logs capture not only detection events but also subsequent actions, including secure deletion confirmations or evidence of migration into the CDE. This detail is often overlooked.
• Rapid Response: An effective control not only documents the incident response but shows a prompt, consistent reaction time from detection to remediation across multiple incidents.
• Beyond Policy: Always supplement policy review with interviews and system log reviews to ensure operational effectiveness and prevent faked compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10.7 Incident response procedures are in place, to be initiated upon the detection of stored PAN anywhere it is not expected, and include:
- Determining what to do if PAN is discovered outside the CDE, including its retrieval, secure deletion, and/or migration into the currently defined CDE, as applicable.
- Identifying whether sensitive authentication data is stored with PAN.
- Determining where the account data came from and how it ended up where it was not expected.
- Remediating data leaks or process gaps that resulted in the account data being where it was not expected.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.10.7.a Examine documented incident response procedures to verify that procedures for responding to the detection of stored PAN anywhere it is not expected to exist, ready to be initiated, and include all elements specified in this requirement.

12.10.7.b Interview personnel and examine records of response actions to verify that incident response procedures are performed upon detection of stored PAN anywhere it is not expected.

Purpose
Having documented incident response procedures that are followed in the event that stored PAN is found anywhere it is not expected to be, helps to identify the necessary remediation actions and prevent future leaks.

Good Practice
If PAN was found outside the CDE, analysis should be performed to 1) determine whether it was saved independently of other data or with sensitive authentication data, 2) identify the source of the data, and 3) identify the control gaps that resulted in the data being outside the CDE. Entities should consider whether there are contributory factors, such as business processes, user behavior, improper system configurations, etc. that caused the PAN to be stored in an unexpected location. If such contributory factors are present, they should be addressed per this Requirement to prevent recurrence.

Customized Approach Objective
Processes are in place to quickly respond, analyze, and address situations in the event that cleartext PAN is detected where it is not expected.

Applicability Notes
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Security incident response plan is modified and evolved based on lessons learned
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes a version-controlled Security Incident Response Plan (SIRP) with revision history clearly indicating modifications and rationales reflecting lessons learned from past incidents.
• Technical artifacts: Incident review reports, post-incident meeting minutes, automated logs of plan changes, and audit trails that detail update timelines and the incorporation of emerging threat intelligence.
• Operational patterns: Regular scheduled reviews and update cycles, debrief sessions after incidents, and iterative test exercises demonstrating that the plan adapts over time.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes merely add a generic revision note or a dated addendum without substantive changes based on a thorough lessons-learned exercise.
• Failing to involve a full cross-section of incident response stakeholders (technical, managerial, and compliance teams) may present a false appearance of an evolved plan.
• A control may be faked if only policy documents are updated without corresponding evidence of improved practices in actual response events or exercises.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the revision history of the Security Incident Response Plan.
   WHY: A detailed and timestamped revision log, including notes on lessons learned and industry changes, proves active updates rather than cosmetic changes.
   HOW: Access the version control system or document management repository for the SIRP; review entries detailing the rationale behind changes post-incident, and check audit trails for updates after recent incidents or exercises.
   FAIL: Absence of a structured revision history or generic update logs with no clear incident reference indicates a failed control.

2. CHECK: Review post-incident reports and lessons learned documentation.
   WHY: Demonstrates that actual incidents were analyzed and improvements were derived and implemented in the SIRP.
   HOW: Interview key personnel; collect and examine incident debrief reports, meeting minutes, and follow‐up action logs showing specific changes made to the SIRP.
   FAIL: Documentation that merely states a review occurred without specific, actionable follow‐up plans indicates poor implementation.

3. CHECK: Confirm the incorporation of emerging industry threats and developments into the SIRP.
   WHY: Ensures that the plan is not static but dynamically updated to address new threat vectors and vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Look for references in the SIRP to external threat intelligence sources or industry bulletins, and ask incident response team members about recent specific updates based on known emerging threats.
   FAIL: Absence of references to current industry data or a lack of recent update notes tied to emerging trends indicates ineffective evolution of the plan.

4. CHECK: Validate that the update process involves a multi-disciplinary review.
   WHY: Inclusion of varied perspectives (IT, security management, operations, and legal) increases the robustness of lessons learned and ensures comprehensive updates.
   HOW: Interview multiple stakeholders; cross-check attendance records or meeting logs from post-incident review sessions; verify that feedback from departments is documented in the SIRP revisions.
   FAIL: If only a single department’s then-minimal input is evident, or if update sessions are conducted with limited or non-diverse participation, the control is likely inadequate.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on the incident review meeting minutes and revision logs. If these documents clearly map a specific incident to specific plan changes, you can quickly gauge operational compliance.
• Critical Indicator: Look for integration of external threat intelligence. A plan that solely references internal incidents without acknowledging broader cyber trend data is less likely to be effective.
• Detail Matters: Consistency in documentation (dates, names, detailed reasoning) is crucial. Vague entries or batch updates that lump several incidents together without clarity may be a red flag.
• Don’t be fooled by updated cover pages; always drill into the details of revision history and actual changes made in the content of the plan.

This methodology emphasizes verification of not just policy existence but tangible, routinely updated evidence of the control's operation, ensuring that lessons learned are actively shaping security responses.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10.6 The security incident response plan is modified and evolved according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.10.6.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined to modify and evolve the security incident response plan according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.

12.10.6.b Examine the security incident response plan and interview responsible personnel to verify that the incident response plan is modified and evolved according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.

Customized Approach Objective
The effectiveness and accuracy of the incident response plan is reviewed and updated after each invocation.

Guidance
Purpose
Incorporating lessons learned into the incident response plan after an incident occurs and in-step with industry developments, helps keep the plan current and able to react to emerging threats and security trends.

Good Practice
The lessons-learned exercise should include all levels of personnel. Although it is often included as part of the review of the entire incident, it should focus on how the entity's response to the incident could be improved. It is important to not just consider elements of the response that did not have the planned outcomes but also to understand what worked well and whether lessons from those elements that worked well can be applied to areas of the plan that did not. Another way to optimize an entity's incident response plan is to understand the attacks made against other organizations and use that information to fine-tune the entity's detection, containment, mitigation, or recovery procedures.

title: Security incident response plan includes monitoring and responding to alerts
methodology:
Below is the expert methodology for validating that the Security Incident Response Plan (SIRP) includes monitoring and responding to alerts from security monitoring systems (such as IDS/IPS systems, network security controls, file change-detection mechanisms, and unauthorized wireless access point detection) as required by req-12.10.5.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes live SIEM dashboards displaying recent alerts and active tracking of incidents.
   • Technical artifacts include configuration settings from IDS/IPS, integrity monitoring logs (for critical files and payment pages), and network scan reports showing detection of unauthorized wireless devices.
   • Operational patterns such as incident tickets with corresponding alert logs; routine response exercises and post-incident analysis meeting records.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply document an incident response plan without ensuring that technical integrations (like proper SIEM feed configurations or file integrity logs) are in place.
   • Relying on outdated configurations or manual log entries rather than automated alert generation might give a false sense of compliance.
   • Implementations may include static or template procedures that do not reflect dynamic, real-world alert investigation and response.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Verify that monitoring systems (IDS/IPS, network security, file change, and unauthorized wireless access detection) are actively generating alerts and forwarding them to a centralized SIEM.
   WHY: Active generation and centralized collection of alert data prove that the systems are effectively integrated into the incident response framework.
   HOW: Examine SIEM dashboards for recent alerts, review system configuration files for proper forwarding settings, and correlate event logs from multiple systems with corresponding timestamps.
   FAIL: If logs show no evidence of alerts from any of the expected monitoring systems, or if configuration files reveal that feeds are disabled or misdirected.
   
   CHECK: Validate the existence and operational use of documented response procedures that trigger upon receiving alerts.
   WHY: Having a written plan is not sufficient unless it is operationalized; confirmation via incident tickets, escalation logs, and actual response activity shows repeatability and structure.
   HOW: Interview incident-response personnel to confirm process adherence, review incident management systems for automatically generated tickets linked to alerts, and cross-check selected incidents with documented procedures.
   FAIL: Lack of incident tracking records linked to monitored alerts, significant discrepancies between the documented process and what is observed in actual responses, or manual handling without validation.
   
   CHECK: Assess the evidence from change-detection and tamper-detection mechanisms, especially for critical files and (until March 2025) payment pages.
   WHY: These systems are critical to the control’s scope and ensure that unauthorized changes are detected, addressing key risks.
   HOW: Review change-detection logs and integrity monitoring reports, validate alert correlation with file changes, and verify that alerts trigger automated or manual responses.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete logs, alerts that are not linked with any change events, or documentation that merely lists systems without corresponding evidence.
   
   CHECK: Confirm that mechanisms for detecting unauthorized wireless access points are active and integrated within the alert response process.
   WHY: Wireless security is a vulnerable area; active monitoring shows that the organization maintains a proactive stance on potential network intrusions.
   HOW: Review wireless scan reports, check alerts from network monitoring solutions for unauthorized access points, and examine any incident records linked to such alerts.
   FAIL: Absence of wireless monitoring reports, no history of alerts being generated for rogue access points, or findings of known unauthorized devices without remediation actions documented.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated SIEM queries to retrieve alerts across multiple monitoring systems over the last 30 days. Lack of recent alert information is a red flag.
   • Critical Indicator: Choose a sample alert and track its life cycle in the incident management system. Delays or no follow-up indicate poor integration between monitoring and response.
   • Detail: Focus on timestamps and correlation data in logs; discrepancies or gaps often indicate that alert forwarding isn’t working as intended.
   • Tip: Engage with on-duty incident responders to understand real-world challenges and verify that the procedure is not just paper-based but actively practiced.

This comprehensive methodology ensures that you not only validate the existence of documented policies but also prove that the technical and operational components of the Security Incident Response Plan are actively and effectively functioning.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10.5 The security incident response plan includes monitoring and responding to alerts from security monitoring systems, including but not limited to:
 • Intrusion-detection and intrusion-prevention systems.
 • Network security controls.
 • Change-detection mechanisms for critical files.
 • The change-and tamper-detection mechanism for payment pages. This bullet is a best practice until its effective date; refer to Applicability Notes below for details.
 • Detection of unauthorized wireless access points.

Customized Approach Objective
Alerts generated by monitoring and detection technologies are responded to in a structured, repeatable manner.

Applicability Notes
The bullet above (for monitoring and responding to alerts from a change- and tamper-detection mechanism for payment pages) is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required as part of Requirement 12.10.5 and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Incident response personnel are appropriately and periodically trained.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for a regularly updated training calendar or schedule that indicates when incident response training sessions are held. Check digital logs or sign-in sheets from training sessions, along with records of training materials used.
• Technical Artifacts: Obtain copies of the targeted risk analysis that detail the risk-based training frequency and compare them with training attendance records, session agendas, and evaluation results. Examine learning management system (LMS) reports showing completion rates and training timestamps.
• Operational Patterns: Identify recurring training events (e.g., quarterly or annually) and compare against the incident response team’s logs or briefings. Notice if refresher training aligns with updates in the incident response plan.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations might produce outdated or generic training materials and backdated attendance logs to simulate compliance without real, role-specific training.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on documented policies without evidence of training exercises or scenario-based evaluations can be an evasion. Simply having a risk assessment is insufficient if the training events do not demonstrate tailored incident response exercises.
• Technical Gaps: Frequently missed is the detailed correlation between the risk analysis determining training frequency and the actual training frequency. Overlooking simulated incident response scenarios or practical exercises can further indicate superficial implementation.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review the entity’s targeted risk analysis document to verify that it defines the frequency for incident response training as per Requirement 12.3.1.
WHY: This demonstrates that the training schedule is driven by a risk-based approach, rather than arbitrary or infrequent training.
HOW: Obtain and inspect the risk analysis report; cross-check with the incident response training schedule; verify documentation explicitly references risk factors and frequency.
FAIL: Absence of frequency determination tied to risk assessment or a mismatch between documented risk analysis and training frequency.

CHECK: Examine training records, including attendance logs, training materials, and LMS reports for periodic incident response training.
WHY: These artifacts prove that training sessions were actually scheduled and attended according to the defined schedule.
HOW: Review digital logs or sign-in sheets; verify dates, materials, and contents correspond to incident response roles; check for evidence of periodic refresher sessions.
FAIL: Incomplete logs, generic training materials that do not address incident-specific responsibilities, or records that are outdated or lacking timestamps.

CHECK: Interview a sample of incident response personnel to assess their knowledge of their responsibilities and awareness of recent training.
WHY: Direct interview feedback confirms that employees not only attended training but understood their roles and can recall incident response procedures.
HOW: Prepare role-specific questions about incident response steps; corroborate employee responses with documented training objectives; note any gaps in understanding.
FAIL: Inconsistent or vague answers that suggest minimal or no effective training, or if personnel are unaware of their defined response processes.

CHECK: Validate that training sessions include practical components such as incident simulations or role-playing exercises.
WHY: Practical exercises ensure that personnel can apply knowledge in real-world scenarios rather than simply reviewing documentation.
HOW: Request samples of training session agendas, simulation exercises, or after-action review reports; confirm that such activities are integrated into the training schedule.
FAIL: If training sessions appear to be purely theoretical, or if there is no evidence of practical application such as simulations or post-training assessments.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Check the LMS completion reports; automated systems often log detailed information (timestamps, module completions) that are hard to falsify.
• Red Flags: A significant gap between training dates and updates in the incident response plan might indicate that training is not keeping pace with evolving threats.
• Key Detail: Verify that training content is tailored to the specific responsibilities of incident response personnel—generic security awareness training does not meet the requirement.
• Best Practice: Compare the training records with incident reports to ensure that staff involved in incident handling have received updated training shortly before notable incidents occur.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10.4 Personnel responsible for responding to suspected and confirmed security incidents are appropriately and periodically trained on their incident response responsibilities.

Customized Approach Objective
Personnel are knowledgeable about their role and responsibilities in incident response and are able to access assistance and guidance when required.

12.10.4.1 The frequency of periodic training for incident response personnel is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.

Customized Approach Objective
Incident response personnel are trained at a frequency that addresses the entity’s risk.

Applicability Notes
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.10.4 Examine training documentation and interview incident response personnel to verify that personnel are appropriately and periodically trained on their incident response responsibilities.

12.10.4.1.a Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for the frequency of training for incident response personnel to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.

12.10.4.1.b Examine documented results of periodic training of incident response personnel and interview personnel to verify training is performed at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis performed for this requirement.

Guidance
Purpose
Without a trained and readily available incident response team, extended damage to the network could occur, and critical data and systems may become "polluted" by inappropriate handling of the targeted systems. This can hinder the success of a post-incident investigation.

Good Practice
It is important that all personnel involved in incident response are trained and knowledgeable about managing evidence for forensics and investigations.

Purpose
Each entity’s environment and incident response plan are different, and the approach will depend on a number of factors, including the size and complexity of the entity, the degree of change in the environment, the size of the incident response team, and the turnover in personnel.

Performing a risk analysis will allow the entity to determine the optimum frequency for training personnel with incident response responsibilities.

title: Specific personnel are designated to provide 24/7 incident response.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying that specific personnel are designated to provide a 24/7 incident response, per PCI DSS req-12.10.3.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: The organization should maintain an up-to-date, detailed on-call schedule or roster that clearly shows designated personnel covering all shifts round-the-clock. A dedicated incident response team listing with shift assignments, contact details, and escalation paths is a strong indicator.
• Technical artifacts: Logs from incident management platforms (ticketing systems, alerting dashboards) that capture incident alerts and corresponding response actions, especially outside regular business hours. Calendar invitations or duty rosters maintained in centralized systems further support compliance.
• Operational patterns: Regular shift handovers, documented drill exercises, and after-action reviews confirming 24/7 availability of knowledgeable personnel.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations often ‘fake’ compliance by having a static list of names on a policy document without actively maintaining an on-call schedule or ensuring actual availability. 
• Some may rely solely on emergency phone trees that are not monitored or are outdated.
• Superficial compliance might include periodic training records that do not verify current post-assignment or on-call effectiveness outside of normal hours.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify the existence of an up-to-date on-call schedule for incident response team members.
WHY: An accurate, actively maintained schedule demonstrates that specific personnel are designated to handle incidents on a 24/7 basis.
HOW: Request and review the shift roster and on-call calendar; cross-check against logs in incident management systems that record alert responses during off-hours.
FAIL: Schedule is outdated, missing coverage details, or does not span 24/7, indicating a potential gap in incident response readiness.

CHECK: Interview selected incident response team members and the incident response coordinator.
WHY: Direct interviews help confirm personnel availability, familiarity with the incident response process, and that assigned roles are actively recognized beyond the paper policy.
HOW: Ask questions about their roles during off-hour incidents, verify that they know how to activate alerts, and review any recorded after-action reports or drills.
FAIL: Inability to confirm the individual’s role or a lack of clarity about after-hours responsibilities, suggesting inadequate implementation.

CHECK: Review incident response logs from key systems (e.g., SIEM, ticketing systems) to validate actual after-hours incident responses.
WHY: Real-world operational evidence shows that alerts generated outside business hours are indeed met with appropriate responses by designated personnel.
HOW: Examine timestamps, response times, and documented remedial actions for incidents occurring during off-hours. Correlate these logs with the published on-call schedule.
FAIL: Missing or inconsistent logs for after-hours incidents, or significant delays in response times.

CHECK: Verify documented training and drill exercises that include off-hours incident response scenarios.
WHY: Regular training and exercises ensure that personnel are not just nominally on-call but are prepared to react effectively, showing operational readiness.
HOW: Check records for recent drills or simulated incident response exercises, particularly those scheduled for off-hours, and review follow-up action items.
FAIL: No evidence of practical exercises for off-hour incidents or outdated training records.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Ask to see a recent after-hours incident log and directly compare it with the on-call schedule for immediate correlation. A consistent match strongly indicates active monitoring and response.
• Critical Indicator: Ensure that incident response records reflect not just receipt of an alert, but measurable response actions. Delays or lack of documented remediation signal poor operational effectiveness.
• Detail Focus: Assess the clarity of escalation paths and the quality of after-hours communication. Vague or missing instructions during an off-hour incident may indicate a nominal implementation.

By following these validation steps, auditors can assess not only that an on-paper policy exists but that it is enacted effectively in operational practice, ensuring round-the-clock incident response capability.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10.3 Specific personnel are designated to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to suspected or confirmed security incidents.

Customized Approach Objective
Incidents are responded to immediately where appropriate.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.10.3 Examine documentation and interview responsible personnel occupying designated roles to verify that specific personnel are designated to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to security incidents.

Purpose
An incident could occur at any time, therefore if a person who is trained in incident response and familiar with the entity’s plan is available when an incident is detected, the entity’s ability to correctly respond to the incident is increased.

Good Practice
Often, specific personnel are designated to be part of a security incident response team, with the team having overall responsibility for responding to incidents (perhaps on a rotating schedule basis) and managing those incidents in accordance with the plan. The incident response team can consist of core members who are permanently assigned or "on-demand" personnel who may be called up as necessary, depending on their expertise and the specifics of the incident.

Having available resources to respond quickly to incidents minimizes disruption to the organization. 

Examples of types of activity the team or individuals should respond to include any evidence of unauthorized activity, detection of unauthorized wireless access points, critical IDS alerts, and reports of unauthorized critical system or content file changes.

title: The security incident response plan is reviewed, updated, and tested annually.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Evidence exists showing the incident response plan (IRP) has a version history, with entry dates for review, update, and test events. Artifacts such as meeting minutes, change logs, and simulation reports support this.
• Technical artifacts include tickets or system logs registering the IRP update and test, along with records from table-top exercises or simulated incident drills.
• Operationally, you’ll find a pattern of periodic training sessions, formal test reports, and post-mortem documentation that correlate with the annual schedule.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations sometimes produce a dated IRP document secured in an internal repository without actual evidence of exercise or testing.
• Surface-level compliance might include a one-off walkthrough or a presentation that claims to have tested the plan rather than rigorous, documented simulations.
• Frequently missed technical gaps include the absence of follow-up actions or lessons learned from tests, which indicates the process wasn’t genuinely iterative or comprehensive.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the existence of a documented version history for the IRP that lists review, update, and test dates.
WHY: This proves that the plan is being maintained on an annual basis and is continuously updated based on evolving threats or learned deficiencies.
HOW: Review the IRP document’s version history or change log in the document management system; look for timestamps and change summaries from the past 12–18 months.
FAIL: If the document lacks a clear version history or if timestamps are older than 12 months, it suggests the plan has not been reviewed or updated as required.

CHECK: Interview key personnel (e.g., incident response team members, security managers) to confirm that an annual test—such as a table-top exercise or simulation—was conducted.
WHY: Personal testimonies and corroborating evidence from multiple team members validate that the IRP testing is not just documented but actually executed.
HOW: Request a schedule or calendar invite of the exercise, validate meeting minutes, and follow up with team members about their roles and lessons learned.
FAIL: Inconsistent or vague responses, or lack of recollection of a formal test, indicate potential non-compliance.

CHECK: Review after-action reports or debrief documents generated from the most recent IRP test.
WHY: Detailed after-action reports confirm that the simulation was comprehensive and that identified gaps led to actionable improvements.
HOW: Obtain copies of the test reports, noting identified deficiencies, action plan timelines, and any updates applied as a result.
FAIL: Absence of such reports, or reports that merely acknowledge testing without actionable outcomes, signals inadequate testing practices.

CHECK: Inspect change management systems or ticketing logs to determine if updates to the IRP were formally recorded, reviewed, and approved.
WHY: This system-generated evidence limits the risk of faked compliance by proving that the update and test processes were tracked in an auditable way.
HOW: Extract logs showing IRP-related change tickets or approval workflows from the relevant IT systems, verifying dates and details of modifications.
FAIL: Lack of audit trail or change entries pertaining to the IRP in the past 12 months suggests the plan might not have been maintained properly.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcuts: Check the version control or document management system first; outdated timestamps can quickly flag a problem.
• Critical Indicator: Absence of a robust, interactive exercise (like a simulated incident with after-action analysis) is a red flag.
• Details that Matter Most: Look for comprehensive after-action reports and clear remediation steps originating from the identified lapses in the test exercises. Simple sign-off documents without detailed follow-up are not sufficient.
• Remember: Direct evidence from systems (logs, tickets) combined with corroborated interviews provides the strongest assurance that the IRP is kept current and operationally tested as mandated.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10.2 At least once every 12 months, the security incident response plan is:
- Reviewed and the content is updated as needed.
- Tested, including all elements listed in Requirement 12.10.1.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.10.2 Interview personnel and review documentation to verify that, at least once every 12 months, the security incident response plan is:
- Reviewed and updated as needed.
- Tested, including all elements listed in Requirement 12.10.1.

Customized Approach Objective
The incident response plan is kept current and tested periodically.

Purpose
Proper testing of the security incident response plan can identify broken business processes and ensure key steps are not missed, which could result in increased exposure during an incident. Periodic testing of the plan ensures that the processes remain viable, as well as ensuring that all relevant personnel in the organization are familiar with the plan.

Good Practice
The test of the incident response plan can include simulated incidents and the corresponding responses in the form of a "table-top exercise" that includes participation by relevant personnel. A review of the incident and the quality of the response can provide entities with the assurance that all required elements are included in the plan.

title: An incident response plan is in place and ready to be activated.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes a documented incident response plan that is easily accessible, version-controlled, and includes details like roles, responsibilities, communication strategies, and notification procedures. Updated contact lists, specific containment, mitigation steps, and recovery procedures are key indicators.
• Technical artifacts include incident logs, drill or test records, after-action reports, and email or ticketing system records that confirm the plan has been operationally activated. Artifacts showing periodic reviews and updates are essential.
• Operational patterns such as regular testing of the plan (exercises or simulated responses), immediate follow-up on alerts, and evidence of coordination with external stakeholders are indicators of active implementation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might include the incident response plan in documentation without evidence of active testing or communication exercises. Simply having the plan on file without drill logs or updated contact info can be a sign of faked compliance.
• A surface-level implementation might consist solely of high-level procedural text that lacks granular details like specific roles, escalation paths, or documented containment procedures. It might also omit critical contact updates or not cover all critical system components.
• Frequently missed technical gaps include lack of integration with actual alert mechanisms, absence of drill logs or incident reports that prove the plan’s operational readiness, and incomplete linkage to legal reporting requirements or external contacts.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify the existence and completeness of the incident response plan.
WHY: A thorough plan that incorporates roles, responsibilities, communication strategies, and specific procedures for various incident types proves that the organization has planned for incident response.
HOW: Request the latest copy of the incident response plan, ensure it includes details on containment, mitigation, business continuity, data backup, and compliance with notification requirements. Cross-check with industry frameworks or payment brand guidelines.
FAIL: Missing sections in the plan, absence of documented procedures for mitigation, or lack of coverage for specific key areas (e.g., backup processes, legal reporting requirements) indicate a failure to meet requirements.

CHECK: Confirm that the incident response plan is regularly updated and contains current contact information.
WHY: Up-to-date contacts and version-controlled documentation demonstrate operational readiness and the ability to activate the plan effectively during an incident.
HOW: Review the plan’s version history and update logs. Interview responsible personnel to validate that contact lists and escalation matrices are maintained. Look for evidence of periodic review meetings or communications about updates.
FAIL: Outdated or missing contact information and an absence of revision records undermine the plan’s practical readiness.

CHECK: Review evidence of incident response testing or exercises.
WHY: Drills or simulation exercises provide tangible proof that the plan is not just theoretical but actively practiced, ensuring that team members are prepared to respond to incidents.
HOW: Examine drill records, after-action reports, or logs from simulated incidents. Interview stakeholders responsible for executing the plan and confirm that exercises are regularly and systematically conducted.
FAIL: No evidence of testing activities or repeated failures in drills indicates a potential gap in the plan’s practical applicability.

CHECK: Analyze historical incident logs and response records.
WHY: Real-world execution of the incident response plan, even if only partial, establishes that the plan is operational and effective in addressing incidents.
HOW: Review incident ticketing systems, logs, and reports from recently handled incidents. Verify the timeline of responses and actions taken to contain and remediate incidents. Confirm alignment with documented procedures in the plan.
FAIL: Lack of any recorded incident responses or records that do not match documented procedures suggests the plan may not be active or properly followed.

EXPERT TIPS
• Focus your validation efforts on operational evidence such as updated drill logs, version control of the incident response plan, and real incident follow-up reports rather than solely on the existence of documentation.
• Cross-check the plan with actual execution: if the plan includes vendor notifications and legal reporting steps, verify through interviews and third-party confirmations that these channels have been tested or activated.
• A critical indicator of poor implementation is a disconnect between documented procedures and actual incident handling; always check for reconciliation between policy and practice.
• Use automated tools where possible to review system logs and incident tracking systems to corroborate that response actions were timely and adhered to the plan.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.10 Suspected and confirmed security incidents that could impact the CDE are responded to immediately.

12.10.1 An incident response plan exists and is ready to be activated in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident. The plan includes, but is not limited to:
• Roles, responsibilities, and communication and contact strategies in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident, including notification of payment brands and acquirers, at a minimum.
• Incident response procedures with specific containment and mitigation activities for different types of incidents.
• Business recovery and continuity procedures.
• Data backup processes.
• Analysis of legal requirements for reporting compromises.
• Coverage and responses of all critical system components.
• Reference or inclusion of incident response procedures from the payment brands.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.10.1.a Examine the incident response plan to verify that the plan exists and includes at least the elements specified in this requirement.

12.10.1.b Interview personnel and examine documentation from previously reported incidents or alerts to verify that the documented incident response plan and procedures were followed.

Purpose
Without a comprehensive incident response plan that is properly disseminated, read, and understood by the parties responsible, confusion and lack of a unified response could create further downtime for the business, unnecessary public media exposure, as well as risk of financial and/or reputational loss and legal liabilities.

Good Practice
The incident response plan should be thorough and contain all the key elements for stakeholders (for example, legal, communications) to allow the entity to respond effectively in the event of a breach that could impact account data. It is important to keep the plan up to date with current contact information of all individuals designated as having a role in incident response. Other relevant parties for notifications may include customers, financial institutions (acquirers and issuers), and business partners.
Entities should consider how to address all compromises of data within the CDE in their incident response plans, including compromises to account data, wireless encryption keys, encryption keys used for transmission and storage or account data or cardholder data, etc.

title: TPSPs support customer information requests on compliance and responsibilities.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying that TPSPs support their customers’ information requests regarding compliance status and shared responsibilities (per PCI DSS req-12.9.2):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
 • Visible evidence includes maintaining a current Attestation of Compliance (AOC) or other validated reports shared with customers. 
 • Technical artifacts such as timestamped distribution logs, customer service request tracking systems, and email records with compliance information attachments demonstrate compliance. 
 • Operational patterns include regular, documented processes and simulated requests that show response timeliness and detailed remediation steps when needed.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
 • Organizations sometimes merely produce static, outdated scans of documents or PDFs that state compliance without real-time data or assurance the scope covers all customer services.
 • Surface-level implementations might include having a generic policy document that is never reinforced by actual transaction-level evidence or logs, creating a false sense of compliance.
 • Frequently missed technical gaps include failure to keep evidence current (e.g., using an old AOC) or not distinguishing between TPSP responsibilities vs. customer responsibilities, which can mislead customers.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify the availability and currency of the TPSP’s PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance or equivalent evidence provided to customers.
WHY: A current AOC or alternate evidence clearly demonstrates that the TPSP has been validated against PCI DSS requirements and covers all relevant services.
HOW: Request a sample compliance report/AOC and check its effective date and scope; compare it against the service agreements for each customer.
FAIL: The documentation is outdated, missing scope details, or not available at all.

CHECK: Assess the documented process that manages and tracks customer requests for compliance information.
WHY: Documented, repeatable processes backed by system logs show that the TPSP actively supports customer compliance efforts.
HOW: Review the flowcharts/policy documentation and sample logs of customer requests with timestamps and responses. Verify that requests for 12.8.4 and 12.8.5 requirements are tracked and fulfilled.
FAIL: No documented process exists or the logs do not reflect actual operational requests and responses.

CHECK: Test the request procedure by simulating a customer inquiry for compliance information.
WHY: A live simulation ensures that the system works as designed, with timely and correct information provided to customers.
HOW: Initiate a formal request from a test account (if allowed) and then track the response including delivery of separate details on compliance status and segmentation of responsibilities (TPSP vs. customer). 
FAIL: The simulated request results in delayed, generic, or incomplete responses lacking needed detail on shared responsibilities.

CHECK: Interview key personnel and verify internal training records regarding handling customer inquiries on compliance matters.
WHY: Personnel awareness ensures that policies and procedures are effectively operationalized rather than merely existing on paper.
HOW: Conduct interviews with compliance officers or customer support teams and review training documentation and periodic review logs for how compliance data is managed and distributed.
FAIL: Interviewees are unable to detail the process, or training records are missing/inadequate.

4. EXPERT TIPS
 • A quick shortcut is to review any recent evidence of customer communications or published notices on the provider’s website regarding compliance support. Outdated or no evidence suggests poor implementation.
 • Pay special attention to evidentiary gaps – for instance, if the AOC does not reference all customer-facing services or the request process lacks clear segregation of typically shared responsibilities.
 • In practice, the critical indicator of a robust implementation is the consistency between the documented process and the actual operational logs; any mismatch indicates a superficial, paper-based compliance attempt.
 • Always verify that the documentation emphasizes responsibilities – customers often need explicit clarification on what the TPSP covers versus what remains their responsibility.

This structured approach, emphasizing both real-time evidence and practical, operational checks, provides a comprehensive audit methodology for ensuring TPSPs adequately support customer requests for compliance information.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.9.2 Additional requirement for service providers only: TPSPs support their customers’ requests for information to meet Requirements 12.8.4 and 12.8.5 by providing the following upon customer request:
- PCI DSS compliance status information (Requirement 12.8.4).
- Information about which PCI DSS requirements are the responsibility of the TPSP and which are the responsibility of the customer, including any shared responsibilities (Requirement 12.8.5), for any service the TPSP provides that meets a PCI DSS requirement(s) on behalf of customers or that can impact security of customers’ cardholder data or sensitive authentication data.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.9.2 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Examine policies and procedures to verify processes are defined for the TPSPs to support customers’ request for information to meet Requirements 12.8.4 and 12.8.5 in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose
If a TPSP does not provide the necessary information to enable its customers to meet their security and compliance requirements, the customers will not be able to protect cardholder data nor meet their own contractual obligations.

Good Practice
If a TPSP has a PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance (AOC), the expectation is that the TPSP should provide that to customers upon request to demonstrate their PCI DSS compliance status. If the TPSP did not undergo a PCI DSS assessment, they may be able to provide other sufficient evidence to demonstrate that it has met the applicable requirements without undergoing a formal compliance validation. For example, the TPSP can provide specific evidence to the entity’s assessor so the assessor can confirm applicable requirements are met. Alternatively, the TPSP can elect to undergo multiple on-demand assessments by each of its customers’ assessors, with each assessment targeted to confirm that applicable requirements are met.

TPSPs should provide sufficient evidence to their customers to verify that the scope of the TPSP’s PCI DSS assessment covered the services applicable to the customer and that the relevant PCI DSS requirements were examined and determined to be in place.

Customized Approach Objective
TPSPs provide information as needed to support their customers’ PCI DSS compliance efforts.

title: TPSPs provide written acknowledgments of their security responsibilities.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Signed written agreements or electronic records containing explicit language that acknowledges the TPSP’s responsibility for the security of account data. These documents should be version‐controlled and clearly indicate the effective date, signatures (or authenticated electronic acceptance), and specific commitments regarding PCI DSS responsibilities.
• Technical Artifacts: Contract templates, policy repositories, and audit logs in a contract management system that store evidence of when and how these acknowledgments were provided. Look for metadata evidencing review and approval processes.
• Operational Patterns: Regular reviews of written agreements as part of ongoing compliance assessments; automated reminders or workflows that trigger update and re-signing of acknowledgments when changes occur in the service or compliance framework.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations may display policy documents or generic customer contracts that include boilerplate language without ensuring the specific PCI DSS acknowledgment elements are present and committed by authorized representatives.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Use of outdated templates or agreements that contain vague responsibility clauses—which may look compliant on paper but lack the detailed acknowledgment of the TPSP’s security responsibilities towards account data handling.
• Technical Gaps: Failure to maintain a centralized, traceable repository of executed agreements, leaving gaps in audit trails that confirm contractual acceptance and periodic reviews.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review a sample of executed agreements from different TPSPs.
   WHY: Confirm that the agreements contain explicit language acknowledging the TPSP’s security responsibilities for account data.
   HOW: Request and examine physical or digital copies, ensuring the document includes dates, signatures (or their electronic equivalent), and specific reference to PCI DSS responsibilities.
   FAIL: Absence of explicit acknowledgment language, missing signature/approval evidence, or outdated templates that don’t match current PCI DSS requirements.

2. CHECK: Inspect the TPSP’s internal policy and procedure documentation related to customer engagement.
   WHY: To validate that procedures and templates are defined to provide the necessary written acknowledgments as a standardized practice.
   HOW: Access documented policies, review templates used for customer agreements, and verify that these include the PCI DSS acknowledgment clause. Confirm version control and update schedules.
   FAIL: Policies or procedures that lack details on how and when written acknowledgments are provided, or if templates omit the specific responsibilities highlighted by PCI DSS.

3. CHECK: Verify audit logs or contract management tool records for tracking the execution and periodic review of these agreements.
   WHY: Consistent operational evidence that acknowledgments are actively managed rather than relying solely on archived documents.
   HOW: Use automated review tools to extract records showing contract signing dates, renewals, and audits performed on agreement compliance.
   FAIL: No audit trail exists, or there are discrepancies in the execution timing and review processes indicating lapses in maintaining current agreements.

4. CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for TPSP relationship management to confirm the operational practices around obtaining and maintaining written acknowledgments.
   WHY: Verbal confirmation complements documentation, ensuring that operational processes align with documented procedures and that acknowledgments are enforced consistently.
   HOW: Discuss how the organization verifies the presence of the acknowledgment before engaging with customers, and probe for any exceptions or remediation steps in the past.
   FAIL: Inconsistent responses or reliance solely on seasonal reviews that do not match operational practices, suggesting potential gaps in ongoing compliance.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus first on the contract management system and review meta-information for timely renewals and updates; digital signatures are often harder to fake.
• Critical Indicators: Look for explicit language within the contracts that outline not only possession but also the processing, transmission, or storage of cardholder data – a missing element here could indicate poor implementation.
• Details That Matter: Ensure the language matches PCI DSS requirements and that any deviations are well-documented with risk reviews. Valid contracts should also mention periodic reviews and updates reflecting changes in compliance requirements.
• Reminder: Even if the documentation exists, cross-referencing with interviews and system logs can provide a deeper level of assurance that the policy is actively implemented and not just present on paper.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.9 Third-party service providers (TPSPs) support their customers’ PCI DSS compliance.

Additional requirement for service providers only:
12.9.1 TPSPs provide written agreements to customers that include acknowledgments that TPSPs are responsible for the security of account data the TPSP possesses or otherwise stores, processes, or transmits on behalf of the customer, or to the extent that the TPSP could impact the security of the customer’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures 
12.9.1.a Examine TPSP policies, procedures, and templates used for written agreements to verify procedures are defined for the TPSP to provide written acknowledgments to customers in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose
In conjunction with Requirement 12.8.2, this requirement is intended to promote a consistent level of understanding between TPSPs and their customers about their applicable PCI DSS responsibilities. The acknowledgment from the TPSP evidences the TPSP’s commitment to maintaining proper security of the account data that it obtains from its customers.

The TPSP’s internal policies and procedures related to their customer engagement process and any templates used for written agreements should include provision of an applicable PCI DSS acknowledgment to its customers. The method by which the TPSP provides written acknowledgment should be agreed between the provider and its customers.

title: Information is maintained regarding PCI DSS responsibilities between TPSPs and the entity.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Documented evidence such as a responsibility matrix or detailed spreadsheet that clearly maps each applicable PCI DSS requirement to the entity, TPSP, or shared responsibility. This matrix is often stored in a centralized, version-controlled repository.
• Records of periodic review meetings (e.g., minutes, email confirmations, change logs) that include updates on responsibility assignments between the entity and TPSP.
• Visible processes in operational logs and change management systems that indicate the maintenance and periodic review of responsibilities.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Relying solely on outdated policy documents that mention that responsibilities are assigned without having a current, dynamic matrix managed by an end-to-end process.
• Minimal or superficial matrices that only include a few requirements instead of a comprehensive coverage, often created as a one-time exercise with no evidence of periodic updates.
• Verbal assurances or interviews that lack corroborating technical artifacts; reliance on documentation created only for audit purposes without regular updates.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Existence of a consolidated responsibility matrix/spreadsheet mapping all PCI DSS requirements to either the entity, the TPSP, or shared responsibility.
   WHY: A comprehensive matrix demonstrates that the entity has identified and documented the division of responsibilities in a detailed manner.
   HOW: Request the most recent version of the matrix; verify that it includes all applicable PCI DSS requirements and clearly denotes distribution of responsibilities. Check version history to ensure periodic updates.
   FAIL: If the matrix is missing key requirements, appears outdated, or cannot be provided in a comprehensive form, this indicates failure.

2. CHECK: Evidence of periodic review and update processes for the responsibility matrix.
   WHY: Regular reviews ensure that any changes in TPSP contracts, environment, or PCI DSS requirements are properly reflected.
   HOW: Look for documented review records (e.g., meeting minutes, change logs, sign-offs) and interview personnel to confirm that a regular schedule (annually or per contract change) exists.
   FAIL: Absence of review records or evidence that the document is static over an extended period suggests non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Verification during interviews that personnel understand the distinction between responsibilities managed by the TPSP versus those managed by the entity.
   WHY: Knowledge among operational staff confirms that the documented matrix is part of the operational landscape and is not just a paper exercise.
   HOW: Conduct interviews with both management and operational teams, cross-check their awareness with documented responsibilities in the matrix.
   FAIL: If staff are unclear or unable to explain responsibility divisions, it indicates that the documented process may not be effectively implemented.

4. CHECK: Inspection of change management systems to correlate updates in the responsibility assignments with changes in third-party service agreements or system configurations.
   WHY: Correlation with change management logs provides operational evidence that the responsibilities are actively managed as the environment evolves.
   HOW: Review change management records for instances where shifts in responsibilities have been recorded; compare these records with the updated responsibility matrix.
   FAIL: Mismatches between change records and the matrix or lack thereof indicates that the process is not dynamic or consistently maintained.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcut: Ask for the most recent contract or amendment with a TPSP and then quickly reference the matrix to see if shared responsibilities are explicitly mapped.
• Critical Indicator of Poor Implementation: A matrix that is merely a checklist without context (e.g., no version, no links to contractual documentation) is a red flag.
• Detail That Matters Most: Ensure every PCI DSS requirement, including sub-requirements, is individually addressed. Gaps on lower-level requirements might indicate deeper operational issues.
• Consider the integration with change management: Real effective implementations show tight integration between contract updates, change management, and the responsibility matrix maintenance.

Summary: The validation should focus on both the existence of a well-documented and comprehensive matrix and on the operational processes that validate its regular updates and practical usage. Verifying against both technical artifacts and personnel understanding is crucial to ensure true operational compliance and not just a documented policy.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.8.5  Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.8.5.a  Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined to maintain information about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity.

12.8.5.b  Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify the entity maintains information about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between both entities.

Customized Approach Objective
Records detailing the PCI DSS requirements and related system components for which each TPSP is solely or jointly responsible, are maintained and reviewed periodically.

Guidance
Purpose
It is important that the entity understands which PCI DSS requirements and sub-requirements its TPSPs have agreed to meet, which requirements are shared between the TPSP and the entity, and for those that are shared, specifics about how the requirements are shared and which entity is responsible for meeting each sub-requirement.

Good Practice
Entities can document these responsibilities via a matrix that identifies all applicable PCI DSS requirements and indicates for each requirement whether the entity or TPSP is responsible for meeting that requirement or whether it is a shared responsibility.

title: Program to monitor TPSPs’ PCI DSS compliance is implemented.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes updated TPSP inventory lists that include documented PCI DSS compliance check dates, automated notifications, and monitoring logs with timestamped entries. 
• Technical artifacts: screenshots or exports of dashboards showing compliance status, audit logs from the compliance monitoring system, and evidence of scheduled recurring scans or reviews. 
• Operational patterns: Regular meeting minutes or action item trackers that reference periodic reviews of TPSPs’ PCI DSS compliance; evidence of follow-ups when a TPSP is found non-compliant.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes merely compile TPSP lists and attach self-attestation certificates from providers without verifying the validity or performing independent checks. 
• Relying on outdated or static documentation (policies and procedures only) without supporting transactional evidence or automated compliance validation is a common surface-level implementation.
• Failing to track the frequency of checks (e.g., using documentation that cites an annual review without artifacts proving that reviews actually occurred within the required timeframe).

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that the organization maintains an updated inventory of TPSPs that includes documented dates and details of PCI DSS compliance checks.
   WHY: A current and detailed TPSP inventory demonstrates an active monitoring program.
   HOW: Review the inventory list and cross-check with system logs to confirm that each provider’s compliance status was updated at least once in the past 12 months.
   FAIL: If the inventory lacks dates or details of the review, or if dates exceed 12 months without updates.

2. CHECK: Inspect system-generated logs or dashboards which record periodic monitoring of TPSPs’ PCI DSS compliance status.
   WHY: Automated, timestamped logs provide observable and indisputable evidence of periodic compliance checks.
   HOW: Access the compliance monitoring tool or dashboard, verify the timestamps on compliance reports, and correlate with scheduled review dates.
   FAIL: Absence of timestamped logs, inconsistent records, or evidence that reviews are not conducted within the mandated period.

3. CHECK: Interview responsible personnel and match interview evidence with documented proof (e.g., meeting minutes, review reports) to verify that TPSP compliance reviews occur at least annually.
   WHY: Direct interviews backed by independent documentation help confirm operational effectiveness beyond mere policy existence.
   HOW: Interview compliance managers, review meeting records, and check for documented action items corresponding to TPSP monitoring.
   FAIL: If responsible personnel are unable to provide consistent details or supporting documents that confirm actual periodic monitoring.

4. CHECK: Confirm the existence and use of automated or manual processes that trigger compliance review notifications when TPSP compliance evidence is due.
   WHY: Automated reminders or scheduled check processes underscore that the review is an integrated part of the operational workflow.
   HOW: Request demonstrations of system settings or calendar events that automate the review process, and check audit trails for notification records.
   FAIL: Lack of automated triggers or absence of notification evidence, suggesting the process might not be reliably executed.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Ask for a recent sample of TPSP compliance reports and immediately verify the date stamps to test if annual check requirements are met.
• Critical Indicator: A common red flag is if the TPSP monitoring program only exists in policy without corresponding operational evidence (e.g., no current logs or dated inventory records).
• Detail to Note: Ensure that each TPSP’s scope of delivered services is considered; monitoring must be specific to services in scope for the PCI DSS assessment, not a general self-attestation.
• Additional Tip: Confirm integration between the compliance monitoring solution and the organization’s incident management system to ensure follow-up on non-compliance instances.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.8.4 A program is implemented to monitor TPSPs' PCI DSS compliance status at least once every 12 months.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.8.4.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined to monitor TPSPs' PCI DSS compliance status at least once every 12 months.

12.8.4.b Examine documentation and interview responsible personnel to verify that the PCI DSS compliance status of each TPSP is monitored at least once every 12 months.

Customized Approach Objective
The PCI DSS compliance status of TPSPs is verified periodically.

Guidance
Purpose
Knowing the PCI DSS compliance status of all engaged TPSPs provides assurance and awareness about whether they comply with the requirements applicable to the services they offer to the organization.

Good Practice
If the TPSP offers a variety of services, the compliance status the entity monitors should be specific to those services delivered to the entity and those services in scope for the entity's PCI DSS assessment.

title: Process for engaging TPSPs is established.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes detailed documented policies and procedures specifically outlining the process for engaging Third-Party Service Providers (TPSPs), including criteria for due diligence.
• Technical artifacts such as logged records of TPSP evaluations, risk assessments, and approval workflows show that due diligence has been actively executed.
• Operational patterns include regular reviews of TPSP engagements, updated questionnaires, and official sign-offs from risk management and procurement teams for each TPSP engagement.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might rely solely on dated or generic policies without evidence that due diligence is actively performed on each TPSP.
• Providing only documentation without corroborating operational evidence (e.g., logs, real contract reviews, risk assessment records) can create a false impression of compliance.
• Some may simulate TPSP assessments with superficial questionnaires that are not aligned with the actual security posture of the TPSP, missing key risk parameters.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that documented procedures exist which detail the process for engaging TPSPs, including due diligence criteria.
WHY: This proves that a formal framework is in place to evaluate TPSPs and mandates specific due diligence checks.
HOW: Review relevant policy documents and process flowcharts; check that they include distinct due diligence steps, roles, and responsibilities.
FAIL: Absence of detailed procedures or reliance on overly generic policies that do not specifically address TPSP due diligence.

CHECK: Inspect records or logs of recent TPSP engagements and the associated due diligence assessments.
WHY: Operational evidence such as records validates that the process is actively followed, not just documented in policy.
HOW: Request evidence such as signed approval forms, risk assessment reports, and evaluation logs for TPSPs engaged in the last review period.
FAIL: Missing or outdated records suggesting that the process isn’t regularly applied or monitored.

CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for TPSP engagements (e.g., procurement, legal, risk management) to confirm their understanding and practical application of the due diligence process.
WHY: Interviews corroborate that the individuals tasked with TPSP assessments are fully aware of and properly execute the defined process.
HOW: Prepare targeted questions about the steps in the TPSP engagement process, criteria for due diligence, and how findings are documented.
FAIL: Inconsistent or vague responses indicating a lack of operational understanding or ad-hoc implementation.

CHECK: Review contract templates and communication records to verify that due diligence outcomes are incorporated into TPSP engagements.
WHY: Embedding due diligence results in contracts confirms that the process impacts actual decision-making and risk acceptance.
HOW: Examine a sample of contracts and associated negotiation emails to check for clauses or references to TPSP risk assessments and due diligence outcomes.
FAIL: Absence of risk-related clauses or missing references in contracts, suggesting due diligence is not a binding part of the engagement process.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Look for evidence of periodic reviews or updates to the TPSP engagement process, as this indicates responsiveness to evolving threat landscapes.
• Focus on recent engagement examples; stale evidence could indicate that the process is not maintained or only performed selectively.
• Pay attention to how exceptions or high-risk TPSPs are handled—detailed remediation or enhanced due diligence steps are critical indicators of a robust process.
• Validate that the process is integrated across departments (e.g., IT security, procurement, and legal) to ensure that TPSP engagements are comprehensively scrutinized.

By following these practical validation checks, you can confirm that the organization has not only established but is also effectively operating a robust process to engage TPSPs with comprehensive due diligence.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.8.3 An established process is implemented for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.8.3.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement.

12.8.3.b Examine evidence and interview responsible personnel to verify the process for engaging TPSPs includes proper due diligence prior to engagement.

Customized Approach Objective
The capability, intent, and resources of a prospective TPSP to adequately protect account data are assessed before the TPSP is engaged.

title: TPSP written agreements are maintained.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Written TPSP agreements are stored in a central, secure repository (electronic or paper) that is regularly reviewed and updated.
   • Each agreement includes explicit acknowledgment language regarding the TPSP’s responsibility for securing cardholder data or sensitive authentication data.
   • Operational patterns include periodic contract reviews and renewal processes that are logged and auditable.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply archive outdated or incomplete agreements without verifying that the required security commitment language is present.
   • Relying solely on documented policies or checklists, without evidence of current contractual signatures and update schedules, can give a false sense of compliance.
   • Agreements that look superficially compliant (e.g., missing explicit language or having ambiguous language) are commonly used to claim compliance without ensuring actual risk mitigation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence and currency of a centralized repository containing all written agreements with TPSPs.
   WHY: A maintained and updated repository demonstrates active and ongoing contractual management.
   HOW: Inspect the repository (electronic document management system or physical files) for dated agreements; check metadata for recent reviews and approvals.
   FAIL: Absence of a central repository or evidence that agreements have not been updated in line with the defined review cycle.

   CHECK: Examine a sample of TPSP agreements to confirm they include the required acknowledgment clauses regarding responsibilities for securing account data.
   WHY: Inclusion of specific acknowledgment language ensures TPSPs are contractually bound to meet PCI DSS obligations.
   HOW: Open and review a representative sample of contracts; confirm they reference responsibilities for protecting cardholder and/or sensitive authentication data, aligning with PCI DSS language.
   FAIL: Agreements that lack the explicit acknowledgment terms or reference outdated contractual language.

   CHECK: Interview stakeholders (e.g., contracting, compliance, and information security teams) to confirm they follow a defined process for maintaining and updating TPSP agreements.
   WHY: Direct interviews verify that the written policies are operationalized and integrated into routine vendor management practices.
   HOW: Ask for descriptions of the review, update, and renewal processes for TPSP agreements; verify that periodic reviews are scheduled and tracked.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or vague descriptions of the process, suggesting that the documentation is not aligned with actual operational practices.

   CHECK: Review change logs or audit trails in the document management system to confirm timely updates of TPSP agreements in response to changes in contractual or compliance requirements.
   WHY: Automated logs or audit trails provide evidence of ongoing commitment and adaptation to changing security requirements.
   HOW: Check the version history and change logs for multiple TPSP agreements to identify recent updates and approvals.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete change logs, or evidence that agreements are rarely updated despite changes in processes or standards.

4. EXPERT TIPS
