   • Quick verification tip: If the contract repository is managed using a formal system (with version control), look for regular update intervals and automated alerts – a sign that the process is institutionalized.
   • Critical indicators of poor implementation include contracts that are one-time snapshots without periodic review dates or stakeholder sign-offs.
   • In practice, the details in acknowledgment clauses (e.g., explicit responsibilities, reference to penalty clauses for non-compliance) are crucial – don’t let generic language mask weak commitment.
   • When interviewing, pay close attention to whether staff can quickly provide concrete examples of recent contract renegotiations or updates, which indicates that the process is not just paper-compliant but is actively managed.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
12.8.2 Written agreements with TPSPs are maintained as follows:  
- Written agreements are maintained with all TPSPs with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of the CDE.  
- Written agreements include acknowledgments from TPSPs that TPSPs are responsible for the security of account data the TPSPs possess or otherwise store, process, or transmit on behalf of the entity, or to the extent that the TPSP could impact the security of the entity’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
12.8.2.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined to maintain written agreements with all TPSPs in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.  
12.8.2.b Examine written agreements with TPSPs to verify they are maintained in accordance with all elements as specified in this requirement.

Purpose
The written acknowledgment from a TPSP demonstrates its commitment to maintaining proper security of account data that it obtains from its customers and that the TPSP is fully aware of the assets that could be affected during the provisioning of the TPSP’s service. In conjunction with Requirement 12.9.1, this requirement is intended to promote a consistent level of understanding between parties about their applicable PCI DSS responsibilities. For example, the agreement may include the applicable PCI DSS requirements to be maintained as part of the provided service.

Customized Approach Objective
Records are maintained of each TPSP’s acknowledgment of its responsibility to protect account data.

title: A list of all third-party service providers with account data access or impact on security is maintained with service descriptions.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: A live, regularly updated spreadsheet or database exists that lists every third-party service provider (TPSP) with which account data is shared or which could affect account data security. The list includes service descriptions for each TPSP.
• Technical Artifacts: System configurations, asset inventory records, or dedicated vendor management databases which log TPSP details. Evidence of synchronization with ticketing systems or risk management tools can further confirm active maintenance.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent periodic reviews, change logs evidencing updates to the list when new TPSPs are engaged or existing ones altered, and audit trails showing changes in TPSP details.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Over-reliance on static, outdated policy documents with claims of an updated TPSP list, which haven’t been operationally checked. A centrally stored, unused document that is not reviewed regularly may be presented as compliance evidence.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Entries included without actionable details (e.g., generic names with no descriptions) or lists that are maintained as ad hoc records rather than through a formalized process. Some organizations might also simply reference vendor contracts without a consolidated or regularly updated list.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of integration with risk management systems and absence of automated mechanisms to flag changes in TPSP status. Failure to document service impacts and periodic reviews constitutes a common shortfall.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify existence of an actively maintained TPSP inventory list that includes service descriptions.
WHY: An actively maintained list confirms that the organization tracks all providers affecting account data security, not just documented policies.
HOW: Request a live copy or screen-share session of the current TPSP inventory; cross-check entries with recent change logs and risk management reports.
FAIL: Outdated, incomplete, or ad hoc compilations that do not include current service descriptions, or if the list is solely in policy documentation without evidence of operational updates.

CHECK: Review change logs or audit trails associated with the TPSP inventory.
WHY: Evidence of regular updates and reviews indicates active management of risk to account data and confirms operationalizing the control.
HOW: Inspect audit logs, ticketing systems, and periodic review documentation; interview responsible personnel to discuss update cycles.
FAIL: Absence of change records, irregular updates, or discrepancies between policy and actual operational practices.

CHECK: Cross-reference the list against contracts or service agreements with TPSPs.
WHY: Contracts or service level agreements should align with the inventory, confirming that all TPSPs with access or impact are documented.
HOW: Select a sample of TPSP entries from the inventory and verify against corresponding contractual documents to ensure each entry has a matching agreement with outlined service descriptions.
FAIL: Missing contract evidence for listed TPSPs or presence of TPSPs in contracts not recorded on the maintained list.

CHECK: Validate the integration of the TPSP list with risk assessment frameworks.
WHY: Integration helps ensure that any new or modified TPSP is evaluated for its risk impact on account data security, aligning the inventory with broader security controls.
HOW: Interview risk management teams; request documentation that shows the TPSP list is a component of periodic risk assessments; verify any escalation process documentation.
FAIL: Lack of integration in risk assessment reports or evidence that the list is treated as a standalone document without follow-up risk analysis.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Focus on the system or database view rather than solely relying on static policy documents; ask for evidence of automated alerts or reminders for periodic review.
• Critical Indicators: Look for detailed, individualized service descriptions and change records; the absence of these points to a superficial control implementation.
• Key Details Matter: Verify that the list not only includes TPSP names but also key attributes such as the type of data accessed, frequency of updates, and evidence of risk review. Watch out for generic entries like 'vendor X' without context or explanation.

This multi-faceted validation methodology provides a strong basis for ensuring that organizations truly manage risks associated with third-party service providers by maintaining a comprehensive and current TPSP inventory.
content:
12.8 Risk to information assets associated with third-party service provider (TPSP) relationships is managed.

### Defined Approach Requirements

12.8.1 A list of all third-party service providers (TPSPs) with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of account data is maintained, including a description for each of the services provided.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.8.1.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined to maintain a list of TPSPs, including a description for each of the services provided, for all TPSPs with whom account data is shared or that could affect the security of account data.

12.8.1.b Examine documentation to verify that a list of all TPSPs is maintained that includes a description of the services provided.

### Customized Approach Objective

Records are maintained of TPSPs and the services provided.

### Applicability Notes

The use of a PCI DSS compliant TPSP does not make an entity PCI DSS compliant, nor does it remove the entity’s responsibility for its own PCI DSS compliance.

### Purpose

Maintaining a list of all TPSPs identifies where potential risk extends outside the organization and defines the organization’s extended attack surface.

### Examples

Different types of TPSPs include those that:
- Store, process, or transmit account data on the entity’s behalf (such as payment gateways, payment processors, payment service providers (PSPs), and off-site storage providers).
- Manage system components included in the entity’s PCI DSS assessment (such as providers of network security control services, anti-malware services, and security incident and event management (SIEM); contact and call centers; web-hosting companies; and IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and FaaS cloud providers).
- Could impact the security of the entity’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data (such as vendors providing support via remote access, and bespoke software developers).

title: Potential personnel who will have access to the CDE are screened, within legal constraints, prior to hire.
methodology:
Control: PCI-DSS Req-12.7.1 – Screening of Potential Personnel with CDE Access

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: HR records and onboarding logs that confirm background screening was conducted prior to hire.
  WHY: This evidence shows that the organization actively screens potential personnel before granting access to the CDE.
  HOW: Look for digital records in the HR system indicating background check completion, including dates and results aligned with local legal constraints.
  FAIL: Absence of records, incomplete screening logs, or HR sign-offs that merely reference an internal policy without evidence of actual background checks.

• CHECK: System integration between HR and access control databases.
  WHY: Demonstrates that only screened personnel receive CDE access, ensuring real process linkage between employee onboarding and access provisioning.
  HOW: Verify automated audits or logs where HR screening records are correlated with system access assignments, and confirm the time stamp shows screening occurred prior to system access being granted.
  FAIL: Manual, unsystematic approvals or discrepancies between HR issued screening records and access control provisioning logs.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might maintain a screening policy document without applying it. For example, submitting a document that says 'all potential hires are screened' but lacking individual screening records.
• Relying solely on self-attestation by HR personnel, which can be faked with generic, non-specific sign-offs that do not tie to actual background check reports.
• Failing to update screening practices when personnel are transferred to roles with CDE access, thus bypassing re-screening requirements.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK 1: Review a random sample of new hire onboarding files for personnel granted CDE access. 
  WHY: The physical or digital records should show detailed screening results such as criminal record checks, credit checks, and reference checks as per the risk profile of the position.
  HOW: Access the HR management system and extract records for personnel designated for CDE access. Confirm that the screening details (date, type of check, outcome) are included.
  FAIL: Records missing screening details, dated after access was granted, or not linked to the specific individual’s role with CDE access.

• CHECK 2: Validate integration and audit logs between HR and access control systems.
  WHY: Automated logs correlate HR screening results with system access provisioning, ensuring that only pre-screened personnel have access to the CDE.
  HOW: Query system logs and audit trails in the access control system. Look for timestamps that match the pre-hire screening completion, and corroborate with HR records.
  FAIL: Audit entries missing timestamps, or discrepancies indicating that access may have been granted before screening completion.

• CHECK 3: Interview HR and system administrators about the operational process for screening candidates and linking screening outcomes to access rights.
  WHY: Direct interviews can reveal if there are workarounds or non-compliant practices that documentation alone may not show.
  HOW: Ask for a walkthrough of the hiring process, ensuring that screening outcomes are systematically reviewed before access is provisioned. Request demonstration of the integration between HR and access systems.
  FAIL: Over-reliance on manual approvals with no system validation, or inability to explain the workflow linking screening to CDE access.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• TIP: Look for a direct audit trail in the HR system that timestamps when a candidate’s background screening was completed relative to their access assignment. This linkage is a strong indicator of compliance.
• TIP: Pay special attention to positions with administrative or elevated access to the CDE. They should have more rigorous, detailed screening results compared to lower-risk roles.
• TIP: Ensure that the screening process is not just a checkbox; verify supporting evidence (e.g., third-party screening reports) which validate that background checks were substantive and compliant with local legal constraints.
• TIP: Check for periodic reviews of the screening process itself – an absence of re-certification or periodic audits can be a red flag.

This methodology ensures that merely having a screening policy is not enough – the control’s effectiveness is demonstrated via integrated, verifiable processes that actively enforce screening prior to granting CDE access.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## 12.7 Personnel are screened to reduce risks from insider threats.

### Defined Approach Requirements

**12.7.1** Potential personnel who will have access to the CDE are screened, within the constraints of local laws, prior to hire to minimize the risk of attacks from internal sources.

### Customized Approach Objective

The risk related to allowing new members of staff access to the CDE is understood and managed.

### Applicability Notes

For those potential personnel to be hired for positions such as store cashiers, who only have access to one card number at a time when facilitating a transaction, this requirement is a recommendation only.

---

## Purpose

Performing thorough screening prior to hiring potential personnel who are expected to be given access to the CDE provides entities with the information necessary to make informed risk decisions regarding personnel they hire that will have access to the CDE.

Other benefits of screening potential personnel include helping to ensure workplace safety and confirming information provided by prospective employees on their resumes.

## Good Practice

Entities should consider screening for existing personnel anytime they transfer into roles where they have access to the CDE from roles where they did not have this access.

To be effective, the level of screening should be appropriate for the position. For example, positions requiring greater responsibility or that have administrative access to critical data or systems may warrant more detailed or more frequent screening than positions with less responsibility and access.

## Examples

Screening options can include, as appropriate for the entity’s region, previous employment history, review of public information/social media resources, criminal record, credit history, and reference checks.

title: Security awareness training includes awareness of acceptable use of end-user technologies in accordance with Requirement 12.2.1.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes training modules (slides, videos, documents) that explicitly discuss the acceptable use of end-user technologies in line with Requirement 12.2.1.
   • Technical artifacts include Learning Management System (LMS) records, attendance logs, and quiz/assessment reports that verify employees have been exposed to the content.
   • Operational patterns showing periodic, recurring training updates and refresher sessions indicate the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply attach or reference a static acceptable use policy document without integrating it into interactive training sessions.
   • Superficial implementations often rely on one-off awareness sessions with no follow-up assessments or practical testing of employee knowledge.
   • Frequently missed technical gaps include failing to validate attendance logs or not correlating training content with updated policy revisions (e.g., updates to Requirement 12.2.1).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Training Curriculum Inclusion
   WHY: Ensuring the training materials explicitly cover the acceptable use of end-user technologies confirms that the critical content is addressed.
   HOW: Review the training content (slides, videos, documentation) from the LMS to confirm presence of sections dedicated to acceptable use in accordance with Requirement 12.2.1. Interview a sample of trainers and trainees for confirmation.
   FAIL: Absence of content or overly generic training materials that do not refer directly to acceptable use topics as aligned with Requirement 12.2.1.

   CHECK: Validate Training Participation and Completion Records
   WHY: Participation logs and completion records demonstrate that the training has been systematically deployed across the organization.
   HOW: Examine LMS records and attendance logs to confirm that all relevant employees have completed the designated training cycle, and look for timestamps that indicate recurring sessions.
   FAIL: Incomplete, outdated, or non-existent records which might indicate that the training has not been broadly or recently administered.

   CHECK: Assess Employee Awareness Through Testing or Surveys
   WHY: Post-training assessments or surveys provide observable evidence that employees have internalized the acceptable use practices.
   HOW: Evaluate quiz results or conduct a random sampling of employee interviews/surveys that include questions related to the acceptable use policy. Compare the results against expected knowledge levels.
   FAIL: Poor performance on assessments, absence of testing, or survey results that indicate employees are unaware of acceptable use responsibilities.

   CHECK: Conduct Random Interviews with Personnel
   WHY: Direct interviews can reveal the depth of understanding and practical awareness that may not be evident from documentation alone.
   HOW: Randomly select a sample of employees and inquire about their responsibilities for the acceptable use of end-user technologies and where they can seek guidance. Verify if their responses align with training content.
   FAIL: Responses that are vague, incorrect, or show a lack of practical understanding of the acceptable use principles as defined in Requirement 12.2.1.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcuts: Use automated reporting from the LMS and trigger alerts for non-completion rates to quickly identify gaps.
   • Critical indicators: Look for refresher training dates and updates in the training materials that correlate with revisions in the acceptable use policy.
   • Key Detail: Ensure that training is not simply a one-time event; evidence of recurring sessions and updated content is crucial to verify ongoing compliance.
   • Don’t rely solely on policy documents: Validate through observable, interactive evidence such as assessments and interviews which are harder to fake.

title: Security awareness training includes content on threats and vulnerabilities that impact cardholder data and sensitive authentication data.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for control req-12.6.3.1 (Security awareness training includes content on threats and vulnerabilities that impact cardholder data and sensitive authentication data), organized into practical signs of implementation, common evasions, concrete validation steps, and expert tips.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Updated training materials (slides, videos, course documents) clearly covering phishing, social engineering, and related vulnerabilities; announcements of refresher sessions; records of simulated phishing tests.
• Technical Artifacts: Logs from the Learning Management System (LMS) showing course enrollment and completion status; copies of recent training content with examples of phishing/social engineering; reports or metrics from periodic phishing simulation campaigns.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled training sessions and periodic re-assessments; incident reports where employees have reported suspicious emails; evidence of follow-up actions after simulations.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations may show a documented training policy or old training material that nominally covers the topics without updating content to reflect current threat profiles.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on one-time training for new hires without periodic refreshers or simulated exercises; using generic awareness content without industry-specific scenarios related to cardholder data.
• Frequently Missed Gaps: Lack of evidence linking training content directly with actual attack vectors; untracked or poorly documented training attendance and effectiveness metrics; no demonstration of hands-on or interactive elements (like phishing simulations).

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Training Content Review
- WHY: Ensures that the training material specifically covers phishing, social engineering, and other vulnerabilities impacting cardholder data and sensitive authentication data.
- HOW: Examine a sample of current training materials (slides, videos, course documents) to verify inclusion of targeted topics (e.g. how to identify and report phishing, social engineering tactics). Interview trainers or review course curriculum outlines.
- FAIL: Materials lack current examples on phishing/social engineering or are outdated and not aligned with recent threat trends.

CHECK 2: Training Participation and Recency
- WHY: Validates that all relevant personnel have completed the updated training in a timely fashion.
- HOW: Query the LMS or training management system for training logs showing attendance, completion dates, and recertification cycles. Sample employee records for recency of training.
- FAIL: Significant gaps or outdated training records; incomplete training of employees.

CHECK 3: Simulation and Test Evidence
- WHY: Verifies that the training is reinforced by practical exercises such as simulated phishing emails and awareness tests, indicating operational readiness.
- HOW: Review logs and reports of phishing simulation campaigns. Check for follow-up actions and improvements reported post simulation. Interview IT/security staff and a sample of employees regarding their experience with phishing tests.
- FAIL: Absence of simulation records or periodic testing, or simulations that have not been followed up with remedial training.

CHECK 4: Employee Awareness Validation
- WHY: Ensures that personnel understand how to detect and report potential phishing and social engineering attacks in practice.
- HOW: Conduct spot interviews with randomly selected personnel or use surveys to assess their recall of training details. Verify that they can articulate reporting mechanisms and immediate response actions.
- FAIL: Widespread inability among personnel to describe key elements of the training, particularly reporting procedures and recent phishing examples.

CHECK 5: Integration with Incident Response
- WHY: Demonstrates that training effectiveness translates into tangible operational outcomes such as increased reporting and efficient incident handling.
- HOW: Review incident response logs for examples where employees reported suspicious activity consistent with training topics. Correlate these reports with the training timeline.
- FAIL: Lack of reports or incongruous incident data that suggests employees did not apply the training in practice.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on LMS logs and phishing simulation outcomes since they are automated and hard to fake compared to static policy documents.
• Critical Indicators: Outdated training content, irregular recertification cycles, and low reporting rates post-training are red flags.
• Detail Matters: Ensure that training content details not only the identification of phishing/social engineering attacks, but also the exact reporting procedures and rapid follow-up actions.

Using these steps, auditors can determine whether the security awareness training is effectively addressing the real-world threats and vulnerabilities relevant to cardholder and sensitive authentication data, and is producing measurable improvements in employee behavior, rather than serving as a box-check exercise.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements

### 12.6.3.1 Security awareness training includes awareness of threats and vulnerabilities that could impact the security of cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data, including but not limited to:
- Phishing and related attacks.
- Social engineering.

## Customized Approach Objective

Personnel are knowledgeable about their own human vulnerabilities and how threat actors will attempt to exploit such vulnerabilities. Personnel are able to access assistance and guidance when required.

## Applicability Notes

See Requirement 5.4.1 for guidance on the difference between technical and automated controls to detect and protect users from phishing attacks, and this requirement for providing users security awareness training about phishing and social engineering. These are two separate and distinct requirements, and one is not met by implementing controls required by the other one. This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 12.6.3.1 Examine security awareness training content to verify it includes all elements specified in this requirement.

## Purpose

Educating personnel on how to detect, react to, and report potential phishing and related attacks and social engineering attempts is essential to minimizing the probability of successful attacks.

## Good Practice

An effective security awareness program should include examples of phishing emails and periodic testing to determine the prevalence of personnel reporting such attacks. Training material an entity can consider for this topic include:
- How to identify phishing and other social engineering attacks.
- How to react to suspected phishing and social engineering.
- Where and how to report suspected phishing and social engineering activity.

An emphasis on reporting allows the organization to reward positive behavior, to optimize technical defenses (see Requirement 5.4.1), and to take immediate action to remove similar phishing emails that evaded technical defenses from recipient inboxes.

title: Personnel receive security awareness training upon hire and at least once every 12 months using multiple methods.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-12.6.3:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes training attendance logs for both new hires and annual refreshers, as well as records showing that multiple communication methods (e.g., web-based modules, in-person sessions, printed materials) are used.
   • Technical artifacts include timestamps in learning management systems (LMS), electronic acknowledgment records stored in HR or security systems, and copies or screenshots of the training materials that demonstrate varied delivery methods.
   • Operational patterns to note are regularly scheduled training events (triggered by onboarding and annual cycles) and employee acknowledgments that are automatically captured.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply upload a generic training module or tick-off a training checklist without verifying actual user engagement, thus faking compliance.
   • Surface-level implementations include providing a one-size-fits-all presentation without interactive or varied content; employees may click through electronic acknowledgments without understanding.
   • Frequent gaps include missing validation that training content has been absorbed or failing to track refresher cycles for all personnel, particularly when role changes occur.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review LMS and HR system logs for evidence of security awareness training event records (both at new hire and annual intervals).
   WHY: Authentic timestamped training logs demonstrate that all personnel have completed the required training cycles.
   HOW: Extract a sample of records showing new hire training dates and periodic training renewal within the past 12 months; reconcile these with the employee roster.
   FAIL: Gaps or missing records for a significant portion of staff indicate check failure.

   CHECK: Verify electronic or written acknowledgment records indicating that personnel have reviewed and understood the organization’s security policies.
   WHY: Acknowledgment records provide evidence that the training content was not only delivered but also confirmed by the employees.
   HOW: Sample acknowledgment receipts from the training system, capture digital signatures, and cross-reference these with scheduled training dates.
   FAIL: Absence of such records or bulk acknowledgments submitted simultaneously around the same date can indicate superficial implementation.

   CHECK: Validate the diversity of training formats by reviewing security awareness training materials.
   WHY: Multiple communication methods (e.g., web-based content, in-person instruction, distributed posters or emails) ensure a broader reach and reinforce learning.
   HOW: Review the training syllabus, presentation slides, video content, and any printed materials; interview a few personnel to confirm they recall different training methods.
   FAIL: If only one mode of training delivery is evident (e.g., solely a PowerPoint presentation without any interactive or varied follow-up), this is a red flag.

   CHECK: Interview a sample of employees across various departments to confirm they not only attended the training but also understood key security responsibilities.
   WHY: Direct feedback from personnel validates the operational effectiveness of the training program beyond mere procedural ticking.
   HOW: Conduct brief interviews or surveys focusing on key training points such as their role in protecting cardholder data and awareness of security policies.
   FAIL: Uniform inability to recall training details or express understanding indicates inadequate training content retention and engagement.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Start by pulling system-generated training logs and acknowledgment receipts from the LMS. Spot check against HR records to quickly identify any discrepancies.
   • Indicator: Multiple training delivery methods should be clearly reflected in documented materials; the absence of varied methods (like only online modules) may suggest a weak program.
   • Detail Focus: Look for automated alerts or reminders for overdue training as they offer evidence that the system actively manages compliance.
   • Verify Role Transitions: Check that personnel changing roles also receive additional or tailored training, ensuring the training remains relevant to their impact on cardholder data security.

This methodology emphasizes gathering and analyzing operational evidence and technical artifacts that prove continuous, non-faked engagement with security awareness training as required by PCI-DSS req-12.6.3.
content:
## Requirements and Testing Procedures

### Defined Approach Requirements

**12.6.3 Personnel receive security awareness training as follows:**  
- Upon hire and at least once every 12 months.  
- Multiple methods of communication are used.  
- Personnel acknowledge at least once every 12 months that they have read and understood the information security policy and procedures.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**12.6.3.a Examine security awareness program records to verify that personnel attend security awareness training upon hire and at least once every 12 months.**  
**12.6.3.b Examine security awareness program materials to verify the program includes multiple methods of communicating awareness and educating personnel.**  
**12.6.3.c Interview personnel to verify they have completed awareness training and are aware of their role in protecting cardholder data.**  
**12.6.3.d Examine security awareness program materials and personnel acknowledgments to verify that personnel acknowledge at least once every 12 months that they have read and understand the information security policy and procedures.**

### Purpose

Training of personnel ensures they receive the information about the importance of information security and that they understand their role in protecting the organization. Requiring an acknowledgment by personnel helps ensure that they have read and understood the security policies and procedures, and that they have made and will continue to make a commitment to comply with these policies.

### Good Practice

Entities may incorporate new-hire training as part of the Human Resources onboarding process. Training should outline the security-related "dos" and "don’ts." Periodic refresher training reinforces key security processes and procedures that may be forgotten or bypassed. Entities should consider requiring security awareness training anytime personnel transfer into roles where they can impact the security of cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data from roles where they did not have this impact.

### Customized Approach Objective

Personnel remain knowledgeable about the threat landscape, their responsibility for the operation of relevant security controls, and are able to access assistance and guidance when required.

### Examples

Different methods that can be used to provide security awareness and education include posters, letters, web-based training, in-person training, team meetings, and incentives. Personnel acknowledgments may be recorded in writing or electronically.

title: The security awareness program is reviewed at least once every 12 months and updated as needed.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Review meeting minutes, internal communications, and change logs related to the security awareness program. 
  WHY: Visible evidence such as documented meeting minutes and calendar entries prove that a scheduled review occurred within the past 12 months.
  HOW: Ask for the calendar of awareness program reviews over the past year, inspect documented meeting minutes with dates and action items confirming that the content was reviewed, and verify that updates were recorded in version-controlled documentation.
  FAIL: No evidence of a timestamped review, missing signatures, or any record of updates to the content raises doubt about the review process.

• CHECK: Verify training logs and awareness session records to see if updated content has been disseminated post-review.
  WHY: Operational patterns such as training session scheduling and attendance records demonstrate that updates have been communicated and integrated into ongoing employee education.
  HOW: Collect records from the HR system and training management systems that list scheduled sessions along with the reviewed and updated content, cross-check dates to the review timeline.
  FAIL: If training logs indicate stale content or training on outdated policies without any communication of updates, implementation is questionable.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply re-date old content without making substantive content improvements, faking an annual review.
• A review might be performed only on paper while the actual training or communication to personnel is deferred or not executed.
• Technical gaps include not linking review findings with updates in threat intelligence or failing to demonstrate that training content truly reflects emerging threats.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify the documented review process by reviewing logs, calendar events, and electronic signatures on the awareness program revision documentation.
   WHY: Automated logs and digital documentation that have timestamps provide reliable evidence of an actual review process.
   HOW: Request system logs from document management systems (DMS) or email systems that record review notifications, confirm changes in version history.
   FAIL: Absence of version history or untraceable document modifications indicates a superficial review.

2. CHECK: Examine updated training materials and verify dated annotations that show content revisions.
   WHY: Clearly marked update dates in training content evidence that reviews are not merely scheduled, but actively lead to content modifications.
   HOW: Compare previous and current versions of security awareness materials ensuring that the update date is within the past 12 months and that new threat/vulnerability information has been included.
   FAIL: If materials remain unchanged for over a year or lack reference to new threat information, this indicates noncompliance.

3. CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for the security awareness program to confirm awareness of recent changes and rationale behind updates.
   WHY: Direct operational feedback from program administrators and trainers validates that the review has been effectively communicated and implemented.
   HOW: Conduct interviews and ask open-ended questions about the review process, the last update, and the impact on program content, ensuring consistency with documented evidence.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between interview responses and documentation or the inability of staff to describe recent updates imply a gap in implementation.

4. CHECK: Audit training session attendance records to correlate updated training sessions with the documented review timeline.
   WHY: Matching attendance records with review dates shows that content updates are disseminated to personnel in a timely manner.
   HOW: Cross-reference the review documentation dates with training logs, verifying training sessions reflect updated content.
   FAIL: If training logs show sessions with outdated content post review, it indicates a failure in operationalizing the updated awareness program.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Look for electronic document management systems that automatically log review activities and update timestamps. Automated evidence is more reliable than manually maintained paper records.
• If the review documents lack explicit references to new threat intelligence or specific actionable improvements, it might be a paper review rather than an effective one. 
• Ensure that interview questions probe into both the review process and the actual updates applied—differences between policy and practice are common.
• Pay attention to the consistency among training logs, review documentation, and interview responses; mismatches indicate an ineffective review process.

This methodology focuses on evidence that is difficult to fabricate, aiming to ensure that the security awareness program has been truly reviewed and updated with actionable changes correlating to current threat environments.
content:
### Requirements and Testing Procedures

#### Defined Approach Requirements

**12.6.2** The security awareness program is:
- Reviewed at least once every 12 months, and
- Updated as needed to address any new threats and vulnerabilities that may impact the security of the entity’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data, or the information provided to personnel about their role in protecting cardholder data.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**12.6.2** Examine security awareness program content, evidence of reviews, and interview personnel to verify that the security awareness program is in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

#### Customized Approach Objective

The content of security awareness material is reviewed and updated periodically.

#### Applicability Notes

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

--- 
Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures, v4.0.1

June 2024

title: A formal security awareness program is implemented to educate all personnel about the entity’s information security policies and procedures.
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for req-12.6.1:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes training schedules, sign-up sheets, attendance records, and email notifications confirming that all personnel have participated in the security awareness program.
   • Technical artifacts that prove compliance are digital training materials (e-learning modules, presentations, quizzes), logs from learning management systems (LMS) showing completion rates, and records of periodic refresher training.
   • Operational patterns include routine rollouts for new hires, regular security awareness updates/refreshers, and documented evidence that training content is synchronized with updates in the security policies and evolving threat landscapes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may merely produce dated and vague training slides or generic attendance lists that do not demonstrate interactive or comprehension-tested awareness.
   • Some may meet the letter of the requirement by sending out a one-time email announcement rather than implementing a living, interactive, and evolving training program.
   • Technical gaps often include incomplete logs (missing timing or participant data), failure to include non-office or third-party vendors, or evidence that the training material doesn’t cover updated threats and policy changes.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Review the LMS or training platform to extract automated logs showing which employees completed the training.
     WHY: Automated logs provide tamper-resistant and current evidence that the training was actively completed by all relevant personnel.
     HOW: Access the LMS reports, verify individual employee completion with timestamps, and correlate with HR records to ensure full coverage.
     FAIL: Incomplete logs, missing employee IDs, or significant gaps between HR records and training completion.

   • CHECK: Examine the training content version history and its update schedule.
     WHY: Ensuring the content is current supports that personnel are educated based on the latest security policies and emerging threats.
     HOW: Compare the training materials’ dates with recent updates to information security policies; confirm that refreshers and updates are periodically scheduled.
     FAIL: Outdated materials that do not reflect current security policies, or absence of a documented update schedule.

   • CHECK: Interview a sample of employees from varied departments and roles to assess their understanding of key security policies and procedures.
     WHY: Direct engagement shows that the training has translated into operational awareness among personnel.
     HOW: Prepare a set of targeted questions about the company’s security policies and procedures, and document the responses to gauge comprehension.
     FAIL: Consistent inability of employees to articulate their security responsibilities or reference relevant aspects of the policies.

   • CHECK: Verify inclusion of non-office personnel in the training program.
     WHY: A truly comprehensive security awareness program must cover all personnel, including those in remote or field roles.
     HOW: Cross-reference HR data to check if training records reflect participation from all relevant employee groups including remote, contract, and field staff.
     FAIL: Missing training evidence for any significant segment of the workforce.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use centralized dashboards from the LMS to quickly identify training gaps rather than solely relying on manual logs or emails.
   • Critical Indicator: Outdated or static training materials often signal that the program is not agile—ensure that content visibly reflects recent threat scenarios and policy changes.
   • Detail Matter: The granularity of the audit trail in the LMS, including timestamp accuracy and linkage to HR records, is critical to prevent cosmetic compliance and indicate real, effective implementation.
   • Additional Step: Validate periodic assessments (e.g., simulated phishing exercises) to see if they complement the training program by measuring real-world application.

Overall, these checks emphasize operational effectiveness and evidence authenticity over mere existence of policies, ensuring that the security awareness program is active, current, and comprehensive.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures  

### 12.6 Security awareness education is an ongoing activity.  

| **Defined Approach Requirements** | **Defined Approach Testing Procedures** | **Purpose** |  
|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------|  
| **12.6.1** A formal security awareness program is implemented to make all personnel aware of the entity’s information security policy and procedures, and their role in protecting the cardholder data. | **12.6.1** Examine the security awareness program to verify it provides awareness to all personnel about the entity’s information security policy and procedures, and personnel’s role in protecting the cardholder data. | If personnel are not educated about their company’s information security policies and procedures and their own security responsibilities, security safeguards and processes that have been implemented may become ineffective through unintentional errors or intentional actions. |  

### Customized Approach Objective  

Personnel are knowledgeable about the threat landscape, their responsibility for the operation of relevant security controls, and are able to access assistance and guidance when required.

title: Additional requirement for service providers only: Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Meeting minutes, change records, and formal communications to executive management that document the review following significant organizational changes are available. 
• Technical Artifacts: Archived documentation detailing scope assessments, internal memos, policy revision logs, and review reports that relate directly to organizational shifts. Artifact timestamp consistency and signatures help verify authenticity.
• Operational Patterns: There is a recurring process triggered by defined thresholds for 'significant changes', supported by change management logs or automated notifications to relevant stakeholders.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may create generic minutes or retrospective reports that appear complete without tying the review directly to an actual event. 
• Surface-Level Implementations: Pre-fabricated templates or retrospective addendums that do not reflect real-time assessment of PCI DSS scope may be used. 
• Technical Gaps: Reviews that are documented but lack evidence of communication to executive management or fail to cover all aspects affecting security and compliance are common.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify that there is documented evidence (meeting minutes, internal review reports) immediately following any significant organizational change.
WHY: This confirms that the organization triggers the review process as required and documents the impact to PCI DSS scope.
HOW: Request recent organizational change logs and cross-reference with documented review reports; verify timelines and review contents.
FAIL: If the documentation is missing, dated inconsistently, or generic without direct reference to the specific organizational change.

CHECK: Confirm that the review includes an analysis of the impact on PCI DSS scope, with explicit documentation of any changes to the applicability of controls.
WHY: To ensure that critical security controls are re-evaluated and adjusted if needed after significant changes.
HOW: Examine review reports, policy change documents, and validated risk assessment results; check for detailed analysis and discussion of scope impact.
FAIL: The absence of detailed analysis covering scope impacts or reliance on generic risk assessments that don’t reference the organizational change event.

CHECK: Interview key personnel (such as compliance officers or IT security managers) to verify that the reviews were part of a formal process and that the results were communicated to executive management.
WHY: This step confirms the operational aspect of the control and that communication has been made to the decision makers.
HOW: Use structured interview questions focusing on process triggers, documentation production, and reporting details to executive management; compare oral confirmation with documented evidence.
FAIL: If personnel are unaware of the process, or if there is a disconnect between what is documented and what is communicated to executives.

CHECK: Review change management records to correlate significant organizational changes with the documented reviews.
WHY: To ensure that every qualifying organizational change has a corresponding documented PCI DSS scope review.
HOW: Audit change management logs to identify any major restructuring events; map these events to review documentation and executive management communications.
FAIL: If the logs show significant changes without associated review records, or if reviews are done only sporadically without a systematic approach.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Check for timeline consistency; a review document’s date should closely follow the change event date and be referenced in both change logs and meeting minutes.
• Critical Indicator: The absence of comments on scope impact in review documentation or missing signatures from key stakeholders strongly suggests insufficient review.
• Detail That Matters: Look for explicit mention of the impact on each relevant PCI DSS control and evidence that the results were escalated to executive management (such as email confirmations or board meeting references).
• Additional Advice: Do not solely rely on static policy documents; emphasize evidence of actual operational reviews and communications following each qualifying change.

This comprehensive validation methodology ensures that organizations not only have a documented process in place but also prove that the process is executed effectively when significant organizational changes occur.
content:
### Defined Approach Requirements

**12.5.3 Additional requirement for service providers only:** Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls, with results communicated to executive management.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**12.5.3.a Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:** Examine documentation (for example, meeting minutes) and policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined such that a significant change to organizational structure results in documented review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls.

**12.5.3.b Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:** Examine documentation (for example, meeting minutes) and interview responsible personnel to verify that significant changes to organizational structure resulted in documented reviews that included all elements specified in this requirement, with results communicated to executive management.

### Customized Approach Objective

PCI DSS scope is confirmed after significant organizational change.

### Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Additional requirement for service providers only: PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every six months and upon significant change.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Regularly updated documentation (electronic or paper) that lists the current PCI DSS scope with dates of the last review and updates, including supplementary records around significant environment changes.
• Technical Artifacts: System-generated reports, change management logs, automated scheduler records, and version-controlled scope documents stored in a central repository.
• Operational Patterns: Periodic (at least biannual) review cycles evidenced by calendar invites, meeting notes, and audit trails that trigger scope validation after significant changes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may merely attach a dated document without demonstrating its alignment with actual network or system changes. They may reuse old records or simply add a timestamp without proper review.
• Surface-level Implementations: Relying solely on policy existence rather than examining actual changes. Documents might be ceremonially updated without any technical verification or correlation with change management logs.
• Technical Gaps: Failing to integrate scope revision triggers with system logs or change management systems, thus missing automated evidence of an actual review process.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify documented PCI DSS scope review records that include dates and signatures for reviews performed at least every six months and following significant changes.
WHY: Demonstrates that periodic reviews are being formally executed and recorded.
HOW: Access the central repository or file share where scope review records are maintained; check for dated entries that match the six-month interval and post-change events; cross-reference with the change management system for corresponding triggers.
FAIL: Documentation is missing or the records do not reflect the required review frequency or trigger events (date stamps not matching recent changes).

CHECK: Interview key personnel responsible for scope management to confirm their understanding of the six-month review process and actions taken on significant changes.
WHY: Personnel interviews verify that the process is embedded within operations and not just a paper exercise.
HOW: Schedule interviews with IT governance, compliance officers, or service delivery leads; ask them to describe the process, recent instances of changes affecting the scope, and how reviews were conducted.
FAIL: Inability of personnel to articulate the process or evidence of inertia in management actions following significant system changes.

CHECK: Correlate the documented scope with actual technical artifacts such as network diagrams, asset inventories, and change management logs.
WHY: Validates that the documented scope accurately reflects the in-use environment and that reviews are comprehensive.
HOW: Compare the latest scope document with network diagrams, firewall rules, and asset inventories; check logs in the change management system to identify if changes were incorporated.
FAIL: Discrepancies exist between documented scope and system configurations, or significant changes are present in technical artifacts that are not mirrored in scope documentation.

CHECK: Confirm the use of automated alerts or scheduled tasks (if available) that trigger scope reviews following significant configuration changes.
WHY: Automated triggers reduce the risk of missing reviews and provide an operational check that the process is systematic.
HOW: Inspect the change management or monitoring system for automated workflows, emails, or ticketing triggers related to scope review events; verify logs for evidence of recurring alerts.
FAIL: No automated triggers are found, or records do not indicate any scheduled or reactive reviews based on system changes.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Prioritize evidence that links change management system entries directly to scope documentation. Automated logs and digital signatures are harder to fake.
• Critical Indicators: Missing or outdated scope documents, lack of alignment between technical configurations and documented records, or personnel unaware of the review process are red flags.
• Key Details: Ensure that review dates, change-related triggers, and personnel responsibilities are clearly marked in documentation. Cross-reference multiple sources to confirm consistency and accuracy.

Together, these checks and observations ensure that the PCI DSS scope is not only documented but actively confirmed and updated, reflecting the true state of the in-scope environment for service providers.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

**12.5.2.1 Additional requirement for service providers only:** PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every six months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes all the elements specified in Requirement 12.5.2.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**12.5.2.1.a Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:** Examine documented results of scope reviews and interview personnel to verify that reviews per Requirement 12.5.2 are performed:
- At least once every six months, and
- After significant changes

**12.5.2.1.b Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:** Examine documented results of scope reviews to verify that scoping validation includes all elements specified in Requirement 12.5.2.

### Customized Approach Objective

The accuracy of PCI DSS scope is verified to be continuously accurate by comprehensive analysis and appropriate technical measures.

### Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
-------------------------------------------------
• Documented Scope Reviews: Look for scope review documents (reports, spreadsheets, or databases) that are dated within the last 12 months and after any noted significant changes. These documents should list all systems, data flows (authorization, capture, settlement, chargebacks, refunds) and channels (card-present, card-not-present, e-commerce) relevant to PCI DSS.
• Updated Data-flow Diagrams: Check that Requirement 1.2.4 diagrams are current and reflect all system components, storage locations, and transfers of cardholder data (CHD) and sensitive authentication data (SAD).
• Change Management Evidence: Evidence from change management tools or meeting minutes confirming that scoping reviews are triggered by significant environment changes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
-------------------------------------------------
• Outdated Documentation: Some organizations might maintain a document labeled as a scope review but fail to update it with changes in the environment or ignore additions such as third-party connections.
• Superficial Reviews: Entities sometimes only address a subset of required elements (e.g., data flows without including segmentation controls) and then mark the review as complete.
• Paper Compliance: Relying solely on static policies without evidence of active technical or operational follow-up (e.g., not validating actual network segmentation or updated connectivity details).

VALIDATION STEPS
-------------------------------------------------
CHECK: Verify the existence of a dated, comprehensive PCI DSS scope review document
WHY: A current, detailed scope document (including data flows, system components, segmentation controls, and third-party connections) shows that the entity actively tracks and updates PCI DSS scope at least annually or after significant changes.
HOW: • Obtain the most recent scope review document.
      • Confirm the document is dated within the last 12 months or correlates with a known significant change.
      • Verify that it includes details on all payment stages and acceptance channels.
FAIL: Missing or outdated document, or document that only partially covers required elements.

CHECK: Examine updated data-flow diagrams aligned with Requirement 1.2.4
WHY: Current diagrams reflecting all data storage, processing, and transmission points provide a visual confirmation of the PCI DSS environment.
HOW: • Request the latest network and data-flow diagrams.
      • Confirm that they illustrate all in-scope systems, including any third-party interfaces and segmentation controls.
      • Check for revision dates that coincide with the annual review or post-change update.
FAIL: Diagrams are outdated, incomplete, or lack clear identification of all key components.

CHECK: Interview key personnel responsible for the scope reviews
WHY: Interviews provide evidence that personnel understand and actively implement PCI DSS scoping reviews and have real familiarity with what elements must be included.
HOW: • Interview IT security managers, compliance officers, or network engineers.
      • Ask how and when the scope was last reviewed and what triggers a scope review apart from the annual cycle.
FAIL: Personnel cannot clearly describe recent reviews, or key details of the scoping process are unfamiliar or inconsistent.

CHECK: Review change management records and incident logs
WHY: Change management records that trigger a new scope review confirm that the entity actively monitors and updates the PCI DSS environment as changes occur.
HOW: • Request change management logs or meeting minutes that note significant changes to the network or systems (e.g., new connections, integrations with third-party vendors).
      • Verify that these record triggers adequately connect to the scope review process.
FAIL: Absence of linkage between change events and scope review activities, or records that do not reference PCI DSS scoping requirements.

EXPERT TIPS
-------------------------------------------------
• Quick Shortcut: Look for revision dates or version numbers on diagrams and scope documents – a lack of these often indicates poor practice.
• Critical Detail: Confirm that all entries explicitly list data flows, storage locations, and system components – incomplete lists are a red flag for out-of-date scoping that could miss critical components.
• Focus on Integration: Don’t rely solely on policy documents; validate that operational tools (change management systems, network monitoring) tie into updates on the scope review documents.
• Corroborate: Cross-check the evidence from documentation with interviews and system logs to ensure that documented practices are consistently applied in operations.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

### 12.5.2 PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every 12 months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes:

- Identifying all data flows for the various payment stages (for example, authorization, capture settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and acceptance channels (for example, card-present, card-not-present, and e-commerce).
- Updating all data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4.
- Identifying all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted, including but not limited to: 1) any locations outside of the currently defined CDE, 2) applications that process CHD, 3) transmissions between systems and networks, and 4) file backups.
- Identifying all system components in the CDE, connected to the CDE, or that could impact security of the CDE.
- Identifying all segmentation controls in use and the environment(s) from which the CDE is segmented, including justification for environments being out of scope.
- Identifying all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE.
- Confirming that all identified data flows, account data, system components, segmentation controls, and connections from third parties with access to the CDE are included in scope.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 12.5.2.a Examine documented results of scope reviews and interview personnel to verify that the reviews are performed:

- At least once every 12 months.
- After significant changes to the in-scope environment.

### 12.5.2.b Examine documented results of scope reviews performed by the entity to verify that PCI DSS scoping confirmation activity includes all elements specified in this requirement.

## Guidance

### Purpose

Frequent validation of PCI DSS scope helps to ensure PCI DSS scope remains up to date and aligned with changing business objectives, and therefore that security controls are protecting all appropriate system components.

### Good Practice

Accurate scoping involves critically evaluating the CDE and all connected system components to determine the necessary coverage for PCI DSS requirements. Scoping activities, including careful analysis and ongoing monitoring, help to ensure that in-scope systems are appropriately secured. When documenting account data locations, the entity can consider creating a table or spreadsheet that includes the following information:

- Data stores (databases, files, cloud, etc.), including the purpose of data storage and the retention period,
- Which CHD elements are stored (PAN, expiry date, cardholder name, and/or any elements of SAD prior to completion of authorization),
- How data is secured (type of encryption and strength, hashing algorithm and strength, truncation, tokenization),
- How access to data stores is logged, including a description of logging mechanism(s) in use (enterprise solution, application level, operating system level, etc.).

### Customized Approach Objective (from continuation on PDF page 310)

PCI DSS scope is verified periodically, and after significant changes, by comprehensive analysis and appropriate technical measures.

### Applicability Notes (from PDF page 310)

This annual confirmation of PCI DSS scope is an activity expected to be performed by the entity under assessment, and is not the same, nor is it intended to be replaced by, the scoping confirmation performed by the entity's assessor during the annual assessment.

title: An inventory of system components that are in scope for PCI DSS is maintained and kept current.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - CHECK: Confirm the existence of a centralized repository (whether a database, file system, or inventory management tool) that lists all in-scope system components with details on function/use.
     WHY: A maintained and centralized inventory that is actively updated is a strong indicator that the organization tracks PCI DSS scope accurately.
     HOW: Request a live demo of the inventory system, check version or modification dates, and review sample records to ensure a description of function/use is provided.
     FAIL: If the repository is a static document, rarely updated, or does not contain detailed descriptions, the check fails.
   
   - CHECK: Look for integration or linkage between the inventory system and change management or asset tracking systems.
     WHY: Automated integration shows the inventory is actively maintained as part of broader operational processes.
     HOW: Inspect system logs, asset management integration workflows, or reports that automatically capture system changes.
     FAIL: Lack of integration indicates reliance on manual updates, which can lead to outdated or incomplete records.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations may present an outdated inventory file where recent assets are missing.
   - Some might include a generic list without the required detailed function/use descriptions.
   - The inventory might look complete on paper but lack formal integration with ticketing or change management systems, casting doubt on its current accuracy.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   - CHECK: Retrieve the inventory for a sample period and cross-check with change management records to verify new components are added timely.
     WHY: This real-time evidence verifies that updates are in sync with operations rather than being a one-time snapshot.
     HOW: Audit a few recent change tickets or deployment logs and confirm corresponding entries in the inventory with proper details.
     FAIL: If new assets appear in change logs but are missing from the inventory, the control is ineffective.
   
   - CHECK: Interview responsible personnel for the inventory (asset owner or IT manager) and ask about the process to update the inventory on asset changes.
     WHY: Direct confirmation from owners of operational procedures affirms that the inventory maintenance process is active.
     HOW: Ask for a walk-through of the procedural steps, review any automation used, and check meeting notes or logs that demonstrate active review.
     FAIL: If personnel are unaware of update procedures or if they rely solely on static manual processes, this indicates weak implementation.
   
   - CHECK: Verify that asset records include detailed descriptions of function/use for each component, differentiating between PCI-relevant assets and others.
     WHY: A granular description helps isolate the in-scope assets necessary for PCI DSS adherence.
     HOW: Inspect a random sample of asset entries ensuring each has a field or description indicating function and role within the PCI environment.
     FAIL: Incomplete or generic descriptions that do not detail function/use will fail the review.
   
   - CHECK: Validate that the inventory tool supports scheduled audits or reminders to prompt periodic reviews.
     WHY: Automated prompts support the technology in maintaining an up-to-date record.
     HOW: Review the system settings or audit logs showing scheduled reviews, or verify calendar alerts tied to the inventory tool.
     FAIL: Absence of any scheduled review mechanism signals a gap in ensuring data currency.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Tip: Always correlate the inventory with external change management and incident logs; manual inventories tend to become outdated quickly if they are not integrated into operational workflows.
   - Tip: Look for audit trails within the inventory tool – these trails are critical when evidence of regular updates is required.
   - Tip: Ensure that inventory records can be exported in a machine-readable format to facilitate automated cross-checks with vulnerability scanners or asset management platforms.
   - Tip: Focus on the quality of asset descriptions; if they are vague, it may be an indicator of a rushed or superficial process that might leave gaps in coverage.
   - Tip: Verify that the person or department responsible for the inventory review is identified and has signed off on regular updates, ensuring accountability and sustained compliance.
content:
## 12.5 PCI DSS scope is documented and validated.

### Defined Approach Requirements

**12.5.1** An inventory of system components that are in scope for PCI DSS, including a description of function/use, is maintained and kept current.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**12.5.1.a** Examine the inventory to verify it includes all in-scope system components and a description of function/use for each.

**12.5.1.b** Interview personnel to verify the inventory is kept current.

### Purpose

Maintaining a current list of all system components will enable an organization to define the scope of its environment and implement PCI DSS requirements accurately and efficiently. Without an inventory, some system components could be overlooked and be inadvertently excluded from the organization’s configuration standards.

### Good Practice

If an entity keeps an inventory of all assets, those system components in scope for PCI DSS should be clearly identifiable among the other assets. Inventories should include containers or images that may be instantiated. Assigning an owner to the inventory helps to ensure the inventory stays current.

### Examples

Methods to maintain an inventory include as a database, as a series of files, or in an inventory-management tool.

title: Additional requirement for service providers only: Reviews conducted in accordance with Requirement 12.4.2 are documented to include review results, remediation actions, and sign-off.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible Evidence: Look for documented review records that include detailed results, specific remediation actions taken, and a clear sign-off by designated PCI compliance personnel. These may be in the form of digital reports, PDF files, or logged entries in a configuration management database.
• Technical Artifacts: Check for audit logs and version control histories that record updates, approvals, or modifications to the review documents. Look for timestamped entries, electronic signatures, or automated workflow systems that can indicate consistent reviews and follow-ups.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled reviews with consistent documentation across multiple cycles. Evidence that any identified issues are not just documented, but also show subsequent remediation actions and final closure by the responsible personnel.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Faking Documentation: Organizations might produce one-off documents that claim the review process was followed without proper supporting evidence, such as fillers in the results section or retroactively added sign-offs without matching audit logs.
• Surface-Level Implementation: Using static documents that are not updated regularly or lack correlation with automated system logs. The review process may appear complete on paper but is not integrated with tracking systems or supported by digital workflows.
• Missing Technical Gaps: Incomplete evidence where the documented remediation actions are not linked to specific findings from the review, or sign-off sections where the responsible personnel cannot be verified through independent logs or digital signatures.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review Documentation Content
   WHY: Comprehensive documentation including review results, detailed remediation actions, and explicit sign-off directly demonstrates adherence to the requirement.
   HOW: Collect review reports and meeting minutes related to Requirement 12.4.2. Verify that each report includes:
       - A clear list of review results
       - Documented remediation actions for any non-performed tasks
       - Sign-off or approval from personnel assigned to PCI DSS compliance
   FAIL: Missing elements (e.g., absence of remediation actions or sign-off) indicate non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Cross-Reference with Audit/Version Control Logs
   WHY: Audit and version control logs help prove that documentation wasn't created or modified without proper process, and that sign-offs were genuine and timely.
   HOW: Obtain system-generated audit logs or version control histories of the review documents. Compare timestamps and user IDs with the documented sign-offs and remediation entries. Confirm that documents were updated in alignment with review schedules.
   FAIL: Discrepancies in timestamps, absence of digital audit trails, or evidence of last-minute modifications without proper sign-off indicate a failure in the control implementation.

3. CHECK: Interview Responsible Personnel
   WHY: Verbal confirmation from PCI compliance staff verifies that documented reviews are actively carried out and integrated into the operational process.
   HOW: Interview the personnel responsible for PCI DSS compliance and those who signed-off on reviews. Ask for their process and corroborate their responses with available documentation. Validate if they receive automated alerts or reminders for pending reviews and remediation follow-ups.
   FAIL: Lack of clarity on processes, inability to explain documented sign-off details, or discrepancies between interview feedback and written records suggest that the control might not be effectively implemented.

4. CHECK: Verify Remediation Tracking
   WHY: Effective controls must demonstrate that issues identified during reviews are tracked and resolved promptly.
   HOW: Inspect supplemental logs or remediation tracking tools used by the organization. Verify that each review finding from the documentation is linked to a remediation ticket or action entry with clear status updates and closure notes.
   FAIL: Absence of cross-referenced remediation tickets, outdated follow-up actions, or missing statuses may indicate that review findings are not being properly acted upon.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Prioritize the review of electronic workflows and version control systems as they reduce the risk of manual forgery. Timestamps and unique digital signatures are hard to fake.
• Critical Indicators: Always check the correlation between documented findings and automated system logs. Consistency in process timelines (e.g., review frequencies, remediation closures) is key.
• Detail to Note: The clarity and specificity of remediation logs and real-time audit trails are the best indicators that reviews are not merely paper exercises but an operationally effective part of the compliance program.
• Additional Advice: If possible, request a live demonstration of how review documents are updated and approved within their workflow system to verify process automation and real-time compliance management.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

### 12.4.2.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: 
Reviews conducted in accordance with Requirement 12.4.2 are documented to include:

- Results of the reviews.
- Documented remediation actions taken for any tasks that were found to not be performed at Requirement 12.4.2.
- Review and sign-off of results by personnel assigned responsibility for the PCI DSS compliance program.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 12.4.2.1 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: 
Examine documentation from the reviews conducted in accordance with PCI DSS Requirement 12.4.2 to verify the documentation includes all elements specified in this requirement.

## Customized Approach Objective

Findings from operational effectiveness reviews are evaluated by management; appropriate remediation activities are implemented.

## Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

### Purpose

The intent of these independent checks is to confirm whether security activities are being performed on an ongoing basis. These reviews can also be used to verify that appropriate evidence is being maintained—for example, audit logs, vulnerability scan reports, reviews of network security control rulesets—to assist in the entity's preparation for its next PCI DSS assessment.
