title: Additional requirement for service providers only: Reviews are performed at least once every three months to confirm that personnel are performing their tasks in accordance with all security policies and operational procedures.
methodology:
Control ID: req-12.4.2 – Quarterly Independent Reviews

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Records and logs showing that reviews (daily log reviews, configuration reviews, change-management reviews, etc.) are conducted on a quarterly basis by a team separate from operational personnel. Evidence includes audit trails, meeting minutes, review sign-off sheets, and timestamped event logs.
• Technical Artifacts: Automated logs from SIEM systems, configuration management databases with review histories, and scheduled job logs that document the execution of review tasks. Reports generated by change-management systems also prove that reviews correspond to actual operations.
• Operational Patterns: Evidence of a rotation of personnel performing independent reviews, documented exclusion criteria (ensuring that those who perform routine tasks do not perform the reviews), and consistent periodicity (at least every three months) as per schedule.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations may produce static policy documents or checklists that are signed off by the same personnel responsible for the task. They might also generate dated reports without actual independent verification.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Sole reliance on automated report generation without cross-checking by independent personnel, or quarterly reviews that are performed late or on an ad-hoc basis but documented as meeting requirements.
• Technical Gaps: Failure to ensure that the review records include evidence of independence (e.g., reviewer identity vs. task performer), and missing integration between operational logs and review sign-offs.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Independent Review Record Verification
  WHY: Checking review records (e.g., meeting minutes, review logs) proves that independent personnel verified that tasks (log reviews, configuration checks, change-management) occurred as prescribed.
  HOW: Examine a sample of review records for at least one quarterly cycle; verify timestamps and identity of review personnel; cross-check with task execution logs to ensure separation of roles.
  FAIL: Lack of dated review records, duplicate names of reviewers and task performers, or evidences that reviews were not performed within the required quarterly cycle.

• CHECK: Automated Log and Alert Correlation
  WHY: Automated logs and SIEM alerts coupled with review signatures confirm that independent reviews of daily logs and security alerts are actively performed.
  HOW: Query SIEM logs and obtain records showing automated alerts and subsequent review actions; verify that review events are not generated by the same system or personnel responsible for the alert generation.
  FAIL: Gaps in log correlation, missing evidence of independent review actions, or lack of automated alert review markers.

• CHECK: Interview with Independent Reviewers
  WHY: Direct interviews corroborate that independent personnel actively participate in periodic reviews and understand their role separate from operations.
  HOW: Interview a sample of personnel identified as reviewers; ask them to describe their process, frequency, and evidence retention; verify that their account aligns with documented records.
  FAIL: Inability to identify independent reviewers or if personnel indicate overlapping roles with operational tasks.

• CHECK: Policy-to-Operational Evidence Correlation
  WHY: Ensuring that the documented process is actually in place operationally provides strong evidence of compliance beyond mere policy existence.
  HOW: Cross-reference policy documents with actual system records and review logs; verify that the review schedule is adhered to in practice by matching review records against the documented timeline.
  FAIL: Disconnect between policy documentation and operational evidence, such as outdated policies or lack of records supporting the execution of dates specified in policies.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Focus on timestamp logs in automated review tools and independent reviewer signatures. Consistency and matching ID separation are key indicators of a valid process.
• Critical Indicators: Evidence that the reviews are conducted by personnel not performing the operational tasks is critical. Look for reviewer role designations and independent audit logs.
• Details That Matter: In practice, the timing (quarterly adherence) and the independence of the reviewer are paramount. Document any discrepancies where the same individual appears on both sides of the process or where records are backdated or incomplete.

This methodology combines documentation review, system evidence, and personnel interviews to ensure that the quarterly independent reviews are not only scheduled but are effectively executed per req-12.4.2.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

### 12.4.2 Additional requirement for service providers only: Reviews are performed at least once every three months to confirm that personnel are performing their tasks in accordance with all security policies and operational procedures. Reviews are performed by personnel other than those responsible for performing the given task and include, but are not limited to, the following tasks:
- Daily log reviews.
- Configuration reviews for network security controls.
- Applying configuration standards to new systems.
- Responding to security alerts.
- Change-management processes.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 12.4.2.a Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for conducting reviews to confirm that personnel are performing their tasks in accordance with all security policies and operational procedures, including but not limited to the tasks specified in this requirement.

### 12.4.2.b Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Interview responsible personnel and examine records of reviews to verify that reviews are performed:
- At least once every three months.
- By personnel other than those responsible for performing the given task.

## Customized Approach Objective

The operational effectiveness of critical PCI DSS controls is verified periodically by manual inspection of records.

## Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

title: Additional requirement for service providers only: Responsibility is established by executive management for the protection of cardholder data.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Existence of an officially documented and approved PCI DSS compliance charter and assignment of responsibility to C-level executives, board members, or a designated compliance officer.
  WHY: This provides visible, traceable evidence that executive management has formally accepted accountability for protecting cardholder data.
  HOW: Review documentation such as meeting minutes from board or executive committees, official policy documents that outline PCI DSS roles, and organizational charts that clearly tie compliance to executive roles.
  FAIL: No documented charter or vague attribution of responsibility that cannot be directly linked to an executive-level role.

• CHECK: Communication channels that reinforce executive management’s oversight, such as periodic compliance review summaries or dashboard reports shared with the board.
  WHY: Regular and direct communication from executives indicates active oversight rather than a one-time approval of documents.
  HOW: Verify that there are recurring board or C-level reports on PCI DSS status, review internal memos or dashboards showing key compliance metrics, and check for scheduled executive reviews.
  FAIL: Absence of regular compliance metrics or evidence that executive oversight is only nominal and not integrated into ongoing reporting.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might produce well-crafted policy documents without operational follow-through, relying on paper compliance rather than actual risk management or enforcement.
• A superficial assignment of responsibility where the executive title is used without true accountability (e.g., an IT manager being indirectly controlled by an executive without meaningful engagement).
• Reporting mechanisms that are outdated or crafted solely to satisfy auditors but not used in decision-making processes.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Validate that the PCI DSS compliance program charter is signed and backed by top-level executives.
   WHY: Sign-off by executives confirms acknowledgment and adoption of compliance responsibility at the highest level.
   HOW: Locate the signed charter document, compare it with the organizational structure, and ensure it delineates specific executive responsibilities for PCI DSS.
   FAIL: Unsigned or generic compliance documents without executive attribution.

2. CHECK: Review executive meeting minutes or agenda items for discussions on PCI DSS compliance and cardholder data protection.
   WHY: Regular discussion topics in executive meetings indicate ongoing oversight rather than one-off assignments.
   HOW: Obtain and inspect a series of board or executive committee meeting minutes for references that specifically address PCI DSS and related operational updates.
   FAIL: Minutes that lack any mention of PCI DSS, cardholder data protection, or that show only generic risk management topics.

3. CHECK: Examine internal reporting systems and dashboards that provide real-time or periodic updates on PCI DSS compliance to executive management.
   WHY: Automated or regularly updated reporting systems reduce the risk of faked compliance by tracking operational performance, not just documented policies.
   HOW: Request demonstrations of the reporting dashboards, review data outputs over the past quarter, and verify that they include compliance metrics tied to executive responsibilities.
   FAIL: Inconsistent, outdated, or overly static reports that look hand-crafted and avoid detailing operational shortcomings.

4. CHECK: Interview key personnel at both executive and operational levels to assess their understanding of PCI DSS responsibilities and the mechanisms for protecting cardholder data.
   WHY: Direct questioning can reveal whether the documented responsibilities have translated into actionable practices and if the responsibilities are truly understood.
   HOW: Conduct interviews using structured questionnaires focusing on accountability, recent PCI DSS program initiatives, and specific decisions influenced by compliance findings.
   FAIL: Inconsistent answers, lack of clarity about roles, or evidence that the executive directives are not reflected in day-to-day operations.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut Verification: Check if executive emails or memos reference recent PCI DSS program improvements – this is a strong indicator that the responsibility is active rather than merely documented.
• Critical Indicator: Look for alignment between the documentation (the charter) and tangible outputs (meeting minutes, dashboards). A disconnect is a red flag.
• Practical Insight: Validate if there’s evidence of periodic internal audits or third-party reviews fed back into executive decision-making. This chain of evidence strengthens the case for true engagement.
• Avoid relying solely on static policy documents – ensure that demonstrated behavior (e.g., data-driven reports, direct interviews) supports what is written on paper.

This methodology ensures visible, technical, and operational evidence is collected, minimizing the risk of superficial or faked compliance, and confirms that executive management is truly accountable in practice.
content:
12.4 PCI DSS compliance is managed.

### Defined Approach Requirements

12.4.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: Responsibility is established by executive management for the protection of cardholder data and a PCI DSS compliance program to include:
- Overall accountability for maintaining PCI DSS compliance.
- Defining a charter for a PCI DSS compliance program and communication to executive management.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.4.1 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Examine documentation to verify that executive management has established responsibility for the protection of cardholder data and a PCI DSS compliance program in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

### Purpose

Executive management assignment of PCI DSS compliance responsibilities ensures executive-level visibility into the PCI DSS compliance program and allows for the opportunity to ask appropriate questions to determine the effectiveness of the program and influence strategic priorities.

### Customized Approach Objective

Executives are responsible and accountable for security of cardholder data.

### Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider. Executive management may include C-level positions, board of directors, or equivalent. The specific titles will depend on the particular organizational structure. Responsibility for the PCI DSS compliance program may be assigned to individual roles and/or business units within the organization.

title: Hardware and software technologies in use are reviewed for security support and updates annually.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of a regularly updated inventory that lists all hardware and software technologies along with version numbers and vendor support status.
• Meeting minutes or review reports signed off by senior management that document annual reviews of vendor security updates and end‐of-life (EOL) announcements.
• Actual correspondence or vendor bulletins that are referenced during reviews, along with remediation plans for technologies flagged as unsupported or soon to be obsolete.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Relying solely on static documentation (e.g., dated spreadsheets) without linking to current vendor communications or dynamic IT asset management systems.
• Producing an annual report that is a superficial checklist rather than a substantive review with evidence of follow‐up actions.
• Failing to integrate EOL announcements and security bulletin information, or having review reports that simply reiterate policy without operational evidence of remediation planning.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify the existence of a current IT asset inventory and review schedule.
  WHY: A comprehensive, up-to-date inventory with review schedules shows that the organization actively monitors technology versions and vendor support status.
  HOW: Ask for the latest version of the IT asset inventory and documentation of the annual review process; cross-check that it lists hardware/software, their current firmware/software versions, and documented vendor support status including EOL notices.
  FAIL: If the inventory is outdated, incomplete, or lacks references to vendor support status and related review dates.

• CHECK: Assess documented evidence of annual review meetings and management-approved remediation plans.
  WHY: Meeting reports and senior management approvals cascade accountability and ensure remediation plans for obsolete or unsupported technologies are in place.
  HOW: Review meeting minutes, review reports, and approved remediation plans that show discussion of vendor EOL announcements and decisions on technology upgrades/replacements.
  FAIL: If the documentation lacks clear timelines, signatures, or references specific technologies with upcoming or current vendor EOL statuses.

• CHECK: Inspect evidence correlating vendor communications to internal reviews.
  WHY: Demonstrating that the organization actively monitors vendor bulletins or security advisories for updates confirms the control is not merely paperwork but reflects operational vigilance.
  HOW: Request copies of vendor security bulletins or release notes that were used during the review process; verify that these documents are referenced in the internal review documentation.
  FAIL: When there’s no linkage between vendor announcements and the organization’s review documents, or if vendor support issues are overlooked.

• CHECK: Validate that the review explicitly considers PCI DSS compliance impact.
  WHY: Confirming that the review process includes analysis regarding PCI DSS controls ensures that any technology changes will not undermine compliance.
  HOW: Interview IT security and compliance personnel and review risk assessments to confirm that changes in vendor support and technology trends are evaluated in the context of PCI DSS requirements.
  FAIL: When the review documents or personnel interviews indicate that the impact on PCI DSS compliance is ignored or superficially treated.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Prioritize reviews that include direct references (e.g., URLs, bulletin issue numbers) to vendor communications; these are difficult to fake and clearly demonstrate active monitoring.
• Indicator of poor implementation: A review process that simply recycles an old spreadsheet or policy document without any update or actionable remediation items.
• Critical detail: Ensure that remediation plans include clear timelines and are signed off by senior management, as this creates accountability for addressing unsupported technologies.
• Best practice: Use automated asset management tools where possible to generate evidence for current versions and patch levels; manual inventories are often incomplete or outdated.

Following these steps will provide robust, operational validation that the organization’s hardware and software technologies are confirmed annually for security support, vendor updates, and continued PCI DSS compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

12.3.4 Hardware and software technologies in use are reviewed at least once every 12 months, including at least the following:

- Analysis that the technologies continue to receive security fixes from vendors promptly.
- Analysis that the technologies continue to support (and do not preclude) the entity’s PCI DSS compliance.
- Documentation of any industry announcements or trends related to a technology, such as when a vendor has announced "end of life" plans for a technology.
- Documentation of a plan, approved by senior management, to remediate outdated technologies, including those for which vendors have announced "end of life" plans.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.3.4 Examine documentation for the review of hardware and software technologies in use and interview personnel to verify that the review is in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Customized Approach Objective

The entity’s hardware and software technologies are up to date and supported by the vendor. Plans to remove or replace all unsupported system components are reviewed periodically.

Applicability Notes

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Guidance

Purpose

Hardware and software technologies are constantly evolving, and organizations need to be aware of changes to the technologies they use, as well as the evolving threats to those technologies to ensure that they can prepare for, and manage, vulnerabilities in hardware and software that will not be remediated by the vendor or developer. 

Good Practice

Organizations should review firmware versions to ensure they remain current and supported by the vendors. Organizations also need to be aware of changes made by technology vendors to their products or processes to understand how such changes may impact the organization’s use of the technology. Regular reviews of technologies that impact or influence PCI DSS controls can assist with purchasing, usage, and deployment strategies, and ensure controls that rely on those technologies remain effective. These reviews include, but are not limited to, reviewing technologies that are no longer supported by the vendor and/or no longer meet the security needs of the organization.

title: Cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use are documented and reviewed annually.
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for verifying the implementation of req-12.3.3:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes an up-to-date, centrally stored inventory documenting all cryptographic cipher suites and protocols. This inventory should list details about purpose, where each is deployed, and expected review dates.
   • Technical artifacts such as version-controlled configuration files, system dashboards displaying active cipher suites, meeting minutes for annual reviews, and audit logs showing periodic updates.
   • Operational patterns include scheduled annual reviews with explicit action items when trends in cryptographic vulnerabilities are noted, along with a defined response plan to upgrade or deprecate algorithms.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply produce a static copy of an old inventory or documentation, giving the appearance of compliance without actually updating the list in operational systems.
   • A surface-level implementation might involve a policy document that states the requirement is met without automated or technical correlation between deployed protocols and documented cipher suites.
   • Frequently missed technical gaps include failure to tie inventory data back to actual configurations or using undocumented internal tools that are not easily correlated to published documentation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of an up-to-date inventory of cryptographic suites.
   WHY: This inventory confirms that the organization knows exactly which cipher suites and protocols are in use, including their purposes and deployment locations.
   HOW: Review the centrally stored inventory, check the versioning or update logs, and confirm review dates are within the past 12 months.
   FAIL: If the inventory is outdated (older than 12 months), incomplete, or fails to specify where and how the cipher suites are implemented, the check has failed.

   CHECK: Inspect meeting minutes or logged evidence of the annual review process.
   WHY: Actual records of review meetings support the claim that the inventory is periodically assessed and reflects current industry trends and vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Examine meeting minutes, change logs, or automated reports that highlight review actions and decisions taken in response to evolving risks (e.g., references to NIST SP 800-131a guidance).
   FAIL: Absence of meeting records, outdated meeting notes, or no actionable items related to cryptographic vulnerability management indicates non-compliance.

   CHECK: Correlate the documented cipher suites with live system configurations and monitoring dashboards.
   WHY: Automated correlation verifies operational effectiveness by ensuring that the documented data matches current system states.
   HOW: Use automated configuration scans or query system settings on load balancers, web servers, and endpoints to extract active cipher suite configurations. Compare these findings with the documented inventory.
   FAIL: Significant discrepancies between live system configurations and the documented inventory signal a failure in maintaining applicable control.

   CHECK: Evaluate the documented plan for addressing cryptographic vulnerabilities and the process for monitoring industry trends.
   WHY: A well-documented and tested plan confirms the organization’s preparation to respond effectively to emerging cryptographic threats.
   HOW: Review the plan document to check for defined processes, responsible parties, timelines, and references to external standards (e.g., NIST guidelines). Interview key personnel to validate awareness and operational readiness.
   FAIL: A lack of a clear action plan, missing assigned responsibilities, or no evidence of any industry trend monitoring indicates inadequate control implementation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification shortcuts include cross-referencing automated configuration scans with documented evidence. If discrepancies pop up, this often reveals a manual update process that isn’t consistently followed.
   • Pay special attention to evidence showing proactive change management in response to newly identified vulnerabilities—a reactive or static approach is a red flag.
   • Interviewing personnel responsible for managing these controls can quickly reveal whether the process is meaningful or just documentation for audit purposes; emphasis should be on operational effectiveness rather than paper compliance.
   • When possible, leverage centralized tools that automatically track and log cipher suite configurations, as these provide hard-to-fake, observable evidence of control execution.

This methodology ensures that compliance with the control isn’t just documented on paper but is actively and effectively managed within the organization's cryptographic environment.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

12.3.3
Cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use are documented and reviewed at least once every 12 months, including at least the following:
- An up-to-date inventory of all cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use, including purpose and where used.
- Active monitoring of industry trends regarding continued viability of all cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use.
- Documentation of a plan, to respond to anticipated changes in cryptographic vulnerabilities.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.3.3
Examine documentation for cryptographic suites and protocols in use and interview personnel to verify the documentation and review is in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Protocols and encryption strengths may quickly change or be deprecated due to identification of vulnerabilities or design flaws. In order to support current and future data security needs, entities need to know where cryptography is used and understand how they would be able to respond rapidly to changes impacting the strength of their cryptographic implementations.

Good Practice

Cryptographic agility is important to ensure an alternative to the original encryption method or cryptographic primitive is available, with plans to upgrade to the alternative without significant change to system infrastructure. For example, if the entity is aware of when protocols or algorithms will be deprecated by standards bodies, proactive plans will help the entity to upgrade before the deprecation is impactful to operations.

Definitions

"Cryptographic agility" refers to the ability to monitor and manage the encryption and related verification technologies deployed across an organization.

Further Information

Refer to NIST SP 800-131a, Transitioning the Use of Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Lengths.

title: A targeted risk analysis using a customized approach is performed and documented.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence: Look for a dedicated risk analysis report that includes a customized controls matrix. The document should reference originally identified risk drivers and tailored mitigations for specific PCI DSS requirements. It must also show a clearly marked sign‐off page from senior management and periodic revision dates (at least annually).
   • Technical Artifacts: Version-controlled documents in document management systems, metadata timestamps indicating an update within the last 12 months, and meeting minutes discussing risk issues.
   • Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled review meetings or risk assessment cycles documented in incident or change logs, with communications from risk owners and management.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations can easily repurpose generic or outdated templates that merely mimic customization without addressing specific risk conditions. A superficial check‐off with forged or cursory senior management signatures might be provided.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Using a standardized risk analysis template without real adjustments for individual PCI DSS requirements, or simply copying previous year’s analysis with minimal updates.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing integration with the actual control environments (e.g., risk drivers not mapped to specific security controls), and lack of metadata from versioning to prove recent updates.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Risk Analysis Documentation
   WHY: A genuine, customized risk analysis report demonstrates that the organization has tailored its risk identification and control mapping approach for each PCI DSS requirement using a customized controls matrix.
   HOW: Access the risk analysis report, confirm it includes detailed sections for risk, controls mapping, and evidence of senior management review (e.g., sign-off pages). Check that each element aligns with Appendix D requirements.
   FAIL: A report that is generic, lacks evidence of customization, or does not include clear management sign-off will indicate failure.

   CHECK: Confirm Recency and Regularity of the Analysis
   WHY: The control requires that a targeted risk analysis be performed at least once every 12 months.
   HOW: Verify document metadata, revision dates, and supporting meeting minutes or change logs that explicitly state the risk analysis cycle. Compare current reviews with a timestamp within the last year.
   FAIL: Documentation with dates older than 12 months or absence of any periodic review evidence indicates non-compliance.

   CHECK: Validate Customization in the Risk Assessment
   WHY: Customized risk analysis must address specific PCI DSS requirements, not just adopt a one-size-fits-all approach.
   HOW: Review the risk analysis for explicit mappings between identified risks and the controls meant to mitigate them. Ensure that narrative sections clearly articulate why chosen controls exceed or match the required security posture and that there is clear alignment with PCI DSS requirements.
   FAIL: A risk analysis that appears to be a generic fill-in-the-blank document lacking tailored risk drivers or detailed mapping to PCI DSS requirements is a red flag.

   CHECK: Review Approval Evidence
   WHY: Senior management approval is a critical indicator of oversight and formal acceptance of the risk analysis.
   HOW: Check for a sign-off sheet or an approval log within the documentation that includes dates and names of authorized personnel. Confirm that the approval is timely and correlates with the risk analysis documentation.
   FAIL: Absence of clear, verifiable management approval or signs of back-dated approvals can indicate insufficient oversight.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Look at the document metadata. Often, the file creation and last-modified dates are easy-to-check indicators of whether the report is current and whether the process is on schedule.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to the level of detail in the controls matrix. Superficial or boilerplate narratives typically indicate that the organization has not genuinely customized the risk analysis to its environment.
   • Practical Check: If possible, verify through an interview with the process owner that the risk analysis was indeed a collaborative, targeted process with evidence of tailored discussion—not merely a recycled template.
   • Final Note: Ensure that the risk analysis goes beyond tick-box compliance; it should clearly elucidate how each PCI DSS requirement is addressed uniquely, providing a clear risk rationale and sound mitigation strategy.

This methodology focuses on observable, automated evidence (metadata, version control, and documented meeting minutes) more than just policies, ensuring that the control is not easily faked.
content:
| Requirements and Testing Procedures | Guidance |
|-------------------------------------|---------|
| **Defined Approach Requirements**  |  **Purpose**  |
| 12.3.2 A targeted risk analysis is performed for each PCI DSS requirement that the entity meets with the customized approach, to include:  |  A risk analysis following a repeatable and robust methodology enables an entity to meet the customized approach objective.  |
| • Documented evidence detailing each element specified in Appendix D: Customized Approach (including, at a minimum, a controls matrix and risk analysis). | **Definitions**  |
| • Approval of documented evidence by senior management. |  The customized approach to meeting a PCI DSS requirement allows entities to define the controls used to meet a given requirement’s stated Customized Approach Objective in a way that does not strictly follow the defined requirement.  |
| • Performance of the targeted analysis of risk at least once every 12 months. |  These controls are expected to at least meet or exceed the security provided by the defined requirement and require extensive documentation by the entity using the customized approach.  |
| **Customized Approach Objective** |  **Further Information**  |
| This requirement is part of the customized approach and must be met for those using the customized approach. | See Appendix D: Customized Approach for instructions on how to document the required evidence for the customized approach.  |
| **Applicability Notes** | See PCI DSS v4.x: Sample Templates to Support Customized Approach on the PCI SSC website for templates that entities may use to document their customized controls. Note that while use of the templates is optional, the information specified within each template must be documented and provided to each entity’s assessor.  |

title: A targeted risk analysis is documented for PCI DSS requirements including asset and threat identification and periodic review.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes documented risk analysis reports for each PCI DSS requirement where a targeted risk assessment was needed. These reports should clearly list the assets being protected, the threats identified, factors affecting likelihood/impact, and a justification for the chosen risk treatment or control frequency.
   • Technical artifacts include version-controlled documents, review logs (with dates and reviewer signatures), change management tickets referring to risk updates, and automated alerts that track the review cycles.
   • Operational patterns indicating control activity are the periodic (annual or triggered by environmental changes) updates to the risk analysis, integration of risk analysis findings into overall security controls, and consistent cross-checks during internal audits.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply produce boilerplate or outdated risk analysis documents that superficially mention assets and threats without real identification or correlation to actual risks.
   • Faked compliance can occur when risk analysis documentation is created as a one-time effort and then simply re-filed annually without any meaningful update or verification in the face of evolving threats.
   • Technical gaps include missing detailed links between identified assets, threats, and the specific vulnerabilities or environmental changes, or the lack of proper periodic review logs indicating that the analysis remains current.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Documented targeted risk analysis exists for each relevant PCI DSS requirement.
   WHY: It proves that a formal process has been followed to evaluate risks for controls that allow risk-based frequency determination.
   HOW: Locate and review targeted risk analysis reports, ensuring they list assets, identified threats, influencing factors, and justification for risk treatment frequency. Verify that each document references a specific PCI requirement.
   FAIL: Absence of documentation for one or more PCI DSS requirements, or documents missing core elements (asset/threat identification or justification).

   CHECK: Evidence of periodic review (at least once every 12 months) and update process.
   WHY: Regular reviews ensure that the risk analysis reflects current threats and organizational changes.
   HOW: Examine risk analysis documents for review dates and signatures; cross-check against the organization’s risk management calendar, and confirm that changes in the environment or significant threat updates led to documented reanalysis.
   FAIL: Risk analysis documents with outdated review dates or missing update records after environmental or technological changes.

   CHECK: Detailed asset and threat identification with influencing factors is clearly documented.
   WHY: Comprehensive identification ensures that risks are not underestimated, and that appropriate controls are applied.
   HOW: Randomly select a risk analysis report and verify that it contains specific information on: (a) system components and sensitive data to be protected, (b) clear threats such as malware or credential misuse, and (c) factors like network exposure or high staff turnover aggregating the risk.
   FAIL: Generic descriptions that list assets or threats without context, or missing factors contributing to risk probability and impact.

   CHECK: Process validation for updating risk analyses triggered by changes in the environment.
   WHY: Ensures that the organization actively manages risk, not just on an annual basis but also in response to significant changes.
   HOW: Interview the risk management team or review change management logs/tickets to confirm that risk analyses are revisited promptly when key changes occur (e.g., introduction of new systems, threat intelligence updates).
   FAIL: No evidence of reactive updates when environmental changes occur, or reliance solely on the annual calendar without process triggers for ad hoc reviews.

   CHECK: Correlation between targeted risk analyses and overall risk management strategy.
   WHY: Consistency with broader enterprise risk assessments helps verify that targeted analyses are not isolated efforts but part of a robust risk management program.
   HOW: Cross-reference a sample targeted risk analysis with the enterprise-wide risk assessment (if available) to ensure overlapping risks, threats, and asset profiles are consistently managed.
   FAIL: Noticeable discrepancies between targeted risk analysis findings and the wider enterprise risk assessment, implying a siloed and potentially incomplete risk analysis process.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Check the risk analysis document metadata (timestamps and version control) to quickly assess how frequently the documents are updated.
   • Critical indicator: Look for explicit evidence of risk re-assessment following specific environmental changes, not just a calendar date.
   • Detail that matters: Focus on the granularity of asset and threat descriptions. Overly generic sections are a red flag; detailed, context-driven narratives suggest real implementation.
   • Additional recommendation: Validate that risk analysis documents are integrated into the operational workflow, such as being referenced in incident response planning or change management processes, to confirm that they are actually used in practice.
content:
12.3 Risks to the cardholder data environment are formally identified, evaluated, and managed.

Defined Approach Requirements

12.3.1 For each PCI DSS requirement that specifies completion of a targeted risk analysis, the analysis is documented and includes:
- Identification of the assets being protected.
- Identification of the threat(s) that the requirement is protecting against.
- Identification of factors that contribute to the likelihood and/or impact of a threat being realized.
- Resulting analysis that determines, and includes justification for, how the frequency or processes defined by the entity to meet the requirement minimize the likelihood and/or impact of the threat being realized.
- Review of each targeted risk analysis at least once every 12 months to determine whether the results are still valid or if an updated risk analysis is needed.
- Performance of updated risk analyses when needed, as determined by the annual review.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.3.1 Examine documented policies and procedures to verify a process is defined for performing targeted risk analyses for each PCI DSS requirement that specifies completion of a targeted risk analysis, and that the process includes all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Some PCI DSS requirements allow an entity to define how frequently an activity is performed based on the risk to the entity’s environment. Performing this risk analysis according to a methodology ensures validity and consistency with policies and procedures.

This targeted risk analysis (as opposed to a traditional enterprise-wide risk assessment) focuses on those PCI DSS requirements that allow an entity flexibility about how frequently an entity performs a given control. For this risk analysis, the entity carefully evaluates each PCI DSS requirement that provides this flexibility and determines the frequency that supports adequate security for the entity, and the level of risk the entity is willing to accept.

The risk analysis identifies the specific assets, such as the system components and data—for example, log files, or credentials—that the requirement is intended to protect, as well as the threat(s) or outcomes that the requirement is protecting the assets from—for example, malware, an undetected intruder, or misuse of credentials. Examples of factors that could contribute to likelihood or impact include any that could increase the vulnerability of an asset to a threat—for example, exposure to untrusted networks, complexity of environment, or high staff turnover—as well as the criticality of the system components, or volume and sensitivity of the data, being protected.

(continued on next page)

--- Continued on PDF Page 301 (Doc page 297) ---

12.3.1 (continued)

Reviewing the results of these targeted risk analyses at least once every 12 months and upon changes that could impact the risk to the environment allows the organization to ensure the risk analysis results remain current with organizational changes and evolving threats, trends, and technologies, and that the selected frequencies still adequately address the entity’s risk.

Good Practice

An enterprise-wide risk assessment, which is a point-in-time activity that enables entities to identify threats and associated vulnerabilities, is recommended, but is not required, for entities to determine and understand broader and emerging threats with the potential to negatively impact its business. This enterprise-wide risk assessment could be established as part of an overarching risk management program that is used as an input to the annual review of an organization’s overall information security policy (see Requirement 12.1.1).

Further Information

Refer to the following documents on the PCI SSC website:
- Information Supplement: TRA Guidance
- Sample Template: TRA for Activity Frequency.

title: Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are defined and implemented.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-12.2.1, which ensures that acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are defined, documented, and implemented:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Published acceptable use policy document accessible to employees, intranet pages, training materials, and an updated list of approved hardware/software.
   - Technical Artifacts: Configuration files, asset inventories, and logs from endpoint management systems showing enforcement/actions based on policies. Also, digital signatures or version-control indications to confirm explicit approval by authorized personnel.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular dissemination of policy training, onboarding sessions that reference the policy, periodic reviews and updates, and technical enforcement mechanisms (firewalls, endpoint controls) that restrict unauthorized usage.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Fake Compliance: Organizations may claim that the policy exists and is approved but might not actively enforce it, or the approved product list might be outdated. Simply having a document on file does not guarantee operational control.
   - Surface-level Implementations: A policy that is printed and signed off once without periodic updates or technical controls to validate compliance.
   - Technical Gaps: Lack of integration between policy and technical enforcement tools (e.g., lack of endpoint configuration that enforces approved applications or improper configuration management leading to outdated approved product lists).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   a. CHECK: Verify the existence and currency of the acceptable use policy document including explicit approval signatures.
      WHY: A current and signed policy document confirms that guidelines have been formally authorized and are intended to govern end-user technology usage.
      HOW: Request the latest version of the acceptable use policy document; confirm signatures/approval dates and review change logs to ensure periodic updates. Interview policy owners to understand update frequencies.
      FAIL: If the policy lacks recent review dates, signatures, or version-control evidence, it indicates non-compliance.
   
   b. CHECK: Assess the list of approved end-user technology products (hardware and software) and compare it against the discovered inventories.
      WHY: Ensuring that a definitive, current list of approved products exists is key to preemptively mitigating risks by preventing unauthorized tools.
      HOW: Examine the approved products list document and cross-check with technical inventory reports or asset management systems. Confirm that recently procured devices/software are on the approved list.
      FAIL: Discrepancies between the approved list and the actual inventory, or an outdated product list, signal lapses in operational enforcement.
   
   c. CHECK: Observe evidence of technical controls enforcing these policies (e.g., endpoint configuration management, automated policy reminders, remote attestation).
      WHY: Technical enforcement ensures that policies are not merely theoretical but actively prevent non-compliant usage patterns.
      HOW: Review system configurations or change management logs from endpoint security tools, audit logs, or network access controls that enforce approved end-user behavior.
      FAIL: Lack of technical enforcement logs or controls means the policy is not operationally effective, even if documented.
   
   d. CHECK: Interview a sample of end-users and relevant IT support personnel regarding training and understanding of the acceptable use policies.
      WHY: Worker awareness and confirmed training demonstrate implementation beyond document existence, ensuring that policies are communicated and understood.
      HOW: Ask personnel about key policy points, see training attendance logs, and check for dissemination announcements on internal communication platforms.
      FAIL: If personnel are unaware or unable to detail any aspects of the policy, it indicates that the documentation is not effectively implemented.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Look for digital audit trails from configuration management systems – these are hard to fake, proving technical enforcement of the policy.
   - Critical Indicator: An up-to-date approved product list and frequent reviews of the acceptable use policy are strong indicators of operational control.
   - Detail to Note: Even with a well-documented policy, lack of training sessions or refresher communications to employees often points to a gap between policy and practice.
   - Verification Advice: Use automated scanning tools to verify software/hardware configurations against approved lists, combining human interviews to verify the practical reach of the policy.

By following these steps and picking up on the critical indicators noted above, auditors can confidently assess whether acceptable use policies are both defined and functioning as intended to enforce secure usage of end-user technologies.
content:
12.2 Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are defined and implemented.

Defined Approach Requirements

12.2.1 Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are documented and implemented, including:  
• Explicit approval by authorized parties.  
• Acceptable uses of the technology.  
• List of products approved by the company for employee use, including hardware and software.

Customized Approach Objective

The use of end-user technologies is defined and managed to ensure authorized usage.

Applicability Notes

Examples of end-user technologies for which acceptable use policies are expected include, but are not limited to, remote access and wireless technologies, laptops, tablets, mobile phones, and removable electronic media, email usage, and Internet usage.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.2.1 Examine the acceptable use policies for end-user technologies and interview responsible personnel to verify processes are documented and implemented in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

End-user technologies are a significant investment and may pose significant risk to an organization if not managed properly. Acceptable use policies outline the expected behavior from personnel when using the organization’s information technology and reflect the organization’s risk tolerance. These policies instruct personnel on what they can and cannot do with company equipment and instruct personnel on correct and incorrect uses of company Internet and email resources. Such policies can legally protect an organization and allow it to act when the policies are violated.

Good Practice

It is important that usage policies are supported by technical controls to manage the enforcement of the policies. Structuring polices as simple "do" and "do not" requirements that are linked to a purpose can help remove ambiguity and provide personnel with the context for the requirement.

title: Responsibility for information security is formally assigned to a CISO or an information security knowledgeable executive.
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology to validate that the information security responsibility is formally assigned to a CISO or an information security knowledgeable executive.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: An updated organizational chart clearly shows a designated CISO or an equivalent executive role with information security responsibilities.
• Technical Artifacts: The official information security policy and board-approved governance documents explicitly state the assignment of security responsibilities to this role; additionally, job descriptions and executive resumes/qualifications that list relevant security certifications or experience.
• Operational Patterns: Evidence of regular executive or board-level security meetings, documented oversight of security programs, and proactive communication (e.g., meeting minutes or security memo distribution) where the designated executive is actively involved.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Assignments: Organizations may simply mention the title in policies or org charts without any evidence that the role is actively engaged in security oversight.
• Paper Compliance: Listing the role on documentation without corresponding meeting minutes, action items, or performance review evidence confirming the executive’s active participation in managing security.
• Role Dilution: Appointing an executive with minimal or no real information security expertise as the designated responsible party.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify Organizational Chart
  WHY: The chart should clearly indicate that the role responsible for information security is positioned within the executive layer.
  HOW: Request the most recent organizational chart; confirm that the CISO or relevant information security executive is shown and appropriately linked to senior management.
  FAIL: The chart does not list a CISO or equivalent, or if the role is placed far from decision-making entities.

• CHECK: Review Information Security Policy Documentation
  WHY: The security policy must formally assign responsibility for information security to a designated executive with proper qualifications.
  HOW: Obtain the current information security policy; look for language that assigns security responsibilities to the CISO or an equivalent executive, and verify board or senior management approval dates.
  FAIL: The policy omits any reference to an executive role with security accountability, or the language is vague and non-binding.

• CHECK: Examine Meeting Minutes and Governance Records
  WHY: Active engagement is demonstrated when the executive is involved in periodic oversight and strategic reviews of the security program.
  HOW: Request minutes from executive, board, or IT governance meetings; look for entries where the designated executive either leads or participates in discussions on security strategy and program updates.
  FAIL: Absence of documented engagement by the security executive in oversight meetings, indicating a nominal appointment only on paper.

• CHECK: Validate Executive Job Descriptions and Qualifications
  WHY: It’s critical that the designated role is filled by an executive who has demonstrable information security experience and relevant credentials.
  HOW: Review job descriptions, resumes, or qualification documentation of the designated executive; check for security-specific certifications (e.g., CISSP, CISM) or extensive prior experience in security roles.
  FAIL: The job description or credentials do not reflect adequate information security expertise, suggesting that the role might not be truly effective in managing security risks.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Cross-check the executive’s participation in security incident post-mortems or strategic risk sessions. Their involvement in these sessions is a strong indicator beyond just a title.
• Critical Indicator: Look at whether there are formal succession or transition plans documented for this role – absence of such can be a red flag for poor implementation.
• Key Detail: Ensure that the documentation and artifacts reflect both policy intent and operational reality. A frequently overlooked detail is that even when policies are sound, if there is no evidence of regular security-focused communication or strategic decision-making involving the executive, the control isn’t effectively implemented.

This multi-faceted validation approach ensures that compliance is not just document-driven but is substantiated with real, operational, and observable evidence of effective implementation.
content:
| Requirements and Testing Procedures | Guidance |
|--------------------------------------|---------|
| **Defined Approach Requirements**    |         |
| 12.1.4 Responsibility for information security is formally assigned to a Chief Information Security Officer or other information security knowledgeable member of executive management. |         |
| **Customized Approach Objective**    | A designated member of executive management is responsible for information security. |
| **Defined Approach Testing Procedures** | **Purpose**  |
| 12.1.4 Examine the information security policy to verify that information security is formally assigned to a Chief Information Security Officer or other information security-knowledgeable member of executive management. | To ensure someone with sufficient authority and responsibility is actively managing and championing the organization's information security program, accountability and responsibility for information security needs to be assigned at the executive level within an organization. |
| **Good Practice**                    |         |
| These executive management positions are often at the most senior level of management and are part of the chief executive level or C-level, typically reporting to the Chief Executive Officer or the Board of Directors. Information security knowledge for this executive management role can be indicated by work experience, education, and/or relevant professional certifications. The expectation is that this individual can provide assurance about the implementation of an effective security program and ensure the right technical experts are employed. Entities should also consider transition and/or succession plans for these key personnel to avoid potential gaps in critical security activities. |         |

title: The security policy clearly defines information security roles and responsibilities for all personnel.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for verifying that the security policy clearly defines information security roles and responsibilities for all personnel (PCI-DSS Req-12.1.3):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes a current, published information security policy that explicitly lists defined roles and responsibilities. Look for distribution records, sign-off sheets, and training attendance logs.
   • Technical artifacts include the policy document (stored in an accessible central repository), configuration management systems indicating policy versioning, and HR records that incorporate role-specific security training.
   • Operational patterns include regular acknowledgment exercises (e.g., annual training, e-signatures) and role-specific training sessions that are tracked and auditable.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes show a generic policy without tailored, role-specific details; this superficially meets policy requirements without demonstrating practical understanding.
   • Relying solely on documentation without evidence of personnel engagement or periodic re-training can be a red flag.
   • Using outdated training records or infrequent policy reviews that don’t reflect current roles and responsibilities is a common pitfall.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the security policy document explicitly defines the roles and responsibilities for information security.
   WHY: This demonstrates that the organization has formally addressed the necessary responsibilities, leaving less room for ambiguity.
   HOW: Retrieve the current security policy from a controlled document repository; confirm sections clearly list individual roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths.
   FAIL: The policy is generic, lacking detailed role delineation, or the document is outdated.

   CHECK: Interview personnel across various departments to confirm they understand their specific information security responsibilities.
   WHY: Direct interviews reveal whether personnel are aware and properly trained, ensuring the policy isn’t just theoretical.
   HOW: Choose a sample across high-risk, operational, and support roles; ask how their roles interact with security processes and what actions they take in case of a breach.
   FAIL: Personnel are unaware of their responsibilities or provide vague, inconsistent answers about the security policy.

   CHECK: Examine personnel acknowledgment records or training certificates confirming they have read and accepted the security responsibilities.
   WHY: Documented acknowledgment strengthens evidence that the policy is effectively communicated and accepted.
   HOW: Review HR or compliance logs for signed acknowledgments, e-learning completion records, or meeting minutes that show training sessions in which roles were explained.
   FAIL: Missing, incomplete, or outdated acknowledgment records indicate compliance is in name only.

   CHECK: Confirm the existence of routine reviews or updates of the security policy to accommodate new roles or changes in responsibilities.
   WHY: Regular updates indicate the organization actively enforces the policy and adapts to evolving security requirements.
   HOW: Check for documented review cycles, update logs, or meeting minutes that capture policy revisions.
   FAIL: Lack of evidence for periodic updates, an outdated policy version, or absence of review logs may imply poor operational effectiveness.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus on discussions during interviews and ask for specific examples of responsibility in action; concrete examples are hard to fake.
   • Indicator: A well-maintained document management system that records versions and updates of the security policy is a strong positive sign.
   • Detail: Look beyond generic training programs—verify that training/testing is tailored to the specific roles to ensure real understanding.
   • Watch out: Inadequate evidence of personnel acknowledgment despite a well-documented policy is often a red flag for non-operational compliance.

Overall, these practical checks ensure that both the policy and its implementation are effective and not merely a paper exercise.
content:
12.1.3 The security policy clearly defines information security roles and responsibilities for all personnel, and all personnel are aware of and acknowledge their information security responsibilities.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.1.3.a Examine the information security policy to verify that they clearly define information security roles and responsibilities for all personnel.

12.1.3.b Interview personnel in various roles to verify they understand their information security responsibilities.

12.1.3.c Examine documented evidence to verify personnel acknowledge their information security responsibilities.

Purpose

Without clearly defined security roles and responsibilities assigned, there could be misuse of the organization’s information assets or inconsistent interaction with information security personnel, leading to insecure implementation of technologies or use of outdated or insecure technologies.

Customized Approach Objective

Personnel understand their role in protecting the entity’s cardholder data.

title: The information security policy is reviewed at least once every 12 months and updated as needed.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: A formally approved information security policy document that includes revision history and dates of review. Meeting minutes or review logs from an annual governance or risk committee that confirm the review process was executed within the past 12 months.
• Technical Artifacts: Document management system logs showing version control, electronic signatures from responsible personnel, and audit trails for policy updates. Access logs that indicate controlled distribution to key staff.
• Operational Patterns: Regular annual training sessions referencing the updated policy and evidence that updated policies are communicated to new hires and existing staff. Routine dashboard reviews by management linking policy reviews with measurable risk outcomes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may list an annual review on paper without systematic revision tracking or may re-use old versions with altered cover dates. In some cases, a one-slide update in a training presentation may be mistaken as a comprehensive policy update.
• Surface-level Implementations: Simply holding an annual meeting without documented outcomes or failing to integrate changes in the policy into operational procedures.
• Frequent Technical Gaps: Lack of version control in a central document repository and absence of automated reminders and audit trails that make the actual review process transparent.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review the policy document for a revision history section.
   WHY: A clear revision history that includes dates and changes demonstrates an ongoing formal review process.
   HOW: Inspect the information security policy document in the document management system; verify that there is an entry showing a review date not older than 12 months.
   FAIL: No revision history, outdated review dates, or missing approvals indicating the policy wasn’t reviewed as required.

2. CHECK: Validate meeting minutes or review logs showing that the annual policy review meeting took place.
   WHY: Minutes and logs provide independent evidence that the review process has been performed beyond just having a document on paper.
   HOW: Request and audit the minutes from governance or risk committees for a meeting in the past 12 months where policy review was on the agenda, including related action items.
   FAIL: Absence of meeting records, or records that do not confirm a detailed review and decision-making related to the security policy.

3. CHECK: Confirm that changes in the policy are reflected in operational communications and training.
   WHY: Operational effectiveness is proven when updated policies are disseminated and embedded in daily practices.
   HOW: Interview a sample of employees, review training records, and check if updated policy notifications or newsletters were issued shortly after the policy review.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies in employee awareness or lack of evidence of updated training, indicating the review has not effectively been integrated into operations.

4. CHECK: Verify automated system logs for document version control and update alerts.
   WHY: Automated logs provide objective evidence of the policy review and update process, reducing the risk of manipulated manual records.
   HOW: Inspect the document management system for automated audit logs showing policy update activity, including dates and responsible users.
   FAIL: Lack of audit trails or automated version control indications that the review process may be inadequately controlled.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Directly access the central document repository and check the metadata for the policy file. Look for automated time stamps that can’t be easily altered.
• Critical Indicator: Incomplete or poorly detailed revision histories are a red flag; always validate against multiple evidence sources (minutes, logs, training records).
• Key Detail: Ensure that the review isn’t simply a tick-box exercise; check for specific updates that reflect evolving business or risk environments rather than minor cosmetic changes.
content:
12.1.2 The information security policy is:
- Reviewed at least once every 12 months.
- Updated as needed to reflect changes to business objectives or risks to the environment.

Customized Approach Objective

The information security policy continues to reflect the organization’s strategic objectives and principles.

title: An overall information security policy is established, published, maintained, and disseminated.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• A published information security policy document available in common repositories (intranet, secure document management systems) with version control and change history.
• Evidence of internal communications (e.g., training sessions, email announcements, sign-off receipts) confirming the policy was disseminated to all relevant personnel, vendors, and partners.
• Operational records such as scheduled reviews, updates, and audit logs indicating the policy is actively maintained.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may only circulate a static copy of the policy without any mechanism to ensure that personnel have read or understood it. This results in a paper policy that does not enforce actual security behavior.
• Relying solely on the existence of a document without demonstrating active refinements or evidence of periodic reviews fails to prove maintenance.
• Showing outdated training records or limited dissemination (only to internal staff) can hide non-compliance regarding vendors and business partners.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the existence and accessibility of the current information security policy document.
WHY: A readily available document that is versioned and dated demonstrates that the policy is established and published.
HOW: Access the organization’s secure portal or document management system and locate the latest version of the policy along with its revision history.
FAIL: If the policy document is missing, outdated, or lacks version control evidencing maintenance.

CHECK: Confirm dissemination by reviewing distribution or training records.
WHY: Evidence of training sessions, acknowledgment receipts, or communications proves the policy was disseminated to all relevant stakeholders.
HOW: Check meeting minutes, training attendance records, or email logs that announce or require policy read and acknowledgment. Interview sample personnel to verify receipt and understanding.
FAIL: Absence of training records or any acknowledgment mechanism indicates potential lapses in dissemination efforts.

CHECK: Interview a cross-section of employees, vendors, and business partners about their awareness of the policy.
WHY: Direct feedback confirms that the information security policy is not only published but understood and incorporated into daily operations.
HOW: Conduct structured interviews or surveys that ask about their understanding of security guidelines and responsibilities.
FAIL: If interviews reveal that most have not read the policy or do not understand their responsibilities, the control is not effectively implemented.

CHECK: Review the change management and periodic review process for the security policy.
WHY: A documented process that schedules reviews and updates proves that the policy is actively maintained.
HOW: Examine change logs, internal memos, and meeting records that discuss the policy's periodic updates and rationale for changes.
FAIL: Lack of evidence showing regular review or updates to the policy indicates that it may not be current or properly maintained.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Focus on the revision history and update logs rather than just the document’s date, as this indicates ongoing maintenance.
• Ensure the dissemination evidence covers all stakeholder groups (internal personnel, third-party vendors, and partners) – often, external entities are overlooked.
• Don’t rely solely on a single interview or record; corroborate multiple sources to confirm that the policy is actionable and well-integrated into the company’s culture.
• Watch for signs of poor implementation such as inconsistent training records or outdated communication channels, which are red flags for compliance gaps.
content:
12.1 A comprehensive information security policy that governs and provides direction for protection of the entity’s information assets is known and current.

Defined Approach Requirements

12.1.1 An overall information security policy is:
- Established.
- Published.
- Maintained.
- Disseminated to all relevant personnel, as well as to relevant vendors and business partners.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

12.1.1 Examine the information security policy and interview personnel to verify that the overall information security policy is managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

An organization’s overall information security policy ties to and governs all other policies and procedures that define protection of cardholder data.

The information security policy communicates management’s intent and objectives regarding the protection of its most valuable assets, including cardholder data.

Without an information security policy, individuals will make their own value decisions on the controls that are required within the organization which may result in the organization neither meeting its legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations, nor being able to adequately protect its assets in a consistent manner.

To ensure the policy is implemented, it is important that all relevant personnel within the organization, as well as relevant third parties, vendors, and business partners are aware of the organization’s information security policy and their responsibilities for protecting information assets.

Customized Approach Objective

The strategic objectives and principles of information security are defined, adopted, and known to all personnel.

title: Unauthorized changes on payment pages are detected and responded to.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• A live tamper‐detection system actively comparing current HTTP headers and JavaScript (or other active content) received in consumer browsers with a known baseline is in place. Screenshots, configuration files, and scheduled job logs provide evidence of weekly or risk‐based (as determined by the entity’s risk analysis) monitoring.
• Technical artifacts include configuration settings for synthetic monitoring tools, scripts embedded for integrity checks, CSP configurations with report-uri/report-to values, and log files that show alerts generated when changes are detected.
• Operational patterns involve regular (at least weekly or risk-analysis defined frequency) generation of alerts, reviews of incident logs, and documented responses (including investigation and remediation) to detected changes on payment pages.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply update baselines immediately after a change, effectively masking tampering events, or they might simulate alerts by generating periodic logs without any actual comparison checks.
• Superficial implementations might focus on static configuration reporting and ignore dynamic page content or the client-side assembly process, making the control ineffective against real-time modifications.
• Gaps often occur when only server-side checks are performed, ignoring client-side content assembly or when the risk analysis defining the scanning frequency isn’t comprehensive.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify the existence and configuration of a tamper-detection mechanism on payment pages
WHY: This confirms that active controls are in place to monitor changes to HTTP headers and active content.
HOW: Inspect system configuration files, synthetic monitoring tool dashboards, and automated job settings (scheduled at least weekly or defined by risk analysis). Examine screenshots or system outputs showing the baseline comparison.
FAIL: Absence of any automated monitoring, out-of-date configuration, or manual-only processes indicates non-compliance.

CHECK: Assess alerting and logging related to detection events
WHY: Timely alerting coupled with evidence of incident logs demonstrates operational functionality.
HOW: Review logs and incident reports for alerts triggered by suspected changes. Interview staff to confirm that alerts prompt an immediate review and remediation.
FAIL: Lack of event logs or alerts, or evidence of alerts being ignored, indicates a bypass of effective monitoring.

CHECK: Test the mechanism’s sensitivity by comparing current content to a known good baseline
WHY: Validates the real-time ability of the mechanism to detect unauthorized code changes.
HOW: Use a controlled test environment or simulation where minor, non-destructive changes are introduced to the payment page. Confirm that an alert is generated and that logs capture these changes with proper details.
FAIL: Failure to detect and alert (or false negatives) shows ineffective implementation.

CHECK: Examine the risk analysis that determines monitoring frequency (if not weekly)
WHY: Ensures that any deviation from a weekly schedule is justified through a documented and robust risk assessment.
HOW: Review the targeted risk analysis documentation and compare it against the configured monitoring schedule.
FAIL: If the risk analysis is missing, outdated, or not aligned with the frequency settings, then the control is not properly implemented.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include cross-referencing automated tool outputs (like synthetic user monitoring dashboards) with live alerts and incident logs.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include static baselines that update with every detected change without verification, and mock alerts with no corresponding log evidence.
• Focus on the integration of client-side monitoring (examining content as received by the browser) rather than just server-side configurations, as this is where tampering would directly affect the consumer.
• Ensure that changes in CSP settings are monitored, as this is a common technique used by skimmers to bypass traditional detection methods.
• Prioritize operational evidence (alerts triggered, logs generated, incidents handled) over mere policy documents; the control’s operational effectiveness is the ultimate indicator of compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.6.1 A change- and tamper-detection mechanism is deployed as follows:
- To alert personnel to unauthorized modification (including indicators of compromise, changes, additions, and deletions) to the security-impacting HTTP headers and the script contents of payment pages as received by the consumer browser.
- The mechanism is configured to evaluate the received HTTP headers and payment pages.
- The mechanism functions are performed as follows:
  - At least weekly  
  OR  
  - Periodically (at the frequency defined in the entity's targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1).

Customized Approach Objective

E-commerce skimming code or techniques cannot be added to payment pages as received by the consumer browser without a timely alert being generated. Anti-skimming measures cannot be removed from payment pages without a prompt alert being generated.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.6.1 Examine system settings, monitored payment pages, and results from monitoring activities to verify the use of a change- and tamper-detection mechanism.

11.6.1.b Examine configuration settings to verify the mechanism is configured in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

11.6.1.c If the mechanism functions are performed at an entity-defined frequency, examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for determining the frequency to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified at Requirement 12.3.1.

11.6.1.d Examine configuration settings and interview personnel to verify the mechanism functions are performed either:
- At least weekly  
  OR  
- At the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis performed for this requirement.

Purpose

Many web pages now rely on assembling objects, including active content (primarily JavaScript), from multiple internet locations. Additionally, the content of many web pages is defined using content management and tag management systems that may not be possible to monitor using traditional change detection mechanisms.

Therefore, the only place to detect changes or indicators of malicious activity is in the consumer browser as the page is constructed and all JavaScript interpreted.

By comparing the current version of the HTTP header and the active content of payment pages as received by the consumer browser with prior or known versions, it is possible to detect unauthorized changes that may indicate a skimming attack, or an attempt to disable a control designed to protect against, or to detect, skimming attacks.

Additionally, by looking for known indicators of compromise and script elements or behavior typical of skimmers, suspicious alerts can be raised.

Applicability Notes (from PDF page 293, Doc page 289):
