This requirement also applies to entities with a webpage(s) that includes a TPPS’/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form (for example, one or more inline frames or iframes.)  
This requirement does not apply to an entity for scripts in a TPPS’/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form (for example, one or more iframes), where the entity includes a TPPS’/payment processor’s payment page/form on its webpage.  
Scripts in the TPPS’/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form are the responsibility of the TPPS/payment processor to manage in accordance with this requirement.  
The intention of this requirement is not that an entity installs software in the systems or browsers of its consumers, but rather that the entity uses techniques such as those described under Examples in the Guidance column to prevent and detect unexpected script activities.  
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Good Practice (from PDF page 293, Doc page 289):

Where an entity includes a TPPS’/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form on its webpage, the entity should expect the TPPS/payment processor to provide evidence that the TPPS/payment processor is meeting this requirement, in accordance with the TPPS/payment processor’s PCI DSS assessment and Requirement 12.9.

Examples (from PDF page 293, Doc page 289):

Mechanisms that detect and report on changes to the headers and content of the payment page could include, but are not limited to, a combination of the following techniques:
- Violations of the Content Security Policy (CSP) can be reported to the entity using the report-to or report-uri CSP directives.
- Changes to the CSP itself can indicate tampering.
- External monitoring by systems that request and analyze the received web pages (also known as synthetic user monitoring) can detect changes to JavaScript in payment pages and alert personnel.
- Embedding tamper-resistant, tamper-detection script in the payment page can alert and block when malicious script behavior is detected.
- Reverse proxies and Content Delivery Networks can detect changes in scripts and alert personnel.

The above list of mechanisms is not exhaustive, and the use of any one mechanism is not necessarily a full detection and reporting mechanism.
Often, these mechanisms are subscription or cloud-based, but can also be based on custom and bespoke solutions.

title: Deploy change-detection mechanisms to alert on unauthorized file modifications.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for PCI DSS req-11.5.2 – deploying change-detection mechanisms to alert on unauthorized file modifications.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible signs include active File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) software installed on key systems with a predefined list of critical files. Administrators should have a dashboard or interface that displays real-time alerts or logs of file changes.
• Technical artifacts such as configuration files of the FIM tool (which list the monitored files and directories), baseline databases containing hash values of critical files, and log files or alerts generated by the tool.
• Operational patterns include regular (at least weekly) scheduled scans against a stored baseline and incident alerts being received or investigated by security personnel.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might show screenshots of FIM dashboards without data proving historical monitoring. Some might claim the tool is active but fail to include critical files or schedule scans only sporadically.
• A superficial implementation might involve only enabling logging, while the change-detection mechanism is not configured to trigger alerts on unauthorized modifications.
• Gaps often observed include omission of custom or application-specific critical files from the monitoring list, or failure to review and update baseline configurations regularly.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify FIM Tool Configuration for Critical Files
WHY: Ensures that the FIM tool is not only installed but is actively configured to monitor all designated critical files and directories.
HOW: Examine the FIM configuration files (or dashboard settings) to check that critical system files, application executables, and configuration files (including custom ones determined by risk assessment) are on the monitoring list. Confirm that baseline hash values are set and updated at intervals.
FAIL: Missing critical files, outdated baseline, or incomplete configuration settings indicate the control is inadequately implemented.

CHECK 2: Review Alert and Log Records for Unauthorized File Modifications
WHY: Validates that the mechanism is actively logging changes and that alerts are generated in a timely manner when unauthorized modifications occur.
HOW: Access and review logs or alert notifications generated by the FIM tool over a defined period. Verify that alerts contain details correlating with file additions, deletions, or modifications. Check timestamps to ensure that scans occur at least weekly.
FAIL: No logs, synthetic or incomplete alert records, or lack of evidence that alerts are processed by security teams indicate poor implementation.

CHECK 3: Test Alert Generation by Simulated File Modification
WHY: A controlled test confirms that the change-detection mechanism actively monitors file integrity and triggers alerts in real time.
HOW: With appropriate permissions and maintenance windows, modify a non-production copy of a designated critical file. Observe if an alert is triggered and review alert logs for correct recording of the event.
FAIL: If no alert is generated or if the alert details are missing or incorrect, then the mechanism is not functioning properly.

CHECK 4: Validate Scheduled Frequency and Automation of Scans
WHY: Ensures that scheduled baseline comparisons are conducted at least once per week as required, demonstrating the operational cadence of the FIM.
HOW: Review system scheduler settings or automated task logs that show when baseline comparisons are executed. Confirm through automated logs that the FIM tool performed scheduled scans and updated comparisions.
FAIL: Irregular or absent scheduling entries or logs, or manually triggered scans only, indicate non-compliance with the frequency requirement.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Directly query the FIM dashboard or use automated scripts to extract recent alert metadata instead of relying solely on static screenshots or documentation.
• Critical Indicator: Pay special attention to whether custom critical files (such as application-specific configuration files) have been added to the monitoring list. This detail is frequently overlooked.
• Detail Matter: Check the integration of alerting with incident response workflows – if alerts are not routed to responsible parties or are not regularly reviewed, the control’s practical effectiveness is compromised.
• Real-world Validation: Use logging correlation from SIEM or another centralized monitoring platform to verify that alerts from the FIM tool are not only generated but are acted upon.

This methodology focuses on verifying the active, operational implementation of the change-detection mechanism and emphasizes evidence that cannot be easily faked by superficial compliance measures.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.5.2 A change-detection mechanism (for example, file integrity monitoring tools) is deployed as follows:
- To alert personnel to unauthorized modification (including changes, additions, and deletions) of critical files.
- To perform critical file comparisons at least once weekly.

Customized Approach Objective

Critical files cannot be modified by unauthorized personnel without an alert being generated.

---

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.5.2.a Examine system settings, monitored files, and results from monitoring activities to verify the use of a change-detection mechanism.

11.5.2.b Examine settings for the change-detection mechanism to verify it is configured in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Guidance

Purpose

Changes to critical system, configuration, or content files can be an indicator an attacker has accessed an organization’s system. Such changes can allow an attacker to take additional malicious actions, access cardholder data, and/or conduct activities without detection or record.

A change detection mechanism will detect and evaluate such changes to critical files and generate alerts that can be responded to following defined processes so that personnel can take appropriate actions.

Applicability Notes

For change-detection purposes, critical files are usually those that do not regularly change, but the modification of which could indicate a system compromise or risk of compromise. Change-detection mechanisms such as file integrity monitoring products usually come pre-configured with critical files for the related operating system. Other critical files, such as those for custom applications, must be evaluated and defined by the entity (that is, the merchant or service provider).

Guidance

If not implemented properly and the output of the change-detection solution monitored, a malicious individual could add, remove, or alter configuration file contents, operating system programs, or application executables. Unauthorized changes, if undetected, could render existing security controls ineffective and/or result in cardholder data being stolen with no perceptible impact to normal processing.

Good Practice

Examples of the types of files that should be monitored include, but are not limited to:
- System executables.
- Application executables.
- Configuration and parameter files.
- Centrally stored, historical, or archived audit logs.
- Additional critical files determined by entity (for example, through risk assessment or other means).

Examples

Change-detection solutions such as file integrity monitoring (FIM) tools check for changes, additions, and deletions to critical files, and notify when such changes are detected.

title: For service providers, detect and address covert malware communication channels.
methodology:
Below is the detailed validation methodology for PCI DSS req-11.5.1.1 for service providers, focusing on the detection and addressing of covert malware communication channels.

────────────────────────────────────────────
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
────────────────────────────────────────────
• Visible evidence includes active IDS/IPS systems that are configured to inspect traffic patterns for covert or command-and-control (C2) communications.
• Technical artifacts such as live alert logs, configuration files for intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS), and documented response procedures tied to covert malware communications.
• Operational patterns where alerts are generated in real-time, automatically correlated to incident-response actions, and staff can demonstrate knowledge through incident debriefs or training records.

────────────────────────────────────────────
2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
────────────────────────────────────────────
• Organizations may only configure signature-based detection without proper tuning or updating threat intelligence, resulting in false negatives.
• Some may have static documentation on procedures without evidence that the systems are actively monitoring or that personnel are effectively responding to alerts.
• Surface-level compliance may include merely enabling logging while not having effective mechanisms that analyze or act on indicators of covert C2 communications.

────────────────────────────────────────────
3. VALIDATION STEPS
────────────────────────────────────────────
CHECK: Review IDS/IPS Configuration for C2 Traffic Detection
WHY: Validates that automated systems are configured to inspect for covert malware communication signatures and behaviors.
HOW: Inspect configuration files and rule sets of IDS/IPS devices; verify that signatures for malicious or unusual outbound communications are present and current.
FAIL: Absence of configurations for detecting malware command channels or use of outdated signatures that don’t match current threat intelligence.

CHECK: Analyze Alert Logs and Incident Response Correlation
WHY: Demonstrates that the detection systems are actively monitoring, generating alerts, and that these alerts feed into a responsive incident management process.
HOW: Retrieve a sample of recent alert logs from IDS/IPS devices; check if alerts related to suspicious or anomalous outbound communications occurred and review the response actions taken (automation or manual).
FAIL: Lack of recent alerts pertaining to covert communications or alerts that do not result in documented incident-response actions.

CHECK: Examine the Incident Response Plan (IRP) and Evidence of Execution
WHY: Ensures the IRP specifically addresses responses to discovered covert malware communications, aligning with the control requirements.
HOW: Review the documented IRP sections that detail responses to detected covert comms; interview incident response personnel to confirm familiarity and recent training or exercises addressing such scenarios.
FAIL: An IRP that does not mention covert communication handling or personnel lacking awareness/training on responding to such alerts.

CHECK: Interview and Observe Security Personnel Knowledge
WHY: Confirms that personnel are trained, knowledgeable, and actively maintain awareness regarding covert malware communication techniques and response procedures.
HOW: Conduct interviews with key security staff and observe live or simulated incident response exercises; ask for specific examples of handling alerts related to covert channels.
FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly articulate detection, alerting, or response procedures regarding covert malware communications.

────────────────────────────────────────────
4. EXPERT TIPS
────────────────────────────────────────────
• Shortcut: Directly sample live alert dashboards during peak traffic hours. Real-time evidence of alert generation tied to anomalous communication patterns is hard to fake.
• Critical Indicator: Ensure that signature and anomaly detection rules are frequently updated to reflect emerging threats; static or outdated rules often indicate poor implementation.
• Detail: Don’t solely rely on documentation and policies; always correlate with live logs, system configurations, and personnel interviews to ensure operational effectiveness.
• Practical Observation: In environments with high service provider turnover, verified, periodic training logs and incident drill records are a strong signal of operational readiness.

This multi-pronged approach provides a robust, evidence-based validation ensuring not only the presence of controls but also their active, effective operation in preventing covert malware communications.
content:
Additional Defined Approach Requirements

11.5.1.1 Additional requirement for service providers only:
Intrusion-detection and/or intrusion-prevention techniques detect, alert on/prevent, and address covert malware communication channels.

Additional Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.5.1.a Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:
Examine documentation and configuration settings to verify that methods to detect and alert on/prevent covert malware communication channels are in place and operating.

11.5.1.b Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:
Examine the entity’s incident-response plan (Requirement 12.10.1) to verify it requires and defines a response in the event that covert malware communication channels are detected.

11.5.1.c Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only:
Interview responsible personnel and observe processes to verify that personnel maintain knowledge of covert malware communication and control techniques and are knowledgeable about how to respond when malware is suspected.

Customized Approach Objective

Mechanisms are in place to detect and alert/prevent covert communications with command-and-control systems. Alerts generated by these mechanisms are responded to by personnel, or by automated means that ensure that such communications are blocked.

Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider. This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Deploy intrusion-detection and prevention techniques.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for confirming that intrusion-detection and prevention techniques are both deployed and effective, in line with control req-11.5.1:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible network diagrams showing IDS/IPS sensors at the CDE perimeter and at all critical segmentation points, including DMZs and inter-network boundaries.
   • Live system dashboards or SIEM consoles demonstrating continuous monitoring and a history of intrusion alerts.
   • System configurations and update logs revealing that IDS/IPS engines, baselines, and signatures are automatically updated on a regular schedule.
   • Operational incident logs or alert reports that detail personnel notifications when suspicious activity is detected.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might claim IDS/IPS is deployed by presenting outdated network diagrams or static policy documents that do not reflect actual sensor placements.
   • Relying solely on periodic manual updates rather than automated signature and baseline updates can result in stale, ineffective protection.
   • Overreliance on documentation without verifying live alerts or system logs, or using generic ‘dummy’ rules that trigger in test environments only.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify IDS/IPS sensor placement on network diagrams.
   WHY: Ensures that all traffic at the perimeter and other critical junctures is monitored, a key requirement of the control.
   HOW: Review updated network diagrams, cross-check sensor IP addresses against live configurations, and confirm sensor physical/logical positioning in the infrastructure.
   FAIL: Gaps in diagrams or mismatches with live configurations indicating unmonitored segments.

   CHECK: Inspect system logs and SIEM alerts for evidence of active detection and incident escalation.
   WHY: Demonstrates that the monitoring system is actively capturing threats and automatically alerting personnel in real time.
   HOW: Retrieve recent logs from IDS/IPS systems, verify timestamps of alerts, and confirm that there are records of notifications being issued to designated security teams.
   FAIL: Absence of recent alerts or improper integration with alerting mechanisms suggests the control is either misconfigured or not functioning.

   CHECK: Validate that IDS/IPS signatures, baselines, and engine updates are current.
   WHY: Consistent updates are critical for ensuring detection of evolving threats and maintaining an effective defense.
   HOW: Examine configuration files or system dashboards that display update histories and scheduled update tasks (e.g., cron jobs, push logs from vendor systems).
   FAIL: Evidence of outdated signatures or lack of update logs signifies operational risk and non-compliance with the control.

   CHECK: Interview operational staff and review escalation procedures.
   WHY: Confirms that not only is the technology in place, but the human response to alerts is defined and effective.
   HOW: Conduct brief interviews or review documented incident response processes/logs to ensure that alerts result in timely investigations and interventions.
   FAIL: Inability to trace recent alerts to personnel actions signals a breakdown between detection and response.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Quickly verify sensor placement by correlating live network interface statistics (e.g., using SNMP or NetFlow data) with documented sensor positions.
   • Look for anomalies in update logs (e.g., irregular update intervals) that might indicate an automated system has been tampered with.
   • Double-check the SIEM’s correlation rules; outdated or improperly tuned rules can mask real intrusions or set off too many false positives.
   • Prioritize automated/observable evidence over solely policy documents; running test probes (with permission) can expose real-time responsiveness of the IDS/IPS, which arrangements on paper may not reflect.

This methodology focuses on extracting real operational evidence that cannot be easily faked and provides a robust assessment of the control's effectiveness in actively monitoring and protecting the environment.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.5.1 Intrusion-detection and/or intrusion-prevention techniques are used to detect and/or prevent intrusions into the network as follows:
 - All traffic is monitored at the perimeter of the CDE.
 - All traffic is monitored at critical points in the CDE.
 - Personnel are alerted to suspected compromises.
 - All intrusion-detection and prevention engines, baselines, and signatures are kept up to date.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.5.1.a Examine system configurations and network diagrams to verify that intrusion-detection and/or intrusion-prevention techniques are in place to monitor all traffic:
 - At the perimeter of the CDE.
 - At critical points in the CDE.

11.5.1.b Examine system configurations and interview responsible personnel to verify intrusion-detection and/or intrusion-prevention techniques alert personnel of suspected compromises.

11.5.1.c Examine system configurations and vendor documentation to verify intrusion-detection and/or intrusion-prevention techniques are configured to keep all engines, baselines, and signatures up to date.

Purpose

Intrusion-detection and/or intrusion-prevention techniques (such as IDS/IPS) compare the traffic coming into the network with known “signatures” and/or behaviors of thousands of compromise types (hacker tools, Trojans, and other malware), and then send alerts and/or stop the attempt as it happens. Without a proactive approach to detect unauthorized activity, attacks on (or misuse of) computer resources could go unnoticed for long periods of time. The impact of an intrusion into the CDE is, in many ways, a factor of the time that an attacker has in the environment before being detected.

Good Practice

Security alerts generated by these techniques should be continually monitored, so that the attempted or actual intrusions can be stopped, and potential damage limited.

Definitions

Critical locations could include, but are not limited to, network security controls between network segments (for example, between a DMZ and an internal network or between an in-scope and out-of-scope network) and points protecting connections between a less trusted and a more trusted system component.

title: For multi-tenant service providers, support customer penetration testing.
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for verifying that a multi-tenant service provider supports customer penetration testing, in line with PCI DSS req-11.4.7.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for documented procedures and communications showing how customers initiate and undergo penetration tests. This might include tenant-specific request logs, scheduled testing windows, and official responses or escalations.
   • Technical Artifacts: Check helpdesk/ticketing systems, change management logs, and network diagrams demonstrating isolation of tenant environments during testing. Supporting evidence might also include segregated infrastructure controls that ensure one tenant’s test does not impact others.
   • Operational Patterns: Observe recurring test request processes and confirm that customer penetration testing requests are routinely tracked, approved, and accompanied by resulting test reports.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply upload a generic penetration testing policy or periodic test reports that do not reflect tenant-specific processes. These can be superficial documents with no accompanying operational logs.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Providers may have a stated procedure for penetration testing support on documentation, but not provide test request and reaction logs or fail to isolate test environments from production.
   • Technical Gaps: A common gap is the lack of detailed ticketing/throttling evidence showing that customer-specific requests are not only received but are actively known and managed by security teams.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review evidence of an operational process for supporting customer penetration test requests.
   WHY: This confirms that procedures are in place, and that the service provider actively supports and tracks penetration testing requests without impacting other tenants.
   HOW: Interview service desk/security personnel; review communication logs (emails, support tickets) and documented procedures showing how requests are handled and tracked.
   FAIL: No evidence of ticketing logs or customer communications confirming that penetration test requests are received and actioned.

   CHECK: Verify that network segregation and testing isolation controls are in effect for tenant environments.
   WHY: Ensures that when penetration testing occurs, tests are contained and do not interfere with other customers’ systems—a fundamental requirement in a multi-tenant setting.
   HOW: Examine network diagrams, infrastructure configurations, and incident logs to check that testing environments are segregated. Look for documented change management procedures that account for testing impacts.
   FAIL: Lack of clear network segmentation or absence of any evidence that testing activities are isolated from production or non-targeted tenant environments.

   CHECK: Confirm existence of SLAs or contractual agreements that specifically support customer penetration testing.
   WHY: Formalizing the testing process (through SLAs or MOUs) is a strong indicator that the organization has operationalized its support for customer tests in a measurable way.
   HOW: Request review of customer contract addendums, SLAs, or documented standard operating procedures (SOPs) demonstrating that customers’ penetration testing is acknowledged and supported with defined timelines and escalation processes.
   FAIL: No formal documented agreement or the presence of outdated/irrelevant policies that do not address the unique requirements of multi-tenant penetration testing.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • When evaluating evidence, prioritize automated logs and system-generated support tickets over static policy documents, as these are less susceptible to manipulation.
   • Look for communication time stamps and consistent follow-through on test requests: delays or gaps can indicate inadequate process maturity.
   • Verify that technical diagrams clearly illustrate tenant isolation and that any notes on testing windows reflect safeguards for protecting non-targeted tenants.
   • In interviews, ask detailed questions about emergency protocols during testing. Inadequate responses may signal that testing can inadvertently impact the multi-tenant environment.
   • Check that all evidence is current and that any penetration test results provided are tied to unique customer requests rather than generic test outcomes.

Overall, effective validation will demonstrate that the service provider not only allows penetration testing but has established robust, repeatable, and isolated processes to support such activities for each tenant without compromising the overall environment.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.4.7 Additional requirement for multi-tenant service providers only: Multi-tenant service providers support their customers for external penetration testing per Requirement 11.4.3 and 11.4.4.

Customized Approach Objective

Multi-tenant service providers support their customers' need for technical testing either by providing access or evidence that comparable technical testing has been undertaken.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.4.7 Additional testing procedure for multi-tenant service providers only: Examine evidence to verify that multi-tenant service providers support their customers for external penetration testing per Requirement 11.4.3 and 11.4.4.

Guidance

Purpose

Entities need to conduct penetration tests in accordance with PCI DSS to simulate attacker behavior and discover vulnerabilities in their environment. In shared and cloud environments, the multi-tenant service provider may be concerned about the activities of a penetration tester affecting other customers' systems. Multi-tenant service providers cannot forbid penetration testing because this would leave their customers' systems open to exploitation. Therefore, multi-tenant service providers must support customer requests to conduct penetration testing or for penetration testing results.

(Continued on next page with Applicability Notes regarding multi-tenant service providers.)

title: For service providers, test segmentation controls at increased frequency.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for dated penetration test reports that explicitly cover segmentation controls along with documented test intervals (at least every six months and following any network changes).
   • Technical Artifacts: Evidence includes network diagrams showing segmentation boundaries, automated test logs or scan outputs verifying that the CDE is isolated from out-of-scope systems, and change management logs that reference network segmentation changes.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular scheduling of tests in calendars or ticketing systems, and ongoing review meetings where results from segmentation testing are discussed for timely remediation.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations may simply recycle old test reports or produce reports that do not clearly reference segmentation controls specifically, thereby covering only parts of the network.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Some may have superficial checklists stating tests were done without granular evidence proving isolation between CDE and out-of-scope systems.
   • Technical Gaps: Incomplete coverage of all segmentation control mechanisms, lack of integration between automated scanning logs and penetration testing results, and omission of testing after significant network changes are common oversights.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review the most recent penetration test report for segmentation controls.
   WHY: This ensures that the required testing frequency and scope (covering segmentation controls and any changes) are met according to the defined testing methodology.
   HOW: Request and cross-check the test report dates, scope details (ensuring all segmentation methods are included), and verify that tests were performed at least every six months and post any significant change.
   FAIL: If test reports are missing, dated beyond six months, or do not clearly address the segmentation controls in use.

   CHECK: Verify tester qualifications and independence.
   WHY: Qualified and independent testers reduce conflict of interest and ensure unbiased testing outcomes.
   HOW: Interview personnel or review certification details in the test report to ensure the penetration test was performed by either a qualified internal resource or a third party, and check that documentation indicates organizational independence from the testing target.
   FAIL: If qualifications are not documented, if the tester repeatedly works on the same network without independence, or if there is any evidence of conflict in roles.

   CHECK: Examine technical evidence of segmentation effectiveness.
   WHY: To confirm that segmentation controls are operational and indeed isolate the CDE from out-of-scope systems.
   HOW: Review network diagrams, automated test/scan logs, and vulnerability assessment data that show no paths between the CDE and systems outside the scope. Look for testing that demonstrates no traffic leakage or unauthorized connectivity between segregated network zones.
   FAIL: If network diagrams are outdated, or if technical scans and logs reveal misconfigurations or potential paths between the CDE and lower-segregated segments.

   CHECK: Confirm change management integration with segmentation testing schedules.
   WHY: This ensures that any alterations to segmentation controls trigger additional testing, as required.
   HOW: Check change management records for thorough documentation that any significant network changes prompted timely updated testing and that updated test reports are available.
   FAIL: If there is no linkage between network changes and subsequent segmentation tests, or if management logs lack records of changes triggering tests.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Look for a master schedule or dashboard that aggregates penetration testing dates and scopes. This provides a quick overview if tests are regularly executed as mandated.
   • Critical Indicator: Pay special attention to instances where segmentation controls are updated. Missing or outdated test reports immediately flag a potential compliance gap, as fresh tests are a must post-change.
   • Detail Focus: Ensure that penetration test reports specifically call out segmentation controls and include technical verifications (such as log entries and network configuration checks) rather than generic testing statements.
   • Practical Quick Win: Use automated network discovery and scan tools to cross-reference reported segmentation boundaries with actual network topology in near real time; discrepancies here are hard to fake and indicate testing might not be fully operational.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements  

11.4.6 Additional requirement for service providers only: If segmentation is used to isolate the CDE from other networks, penetration tests are performed on segmentation controls as follows:  
- At least once every six months and after any changes to segmentation controls/methods.  
- Covering all segmentation controls/methods in use.  
- According to the entity’s defined penetration testing methodology.  
- Confirming that the segmentation controls/methods are operational and effective, and isolate the CDE from all out-of-scope systems.  
- Confirming effectiveness of any use of isolation to separate systems with differing security levels (see Requirement 2.2.3).  
- Performed by a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party.  
- Organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures  

11.4.6.a Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Examine the results from the most recent penetration test to verify that the penetration covers and addressed all elements specified in this requirement.

11.4.6.b Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Interview personnel to verify that the test was performed by a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party and that organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Purpose  

Service providers typically have access to greater volumes of cardholder data or can provide an entry point that can be exploited to then compromise multiple other entities. Service providers also typically have larger and more complex networks that are subject to more frequent change. The probability of segmentation controls failing in complex and dynamic networks is greater in service-provider environments. 

Guidance  

Although the requirement specifies that this scope validation is carried out at least once every six months and after significant change, this exercise should be performed as frequently as possible to ensure it remains effective at isolating the CDE from other networks.

Customized Approach Objective  

If segmentation is used, it is verified by technical testing to be continually effective, including after any changes, in isolating the CDE from out-of-scope systems.

Applicability Notes  

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

title: Test segmentation controls via penetration testing when used.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Existence of periodic penetration test reports specifically targeting segmentation controls.
• Artifacts including change management logs tied to segmentation control tests after configuration changes.
• Evidence of tests that validate isolation between the CDE and out-of-scope systems (e.g., port scans, host discovery reports).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might only perform a network scan without simulating real penetration testing techniques or exploiting potential weaknesses.
• Relying solely on static documentation or policies without substantiating with dynamic test results.
• Failing to test all segmentation vectors or neglecting to re-test after changes, thus not verifying effectiveness in the current environment.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that penetration test reports for segmentation controls exist and are dated within the past 12 months (or after any segmentation control changes).
   WHY: Recency and post-change testing confirm ongoing operational effectiveness of segmentation measures.
   HOW: Review penetration testing reports, change management records, and verify the test scheduling process; use automated log review if available.
   FAIL: Absence of recent reports or evidence of missed tests after segmentation infrastructure modifications indicates non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Confirm that the penetration test methodology explicitly covers all segmentation controls and isolation boundaries between the CDE and untrusted networks.
   WHY: This ensures the test is comprehensive and addresses all potential attack paths.
   HOW: Examine the defined penetration testing methodology documentation and correlate it with network topology diagrams; cross-reference test scopes with controls in use.
   FAIL: Methodology lacking specific segmentation testing steps or incomplete coverage of all isolation points suggests a superficial penetration test.

3. CHECK: Interview or obtain independent confirmation that the tester (internal or external) operated with organizational independence and had the necessary qualifications.
   WHY: Independent and qualified testing reduces the risk of conflicts of interest and ensures unbiased results.
   HOW: Verify tester credentials, review third party assessment contracts or internal auditor notes, and check for evidence of organizational separation from network operations.
   FAIL: If the individual is closely involved in network management or lacks recognized penetration testing skills, effectiveness may be compromised.

4. CHECK: Analyze the technical testing outputs (e.g., host discovery, port scanning, exploit attempts) to ensure they demonstrate that out-of-scope systems have no unauthorized access to the CDE.
   WHY: These outputs directly confirm that the network segmentation is robust against lateral movement and intrusion.
   HOW: Request and review technical logs, automated scanning reports, or penetration test detailed summaries that highlight each isolated network segment and its test results.
   FAIL: Any report indicating open ports, responsive hosts where none should be present, or discovered vulnerabilities that allow lateral movement undermines the segmentation control's effectiveness.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Focus on evidence that links scheduled tests with network changes; automated alerts that indicate when segmentation configurations are modified can serve as triggers for re-testing.
• Critical Indicator: Repeated similar findings or unresolved vulnerabilities in test reports signal an ineffective segmentation control strategy.
• Key Detail: Ensure the tester’s methodology is independent from the systems under test – a report produced by personnel who also manage the segmentation controls may not be entirely impartial.
• Observation: Automated network scanning should be cross-verified with manual testing outcomes to ensure no superficial misconfiguration has been overlooked due to automated blind spots.

Overall, the practical approach is to combine documentation examination with technical evidence review and direct validation of operational procedures. This multi-faceted verification ensures that segmentation controls are effective and the periodic testing process is both rigorous and independent.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.4.5 If segmentation is used to isolate the CDE from other networks, penetration tests are performed on segmentation controls as follows:

- At least once every 12 months and after any changes to segmentation controls/methods
- Covering all segmentation controls/methods in use.
- According to the entity’s defined penetration testing methodology.
- Confirming that the segmentation controls/methods are operational and effective, and isolate the CDE from all out-of-scope systems.
- Confirming effectiveness of any use of isolation to separate systems with differing security levels (see Requirement 2.2.3).
- Performed by a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party.
- Organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.4.5.a Examine segmentation controls and review penetration-testing methodology to verify that penetration-testing procedures are defined to test all segmentation methods in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

11.4.5.b Examine the results from the most recent penetration test to verify the penetration test covers and addresses all elements specified in this requirement.

11.4.5.c Interview personnel to verify that the test was performed by a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party and that organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Guidance

Purpose

When an entity uses segmentation controls to isolate the CDE from internal untrusted networks, the security of the CDE is dependent on that segmentation functioning. Many attacks have involved the attacker moving laterally from what an entity deemed an isolated network into the CDE. Using penetration testing tools and techniques to validate that an untrusted network is indeed isolated from the CDE can alert the entity to a failure or misconfiguration of the segmentation controls, which can then be rectified.

Good Practice

Techniques such as host discovery and port scanning can be used to verify out-of-scope segments have no access to the CDE.

Customized Approach Objective

If segmentation is used, it is verified periodically by technical testing to be continually effective, including after any changes, in isolating the CDE from all out-of-scope systems.

title: Correct exploitable vulnerabilities found during penetration testing.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes change logs, remediation tickets, and rescheduled penetration test reports that explicitly reference the correction of previously exploited vulnerabilities.
• Technical artifacts include before-and-after vulnerability scan reports, penetration testing reports, and evidence of automated re-tests (e.g., scripts or security dashboards) showing that the issues were remediated.
• Operational patterns include a recurring process where penetration testing results trigger remediation workflows followed by re-testing, along with risk assessments reflecting the corrections.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes provide documentation that indicates vulnerabilities were addressed without presenting evidence of re-testing or verification.
• Surface-level implementations may show that a ticket was closed without evidence that the vulnerability was fully remediated.
• Frequently missed technical gaps include failing to run a comprehensive re-penetration test or relying solely on vendor statements and checklists without independent validation.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review Penetration Test Reports
WHY: Confirm that exploitable vulnerabilities documented in the initial penetration test are listed as remediated in follow-up reports.
HOW: Compare the initial penetration test report with the follow-up test findings, ensuring that formerly exploitable vulnerabilities are absent or confirmed as fixed.
FAIL: If vulnerabilities remain present, or if a follow-up test was not performed, the control cannot be confirmed as effective.

CHECK: Verify Change Management and Ticketing Evidence
WHY: Demonstrates that remediation process steps were followed and corrective actions were tracked.
HOW: Inspect the change logs and ticketing system for entries correlated with the remediation of penetration test findings, including risk assessments and evidence of re-testing.
FAIL: Missing or incomplete ticket records, or if the tickets do not tie back to the vulnerabilities discovered.

CHECK: Validate Automated Re-Testing or Independent Confirmation
WHY: Independent verification that vulnerabilities have been corrected beyond internal claims.
HOW: Check for automated security scanning or independent penetration test results that corroborate remediation, including timestamps and remediation notes.
FAIL: Lack of third-party validation or only internal unverified confirmations indicate potential for compliance faking.

CHECK: Assess Risk Evaluation Documentation
WHY: Ensures that the corrections are prioritized and applied based on the risk posed by the vulnerability.
HOW: Review the risk assessment documents tied to the remediation process to verify that remediation actions correlate with vulnerabilities of high or exploitable risk.
FAIL: If risk assessment is missing or does not align with remediation priorities, then the control may be superficially implemented.

EXPERT TIPS
• Look for clear linkage between penetration test findings, risk evaluation, and remediation evidence – missing links indicate possible policy gaps.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include outdated penetration test cycles, untimely remediation logs, and absence of automated re-testing outputs.
• A practical shortcut is to verify timestamps: the time between vulnerability discovery and remediation should be short and documented, demonstrating an active security posture.
• Ensure that validation isn't based solely on documentation; verify that technical artifacts (reports, change logs, and system scans) provide observable proof of remediation action.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.4.4 Exploitable vulnerabilities and security weaknesses found during penetration testing are corrected as follows:
• In accordance with the entity’s assessment of the risk posed by the security issue as defined in Requirement 6.3.1.
• Penetration testing is repeated to verify the corrections.

Customized Approach Objective

Vulnerabilities and security weaknesses found while verifying system defenses are mitigated.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.4.4 Examine penetration testing results to verify that noted exploitable vulnerabilities and security weaknesses were corrected in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Guidance

Purpose

The results of a penetration test are usually a prioritized list of vulnerabilities discovered by the exercise. Often a tester will have chained a number of vulnerabilities together to compromise a system component. Remediating the vulnerabilities found by a penetration test significantly reduces the probability that the same vulnerabilities will be exploited by a malicious attacker.

Good Practice

As part of the entity’s assessment of risk, entities should consider how likely the vulnerability is to be exploited and whether there are other controls present in the environment to reduce the risk. Any weaknesses that point to PCI DSS requirements not being met should be addressed.

title: Conduct external penetration testing regularly.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Look for formal penetration test reports that include the scope, methodology, dates, and findings. These reports should be dated within the last 12 months or following any significant changes for the systems/applications.
• Evidence of a defined penetration testing methodology (whether in internal policies or documented approaches) that matches industry best practices.
• Interview responses and documentation confirming that the testers are either a qualified internal resource or a reputable external third party with organizational independence.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may submit outdated test reports or tests performed by personnel who lack true independence (e.g., testers from the team that built the system).
• Generating superficial reports that don’t cover key areas of the penetration test or using a templated report without depth.
• Stating that a test was conducted without linking it to any significant changes in the environment, or using internal resources that are not fully independent.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Review the most recent external penetration test reports
WHY: Valid reports with clear dates, scope, and methodology confirm that tests are performed at least annually and after significant changes
HOW: Obtain the latest penetration test report(s), verify the test dates, compare the scope against system change records, and ensure it includes details on methodology and findings
FAIL: Absence of a recent report, test dates beyond 12 months, or reports that lack details on scope or methodologies

CHECK: Verify that the test scope aligns with both scheduled and post-change requirements
WHY: Ensures that the external penetration test covers all critical infrastructure and applications especially after significant changes
HOW: Cross-reference project change logs with the penetration testing schedule; look for an addendum or separate report addressing tests conducted after major upgrades
FAIL: Test reports that do not reflect changes or an outdated scope that excludes new system elements

CHECK: Confirm tester qualifications and organizational independence
WHY: Qualified and independent testers are essential to achieving an unbiased and comprehensive assessment
HOW: Interview stakeholders or review documentation that outlines the tester’s credentials, certifications, and contractual independence (ensure the tester is not tied to day-to-day operations)
FAIL: Evidence that the tester is not truly independent or lacks documented qualifications

CHECK: Validate that a defined and repeatable testing methodology is used
WHY: A structured approach ensures consistency and thoroughness in identifying vulnerabilities
HOW: Review the organization’s documented penetration testing methodology, verify its alignment with industry standards, and compare this with the execution details in the test reports
FAIL: Absence of a defined methodology, deviation from documented procedures, or reliance on ad hoc testing methods

EXPERT TIPS:
• Quickly verify compliance by checking that report dates are within the past year and that they include comprehensive details covering both routine and post-change tests.
• Watch out for signs of reusing old reports or summaries that do not match the latest system configuration—this is often a shortcut used to fake compliance.
• During interviews, ask for specifics about how independence is maintained; vague answers or over-reliance on a single tester source can indicate potential conflicts of interest.
• Pay attention to any reports lacking depth or clear remediation/action items; these may be clear indicators that the test was superficial and did not explore the environment comprehensively.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements 

11.4.3 External penetration testing is performed:  
• Per the entity's defined methodology  
• At least once every 12 months  
• After any significant infrastructure or application upgrade or change  
• By a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party  
• Organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV)  

Defined Approach Testing Procedures  

11.4.3.a Examine the scope of work and results from the most recent external penetration test to verify that penetration testing is performed according to all elements specified in this requirement.  

11.4.3.b Interview personnel to verify that the external penetration test was performed by a qualified internal resource or qualified external third party and that organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Customized Approach Objective

External system defenses are verified by technical testing according to the entity's defined methodology as frequently as needed to address evolving and new attacks and threats, and to ensure that significant changes do not introduce unknown vulnerabilities.

Guidance

Further Information
Refer to the Information Supplement: Penetration Testing Guidance on the PCI SSC website for additional guidance.

title: Conduct internal penetration testing regularly.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Recent internal penetration testing reports including documented scope, methodology, findings, and remediation actions. Minutes from management review or risk committee meetings discussing test results further validate the process.
   - Technical Artifacts: Detailed test reports with timestamps, change logs demonstrating tests were repeated after significant upgrades, and evidence of an independent testing team (internal or external) with credentials or qualifications listed.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular scheduling of tests as per a defined calendar, post-upgrade testing confirmations, and a consistent record of follow-up remediation on identified vulnerabilities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faked Compliance: Organizations might submit outdated reports or ones that were generated using semi-automated tools without manual verification. They sometimes simply tick off a test box without ensuring the scope covers relevant internal network segments.
   - Surface-level Implementations: Using an internally developed checklist as a substitute for a full penetration test by a qualified party; reusing previous reports with minor cosmetic changes; or intentionally limiting the reported scope to avoid exposing critical issues.
   - Technical Gaps: Reporting that omits evidence of testing post-significant changes, or examples where the testers lack demonstrable qualifications or the required organizational independence.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   - CHECK: Verify the latest penetration testing report includes a clearly defined scope, up-to-date methodology, and evidence of testing after recent infrastructure or application changes.
     WHY: This confirms that the control is being actively implemented according to defined criteria and reflects current network conditions.
     HOW: Request the most recent internal penetration test report, check for a detailed description of the test scope, timeline, and any references to significant changes that prompted the test. Look for dynamic testing dates and operational evidence (e.g. test logs) that correlate with network updates.
     FAIL: Missing reports, dates older than 12 months, or lack of evidence tying the test to recent significant changes indicate non-compliance.

   - CHECK: Interview and validate that the testers (internal or external) have the necessary qualifications and maintain organizational independence.
     WHY: Qualified testers ensure comprehensive coverage and unbiased testing outcomes, essential for vulnerability detection and risk assessment.
     HOW: Interview security management or personnel involved in selecting testers, review resumes or credentials of the penetration testers, and verify that conflict-of-interest policies or independent status declarations are maintained.
     FAIL: If testers have demonstrable conflicts (e.g., part of the team that supports the tested system) or lack any formal certification/experience records, the test may be compromised.

   - CHECK: Confirm that penetration testing is scheduled and performed at least annually and additionally after any significant infrastructure or application changes.
     WHY: Regular testing ensures ongoing security assurance and verifies that the system remains robust against emerging threats post-changes.
     HOW: Examine the documented testing schedule/calendar, compare test dates with known significant system updates. Verify that test reports reference these changes and include recommendations addressing any faults found post-upgrade.
     FAIL: Absence of a regular testing schedule, or mismatches between test dates and known significant system updates, strongly indicate gaps in compliance.

   - CHECK: Evaluate follow-up actions on remediation efforts for vulnerabilities identified in the penetration tests.
     WHY: Remediation tracking confirms that the penetration tests lead to actionable improvements, demonstrating operational effectiveness rather than mere documentation exercises.
     HOW: Review vulnerability tracking systems or remediation logs, and interview stakeholders to confirm that identified risks were appropriately managed.
     FAIL: Unaddressed or poorly prioritized vulnerabilities despite repeated testing indicate lack of operational follow-through.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Ask for a brief but detailed walkthrough of the last internal penetration test, focusing on what triggered it, the testing scope, and key findings. Real testers typically provide insightful commentary that goes beyond superficial report details.
   - Critical Indicator: Watch for reticence or vague responses regarding test scheduling following significant changes or details of the tester's qualifications — these are red flags.
   - Practical Focus: Verify automated logs (e.g., test scheduling controls, change management logs) which are more resistant to manipulation than static documentation. Also, saturate the assessment with questions about how remediation was prioritized after vulnerabilities were discovered.
   - Details Matter: The granularity of the penetration test report (e.g., inclusion of exploited vulnerabilities, risk ratings, and remediation timeframes) is as crucial as the test being conducted. Lesser-detailed reports may indicate a tick-box exercise rather than a robust security evaluation.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.4.2 Internal penetration testing is performed:  
• Per the entity's defined methodology,  
• At least once every 12 months  
• After any significant infrastructure or application upgrade or change  
• By a qualified internal resource or qualified external third-party  
• Organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.4.2.a Examine the scope of work and results from the most recent internal penetration test to verify that penetration testing is performed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

11.4.2.b Interview personnel to verify that the internal penetration test was performed by a qualified internal resource or qualified external third-party and that organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Purpose

Internal penetration testing serves two purposes. Firstly, just like an external penetration test, it discovers vulnerabilities and misconfigurations that could be used by an attacker that had managed to get some degree of access to the internal network... (content truncated for brevity)

title: Define and implement a penetration testing methodology.
methodology:
PENETRATION TESTING METHODOLOGY VALIDATION

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documentation & Methodology: Look for a formal, approved penetration testing policy that explicitly outlines industry-accepted methods (e.g., OSSTMM, NIST SP 800-115, or similar standards). This document should detail the various testing dimensions (internal, external, application-layer, network-layer), scope (entire CDE perimeter, critical systems), and review of recent threat intelligence (past 12 months).
• Test Artifacts: Check penetration test reports that include detailed testing plans, scope definitions, identified vulnerabilities, exploitation steps, and risk assessment/remediation tracking. The reports should reflect both network and application testing and include evidence like screenshots, logs, and summaries of segmentation validation.
• Operational Patterns: Evidence of periodic testing schedules and a clear tracking system for remedial actions (retention of test results for at least 12 months) show that the methodology is actively implemented and continuously improved.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Documentation: Organizations may have a polished methodology document that looks compliant on paper but may not be followed in practice. A superficial document without evidence of executed tests in reports or remediation tracking often signals mere box-checking.
• Incomplete Scoping: Some implementations might only test a subset of systems or only run vulnerability scans rather than full exploitation, missing comprehensive coverage. Check for absence of tests validating segmentation or lack of testing from both internal and external perspectives.
• Lack of Historical Context: An absence of efforts to incorporate threat intelligence or review vulnerabilities from the last 12 months is a red flag.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify the existence of a documented and approved penetration testing methodology.
WHY: A formal, documented methodology that covers both internal and external testing, industry-accepted approaches, and scope specifics is critical evidence of compliance.
HOW: Interview key personnel (e.g., security team leads), and review the policy document to ensure it mentions application-layer and network-layer testing as well as criteria for evaluating segmentation.
FAIL: If the policy is generic, outdated, or does not specify testing from multiple network perspectives and layers, then it is insufficient.

CHECK: Confirm that penetration test reports include comprehensive scope and methodologies with evidence from both internal and external testing.
WHY: Detailed reports proving that tests encompass the entire CDE perimeter and include assessments for segmentation and vulnerabilities (such as injection flaws, misconfigurations) demonstrate operational implementation.
HOW: Randomly select several recent penetration test reports. Validate that they include details on both network and application assessments, as well as the methods used, risk assessments, and remediation results.
FAIL: Missing report details that differentiate internal/external testing or evidence that tests only covered high-level scans without active exploitation indicate non-compliance.

CHECK: Validate that the testing approach includes an annual review of vulnerabilities and threat intelligence from the past 12 months.
WHY: Incorporating up-to-date threat intelligence ensures that the penetration testing covers risks most relevant to the organization, a key aspect of thorough risk management.
HOW: Ask for records or summary reports showing how recent vulnerabilities or threat information influenced test scope and methodologies. Cross-check these with the official methodology documentation.
FAIL: Lack of historical vulnerability context or absence of documentation tying recent threats to test planning points to a superficial implementation.

CHECK: Demonstrate that penetration testing results and remediation efforts are retained for at least 12 months.
WHY: Retention of testing and remediation evidence is important for trend analysis and audits, ensuring continuity in risk management and improvement.
HOW: Verify storage locations (secured file servers, SIEM, or dedicated compliance tool logs) for test reports and remediation records. Validate that the dates show retention covering the necessary period.
FAIL: If records are missing, purged too early, or if audit trails are incomplete, then the retention control is not effectively implemented.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcuts: Use automated tools to search for document versions and timestamps in the document management system to confirm periodic updates and correct retention periods.
• Key Indicator: A robust methodology will explicitly reference both third-party standards and risk-based adjustments to the penetration test scope. If your interviews reveal that testers are simply checking boxes without adapting to emerging threats, that is a strong indicator of poor implementation.
• Critical Details: Ensure that the evidence isn’t solely documentation – ask for live demonstration samples or walk-throughs of how recent test data was integrated into the risk management process. This reduces the risk of simply faked compliance.
• Look for evidence of remediation follow-ups from critical vulnerabilities discovered during testing. This ties the test process into the incident management cycle and verifies operational effectiveness.

This multi-step verification ensures not just the presence of a documented testing approach, but its effective, timely, and comprehensive execution in line with PCI DSS requirements.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.4.1 A penetration testing methodology is defined, documented, and implemented by the entity, and includes:
- Industry-accepted penetration testing approaches.
- Coverage for the entire CDE perimeter and critical systems.
- Testing from both inside and outside the network.
- Testing to validate any segmentation and scope-reduction controls.
- Application-layer penetration testing to identify, at a minimum, the vulnerabilities listed in Requirement 6.2.4.
- Network-layer penetration tests that encompass all components that support network functions as well as operating systems.
- Review and consideration of threats and vulnerabilities experienced in the last 12 months.
- Documented approach to assessing and addressing the risk posed by exploitable vulnerabilities and security weaknesses found during penetration testing.
- Retention of penetration testing results and remediation activities results for at least 12 months.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.4.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that the penetration-testing methodology defined, documented, and implemented by the entity includes all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Attackers spend a lot of time finding external and internal vulnerabilities to leverage to obtain access to cardholder data and then to exfiltrate that data. As such, entities need to test their networks thoroughly, just as an attacker would do. This testing allows the entity to identify and remediate weakness that might be leveraged to compromise the entity's network and data, and then to take appropriate actions to protect the network and system components from such attacks.

Good Practice

Penetration testing techniques will differ based on an organization’s needs and structure and should be suitable for the tested environment—for example, fuzzing, injection, and forgery tests might be appropriate. The type, depth, and complexity of the testing will depend on the specific environment and the needs of the organization.

Definitions

Penetration tests simulate a real-world attack situation intending to identify how far an attacker could penetrate an environment, given differing amounts of information provided to the tester. This allows an entity to better understand its potential exposure and develop a strategy to defend against attacks. A penetration test differs from a vulnerability scan, as a penetration test is an active process that usually includes exploiting identified vulnerabilities.

title: External vulnerability scans are performed after significant changes.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes change control records paired with external scan reports that indicate scans were executed immediately after significant changes. Scan reports should detail vulnerabilities with CVSS scores, especially those 4.0 and above, and show subsequent remediation/rescans where required.
• Technical artifacts such as timestamped scan logs, change request approvals, and remediation verification records are key. Additionally, documentation verifying the qualifications and organizational independence of the personnel performing the scans (or third-party scan contracts) is a strong indicator.
• Operationally, recurring patterns such as routine post-change scanning events and systematic documentation linking these scans with change management events are signs that the control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations sometimes create or maintain a policy and minimal documentation that merely lists intended scanning events without the corresponding detailed evidence (e.g., only a checklist or tick-box entries in change control systems).
• Some may use automated tools that are not properly independent or qualified, allowing internal teams to generate favorable scan reports without truly verifying the external view.
• Surface-level implementations include scans run on a fixed schedule or on a non-validated list of assets, rather than being dynamically triggered by significant changes.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Cross-reference Change Control Records with Scan Report Dates
WHY: This ensures that scans are conducted immediately following any documented significant change.
HOW: Obtain change control logs and identify significant change events; then, access corresponding external scan reports and verify that their timestamps are post-change. Confirm that the scan report includes evidence of vulnerability scores (notably 4.0 or higher) and remediation actions.
FAIL: If scan report dates do not align with or immediately follow documented changes, or if changes lack associated scan reports.

CHECK: Verify Vulnerability Remediation and Re-scan Evidence
WHY: Ensures that vulnerabilities with a CVSS score of 4.0 or above are not only detected but have been acted upon and validated with follow-up scans.
HOW: Review external scan reports for vulnerability details and validate remediation actions by checking for subsequent re-scan reports confirming the resolution. Interview responsible personnel if needed.
FAIL: If vulnerabilities remain unresolved, or if there’s no documented re-scan following remediation efforts.

CHECK: Assess Qualification and Independence of Scan Providers
WHY: Qualified and independent assessments are critical to unbiased vulnerability detection.
HOW: Validate personnel qualifications (or third-party certification/contract) and check documentation that clearly establishes organizational independence (e.g., separate reporting lines, external testing contracts). If performed internally, verify through interviews and job role descriptions.
FAIL: If there is evidence suggesting the scanning personnel are overly integrated with other IT duties without clear separation or if scan provider qualifications cannot be confirmed.

CHECK: Evaluate Consistency of Scan Scheduling with the Change Process
WHY: A process flaw might allow scans to be scheduled arbitrarily rather than as a response to a significant change.
HOW: Compare the organization’s change management calendar to the dates of executed scans. Identify any inconsistencies or delays that would compromise the intended security posture.
FAIL: If scans are not regularly prompted by or conducted immediately following significant change events, or if there is a mismatch between scheduled changes and actual scan activity.

EXPERT TIPS:
• A verification shortcut is to quickly request a consolidated list of significant changes over the past few months along with associated scan reports. Consistency of these records is a strong indicator of effective implementation.
• Critical details include timestamp correlations, the presence of detailed CVSS scoring, and evidence of re-scans after remediation; superficial document checklists are often a red flag.
• Pay close attention to the independence and qualification of the individuals performing the scans – if the same group responsible for change implementation is also conducting scans without proper separation, this is a potential gap.
• If automated tools are used, verify their configuration settings to ensure they are set to trigger post-change scans and properly report vulnerabilities with all required details.

By following these concrete steps, you can validate that external vulnerability scanning after significant changes isn’t just a documented process but an effective, operational security control.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.3.2.1 External vulnerability scans are performed after any significant change as follows:
- Vulnerabilities that are scored 4.0 or higher by the CVSS are resolved.
- Rescans are conducted as needed.
- Scans are performed by qualified personnel and organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.3.2.1.a Examine change control documentation and external scan reports to verify that system components were scanned after any significant changes.

11.3.2.1.b Interview personnel and examine external scan and rescan reports to verify that external scans were performed after significant changes and that vulnerabilities scored 4.0 or higher by the CVSS were resolved.

11.3.2.1.c Interview personnel to verify that external scans are performed by a qualified internal resource(s) or qualified external third party and that organizational independence of the tester exists.

Customized Approach Objective

The security posture of all system components is verified following significant changes to the network or systems, by using tools designed to detect vulnerabilities operating from outside the network. Detected vulnerabilities are assessed and rectified based on a formal risk assessment framework.

Guidance

Purpose

Scanning an environment after any significant changes ensures that changes were completed appropriately such that the security of the environment was not compromised because of the change.

Good Practice

Entities should include the need to perform scans after significant changes as part of the change process and before the change is considered complete. All system components affected by the change will need to be scanned.

title: External vulnerability scans are performed regularly by a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • The most recent ASV scan reports from the last 12 months are available and show that scans occurred at least once every three months.
   • Each scan report clearly identifies the scanning vendor as a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV).
   • Scan reports include remediation details and evidence of follow-up rescans demonstrating that identified vulnerabilities were fixed according to the ASV Program Guide requirements.
   • Operational logs or dashboards may show recurring scheduling of these external scans.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes present outdated or incomplete ASV reports to simulate regular scanning frequency while actual scans aren’t performed.
   • Some may use in-house scanning tools or non-approved vendors labeled as ASV, thus faking compliance without meeting the control’s specific requirements.
   • A superficial review of documentation and policies without verifying operational evidence (e.g., actual scan results and vendor information) can mask non-compliance.
   • Rescan reports may not be consistently linked to initial failing scans, indicating that vulnerability remediation may be incomplete.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify scan frequency over the last 12 months
   WHY: Demonstrates that external vulnerability scans are being performed at least once every three months, meeting PCI DSS requirements.
   HOW: Obtain and review ASV scan reports covering the last 12 months; check report dates to confirm scans occur with at least quarterly frequency.
   FAIL: If there are gaps exceeding three months or missing reports within the 12-month period.

   CHECK: Confirm the scans are conducted by a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor
   WHY: Only approved vendors fulfill the stringent testing requirements of the PCI SSC ASV program.
   HOW: Inspect each scan report for the vendor’s credentials and cross-reference the vendor name with the list available on the PCI SSC website.
   FAIL: If reports are missing vendor details or if the vendor is not on the approved list.

   CHECK: Validate that passing scan criteria per the ASV Program Guide are met
   WHY: A passing scan indicates that vulnerabilities identified were resolved to meet ASV standards, ensuring proper remediation.
   HOW: Review the scan reports and rescan reports for remediation details; verify that each report concludes with a passing result based on ASV program guidelines.
   FAIL: If any report has critical vulnerabilities unresolved or if a rescan does not show a passing outcome.

   CHECK: Assess evidence of vulnerability remediation processes and follow-up actions
   WHY: Demonstrates proactive vulnerability management and confirms that issues identified in scans are remediated in a timely manner.
   HOW: Examine rescan documentation and remediation logs; interview IT personnel to understand the process if required, ensuring that reported vulnerabilities followed a proper fix and confirmation through a rescan.
   FAIL: If there is absence of associated rescan documentation or if follow-up actions are missing.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated tools to extract and review metadata (dates, vendor identifiers) from ASV scan reports to quickly validate scanning frequency and vendor compliance.
   • Critical Indicator: Absence of any passing scan or a single failure among the quarterly reports is often a red flag for overall compliance issues.
   • Detail Orientation: Ensure that each report clearly documents the remediation process – missing or vague remediation details indicate poor operational follow-through.
   • Insight: Corroborate ASV scan reports with internal change management and patch management logs; discrepancies between these sources may reveal areas where the scans are not adequately integrated with overall vulnerability management practices.

This methodology emphasizes operational evidence rather than simply relying on policies or documentation, thus ensuring that controls are effective in practice and not merely paper compliant.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.3.2 External vulnerability scans are performed as follows:
- At least once every three months.
- By a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV).

- Vulnerabilities are resolved and ASV Program Guide requirements for a passing scan are met.
- Rescans are performed as needed to confirm that vulnerabilities are resolved per the ASV Program Guide requirements for a passing scan.

Customized Approach Objective

This requirement is not eligible for the customized approach.

Applicability Notes
