For the initial PCI DSS assessment against this requirement, it is not required that four passing scans be completed within 12 months if the assessor verifies: 1) the most recent scan result was a passing scan, 2) the entity has documented policies and procedures requiring scanning at least once every three months, and 3) vulnerabilities noted in the scan results have been corrected as shown in a re-scan(s).

(continued on next page)

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.3.2.a Examine ASV scan reports from the last 12 months to verify that external vulnerability scans occurred at least once every three months in the most recent 12-month period.

11.3.2.b Examine the ASV scan report from each scan and rescan run in the last 12 months to verify that vulnerabilities are resolved and the ASV Program Guide requirements for a passing scan are met.

11.3.2.c Examine the ASV scan reports to verify that the scans were completed by a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV).

Purpose

Attackers routinely look for unpatched or vulnerable externally facing servers, which can be leveraged to launch a directed attack. Organizations must ensure these externally facing devices are regularly scanned for weaknesses and that vulnerabilities are patched or remediated to protect the entity.
Because external networks are at greater risk of compromise, external vulnerability scanning must be performed at least once every three months by a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV).

Good Practice

While scans are required at least once every three months, more frequent scans are recommended depending on the network complexity, frequency of change, and types of devices, software, and operating systems used. Vulnerabilities identified during external vulnerability scans should be part of a vulnerability management process that includes multiple vulnerability sources, as specified in Requirement 6.3.1.
Multiple scan reports can be combined to show that all systems were scanned and that all applicable vulnerabilities were resolved as part of the three-month vulnerability scan cycle. However, additional documentation may be required to verify non-remediated vulnerabilities are in the process of being resolved.

Further Information

See the ASV Program Guide on the PCI SSC website.

Additional Content from Page 276 (Doc page 272):
However, for subsequent years after the initial PCI DSS assessment, passing scans at least every three months must have occurred. ASV scanning tools can scan a vast array of network types and topologies. Any specifics about the target environment should be worked out between the ASV and scan customer. Refer to the ASV Program Guide published on the PCI SSC website for scan customer responsibilities, scan preparation, etc.

title: Internal vulnerability scans are performed after significant changes.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Change management records that document significant changes along with corresponding scheduling and outcomes of internal vulnerability scans. Look for internal scan reports dated after the change approval and implementation notifications.
   • Technical Artifacts: Internal vulnerability scan reports, rescan documentation that specifically flags high-risk and critical vulnerabilities, and system logs showing scan activities initiated post-change. Evidence that the scanning tool configuration is linked to change events can also be reviewed.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular confirmation within change control processes that scanning events occur post-change; reports showing that vulnerabilities (especially high-risk or critical) are tracked and re-scanned before change closure; and periodic internal audits or review meetings verifying the independence of the scan team.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations may simply attach generic scan reports to change tickets without verifying the scan was triggered by the actual change event or may recycle earlier scan reports after changes occur.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Having a scheduled scanning process rather than scanning triggered by the post-change event; relying solely on automated notifications without manual or independent review; and using internal personnel without demonstrating real independence from the change implementation team.
   • Frequently Missed Gaps: Lack of correlation between change control records and scan reports, absence of documented evidence that high-risk/critical issues were resolved before change closure, and insufficient attribution of scanning responsibility to qualified and independent personnel.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Compare change control logs with corresponding internal vulnerability scan reports.
     WHY: Ensures that scans are indeed performed after every significant change rather than on a fixed schedule.
     HOW: Retrieve recent change tickets, review the date/time stamps, and verify that a vulnerability scan report exists immediately (or shortly) after the change date, including evidence that high-risk/critical vulnerabilities have been addressed.
     FAIL: If scan reports are missing or the scans were performed before the change, this indicates non-compliance.

   • CHECK: Validate that rescan reports are generated when vulnerabilities detected are classified as high-risk or critical.
     WHY: Verifies that the remediation process is active, and any lingering vulnerabilities are followed-up with a re-scan.
     HOW: Locate documentation or additional scan reports attached to change tickets where initial scans detected high-risk/critical issues, and confirm that a rescan was performed after remediation.
     FAIL: Lack of rescan documentation or unchanged high-risk/critical vulnerabilities indicates failure to adhere to requirements.

   • CHECK: Confirm the independence and qualification of the personnel performing the scans.
     WHY: Ensures the integrity and impartiality of the vulnerability assessments following changes.
     HOW: Interview change management and scanning team members, review organizational charts, and examine credentials or role definitions showing that the internal scanning function operates independently from those implementing changes.
     FAIL: If the same individuals or teams responsible for implementing changes are performing the scans, or if qualifications/independence are not documented, then the control is compromised.

   • CHECK: Verify that scan reports clearly identify vulnerabilities by risk level and detail the remediation status for each high-risk/critical issue.
     WHY: Demonstrates that the scans aren’t merely procedural but are used to drive actionable remediation.
     HOW: Inspect a random sampling of internal scan reports tied to changes, noting if vulnerability risks are categorized, any remediation actions are recorded, and if follow-up scans verified resolution.
     FAIL: Reports that merely list vulnerabilities without categorization or evidence that vulnerabilities were remediated indicate inadequate implementation.

   • CHECK: Review change control procedures to confirm that scanning is integrated as part of the change closure process.
     WHY: Ensures that changes are not finalized until a post-change scan validates that the system is secure.
     HOW: Examine policy documents, change management walkthroughs, and audit trails from recent change implementations to verify that an internal scan is a mandatory step before change closure.
     FAIL: If the process allows changes to be closed without documented evidence of a post-change scan, it represents a breach of the control.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Look directly for a timeline correlation between the change approval date and the scan report date. A mismatch often signals scanning is not actually driven by the change.
   • Critical Indicator: Consistent evidence of unresolved high-risk/critical vulnerabilities in scan reports immediately following changes is a red flag.
   • Key Detail: Pay special attention to the staffing and independence of the personnel conducting the scans. Even if reports exist, the lack of true independence can undermine the control’s effectiveness.
   • Additional Tip: Use security orchestration tools (if available) that tie vulnerability scans to change management events to automate verification.
   • Final Note: A series of properly timestamped, correlated documentation is much harder to fake than standalone policy statements or generic periodic scan reports.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.3.1.3 Internal vulnerability scans are performed after any significant change as follows:
* Vulnerabilities that are either high-risk or critical (according to the entity’s vulnerability risk rankings defined at Requirement 6.3.1) are resolved.
* Rescans are conducted as needed.
* Scans are performed by qualified personnel and organizational independence of the tester exists (not required to be a QSA or ASV).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.3.1.3.a Examine change control documentation and internal scan reports to verify that system components were scanned after any significant changes.

11.3.1.3.b Interview personnel and examine internal scan and rescan reports to verify that internal scans were performed after significant changes and that all high-risk vulnerabilities and all critical vulnerabilities were resolved.

11.3.1.3.c Interview personnel to verify that internal scans are performed by a qualified internal resource(s) or qualified external third party and that organizational independence of the tester exists.

Guidance

Purpose

Scanning an environment after any significant changes ensures that changes were completed appropriately such that the security of the environment was not compromised because of the change.

Good Practice

Entities should perform scans after significant changes as part of the change process per Requirement 6.5.2 and before considering the change complete. All system components affected by the change will need to be scanned.

Customized Approach Objective

The security posture of all system components is verified following significant changes to the network or systems, by using automated tools designed to detect vulnerabilities operating inside the network. Detected vulnerabilities are assessed and rectified based on a formal risk assessment framework.

Applicability Notes

Authenticated internal vulnerability scanning per Requirement 11.3.1.2 is not required for scans performed after significant changes.

title: Internal vulnerability scans are performed via authenticated scanning.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for the control 'Internal vulnerability scans are performed via authenticated scanning'.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes configured vulnerability scanning tools explicitly set to use authenticated scanning profiles.
   • Technical artifacts include screenshots or configuration files from the scanning tools showing the use of credentials and sufficient privileges; scan reports that detail system vulnerability data only discoverable via internal access.
   • Operational patterns include periodic authenticated scans for systems that support credentials and documented exceptions for systems that don’t, with corresponding account management controls (per req 8.2.2) for scanning accounts having interactive logon capabilities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might only configure the scanner for unauthenticated scanning on most systems and only run authenticated scans occasionally (or vice versa) then claim overall compliance.
   • Surface-level implementations include using a common, low-privilege account for scanning, which might not reveal deeper vulnerabilities, or relying solely on configuration documentation without evidencing the scan results from authenticated checks.
   • Technical gaps often missed are detailed accounts of systems that cannot accept credentials and mismanagement of scanning accounts that could allow interactive logins without proper control.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Configuration Verification
     WHY: Ensures the scanner is explicitly set up to use credentials for systems that support it.
     HOW: Inspect the vulnerability scan tool configuration files or dashboard settings to verify that authenticated scanning is enabled and that accounts with sufficient privileges are used; confirm that credentials are not generic low-privilege ones.
     FAIL: Lack of any setting for credential-based scanning or usage of basic unauthenticated scanning profiles.

   • CHECK: Scan Report Analysis
     WHY: Confirms that authenticated scanning was actually executed and produced vulnerability data that would be unseen with unauthenticated scanning.
     HOW: Review detailed scan reports and logs. Identify entries that only appear when the scanning tool has proper credentials such as deeper OS-level vulnerabilities, hidden configurations, or locally installed software vulnerabilities.
     FAIL: Scan reports that display only generic vulnerability data or seem identical to what an unauthenticated scan would produce.

   • CHECK: Account Management Review
     WHY: Ensures that if scanning accounts can be used for interactive login, they are managed per the tighter controls described in Requirement 8.2.2, reducing risk of exploitation.
     HOW: Examine account configurations, password policies, login restrictions, and audit logs for accounts used in the scanning process. Interview system administrators to verify proper operational procedures.
     FAIL: If scanning accounts are found with interactive login capabilities and lack additional security restrictions, or if audit records/controls do not meet requirement 8.2.2.

   • CHECK: Documentation and Exception Process
     WHY: Proves that all systems unable to accept credentials for authenticated scanning are clearly identified and managed as exceptions.
     HOW: Review documented procedures and inventories that list systems excluded from authenticated scanning, ensuring that each one is justified and approved.
     FAIL: Presence of systems in the scanning scope without documented exceptions or unjustified omissions in the inventory.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Ask for a live demo of the scan tool showing real-time switch between authenticated and unauthenticated modes. Then, correlate these with deeper vulnerability findings.
   • Critical Indicator: Look for scanning logs that record detailed authentication sessions including timestamps, account identities, and the commands executed – these are harder to fake.
   • Detail Focus: Verify that the scanner’s configuration includes separate profiles for different system types (servers, network appliances, etc.) and that each profile uses credentials with sufficient privileges as evidenced by the depth of the vulnerability data retrieved.
   • Remember: A one-off configuration screen shot isn’t enough; corroborate configuration with actual scan outcomes and operational logs.

This methodology emphasizes automated/observable evidence (e.g., configuration files, scan reports, logs) and focuses on the operational effectiveness of the authenticated scanning processes rather than only relying on policy documentation.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.3.1.2 Internal vulnerability scans are performed via authenticated scanning as follows:
- Systems that are unable to accept credentials for authenticated scanning are documented.
- Sufficient privileges are used for those systems that accept credentials for scanning.
- If accounts used for authenticated scanning can be used for interactive login, they are managed in accordance with Requirement 8.2.2.

Customized Approach Objective

Automated tools used to detect vulnerabilities can detect vulnerabilities local to each system, which are not visible remotely.

Applicability Notes

The authenticated scanning tools can be either host-based or network-based. "Sufficient" privileges are those needed to access system resources such that a thorough scan can be conducted that detects known vulnerabilities. This requirement does not apply to system components that cannot accept credentials for scanning. Examples include some network and security appliances, mainframes, and containers.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.3.1.2.a Examine scan tool configurations to verify that authenticated scanning is used for internal scans, with sufficient privileges, for those systems that accept credentials for scanning.

11.3.1.2.b Examine scan report results and interview personnel to verify that authenticated scans are performed.

11.3.1.2.c If accounts used for authenticated scanning can be used for interactive login, examine the accounts and interview personnel to verify the accounts are managed following all elements specified in Requirement 8.2.2.

11.3.1.2.d Examine documentation to verify that systems that are unable to accept credentials for authenticated scanning are defined.

title: Other applicable vulnerabilities are managed based on targeted risk analysis.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documented risk analysis reports that detail how lower‑risk vulnerabilities are classified and prioritized, including asset inventories, threat and impact evaluations as noted in Requirement 12.3.1.
• Automated scan reports and logs showing periodic scans as well as rescans after identified vulnerabilities have been mitigated.
• Evidence in change management or remediation tracking systems that link vulnerability remediation actions to the targeted risk analysis outcomes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may present generic or outdated risk analysis documentation that does not correlate with current scan outputs or incident response records.
• A paper process where risk analysis is performed once and not revisited, while vulnerabilities continue to appear in periodic scans.
• Superficial rescanning where only a subset of vulnerabilities are rechecked, or the timing of rescans is not aligned with the assessed risk levels.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review the documented targeted risk analysis reports for lower-ranked vulnerabilities.
   WHY: These reports should incorporate asset identification, threat likelihood, and impact, as per Requirement 12.3.1, and drive the remediation schedule.
   HOW: Interview the risk management team, obtain recent risk analysis documentation, and verify that the report explicitly outlines remediation timelines tied to risk levels.
   FAIL: Absence of explicit risk-based remediation timelines or outdated/incomplete analysis that does not include key elements such as identification of assets and threat likelihood.

2. CHECK: Correlate automated scan results with the risk analysis outcomes.
   WHY: Demonstrates that vulnerabilities, even those not high-risk, are being tracked and prioritized as defined by the targeted risk analysis.
   HOW: Extract recent internal scan reports, identify lower-risk vulnerabilities, and verify if their remediation timelines correspond with risk assessments. Check for evidence of rescans confirming vulnerability remediation.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between the documented risk analysis and actual scan outputs, or lack of follow-up rescans for vulnerabilities flagged by the analysis.

3. CHECK: Validate the operational process that ensures continuous review and update of vulnerability data.
   WHY: Ensures the process isn’t static, and lower-risk vulnerabilities are periodically re-assessed and rescanned, reducing potential exposure.
   HOW: Inspect operational logs, change management records, and the scan schedule. Interview the responsible personnel and observe automated scan workflows to confirm the timely execution of rescans.
   FAIL: Evidence of a one-time risk analysis without routine review, potential delays in rescans despite vulnerability remediation action plans, or missing operational logs confirming periodic risk reassessment.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Request the most recent incident or remediation log where a lower-ranked vulnerability was reassessed. The log should clearly timestamp risk analysis, remediation effort, and subsequent rescans confirming the fix.
• Critical Indicator: A well-documented linkage between the automated vulnerability scanner output and the risk assessment tracking system – this digital trail is hard to fake and proves operational effectiveness.
• Detail That Matters: Ensure the risk analysis covers complete asset inventories and threat evaluations; missing these details often indicate a superficial process that could leave exploitable gaps.
• Practical Check: If possible, observe the system triggering automated rescans post-remediation to confirm operational automation and timeliness as required.

This methodology focuses on verifying that lower-risk vulnerabilities are actively managed based on ongoing targeted risk analysis rather than being ignored, relying on both documented evidence and active operational controls.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.3.1.1 All other applicable vulnerabilities (those not ranked as high-risk vulnerabilities or critical vulnerabilities according to the entity’s vulnerability risk rankings defined at Requirement 6.3.1) are managed as follows:
• Addressed based on the risk defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
• Rescans are conducted as needed.

Customized Approach Objective

Lower ranked vulnerabilities (lower than high-risk or critical) are addressed at a frequency in accordance with the entity’s risk.

Applicability Notes

The timeframe for addressing lower-risk vulnerabilities is subject to the results of a risk analysis per Requirement 12.3.1 that includes (minimally) identification of assets being protected, threats, and likelihood and/or impact of a threat being realized.
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.3.1.1.a Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis that defines the risk for addressing all other applicable vulnerabilities to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified at Requirement 12.3.1.

11.3.1.1.b Interview responsible personnel and examine internal scan report results or other documentation to verify that all other applicable vulnerabilities are addressed based on the risk defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, and that the scan process includes rescans as needed to confirm the vulnerabilities have been addressed.

Purpose

All vulnerabilities, regardless of criticality, provide a potential avenue of attack and must therefore be addressed periodically, with the vulnerabilities that expose the most risk addressed more quickly to limit the potential window of attack.

title: Internal vulnerability scans are performed regularly as defined by Requirement 11.
methodology:
1. CHECK: Verify internal scan schedule and frequency
   WHY: Regular scanning at least once every three months ensures timely detection of vulnerabilities that might otherwise be exploited.
   HOW: Review a sample of internal vulnerability scan reports over the last 12 months. Confirm dates indicate scans occur at least every 90 days. Use automated tools or report management systems to extract and chart scan dates. Check tool logs if available.
   FAIL: Gaps exceeding 90 days between scan reports, or missing documentation for an expected scan period, indicate non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Validate remediation and rescan effectiveness
   WHY: Ensuring that high-risk and critical vulnerabilities are confirmed resolved prevents potential exploitation of unpatched vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Select a few scan reports and corresponding rescan results. Verify that vulnerabilities flagged as high or critical are not present in the rescan outputs. Cross-reference with remediation tickets or patch management logs to ensure resolution steps were executed.
   FAIL: Presence of unresolved high-risk or critical vulnerabilities, or absence of corresponding rescan reports confirming remediation, indicates control breakdown.

3. CHECK: Confirm the vulnerability scan tool's currency and configuration
   WHY: Up-to-date scan tools are critical to detect the latest vulnerabilities in systems and devices.
   HOW: Review configuration settings and update history within the scanning tool. Validate through tool records and change logs that vulnerability signatures are regularly updated. Interview personnel responsible for tool maintenance.
   FAIL: Outdated vulnerability definitions or misconfigured settings, as well as a lack of documented update practices, compromise the scan’s effectiveness.

4. CHECK: Assess tester qualifications and independence
   WHY: Use of qualified and independent testers ensures unbiased scan results and adherence to best practices in identifying vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Interview responsible personnel and review training records, certifications, or external third-party engagement contracts. Confirm organizational separation between the tester and IT operations managing the scanned environments.
   FAIL: Evidence that scans are performed by non-qualified personnel or testers with conflicts of interest (e.g., using internal personnel with close ties to the operational areas) undermines the integrity of the scan process.

Expert Tips:
- Use automated querying of scan logs to quickly detect any discrepancies in scan frequency.
- Focus on finding mismatches between remediation tickets and actual scan outcomes; this is a common area for superficial compliance.
- Be alert to signs that management may rely solely on policy documentation or self-generated checklists without evidence from tool logs or actual scan outputs; these are red flags for poor operational compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.3.1 Internal vulnerability scans are performed as follows:
- At least once every three months.
- Vulnerabilities that are either high-risk or critical (according to the entity’s vulnerability risk rankings defined at Requirement 6.3.1) are resolved.
- Rescans are performed that confirm all high-risk and all critical vulnerabilities (as noted above) have been resolved.
- Scan tool is kept up to date with latest vulnerability information.
- Scans are performed by qualified personnel and organizational independence of the tester exists.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.3.1.a Examine internal scan report results from the last 12 months to verify that internal scans occurred at least once every three months in the most recent 12-month period.

11.3.1.b Examine internal scan report results from each scan and rescan run in the last 12 months to verify that all high-risk vulnerabilities and all critical vulnerabilities (defined in PCI DSS Requirement 6.3.1) are resolved.

11.3.1.c Examine scan tool configurations and interview personnel to verify that the scan tool is kept up to date with the latest vulnerability information.

11.3.1.d Interview responsible personnel to verify that the scan was performed by a qualified internal resource(s) or qualified external third party and that organizational independence of the tester exists.

Additional Guidance (from page 271):
A vulnerability scan is a combination of automated tools, techniques, and/or methods run against external and internal devices and servers, designed to expose potential vulnerabilities in applications, operating systems, and network devices that could be found and exploited by malicious individuals.

title: An inventory of authorized wireless access points is maintained with documented business justification.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Documented inventory spreadsheets or databases listing each authorized wireless access point along with a clearly recorded business justification for its existence. This documentation should be timestamped and maintained as a living record.
• Network device configurations (e.g., wireless controllers) that correspond to the documented inventory and include authorized SSIDs or MAC addresses.
• Evidence from wireless scanning tools showing regular comparisons between detected devices and the approved inventory list, ideally with audit logs or change records indicating review cycles.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may present a static document that lists some devices without evidence of continuous review or updates, making it easy to overlook recently added unauthorized devices.
• Relying solely on policy documentation or checklists without correlating the actual network scanning results and configuration backup files.
• Falsifying a list that merely contains known device names or SSIDs without linking to underlying network infrastructure, allowing unauthorized devices to be missed.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Inventory Documentation Review
   WHY: Ensures that the organization maintains an up-to-date, comprehensive record of authorized wireless access points with relevant business justifications.
   HOW: Obtain and inspect the inventory records (spreadsheets, databases, or configuration management systems) and verify that each entry includes a valid business justification, a unique identifier (e.g., MAC address, serial number), and recent update timestamps.
   FAIL: If the inventory is outdated, incomplete, or lacks proper business justification for each entry.

2. CHECK: Network Configuration and Wireless Controller Verification
   WHY: Validates that documented wireless access points correspond to actual network configurations.
   HOW: Access configurations from wireless controllers or management platforms; cross-reference the authorized SSIDs/MAC addresses against the inventory list. Confirm that each item in the inventory is actively configured with matching details.
   FAIL: If there is a discrepancy between the documented inventory and device configuration, or if unauthorized configurations are found.

3. CHECK: Wireless Scanning and Correlation Analysis
   WHY: Confirms that operational monitoring effectively distinguishes authorized from unauthorized wireless access points.
   HOW: Review logs and reports from periodic wireless scans to ensure recognized devices are flagged correctly. Check for evidence that any device not on the documented list was investigated, and review incident logs tied to unauthorized device detection.
   FAIL: If wireless scans detect access points not present on the inventory list without proper justification or if there is an absence of review logs.

4. CHECK: Update and Review Process Validation
   WHY: Ensures that the inventory is maintained as a living document that’s regularly updated based on network changes or periodic reviews.
   HOW: Interview relevant personnel and evaluate change management and incident response records to confirm the existence of a defined process for updating the inventory. Validate that changes in network architecture (new devices decommissioned or added) trigger documented reviews.
   FAIL: If the process is ad hoc, if interviews reveal inconsistent review practices, or if evidence of routine updates is lacking.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcut: Use automated network scanning tools to generate current wireless device lists and immediately compare against the documented inventory. If available, integration with a Configuration Management Database (CMDB) provides additional assurance.
• Critical Indicator: An outdated inventory record or missing timestamps are strong red flags for potential lapses in operational security.
• Practical Focus: Pay special attention to environments with multiple wireless deployments, such as multi-tenant or adjacent operations, because the risk of confusion between authorized and rogue devices is higher.
• Detail Matter: Business justification descriptions must articulate clear and compelling reasons for each device’s presence – generic statements or policies that merely state 'approved' are insufficient.

This comprehensive approach ensures that both the documentation and system configurations are aligned, confirming that the inventory of authorized wireless access points is not only complete but also actively maintained and accurate.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.2.2 An inventory of authorized wireless access points is maintained, including a documented business justification.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.2.2 Examine documentation to verify that an inventory of authorized wireless access points is maintained, and a business justification is documented for all authorized wireless access points.

Customized Approach Objective

Unauthorized wireless access points are not mistaken for authorized wireless access points.

Guidance

Purpose

An inventory of authorized wireless access points can help administrators quickly respond when unauthorized wireless access points are detected. This helps to proactively minimize the exposure of CDE to malicious individuals.

Good Practice

If using a wireless scanner, it is equally important to have a defined list of known access points which, while not attached to the company’s network, will usually be detected during a scan. These non-company devices are often found in multi-tenant buildings or businesses located near one another. However, it is important to verify that these devices are not connected to the entity’s network port or through another network-connected device and given an SSID resembling a nearby business. Scan results should note such devices and how it was determined that these devices could be “ignored.” In addition, detection of any unauthorized wireless access points that are determined to be a threat to the CDE should be managed following the entity’s incident response plan per Requirement 12.10.1.

title: Wireless access points are managed to detect and address unauthorized devices.
methodology:
Below is the detailed validation methodology for PCI DSS req-11.2.1 (Wireless access points are managed to detect and address unauthorized devices):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Regular wireless scan reports, logs from wireless intrusion detection systems (WIDS) or monitoring software that list both authorized and potentially unauthorized devices, as well as alert notifications.
• Technical Artifacts: Configuration files for automated monitoring tools verifying alert settings, documented wireless assessment reports with timestamps confirming quarterly or more frequent scans, and audit logs showing wireless scan activities.
• Operational Patterns: Regular schedule of wireless assessments documented through operational logs, active response records (incident logs) when unauthorized devices are detected, and documented communications (emails/alerts) from monitoring systems to IT personnel.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations may produce outdated or one-off wireless assessment reports while their operational environment is not routinely scanned. They might simply archive reports as evidence without verifying active monitoring or fast notification procedures.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Installing a tool that only scans infrequently or only for limited ranges instead of a full coverage scan can be a superficial fix. Relying solely on policy documents or training sessions without automated monitoring and historical logs are common misleading steps.
• Technical Gaps: Not integrating WIDS with active alerting mechanisms (i.e., email or dashboard notifications) or missing periodic reconfiguration of the tool can mean the control is not fully operational.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Wireless Assessment Documentation
WHY: Up-to-date wireless scans (conducted at least quarterly) prove that both authorized and unauthorized devices are actively detected.
HOW: Review wireless assessment reports, verifying timestamps and scan coverage. Confirm that reports list discovered devices and any incidents of unauthorized access points.
FAIL: Absence of recent reports, outdated scan dates, or incomplete logs that do not cover the entire environment indicate non-compliance.

CHECK: Automated Monitoring and Alert Configuration
WHY: Proper configuration of automated tools ensures that the detection of unauthorized devices triggers real-time notifications to responsible personnel.
HOW: Inspect the configuration settings of your WIDS/monitoring solution. Validate that alerts are enabled, check sample alert logs, and verify that notifications have been sent to the relevant security personnel.
FAIL: Missing or misconfigured alerts, lack of notification logs, or settings that do not cover all wireless bands and access point types indicate a gap in monitoring.

CHECK: Operational Verification via Personnel Interviews
WHY: Interviews can confirm that processes are understood and actively implemented by IT personnel, ensuring responses are triggered as required.
HOW: Interview IT/security staff about the wireless scanning process, frequency, and their role when alerts are received. Ask for live demonstrations or recount recent incidents where unauthorized devices were detected.
FAIL: Personnel are unaware of procedures, provide inconsistent answers, or cannot produce evidence of having acted on alerts.

CHECK: Review of Incident and Remediation Logs
WHY: Evidence of remediation actions, including the removal or investigation of unauthorized devices, demonstrates operational effectiveness in addressing alerts.
HOW: Examine incident management logs and corrective action reports detailing discovery and response times. Verify resolution steps and follow-up documentation.
FAIL: No incident records for unauthorized device alerts, delays in response, or absence of remediation documentation signals that detection is not effectively managed.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Instead of solely relying on historical documents, run a live or ad hoc wireless scan to see if your tool detects known test devices or configured SSIDs properly.
• Critical Indicator: Look for integration between monitoring software and alerting platforms. A lack of correlation in event logs (e.g., scan report vs. alert log) is a red flag.
• Detail Matter: Ensure scans cover all areas (indoor & outdoor) and all wireless frequencies. Narrow scan coverage can miss rogue devices.
• Additional Check: Confirm that tool settings are not only in place but are also actively monitored by comparing automated alert logs against manual checks or independent scans.

This methodology emphasizes not just the existence of policy but the actual operational implementation and verification of wireless access point management, helping ensure that unauthorized devices cannot remain undetected in the environment.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.2.1 Authorized and unauthorized wireless access points are managed as follows:
• The presence of wireless (Wi-Fi) access points is tested for,
• All authorized and unauthorized wireless access points are detected and identified,
• Testing, detection, and identification occurs at least once every three months.
• If automated monitoring is used, personnel are notified via generated alerts.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.2.1.a Examine policies and procedures to verify processes are defined for managing both authorized and unauthorized wireless access points with all elements specified in this requirement.

11.2.1.b Examine the methodology(ies) in use and the resulting documentation, and interview personnel to verify processes are defined to detect and identify both authorized and unauthorized wireless access points in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

11.2.1.c Examine wireless assessment results and interview personnel to verify that wireless assessments were conducted in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

11.2.1.d If automated monitoring is used, examine configuration settings to verify the configuration will generate alerts to notify personnel.

Purpose

Implementation and/or exploitation of wireless technology within a network are common paths for malicious users to gain unauthorized access to the network and cardholder data. Unauthorized wireless devices could be hidden within or attached to a computer or other system component. These devices could also be attached directly to a network port, to a network device such as a switch or router, or inserted as a wireless interface card inside a system component.

Even if a company has a policy prohibiting the use of wireless technologies, an unauthorized wireless device or network could be installed without the company’s knowledge, allowing an attacker to enter the network easily and “invisibly.” Detecting and removing such unauthorized access points reduces the duration and likelihood of such devices being leveraged for an attack.

Customized Approach Objective

Unauthorized wireless access points are identified and addressed periodically.

title: Roles and responsibilities for performing Requirement 11 are documented, assigned, and understood.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Look for official documentation such as responsibility assignment matrices (RACI charts), policies, or procedures that clearly delineate roles and responsibilities for Requirement 11 activities.
WHY: This confirms that roles aren’t simply assumed but are formally assigned and understood.
HOW: Request copies of the responsibility assignment matrix or related procedural documents; verify sections on Requirement 11; check if there are personnel sign-off records or training logs acknowledging role understanding.
FAIL: Absence of detailed written documentation, outdated charts, or missing personnel acknowledgment records.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply reference broad IT roles without linking them directly to Requirement 11 activities, or create documentation that is not regularly updated or disseminated.
• Sometimes, a superficial review of policies occurs without verifying that personnel actually understand their specific day-to-day responsibilities.
• Often, automated systems generate role lists, but manual assignments or insufficient periodic reviews leave gaps in actual accountability.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify Availability and Accuracy of Documentation
   WHY: Clear, current, and detailed documentation ensures everyone knows what is expected regarding Requirement 11.
   HOW: Examine current versions of internal policies, procedures, and a RACI matrix that explicitly mention activities under Requirement 11. Cross-check dates and assigned personnel to confirm currency.
   FAIL: Missing, outdated, or generic documents that do not specifically assign Requirement 11 activities.

2. CHECK: Personnel Interviews for Role Clarity
   WHY: Direct interviews reveal if individuals truly understand their assigned tasks and responsibilities for continuous security monitoring.
   HOW: Interview a sample of personnel who are documented as responsible for Requirement 11. Ask them to describe their day-to-day actions, escalation procedures, and any sign-off process used to acknowledge responsibilities.
   FAIL: Responses that do not match the documented roles or show confusion about specific activities required by Requirement 11.

3. CHECK: Evidence of Periodic Review and Acknowledgment
   WHY: Ongoing monitoring and periodic re-communications ensure that the roles and responsibilities remain active and understood over time.
   HOW: Verify that there is evidence of periodic reviews (e.g. meeting minutes, update logs, or refresher trainings) and that personnel have signed off on understanding their responsibilities.
   FAIL: No records of periodic reviews or training sessions, and lack of documented acknowledgment sign-offs.

4. CHECK: Correlation between Documentation and Operational Logs
   WHY: Cross-referencing documented responsibilities with operational logs (e.g., logs of security monitoring activities, change management records) ensures that the designated personnel are actively performing the assigned tasks.
   HOW: Validate via system logs or incident management tickets that activities aligned with Requirement 11 are executed by the designated individuals. Check for consistency between documented roles and actual instrumented activities.
   FAIL: Mismatches between documented roles and operational evidence, such as task execution by unauthorized staff or unexplained gaps in logging.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Focus interviews on detecting discrepancies between what is on paper and what is performed. If multiple staff express uncertainty about core activities, it’s a red flag.
• Look for evidence of role-specific training or targeted communications – absence of these, even with documentation in place, may indicate poor engagement.
• Be mindful of the refresh cycle: Compliance is not a one-time activity. Ensure that reviews and sign-offs are scheduled appropriately.
• Note that a well-maintained RACI matrix that is actively used during project reviews or audits is a strong indicator of effective operational control.

This methodology emphasizes automated and corroborative evidence of actual practice rather than simply relying on static documentation, ensuring that Requirement 11 activities are truly integrated into the day-to-day operational framework.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 11 are documented, assigned, and understood.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 11 are documented and assigned.
11.1.2.b Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 11 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as documented and are understood.

Customized Approach Objective

Day-to-day responsibilities for performing all the activities in Requirement 11 are allocated. Personnel are accountable for successful, continuous operation of these requirements.

Guidance

Purpose

If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, personnel may not be aware of their day-to-day responsibilities and critical activities may not occur.

Good Practice

Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents. As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities.

Examples

A method to document roles and responsibilities is a responsibility assignment matrix that includes who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed (also called a RACI matrix).

title: All security policies and operational procedures in Requirement 11 are documented, maintained, and in use.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Written policies and procedures manuals that include Requirement 11-related artifacts which are visibly version-controlled and dated.
• Change logs, revision histories, and distribution records embedded within the documentation that prove the documents are maintained over time.
• Evidence through system or policy management dashboards that the documents have been disseminated to and acknowledged by the impacted parties (e.g., a receipt confirmation in an internal portal).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may provide static documents that have never been updated or demonstrate only superficial changes in response to external requirements, rather than substantive changes as processes or technologies evolve.
• Relying solely on policy existence in documentation without ensuring active operational use. For instance, a flyer on the intranet that lists the policy without any actual training or integrated enforcement.
• Using out-of-date or archived versions of documents as ‘evidence’ without showing active maintenance practices or signing off by responsible management.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify documentation version control and update logs
   WHY: Timestamped and version-controlled documents prove that policies and procedures are maintained and updated in alignment with changes.
   HOW: Automatically extract document metadata from the policy management system; check for revision dates and compare against known organizational changes or audit logs.
   FAIL: Presence of documents without revision histories or with timestamps that predate recent process/technology updates.

2. CHECK: Confirm active circulation and employee acknowledgment
   WHY: Evidence of employees having received and acknowledged these policies indicates they are in use and known to all affected parties.
   HOW: Query internal systems for training records, email acknowledgments, or system notifications confirming that employees have read and signed off on the policies. Interview a sample of staff for confirmation.
   FAIL: Lack of automated acknowledgment logs or inconsistent receipt records.

3. CHECK: Review integration with operational procedures and systems
   WHY: Operational systems (ticketing systems, continuous monitoring tools) should reference or enforce these policies, demonstrating their active use in daily operations.
   HOW: Audit sample operational tasks to verify that procedures outlined in the documents are implemented (e.g., security scans, incident response) and cross-reference with policy documentation.
   FAIL: A disconnect between documented procedures and actual operations, such as procedures not followed or missing operational triggers.

4. CHECK: Inspect governance and oversight evidence
   WHY: Formal review cycles and management oversight processes demonstrate that the policies are not only documented but also actively maintained and governed.
   HOW: Review minutes from governance meetings or audit logs from the policy review committee and verify corrective actions taken in response to internal reviews.
   FAIL: Absence of formal review cycles or inconsistencies in management records.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query the document management system for the latest update dates instead of manually scanning document headers.
• Critical Indicator: If employee acknowledgment records are stored in an ad-hoc manner (e.g., paper acknowledgments) or not integrated into a central system, the control might be superficial.
• Detail: Focus on revision logs and real-time updates after organizational changes, as these are key to proving ongoing operational use beyond mere existence of documentation.
• Tip: Interview personnel from different levels (management, IT operations, end users) to gauge the actual use of these procedures rather than relying solely on documented policies.

By following the above checks and validation steps, you can validate the genuine implementation of the control as per Requirement 11.1.1, ensuring not only that the policies are documented but that they are actively maintained and embedded in operations.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

11.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 11 are:
• Documented.
• Kept up to date.
• In use.
• Known to all affected parties.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

11.1.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Requirement 11.1.1 is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout Requirement 11. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in Requirement 11, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated.

Good Practice

It is important to update policies and procedures as needed to address changes in processes, technologies, and business objectives. For this reason, consider updating these documents as soon as possible after a change occurs and not only on a periodic cycle.

Definitions

Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

title: Failures of any critical security control systems are responded to promptly
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes active logging and incident management dashboards that show real-time alerts for critical control failures. Administrators can review incident tickets and remediation records that capture the start and end times of security failures, along with cause analysis and remediation details.
• Technical artifacts include system alerts from monitoring tools, logs demonstrating the notification and escalation of control failures, and entries in a problem management system showing documented responses.
• Operational patterns include consistent, timely responses to alerts, documented root-cause analysis for each failure, and subsequent actions to prevent recurrence.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply archive log files or incident records without showing actual remediation efforts. A history of alerts might be generated automatically without any effective follow-up.
• Some implementations involve superficial or incomplete incident records with dates but missing details about remediation or root cause analysis. They may also fudge durations, merely indicating that an incident was recorded without evidence of actual investigation.
• Automated alert tools might be active, but manual follow-ups and proper escalation procedures needed to completely address failures could be lacking.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Examine incident management records/tickets for critical control failures.
WHY: These records should show identification of cause(s), documented duration, and remediation steps, proving that failures are being managed promptly.
HOW: Review a sample of incident tickets from your monitoring and logging systems, verifying timestamps, cause documentation, and confirmation that remediation actions were taken. Look for alignment with policy requirements in both automated log alerts and manual verification processes.
FAIL: If the tickets are missing start/end times, cause analysis, or remediation details, then the control is not effectively implemented.

CHECK: Verify that monitoring tools are integrated with alert systems and that alerts are promptly escalated and visible on incident dashboards.
WHY: This demonstrates the operational mechanism required for prompt response to failures.
HOW: Access the monitoring tool dashboards, check alert logs for recent critical alerts, and confirm that these alerts resulted in incident tickets. Interview personnel if necessary to trace the alert to the response process.
FAIL: If alerts are generated without corresponding incident tickets or if escalation procedures are unclear, this indicates a breakdown in prompt response.

CHECK: Review a sample of post-mortem analysis reports for critical security control failures.
WHY: Post-mortem analysis reports should confirm that the duration, causes, and remediation actions were properly documented, indicating an effective follow-up process.
HOW: Randomly select several incident responses from the last 6-12 months and verify that each includes detailed analysis, time markers, remediation steps, and any preventive measures implemented.
FAIL: If post-mortem reports are incomplete or absent, it suggests a gap in the effective response to failures.

CHECK: Interview key personnel responsible for incident management to validate their understanding and role in responding to control failures.
WHY: Direct interviews offer practical insights into whether the documented procedures are followed in reality.
HOW: Conduct discussions with IT security managers or incident response teams; ask for real examples of a recent failure and how the process was executed.
FAIL: Interviews revealing vague or inconsistent responses, or lack of clarity on roles, are a red flag for non-operational procedures.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on the linkage between automated alerts and manual incident ticket creation. If you can’t trace an alert to a resolution process, it signals a gap in control.
• Critical details: Precise timestamps and documented remediation actions are key. Look for consistency across these data points rather than isolated evidence.
• Beware of relying solely on documentation; ensure that evidence of effective operational responses (like live monitoring dashboards, integrated alert-to-ticket workflows, and detailed post-mortem reviews) is visible.
• Check if preventive measures to avoid recurrence (such as configuration changes or security patches) are documented; missing these details can indicate only a superficial implementation.

These checks — when combined — validate that failures of critical security control systems are not only being detected but are also fully responded to with prompt, well-documented remediation processes.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

10.7.3 Failures of any critical security control systems are responded to promptly, including but not limited to:
- Restoring security functions.
- Identifying and documenting the duration (date and time from start to end) of the security failure.
- Identifying and documenting the cause(s) of failure and documenting required remediation.
- Identifying and addressing any security issues that arose during the failure.
- Determining whether further actions are required as a result of the security failure.
- Implementing controls to prevent the cause of failure from reoccurring.
- Resuming monitoring of security controls.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.7.3.a Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that processes are defined and implemented to respond to a failure of any critical security control system and include at least all elements specified in this requirement.

10.7.3.b Examine records to verify that failures of critical security control systems are documented to include:
- Identification of cause(s) of the failure.
- Duration (date and time start and end) of the security failure.
- Details of the remediation required to address the root cause.

Guidance

Purpose

If alerts from failures of critical security control systems are not responded to quickly and effectively, attackers may use this time to insert malicious software, gain control of a system, or steal data from the entity’s environment.

Good Practice

Documented evidence (for example, records within a problem management system) should provide support that processes and procedures are in place to respond to security failures. In addition, personnel should be aware of their responsibilities in the event of a failure. Actions and responses to the failure should be captured in the documented evidence.

Applicability Notes (from following page)

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider until 31 March 2025, after which this requirement will apply to all entities. This is a current v3.2.1 requirement that applies to service providers only. However, this requirement is a best practice for all other entities until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Failures of critical security control systems are detected, alerted, and addressed promptly
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Active dashboards displaying real-time alerts from network security controls (firewalls, IDS/IPS), anti‐malware, access control systems, and change-detection monitors. Operators actively using incident response tools and alert management consoles.
• Technical Artifacts: System logs with automated alert entries, incident tickets with time stamps showing prompt response, configuration files for monitoring tools with defined thresholds for failures, and change logs that reveal detection of deviations.
• Operational Patterns: Regular testing exercises or simulated events that trigger alerts, clear follow-up actions (remediation tickets logged), and personnel interviews confirming rapid response to alerts.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Surface-level Compliance: Organizations might merely rely on static log reports or outdated documentation while failing to integrate automated alerting into their operations. Some may schedule periodic reviews instead of continuous monitoring.
• Incomplete Implementations: Setting up monitoring tools without ensuring they trigger real-time alerts, or having a documented process that is not executed in practice. Manual logs or intermittent checks can falsely pass as continuous monitoring.
• Technical Gaps: Failure to correlate alerts with incident response systems, lack of timestamp integration between detection and remediation events, and bypassing automated escalation steps by relying on human intervention alone.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify automated alert generation from critical security control systems
WHY: Automated alerts confirm that detection mechanisms are actively monitoring system failures and can generate real-time notifications.
HOW: Review system and event logs from network security controls, IDS/IPS, anti-malware, and audit logging mechanisms; look for entries where failures triggered alerts; observe alert dashboard in real time; and cross-check with incident management records.
FAIL: Missing or delayed alert entries, inconsistencies in log timestamps, or reliance solely on manual intervention without automated alerts.

CHECK: Check correlation between triggered alerts and remediation ticket creation
WHY: A close correlation proves that alerts are not just generated, but are also acted upon promptly.
HOW: Audit the incident response system to compare timestamps from alert logs with remediation or ticketing system entries. Interview responsible personnel about their workflow when alerts occur.
FAIL: A significant delay or complete absence of remediation tickets, suggesting that alerts are not being properly acted on.

CHECK: Interview operational staff and perform a simulation test
WHY: Direct observation and testing validate the operational effectiveness of the alerting and remediation process.
HOW: Conduct interviews with security operations personnel to confirm they receive and act on automated alerts. Simulate a known failure of a critical system (e.g., disable a network sensor briefly) and verify that an alert is triggered and addressed promptly.
FAIL: Personnel unaware of procedures, inability to reproduce an alert during simulation, or evidence that simulated failures are not addressed.

CHECK: Validate configuration settings in monitoring tools
WHY: Properly configured thresholds and alert mechanisms ensure that system failures are detected as per defined processes.
HOW: Review configuration files and settings in the monitoring tools (e.g., thresholds for failed health checks, rule integrity monitors) to confirm that they match documented policies for all critical security control systems.
FAIL: Configuration discrepancies, indicators that thresholds are not set sensibly, or audit logs showing that critical failure types are not captured.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on cross-referencing alert logs with incident response systems. A lack of timestamp continuity is a strong indicator of poor real-world implementation.
• Critical Indicator: Frequent recurrence of specific alerts without corresponding remediation actions signals that the control is not functioning as intended.
• Detail to Note: Ensure that the simulated tests cover a range of critical controls (e.g., both network and physical access), as focusing on only one area might hide gaps in others.
• Reminder: Rely on operational evidence (e.g., real-time monitoring dashboards, incident response logs) rather than solely documentation to validate that controls are active and effective.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

10.7.2 Failures of critical security control systems are detected, alerted, and addressed promptly, including but not limited to failure of the following critical security control systems:
- Network security controls.
- IDS/IPS.
- Change-detection mechanisms.
- Anti-malware solutions.
- Physical access controls.
- Logical access controls.
- Audit logging mechanisms.
- Segmentation controls (if used).
- Audit log review mechanisms.
- Automated security testing tools (if used).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.7.2.a Examine documentation to verify that processes are defined for the prompt detection and addressing of failures of critical security control systems, including but not limited to failure of all elements specified in this requirement.

10.7.2.b Observe detection and alerting processes and interview personnel to verify that failures of critical security control systems are detected and reported, and that failure of a critical security control results in the generation of an alert.

Guidance

Purpose

Without formal processes to detect and alert when critical security controls fail, failures may go undetected for extended periods and provide attackers ample time to compromise system components and steal account data from the CDE.

Good Practice

The specific types of failures may vary, depending on the function of the device system component and technology in use. However, typical failures include a system no longer performing its security function or not functioning in its intended manner—for example, a firewall erasing its rules or going offline.

Customized Approach Objective

Failures in critical security control systems are promptly identified and addressed.

Applicability Notes

This requirement applies to all entities, including service providers, and will supersede Requirement 10.7.1 as of 31 March 2025. It includes two additional critical security control systems not in Requirement 10.1.1.

title: Failures of critical security control systems are detected, reported, and responded to promptly (service providers only)
methodology:
For PCI DSS req-10.7.1, which mandates that failures of critical security control systems are detected, reported, and responded to promptly, focus your validation on real-time operational effectiveness rather than just having documented policies. Below is a methodology structured with practical signs of implementation, common evasions, concrete validation steps, and expert tips.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Active and continuously monitored dashboards or SIEM consoles that show alerts triggered by failures from systems such as network security controls, IDS/IPS, FIM, anti-malware, physical/logical access, audit logs, and segmentation solutions. Look for up-to-date incident tracking and remediation logs. 
• Technical Artifacts: Automated alert emails or SMS messages, ticketing records, anomaly logs in SIEM reports, configuration screenshots of alert rules covering various failure scenarios, and time-stamped records of alerts followed by corrective actions.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent logging of failures with immediate alerts sent to the appropriate security teams, regular testing (e.g., simulated failure drills) and documented response processes indicating that alerts lead to prompt remediation.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might present policies and documented procedures without real, verifiable real-time monitoring. They may provide test logs that are artificially generated or manually edited post-event.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Implementation of alerting systems that are not integrated into the critical control infrastructure, or relying on periodic, manual checks instead of automated monitoring.
• Missed Technical Gaps: Lack of correlation across different systems (e.g., an IDS/IPS alert not feeding into the central dashboard), delayed alert propagation or unattached escalation processes, which are reported only in post-mortem documentation rather than via live incidents.

3. VALIDATION STEPS (Concrete Checks)

CHECK: Verify Monitoring and Alert Configuration
WHY: Active monitoring ensures that when a failure (e.g., network firewall rules deletion or a ceasing of IDS operation) occurs, an alert is promptly generated.
HOW: Interview SOC staff and review SIEM/system configuration screenshots to confirm that alert rules are specifically defined for each critical security system listed. Cross-check if the registered events are actively pushed to alert channels.
FAIL: If the configurations are outdated, missing, or if the SIEM system does not list the critical control systems, it suggests a failure.

CHECK: Observe Real-Time Alerting in Action
WHY: Live detection and alerting are the core of this control, proving the operational integration of security controls and monitoring.
HOW: Request to observe a simulated failure (or review recent, timestamped incident records) that triggered alerts. Verify that the alert was promptly queued in the incident response system and that notifications were sent to appropriate personnel.
FAIL: No real-time alerts being generated or significant delay in ticket or incident creation suggests inefficacy.

CHECK: Review Historical Incident Logs and Response Times
WHY: Historical evidence shows that when failures occur, they are reported and addressed quickly.
HOW: Analyze incident logs from the SIEM or ticketing system. Look for consistent records where failures from critical systems are detected, reported, and remediated. Check timestamps from detection to response and ensure that they meet the prompt response criteria.
FAIL: If incident logs are sparse, show long delays, or missing correlation between failure detection and response, then the control is not effectively implemented.

CHECK: Validate End-to-End Communication of Alerts
WHY: A robust control system not only detects but also effectively communicates an alert between systems and personnel.
HOW: Review the integration between monitoring systems and the incident management/ticketing system. Check if the alert details (e.g., type of failure, system affected) are clear, actionable, and automatically communicated. Interview staff to confirm receipt of such alerts during drills or live detection.
FAIL: Disjointed or manual ‘forwarding’ of alert information rather than automated integration, or absence of key incident detail in the alert, indicates a gap.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: If you have access to SIEM dashboards, directly verify if alerts for control system failures trigger pre-configured workflows. Run a controlled test if possible.
• Key Indicator: A short response time between alert generation and ticket creation is essential. Excessive delays suggest procedural or technical flaws.
• Detail to Focus On: Ensure that the alert configuration covers all critical security components (not just a subset), and check for consistent integration with escalation protocols. Look for missing coverage in physical or logical access controls as these are often overlooked.

By following these detailed steps, you can practically verify that failures in critical security control systems are detected, reported, and promptly addressed, ensuring compliance with req-10.7.1 for service providers.
content:
Requirements and Testing Procedures

10.7 Failures of critical security control systems are detected, reported, and responded to promptly.

Defined Approach Requirements

10.7.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: Failures of critical security control systems are detected, alerted, and addressed promptly, including but not limited to failure of the following critical security control systems:
- Network security controls.
- IDS/IPS.
- FIM.
- Anti-malware solutions.
- Physical access controls.
- Logical access controls.
- Audit logging mechanisms.
- Segmentation controls (if used).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.7.1.a Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Examine documentation to verify that processes are defined for the prompt detection and addressing of failures of critical security control systems, including but not limited to failure of all elements specified in this requirement.

10.7.1.b Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: Observe detection and alerting processes and interview personnel to verify that failures of critical security control systems are detected and reported, and that failure of a critical security control results in the generation of an alert.

Purpose

Without formal processes to detect and alert when critical security controls fail, failures may go undetected for extended periods and provide attackers ample time to compromise system components and steal account data from the CDE.

Good Practice

The specific types of failures may vary, depending on the function of the device system component and technology in use. Typical failures include a system ceasing to perform its security function or not functioning in its intended manner, such as a firewall erasing all its rules or going offline.

Customized Approach Objective

Failures in critical security control systems are promptly identified and addressed.

Applicability Notes

This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider. This requirement will be superseded by Requirement 10.7.2 as of 31 March 2025.

title: Time synchronization settings and data are protected
methodology:
Control ID: req-10.6.3 - Time synchronization settings and data are protected

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence exists in the form of system configuration files (e.g., /etc/ntp.conf on Unix/Linux or Windows Time service settings) that enforce restricted permissions exclusively for personnel with a business need.
   • Audit logs or SIEM dashboards show recorded attempts to change time settings, indicating that all modifications are logged, monitored, and reviewed.
   • Operational evidence such as defined change management tickets for time configuration changes and documented periodic reviews of time sync logs proves the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes rely on outdated documentation or policy statements instead of enforcing actual operating system-level access restrictions.
   • Superficial implementations might include broad ACLs on time configuration files or relying on logging without ensuring integrity and protection of the logs.
   • A common gap is incomplete audit logging: only recording some events or not linking logs with centralized monitoring, which might allow unauthorized changes to go unnoticed.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

   CHECK: Verify Access Controls on Time Configuration Files
   WHY: Ensuring that critical files (e.g., /etc/ntp.conf or registry settings on Windows controlling time sync) are restricted to authorized users prevents unauthorized changes.
   HOW: Review the file permissions or ACLs on these configuration files and verify that only the correct administrative or service accounts have write and execute rights. Check system settings, such as group memberships and access policies.
   FAIL: If the configuration files have permissive access (e.g., world-readable or writable) or if unauthorized users have access privileges, the control fails.

   CHECK: Confirm that Time Setting Changes are Logged
   WHY: Proper logging ensures that any attempt to change system time is recorded, offering a traceable audit trail for investigations.
   HOW: Review audit log configurations and SIEM dashboards for entries relating to time synchronization changes. Request sample logs showing events when time settings were altered; verify event dates, user identification, and correlation with change management records.
   FAIL: Absence of log entries for time setting changes or evidence that logs can be tampered with indicates non-compliance.

   CHECK: Test Change Authorization for Time Settings
   WHY: Actively verifying enforcement by attempting unauthorized modifications can quickly demonstrate whether only authorized personnel can change time settings.
   HOW: With proper permission and in a controlled environment, simulate an unauthorized attempt (or review test records of previous attempts) to change time settings. Confirm that the system prevents the change and logs the attempt.
   FAIL: If the attempt is successful or not logged, this is a strong indicator that the protection mechanism is ineffective.

   CHECK: Evaluate Review and Monitoring Processes
   WHY: Regular monitoring ensures that any anomalies are detected promptly, and periodic reviews confirm that effective processes are in place.
   HOW: Interview administrators to review defined processes and observe evidence (e.g., change management tickets, audit review logs) that changes to time settings are periodically audited, monitored, and any anomalies are investigated.
   FAIL: Lack of documented review cycles or missing evidence of proactive monitoring and follow-up actions indicates poor operational control.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Focus on verifying actual operating system-level settings rather than relying solely on policy documents. The file system permissions and log integrity are the strongest indicators of implementation.
   • Correlate log events from local system logs with centralized SIEM reports to ensure that time change events aren’t being lost or manipulated.
   • Check for any service accounts that might have override permissions; ensuring that these accounts are strictly controlled is crucial.
   • Validate that your audit logs themselves are protected (read-only or tamper-proof) to avoid attackers covering their tracks.
   • Emphasize testing by simulating unauthorized changes if possible (in a controlled change window) to directly observe the prevention and logging mechanisms in place.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

10.6.3 Time synchronization settings and data are protected as follows:
- Access to time data is restricted to only personnel with a business need.
- Any changes to time settings on critical systems are logged, monitored, and reviewed.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.6.3.a Examine system configurations and time-synchronization settings to verify that access to time data is restricted to only personnel with a business need.

10.6.3.b Examine system configurations and time settings and logs and observe processes to verify that any changes to time settings on critical systems are logged, monitored, and reviewed.

Customized Approach Objective

System time settings cannot be modified by unauthorized personnel.

Guidance

Purpose

Attackers will try to change time configurations to hide their activity. Therefore, restricting the ability to change or modify time synchronization configurations or the system time to administrators will lessen the probability of an attacker successfully changing time configurations.

title: Systems are configured to the correct and consistent time
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible configuration files (e.g., /etc/ntp.conf, /etc/chrony.conf) that reference designated time servers, approved external sources, and peering configurations among internal time servers.
