• Automated logs and system audit records showing regular, successful time updates and synchronization events with the central time server(s).
• Network device configurations (e.g., firewalls, routers) with ACLs allowing NTP traffic only from specified IP ranges to internal time servers.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply declare the use of a time server in policy but fail to enforce it in system configurations or network ACLs.
• Faked logs: scripted or manually altered log files indicating accurate time synchronization while real-time drift occurs in practice.
• Incomplete configuration: systems running outdated NTP/chrony versions that accept time updates from non-approved sources, or misconfigured peering among designated servers.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Inspect system configuration files (e.g., /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf) for references to the designated central time server(s) and approved external sources.
   WHY: Demonstrates that the system is set up to use specific time servers and follows the approved chain of time acquisition.
   HOW: Remotely review or request file copies and validate that only designated time servers and allowed external sources (e.g., references to pool.ntp.org replaced by approved IP addresses) are configured.
   FAIL: Presence of arbitrary or default pool servers; configuration entries allowing updates from unsanctioned sources.

2. CHECK: Examine audit logs or system logs for regular time synchronization events and error messages. 
   WHY: Operational logs provide evidence that the systems are actively synchronizing with the designated time server(s) and maintain accurate time.
   HOW: Use command-line tools (e.g., grep 'ntp' or 'chrony' on syslog files) to verify routine synchronization events and check for any discrepancies or errors indicating unsanctioned time sources.
   FAIL: Lack of synchronization events, or irregular time update intervals and errors that signal misconfiguration.

3. CHECK: Verify network ACLs or firewall rules controlling NTP traffic to ensure only designated servers can receive and distribute time updates.
   WHY: Limits exposure to potential tampering by ensuring internal time servers do not accept time updates from unauthorized external sources.
   HOW: Inspect firewall rule sets or network monitoring logs to confirm that NTP traffic is restricted to and from specified IP addresses.
   FAIL: Overly permissive ACLs/firewall rules permitting updates from all sources.

4. CHECK: Confirm that designated time servers are configured to peer with each other (if multiple servers exist) to cross-validate time sources.
   WHY: Peering between time servers is essential to maintain consistency and accuracy across the network.
   HOW: Check configuration files and server status panels for peer relationships and review synchronization status reports.
   FAIL: Absence of peer configuration or evidence of isolated operation of time servers.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Always treat live configuration and log data as superior to static policies or documentation; look for real-time evidence of accurate synchronization rather than just declared configurations.
• Check for version updates on time synchronization software—older versions may not support required security measures such as restrictions on external sources.
• Be cautious of systems that show signs of forced synchronization during audits while normal operation might rely on less restrictive configurations.
• In complex environments with multiple time servers, slight discrepancies between servers’ time might indicate misconfiguration in peering settings. Focus on consistency and verify that all systems adhere strictly to the designated configuration.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

10.6.2 Systems are configured to the correct and consistent time as follows:
• One or more designated time servers are in use.
• Only the designated central time server(s) receives time from external sources.
• Time received from external sources is based on International Atomic Time or Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).
• The designated time server(s) accept time updates only from specific industry-accepted external sources.
• Where there is more than one designated time server, the time servers peer with one another to keep accurate time.
• Internal systems receive time information only from designated central time server(s).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.6.2 Examine system configuration settings for acquiring, distributing, and storing the correct time to verify the settings are configured in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Using reputable time servers is a critical component of the time synchronization process. Accepting time updates from specific, industry-accepted external sources helps prevent a malicious individual from changing time settings on systems.

Good Practice

Another option to prevent unauthorized use of internal time servers is to encrypt updates with a symmetric key and create access control lists that specify the IP addresses of client machines that will be provided with the time updates.

Customized Approach Objective

The time on all systems is accurate and consistent.

title: System clocks and time are synchronized using time-synchronization technology
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Verify network diagrams or system configuration screenshots that show NTP or alternative time-synchronization technology configured on servers, network equipment, and endpoints.
   • Technical Artifacts: Look for configuration files (e.g., /etc/ntp.conf for Linux, Windows Time Service settings), logs showing regular time-sync updates; check registry settings (for Windows) or crontab entries (for scheduled NTP updates) in Unix-like systems.
   • Operational Patterns: Periodic, automated time-sync events visible in system logs, audit trails for time service patching, and version lists indicating current NTP software (ensuring that vulnerability patches have been applied).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance by having documentation only: Organizations might document time synchronization policies without actual automated configuration or monitoring in place.
   • Surface-level Implementation: Outdated or misconfigured NTP settings that do not synchronize with reliable time sources; systems manually adjusting times can lead to drift and render forensic comparison ineffective.
   • Technical Gaps: Failure to monitor and alert on time drift or sync failures; isolated systems not connected to central time servers showing configuration gaps or unsynchronized clocks.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify NTP/Time-Sync Configuration Files
   WHY: Confirms proper configuration and active use of time-synchronization technology across systems.
   HOW: Review configuration files such as /etc/ntp.conf on Linux hosts or Windows Time Service settings; verify that reference servers are reliable and in use. Check for scheduled services or crontab entries ensuring periodic synchronization.
   FAIL: Missing or misconfigured NTP configuration files, or pointing to non-standard or local time servers with no external verification.

   CHECK: Inspect System and Application Logs for Time-sync Events
   WHY: Demonstrates operational synchronization through log evidence showing scheduled time updates.
   HOW: Analyze system event logs (e.g., /var/log/syslog for Linux, Event Viewer for Windows) to locate periodic entries for time synchronization activities or errors from NTP service.
   FAIL: Absence of periodic log entries related to time sync, or frequent errors indicating time-sync service failures.

   CHECK: Validate Patch and Vulnerability Status of Time Sync Software
   WHY: Ensures that the time synchronization technology is current and maintained, reducing risk from vulnerabilities related to outdated components.
   HOW: Use automated vulnerability scanners or manual inspection of installed software versions against known vulnerabilities. Look for patch records in configuration management databases (CMDB) or patch management systems indicating regular updates.
   FAIL: Discovery of outdated time-sync software versions, missing patches, or unresolved vulnerabilities affecting the NTP or time synchronization software.

   CHECK: Perform a Cross-System Clock Comparison
   WHY: A direct validation that common time is successfully established across systems, a key control objective.
   HOW: Run a script (e.g. using ‘date’ command in Linux or W32tm in Windows) simultaneously on multiple systems and compare reported times. Use a centralized monitoring tool if available to create a snapshot of current times across servers.
   FAIL: Significant deviation in system times (beyond expected variance, typically a few seconds) indicating a failure of synchronization.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Quickly check a few random high-value systems using automated commands (like ‘date’ or equivalent in PowerShell) to verify consistency. If anomalies are found, deeper investigation is warranted.
   • Critical Indicator: Frequent time-sync errors in logs are a red flag. They might indicate network issues, misconfiguration, or vulnerabilities in the time-synchronization service.
   • Details Matter: Ensure external and reliable time sources (e.g., NIST, pool.ntp.org) are used and configure alerts for time drift. Documentation alone is not sufficient; observable, real-time evidence of synchronization is key.
   • Automation: Whenever possible, leverage centralized monitoring tools to continuously track and compare time stamps across systems, reducing manual overhead during audits.

This methodological approach confirms that time-synchronization technology is not only configured but operationally effective, reducing risk during forensic investigations and event correlation.
content:
10.6 Time-synchronization mechanisms support consistent time settings across all systems.

Defined Approach Requirements

10.6.1 System clocks and time are synchronized using time-synchronization technology.

Customized Approach Objective

Common time is established across all systems.

Applicability Notes

Keeping time-synchronization technology current includes managing vulnerabilities and patching the technology according to PCI DSS Requirements 6.3.1 and 6.3.3.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.6.1 Examine system configuration settings to verify that time-synchronization technology is implemented and kept current.

Purpose

Time synchronization technology is used to synchronize clocks on multiple systems. When clocks are not properly synchronized, it can be difficult, if not impossible, to compare log files from different systems and establish an exact sequence of events, which is crucial for forensic analysis following a breach. For post-incident forensics teams, the accuracy and consistency of time across all systems and the time of each activity are critical in determining how the systems were compromised.

Examples

Network Time Protocol (NTP) is one example of time-synchronization technology.

title: Retain audit log history for at least 12 months with three months immediately available
methodology:
Below is a structured methodology to practically validate the implementation of req-10.5.1, which mandates retaining audit log history for at least 12 months with the most recent three months immediately accessible.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Screenshots/configuration files from the log management/SIEM system showing retention parameters clearly set to 12 months with recent logs immediately queryable. Policy documents and operational runbooks that specify the retention and archival processes.
   - Technical Artifacts: System configurations (e.g., SIEM, centralized logging server settings), archival job logs, actual audit logs stored with time stamps covering the full retention period, and log backup schedules.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular review and extraction of logs (via queries or scheduled reports), documented incident response exercises retrieving logs spanning more than three months, and alerting tests that verify the system’s ability to query historical data promptly.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations may provide formal policy documents that state a 12-month retention policy while only maintaining immediate online access for the most recent 3 months without ensuring older logs are actually retrievable (e.g., logs are archived offline without established restoration procedures).
   - Some may run periodic log truncation or manual deletion on older logs due to storage constraints, making the 12-month retention claim only superficial.
   - Relying solely on backup system indicators without verifying the retrieval speed or operational readiness for incident response is another common gap.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Confirm the Audit Log Retention Policy and Configuration Settings
   WHY: Reviewing both policy documents and system configuration provides dual evidence: written commitment and technical implementation.
   HOW: 
     - Obtain and review the official log retention policy documents.
     - Log into the SIEM/log management console to inspect configuration settings, ensuring a 12-month retention period is configured and that logs from the last three months are stored online for immediate access.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between the documented policy and actual system settings (e.g., settings indicate a shorter retention period or archives without immediate restoration options).

   CHECK: Query Audit Logs to Verify Presence of Historical Data
   WHY: Directly querying logs demonstrates that historical data is actually being preserved rather than just documented.
   HOW:
     - Execute automated queries or use search features in the logging system to retrieve logs dated from 12 months ago up to the present.
     - Verify timestamps and completeness of collected logs.
   FAIL: Absence of logs from earlier periods (e.g., older than 3 months show gaps or are missing entirely), indicating failure to retain full 12 months of history.

   CHECK: Validate the Operational Availability of the Most Recent Three Months of Logs
   WHY: Immediate availability of recent logs is critical for timely incident response and compliance with the requirement.
   HOW:
     - Perform a live test where an event is logged and then quickly retrieved from the SIEM or log management system.
     - Interview operational staff and review incident response logs or drill documentation showing timely access to recent logs.
   FAIL: Noticeable delays in query response times or evidence of logs not being queryable in real time for the recent three-month period.

   CHECK: Assess and Test the Archival and Restoration Process for Older Logs
   WHY: A robust process must exist to restore older logs quickly for incident analysis even if these logs are not online.
   HOW:
     - Review documented procedures for log archiving and restoration.
     - Request and review evidence (e.g., test run or recent incident) where logs older than three months were restored and accessed within acceptable timeframes.
   FAIL: Lack of test evidence, undocumented processes, or delays in retrieval processes indicate a weak implementation of historical log retention.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut Verification: Focus on automated query tools in the SIEM that display retention settings and historical data timelines; these are much harder to fake than static documents.
   - Critical Indicator: Ensure that backup storage methods are integrated with a restoration process that’s been tested. Documentation alone is insufficient if test restorations are not performed regularly.
   - Detail to Note: The timestamp integrity in audit logs is paramount; any manipulation or gaps might suggest compliance issues or even tampering.
   - Look for evidence of trending or anomaly detection that relies on long-term log historical data—it indicates a mature log analysis strategy which complements retention requirements.

Following these practical steps will confirm that the organization not only has a policy on record but is effectively operationalizing a 12-month archival period with immediate access to recent logs.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

10.5 Audit log history is retained and available for analysis.

Defined Approach Requirements

10.5.1 Retain audit log history for at least 12 months, with at least the most recent three months immediately available for analysis.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

10.5.1.a Examine documentation to verify that the following is defined:
- Audit log retention policies.
- Procedures for retaining audit log history for at least 12 months, with at least the most recent three months immediately available online.

10.5.1.b Examine configurations of audit log history, interview personnel and examine audit logs to verify that audit logs history is retained for at least 12 months.

10.5.1.c Interview personnel and observe processes to verify that at least the most recent three months' audit log history is immediately available for analysis.

Customized Approach Objective

Historical records of activity are available immediately to support incident response and are retained for at least 12 months.

Purpose

Retaining historical audit logs for at least 12 months is necessary because compromises often go unnoticed for significant lengths of time. Having centrally stored log history allows investigators to better determine the length of time a potential breach was occurring, and the possible system(s) impacted. By having three months of logs immediately available, an entity can quickly identify and minimize impact of a data breach.

Examples

Methods that allow logs to be immediately available include storing logs online, archiving logs, or restoring logs quickly from backups.

title: Exceptions and anomalies identified during log reviews are addressed.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes documented records and task tracking tools (e.g., JIRA, ServiceNow) showing that exceptions and anomalies flagged during log reviews are actively tracked, prioritized, and escalated.
• Technical artifacts like automated alert logs, remediation tickets, and change records prove the anomalies have been addressed.
• Operational patterns such as repeated follow-ups, periodic review reports, and evidence of management sign-off on remediation actions indicate the control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may maintain a “paper trail” of policies and checklists that say exceptions are addressed without providing evidence of follow-through on actual incidents.
• Superficial automated alerts without appropriate escalation procedures might be flagged as compliance, though the anomalies are not truly managed.
• Failing to capture anomalies in a centralized incident management system or missing linkage between log alerts and remediation tasks are frequent gaps.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Verify that log review processes produce anomaly and exception records integrated with the incident management system.
WHY: Automated correlation between log alerts and remediation tickets shows that identified exceptions are not only detected but acted upon.
HOW: Inspect incident management dashboards for evidence of alerts tracked; review a sample of tickets generated post-log review and check their resolution status.
FAIL: Absence of linked incidents or resolutions that correlate with log anomalies indicates the process is ineffective.

CHECK: Interview security personnel and check meeting minutes to confirm that exceptions are reviewed, prioritized, and escalated as needed.
WHY: Direct confirmation from operational teams ensures that documented processes are put into practice.
HOW: Schedule interviews with SOC staff; ask for real-life examples of exceptions addressed; verify consistency between interview responses and documented policies.
FAIL: Inconsistent or vague descriptions of exception management by personnel or evidence that no formal escalation occurred points to a failed implementation.

CHECK: Assess evidence in ticketing systems and email communications for documented resolution of anomalies discovered during log reviews.
WHY: Artifacts from resolution communications validate that exceptions are not just recorded, but actively managed and closed.
HOW: Sample evidence from ticket logs of drawn anomalies; verify ticket closure details, remediation notes, and subsequent monitoring outcomes.
FAIL: If tickets are left open for long periods or lack decisive resolution evidence, the control is not effectively implemented.

CHECK: Review log management and SIEM tool configurations for automated escalation rules for anomalies that exceed defined thresholds.
WHY: Automated escalation rules demonstrate operational controls that trigger timely investigation and response.
HOW: Log into the SIEM system and review alert rules, thresholds, and workflow triggers; confirm that high severity anomalies are linked to notification mechanisms.
FAIL: Missing or misconfigured alert escalation rules indicate potential delays in addressing anomalies.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut verification by pinpointing a few high-severity anomalies and tracing their lifecycle through remediation ticket systems provides clear evidence of issue resolution.
• Pay special attention to anomaly resolution timelines; prolonged open tickets indicate a lack of urgency even if the process exists on paper.
• Ensure that both manual reviews and automated alert triggers show consistent handling of exceptions, as discrepancies often reveal gaps in responsibility or process ownership.
• Validate that the escalation process involves multiple layers (e.g., team lead, management) to reduce reliance on single points of failure.

In summary, real compliance goes beyond policy documents; it is evidenced by the integration of anomaly detection, incident tracking, and actual remediation actions that align with operational practices.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.4.3 Exceptions and anomalies identified during the review process are addressed.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.4.3.a Examine security policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for addressing exceptions and anomalies.
10.4.3.b Observe processes and interview personnel to verify that exceptions and anomalies are addressed.

Customized Approach Objective:
Suspicious or anomalous activities are addressed.

Guidance:
Purpose: Without investigating exceptions, unauthorized activities may go unnoticed.
Good Practice: Establish procedures to record, prioritize, report, and escalate exceptions and anomalies.

title: The frequency of periodic log reviews for other system components is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Check that a targeted risk analysis document exists, and it explicitly states the review frequency for log reviews of lower-risk system components. This document should be current and reflect how risk levels influence the review intervals.
• Look for change logs or version history on the risk analysis documentation indicating that it is periodically updated, demonstrating an ongoing review process.
• Evidence of operational practices such as scheduled log review meetings or automated reminders can signal that the defined review frequency is being actively followed.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply cite an old or generic risk analysis document without clear frequency definitions tailored to specific system components.
• Some may rely solely on policy statements without capturing actual operational follow-up. Just having a documented frequency is not enough if there’s no evidence the schedule is adhered to.
• Superficial or templated documentation that does not reflect the real risk profile of individual systems might be used to appear compliant without genuine risk-tailored review schedules.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the existence and content of the targeted risk analysis document.
WHY: It should contain explicit definitions of review frequencies for all system components, showing that the risk profile has been evaluated and that log review schedules are tailored appropriately.
HOW: Ask for the most recent targeted risk analysis document and review the sections where log review frequency is determined. Check change history for recent updates.
FAIL: The document does not specify review frequencies or appears outdated with no revision history, suggesting it’s merely a placeholder.

CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for log management and risk analysis.
WHY: They should demonstrate understanding of the defined review frequency and be able to explain its rationale in the context of risk levels of different systems.
HOW: Conduct interviews with team leads and auditors responsible for log reviews. Ask them to describe how the frequency is determined and applied to various systems.
FAIL: Personnel are unable to articulate the defined frequencies or claim that the frequency is arbitrarily chosen, indicating a disconnect between documentation and practice.

CHECK: Review evidence of operational adherence (e.g., meeting minutes, system audit logs, or automated scheduling evidence) that logs are being reviewed at the specified frequency.
WHY: This operational evidence confirms that the defined approach is not only documented but is actively implemented.
HOW: Request records showing periodic log review activities, such as meeting minutes, workflow records, or calendar integrations that match the required frequency.
FAIL: Absence of any record of log reviews, or records that show reviews occur significantly less frequently than what the risk analysis document mandates.

CHECK: Correlate the risk analysis with actual log configurations and system classifications.
WHY: Ensuring that the risk analysis influences the operational configuration of log review frequency demonstrates that the approach is risk-based and tailored to lower-risk systems, as required.
HOW: Compare the identified frequency in the risk analysis document for lower-risk components against system settings or review schedules in the log management tools.
FAIL: There is a mismatch between the frequency documented in the risk analysis and the actual review intervals configured in system tools, indicating poor implementation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Focus on the most recent revision of the risk analysis document, and verify change logs to ensure iterative improvement and reassessment of risk levels.
• Prioritize evidence of active operational practice such as meeting minutes or automatically-generated logs over static policy documents.
• Look for consistency between what is documented and what is practiced; discrepancies often reveal merely formal compliance. 
• In interviews, probe for examples of how risk analysis has changed review frequencies when new threats emerge, as this real-world responsiveness is a strong indicator of effective implementation.
• Ensure that both the document and the operational evidence align in terms of review frequency across system components; vague or generic schedules can be a sign of superficial compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.4.2.1 The frequency of periodic log reviews for all other system components (not defined in Requirement 10.4.1) is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, performed in accordance with Requirement 12.3.1.

Customized Approach Objective:
Log reviews for lower-risk system components are performed at a frequency that addresses the entity’s risk.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.4.2.1.a Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis.
10.4.2.1.b Examine documented results and interview personnel to verify the defined frequency.

title: Logs of all other system components are reviewed periodically.
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for PCI DSS req-10.4.2:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for documented records of periodic log reviews that include not just systems covered by req-10.4.1, but also all other components. Evidence might include log review tickets, scheduled review calendars, and screenshots from SIEM dashboards showing review summaries.
   • Technical Artifacts: Check archived log review reports from the SIEM or log management solution, logs of periodic review tasks and automated scripts or scheduled job logs that indicate when reviews were executed. Also examine integration logs showing that 'other system components' are feeding data into a centralized monitoring solution.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular review meetings minutes, recurring alerts raised and followed up upon in the incident/ticket system, and maintenance of comprehensive log sources from all systems consistently indicate active periodic review.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance: Organizations might simply present outdated or static log review reports, utilize logs from only a subset of systems (e.g., those already covered under req-10.4.1), or generate reports from SIEM dashboards without evidence of deeper analysis or follow-up actions on anomalies.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Some entities may schedule a review where staff only glance over summary dashboards during meetings, without deep forensic analysis or cross-system correlations. They might also record meeting minutes that look compliant but lack actual evidence of investigation into flagged events.
   • Technical Gaps: Gaps include logs from peripheral or non-standard systems not integrated into the SIEM; absent or incomplete correlation between flagged anomalies and incident response actions; and failures to capture logs outside a narrow time window.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the log management tool or SIEM is aggregating logs from all system components, including those outside the primary systems covered by 10.4.1.
   WHY: This confirms that reviews are not selectively applied but span the entire environment.
   HOW: For example, pull a configuration or system inventory from the SIEM to ensure all non-core system log sources are listed and actively sending logs. Compare with the documented list of systems.
   FAIL: If logs from significant system components are missing or not integrated, this check fails.

   CHECK: Inspect the archived log review reports and automated job logs for evidence of periodic review.
   WHY: Automated logs and timestamps demonstrate that reviews are actually executed on schedule rather than being theoretical.
   HOW: Request screenshots or log files showing periodic execution timestamps, and verify that each review covers the intended systems and time periods. Validate the consistency of these records over several cycles.
   FAIL: Absence of automated review logs or sporadic, incomplete records indicate non-compliance.

   CHECK: Interview security personnel and verify the follow-up actions in the incident/ticket system for anomalies discovered during these reviews.
   WHY: This highlights that logged events are actively examined, and anomalies lead to tangible response activities.
   HOW: Interview staff responsible for log reviews, then cross-reference a few recent incidents flagged in log reviews with corresponding tickets and remediation actions.
   FAIL: If staff cannot cite recent examples or corresponding tickets are missing, it suggests the review process is superficial.

   CHECK: Verify that the periodic review process documented in policies is actually reflected in operational dashboards and meeting records.
   WHY: Consistency between policy documentation and operational evidence shows that log reviews are part of daily operations rather than merely policy requirements.
   HOW: Examine scheduled review meeting calendars, minutes, and aggregated SIEM dashboards that include timestamps and reviewer comments.
   FAIL: If inconsistencies exist between documented procedures and daily operations (e.g., missing meeting records or SIEM dashboards showing no review activity), this indicates a failure.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Focus on automated evidence: SIEM configurations, automated job logs, and integrated dashboards are much harder to fake than static policy documents.
   • Validate cross-system correlation: In practice, it is critical to ensure that logs from non-critical systems are not overlooked. Check if anomaly tickets reference events from less visible systems.
   • Look for real-time follow-up evidence: The presence of incident tickets or remediation activities linked to log review findings is a very strong indicator of an effective process.
   • Cross-check interview responses: Ensure that multiple staff members provide consistent examples and details of periodic reviews, and ask for specifics such as the frequency and methodology used during reviews.
   • Pay attention to the completeness of the integration: A common pitfall is having extensive SIEM capabilities but failing to feed in logs from all systems, especially those considered peripheral. Verify through configuration reviews.

This multi-layered approach ensures that the review process is not only documented but is also active, effective, and integrated across the organization.
content:
Table Content from PDF page 253:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.4.2 Logs of all other system components (those not specified in Requirement 10.4.1) are reviewed periodically.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.4.2.a Examine security policies and procedures to verify that periodic review processes are defined.
10.4.2.b Examine documented results of log reviews and interview personnel to verify periodic reviews.

Customized Approach Objective:
Potentially suspicious or anomalous activities for other system components are reviewed in accordance with the entity’s risk.

title: Automated mechanisms are used to perform audit log reviews.
methodology:
Control ID: req-10.4.1.1 – Automated mechanisms are used to perform audit log reviews.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Screenshots of the audit log monitoring tool’s dashboard showing regular automatic log review cycles and alerts. Evidence of baseline configurations and thresholds for anomaly detection in the tool settings.
• Technical Artifacts: System configuration files, logs from the automated monitoring tool, alert records with timestamps indicating periodic executions. Reports generated on suspicious activity detections.
• Operational Patterns: Regular automated daily or hourly log reviews with anomalies being acted upon. Integration with incident response workflows.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations might perform manual log reviews or schedule periodic report generation that mimics automation without real-time alerting. A static report generated ad hoc can be mistaken as an automated review.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Simply turning on logging without configuring thresholds or baseline normal activity, which leads to false negatives. Tools configured without tuning to the environment’s specific log profile present gaps in coverage.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of integration between the log review tool and alert notification systems; incomplete log sources being monitored; failure to update the configuration as the environment changes.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify tool configuration settings and automated scheduling parameters.
  WHY: This confirms that the review is not performed manually and that the mechanism is correctly set up to run automatically.
  HOW: Access the configuration settings of the monitoring tool to review scheduled tasks and automatic scan intervals. Check for change logs or configuration history.
  FAIL: No scheduled tasks present or if the automation settings are disabled.

• CHECK: Review a sample of automatically generated audit log review reports.
  WHY: Actual reports are evidence that the system actively flags potential anomalies.
  HOW: Request and analyze the latest reports for consistency, timestamps, and flagged anomalies. Confirm that the tool compares against a baseline of normal activity.
  FAIL: Missing reports, reports with outdated timestamps, or absence of flagged anomalies during periods of high activity.

• CHECK: Test the anomaly detection by injecting a known anomalous log entry (in a test environment).
  WHY: Demonstrates that the mechanism is capable of flagging suspicious activity as designed.
  HOW: Introduce an event that deviates from the established baseline and observe system alerts and report logs.
  FAIL: No alert is generated or the event is not flagged for review.

• CHECK: Examine integration logs between the log review tool and incident response system.
  WHY: Ensures that flagged anomalies trigger appropriate escalation actions.
  HOW: Validate logs that show alerts being passed from the monitoring mechanism to ticketing or incident management systems.
  FAIL: No evidence of handoff, or alerts remain in the log review tool without escalation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Directly query the log monitoring system’s API or use a centralized security dashboard to quickly verify that scheduled log review tasks have been executed.
• Critical Indicators: Pay attention to automation logs – a pattern of skipped tasks or errors in execution is a red flag. Ensure baseline parameters are dynamic and reflect the evolving environment.
• Details that Matter: Verify that the audit log review mechanism covers all critical log sources and is updated along with system changes; integrations with incident response workflows are key for operational effectiveness.

This comprehensive approach ensures that not only is the automation enabled and configured, but it is also effectively identifying and escalating suspicious activity, thus meeting the criteria for req-10.4.1.1.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.4.1.1 Automated mechanisms are used to perform audit log reviews.

Customized Approach Objective:
Potentially suspicious or anomalous activities are identified via a repeatable and consistent mechanism.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.4.1.1 Examine log review mechanisms and interview personnel to verify their use.

Guidance:
Purpose: Automated tools can help in parsing large volumes of log data to identify anomalies.
Good Practice: Establish a baseline for normal activity and adjust tool settings as needed.
Further Information: Refer to the Information Supplement on Effective Daily Log Monitoring.

title: Audit logs reviewed at least once daily include security events, logs from critical systems, and logs from systems performing security functions.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Daily audit review records that are timestamped. These records should list security events, logs from CHD/SAD systems, critical systems, and security function servers.
• A dashboard or SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) platform showing daily alerts or logs review summaries across infrastructures.
• Evidence of workflow approvals or electronic signatures confirming that designated personnel completed the daily review.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply generate a log entry that claims a review was done, without demonstrating evidence that issues were investigated or resolved.
• Maintaining only a checklist or a high-level report without granular drill-down information on specific logs reviewed.
• Relying solely on system-generated notifications with no human oversight, which can be set to trigger without proper analysis.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the existence of daily audit review logs or reports within the SIEM or log management platform.
   WHY: These time-stamped records are concrete, automated evidence demonstrating daily review activity.
   HOW: Log into the SIEM/aggregation tool and extract a sample of review logs showing entries for security events, CHD/SAD systems, critical systems, and security control systems from at least three consecutive days.
   FAIL: No automated logs of activity, missing timestamps, or records that do not clearly correlate with the required systems.

2. CHECK: Interview designated security personnel and review operational checklists or sign-off forms.
   WHY: Confirming that a designated staff is responsible for the task and that reviews repeat reliably further validates the process was not just scheduled on paper.
   HOW: Conduct interviews and inspect signed forms or digital records that indicate logs were reviewed daily, note any process anomalies or deviations from the defined schedule. 
   FAIL: Personnel cannot describe a consistent review process or forms are missing or incomplete.

3. CHECK: Inspect SIEM alerting and escalation procedures related to daily log reviews.
   WHY: Effective and operational controls should include review of critical/alert events that prompt immediate action and show observed oversight of logs.
   HOW: Validate that the system triggers alerts for anomalies from CHD/SAD systems, critical systems, and security devices, and review incident tickets generated from these alerts.
   FAIL: No evidence of alert escalation processes or procedures, indicating automated alerts are not monitored daily by staff.

4. CHECK: Validate integration and correlation mechanisms within logging systems covering all designated systems (security events, CHD/SAD processing, critical systems, and security function components).
   WHY: Logging from multiple sources must be aggregated and correlated to ensure no component is omitted, verifying comprehensive sector coverage.
   HOW: Examine system configuration settings in the SIEM or log aggregator to verify data feeds exist from all required systems. Document a list of feeds and compare against the control requirements.
   FAIL: Incomplete system feed configuration or missing integration for a subset of the required systems.

EXPERT TIPS
• Always cross-reference the review logs with system configuration to ensure every required log source is covered; superficial evidence like dated reports can be faked.
• Look for evidence of incident response tied to the review process – if alerts during review periods were escalated and addressed, it’s a sign of active, effective reviews.
• Pay attention to gaps between log generation times and actual review entries; significant delays indicate a potential failure in real-time monitoring.
• Shortcuts: Ask for an IR (incident response) timeline of a past security event – effective daily reviews will have earlier detection and action timestamps.

Overall, practical verification should blend evidence of automated daily logging, manual review processes, and integrated alert response. The focus must be on observable, time-correlated data rather than just documented policies.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.4.1 The following audit logs are reviewed at least once daily:
• All security events.
• Logs of all system components that store, process, or transmit CHD and/or SAD.
• Logs of all critical system components.
• Logs of all servers and system components that perform security functions (for example, network security controls, IDS/IPS, authentication servers).

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.4.1.a Examine security policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for reviewing the specified log elements.
10.4.1.b Observe processes and interview personnel to verify that reviews occur at least once daily.

Purpose:
Regular log reviews help in early identification of potential breaches.

title: File integrity monitoring mechanisms are used on audit logs to ensure that existing log data cannot be changed without generating alerts.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Audit log directories or files are clearly identified in the file integrity monitoring (FIM) configuration. You can see settings pointing to audit logs stored on disk.
• Evidence of baseline fingerprints or checksums being recorded and stored for audit logs. This might be stored in configuration files or maintained by a FIM tool (e.g., OSSEC, Tripwire).
• Observable alerts in the SIEM or alert management system when modifications to existing log data occur, with a history of triggered alerts that reflect changes in the monitored audit logs.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may configure FIM only on the overall log directory without specifying distinct rules for modifications versus new writes—thus flagging legitimate new log entries while missing deletions/changes.
• Using default or outdated checksum algorithms that may not properly detect subtle modifications can result in false negatives.
• Relying solely on periodic, manual reviews of FIM configurations/settings rather than continuous automated monitoring, which can hide misconfigurations or non-functional alert rules.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify the FIM configuration file/settings for audit logs.
   WHY: Confirms that audit log paths are explicitly monitored with rules differentiating between new additions and modifications.
   HOW: Inspect the configuration file(s) of the FIM tool (for example, checking OSSEC’s ossec.conf or Tripwire’s policy file) to ensure that audit log directories are listed with alert rules that trigger on modifications or deletions of existing data.
   FAIL: If audit log directories are absent or rules are set only for new file creation without monitoring changes or deletions, compliance is compromised.

2. CHECK: Validate that the FIM tool has an established baseline for audit logs.
   WHY: A baseline (e.g., checksum or hash values of files) is necessary to detect any unauthorized modifications to already recorded data.
   HOW: Request evidence such as a recent snapshot report or baseline status showing hash values for current audit logs; verify this comes from an automated process rather than a one-off manual procedure.
   FAIL: If there is no record of baseline creation or the baseline doesn’t cover existing audit records, the FIM cannot reliably detect changes.

3. CHECK: Perform a controlled test modification on a test audit log file.
   WHY: Demonstrates the operational effectiveness of the FIM mechanism in producing an alert when existing data is altered.
   HOW: In a lab or controlled segment of the environment, make a recorded change (e.g., modify a byte in an audit log file that is known to be monitored) and verify that the FIM tool immediately generates an alert which is then received by the SIEM or alerting system.
   FAIL: If modifications are not triggering an alert, or if the alert is delayed/unrecorded, then the monitoring mechanism is not operational.

4. CHECK: Review alert logs for past audit log modifications.
   WHY: Historical evidence of alerts triggered by modifications to audit logs proves that the control has been active and is capable of detecting attempted bypasses.
   HOW: Query the SIEM or FIM alert database for records over a specific period that document modification alerts; verify the records include timestamp and file identifier details.
   FAIL: A lack of any alert history, or alerts that do not specifically reference modifications to audit logs, suggests the control might be missing or misconfigured.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use tools that automate and timestamp discrepancies between the baseline and current state to reduce the likelihood of human error. Shortcuts such as built-in audit log anomaly dashboards can quickly signal issues.
• Pay close attention to the differentiation in rules: the goal is to alert on changes/deletions of existing data. Misconfigured systems that simply flag all changes (including legitimate appended data) may lead to alert fatigue and might be ignored.
• Validate that FIM configurations are consistently pushed across all systems holding audit logs. A single misconfigured endpoint can become an attack vector.
• Look for evidence of regular maintenance of the baseline data, ensuring that alerts are generated only when an unauthorized change happens, not just during routine log rotation or file updates.
content:
Table Information (from PDF page 250):
10.3.4 File integrity monitoring or change-detection mechanisms is used on audit logs to ensure that existing log data cannot be changed without generating alerts.

Customized Approach Objective:
Stored activity records cannot be modified without an alert being generated.

Purpose:
File integrity monitoring systems check for unauthorized changes to audit logs and alert when modifications to existing log data occur.

Good Practice:
Software used for monitoring should be configured to alert for changes or deletions of existing log data, without flagging the addition of new data.

title: Audit log files, including those for external-facing technologies, are promptly backed up to secure internal log servers or media.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for regularly scheduled backup jobs in system and cron logs, backup configuration files pointing to secure and internal log servers, or physical logs of backup executions. 
   • Technical Artifacts: Backup logs showing timestamps and statuses, automated alert logs for backup failures, configuration files for backup tools (e.g., syslog, rsyslog, Splunk, etc.) that include secure remote targets, or evidence of secure media (e.g., encrypted storage devices) used for backups.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular, automated backup processes running in production; consistent, timestamped backup copies that match source log files; and monitoring alerts for backup completion, so that even when the log-generating system is compromised, backup copies are intact.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply set up a backup configuration without verifying that logs are actually being copied, or provide documentation and screenshots that only illustrate configuration settings without evidence of real execution.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Copying logs to a remote server that is not properly secured (e.g., accessible by many users) or having a backup process that only fires sporadically. Some environments might run ad-hoc backups manually, which can be inconsistent and not resistant to tampering.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing encryption in transit/storage, inadequate verification mechanisms (i.e., no file integrity checks), or backup servers that are accessible or not hardened against unauthorized changes.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify automated backup job schedules and logs
   WHY: Consistent, scheduled backups demonstrate that log files are being promptly and regularly backed up, ensuring traceability even in the event of compromise.
   HOW: Review crontab or scheduler configurations (e.g., Windows Task Scheduler) alongside backup logs that record successful backup operations for both internal and external-facing log sources.
   FAIL: Lack of logs showing consistent backup activity or evidence of manual intervention instead of an automated process.

   CHECK: Inspect backup configuration settings and destinations
   WHY: Confirming that backups are targeted to secure, controlled internal servers or encrypted media ensures logs are protected against tampering even if the source is compromised.
   HOW: Examine configuration files for backup tools (e.g., syslog config files, Splunk forwarders, custom scripts) to validate that backup destinations are internal and secured. Check that the destination access controls (firewalls, ACLs) are appropriately configured.
   FAIL: Backup destinations that are publicly accessible or insufficiently protected, or configuration files that point to insecure or external locations.

   CHECK: Validate file integrity and retention controls
   WHY: Ensuring that backups are unaltered and that logs have not been tampered with or lost helps maintain evidence integrity.
   HOW: Perform file integrity checks on a sample backup, review hash values or digital signatures, and confirm that backup logs include metadata (timestamps, file sizes) that correlate to the original logs.
   FAIL: Discrepancies in file sizes, timestamps or lack of cryptographic verification measures, indicating possible tampering.

   CHECK: Test restore procedures from backups
   WHY: Actual restoration confirms that backups are not just being created but are retrievable in a secure, usable form, reflecting operational readiness for incident response.
   HOW: Request evidence of restoration exercises, or if possible, conduct a restoration test of select log files from the backup media or server. Validate that restored logs accurately match the original production logs.
   FAIL: Inability to restore logs or significant differences between the restored and original files.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Look for automated alerting on backup failures and verify that the backup server’s logs indicate attempts to access, modify, or cleanse log data externally. Periodically check for anomalies in backup job execution patterns.
   • Critical Indicators of Poor Implementation: Backup configurations that are outdated, undocumented, or manually triggered instead of automated. Evidence of backups stored in the same network segment as production logs is a red flag.
   • Details That Matter Most: Focus on encryption settings, strict access control on backup destinations, and regular successful restore tests. These factors indicate that the backup process is both robust and secure.

This methodology not only examines if a policy exists but emphasizes operational effectiveness and actual, demonstrable security of audit log backups.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.3.3 Audit log files, including those for external-facing technologies, are promptly backed up to a secure, central, internal log server(s) or other media that is difficult to modify.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.3.3 Examine backup configurations or log files to verify that audit log files are promptly backed up.

Purpose:
Prompt backups ensure that even if the system generating the logs is compromised, the logs remain available and unaltered.

Good Practice:
Entities determine the best method to back up log files, whether via centralized servers or other secure media.

title: Audit log files are protected from modifications by individuals.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible Evidence: Spot configurations in system access control lists (ACLs), file permission settings, and documented separation of audit logs from general file systems. Check logs stored in hardened locations with limited write or admin privileges. 
• Technical Artifacts: File system permission audits (ls -l output on Unix/Linux systems, properties for Windows), change management records demonstrating restricted access, and configuration files detailing network or physical segmentation. 
• Operational Patterns: Regular automated monitoring of file integrity (using tools like OSSEC, Tripwire) and alerts on any unauthorized access attempts to audit logs.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may show screenshots of permission settings or documented policies without verifying that these apply uniformly in production environments. 
• Superficial Implementations: Relying solely on policy documentation or one-time configuration reviews, rather than continuous monitoring and automated alerts.
• Technical Gaps: Overlooking backup log storage locations, using inherited permissions that inadvertently allow broader access, or not segmenting legacy logs stored on less-protected systems.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that audit log files have strict file permissions defined (e.g., owner: root, no write access for others).
WHY: Proper permissions ensure that only designated administrators can modify logs, preventing tampering by unauthorized users.
HOW: Execute commands (e.g., ls -l, icacls /dump) on log file directories; cross-reference with defined configuration baselines.
FAIL: If permissions are overly permissive (e.g., 666 or if general user groups can modify logs), then the check is failed.

CHECK: Confirm that audit log storage is physically or logically segregated from other systems.
WHY: Segregation prevents direct access by individuals who have access to other parts of the system but should not be modifying logs.
HOW: Review network diagrams, storage configurations, and talk to system administrators about separation strategies. Validate if storage on dedicated log servers or read-only partitions is enforced.
FAIL: If audit logs reside on shared partitions or are accessible via standard file server paths without additional access controls, then the protection is inadequate.

CHECK: Inspect and test the functionality of integrity monitoring tools in place for audit logs.
WHY: Automated integrity monitoring reduces the risks of unnoticed modifications and validates operational oversight of log files.
HOW: Request operational logs or alerts from monitoring tools like Tripwire, OSSEC, or similar, then verify that alerts trigger on simulated unauthorized modifications.
FAIL: If integrity logs aren’t generated or if simulated changes are not flagged, the system isn’t effectively monitoring for tampering.

CHECK: Interview system administrators regarding their procedures for modifying audit logs during legitimate maintenance or troubleshooting.
WHY: Verbal confirmation and review of approved processes ensure that manual interventions are controlled and tracked, minimizing risk.
HOW: Ask for documented procedures, then cross-check whether audit logs of such interventions are recorded and approved. Ask for a walkthrough of any recent modifications and check for anomaly alerts.
FAIL: Lack of clear, controlled procedures or evidence of ad hoc and undocumented modifications signal a failure in control enforcement.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcuts: Automate permission checks using scripts and validate file integrity with scheduled checksum comparisons. These serve as quick-and-reliable indicators.
• Critical Indicators: Pay attention to inheritance in file permissions or cases where backups of logs are stored without similar protections. Such details are often the weak links in an otherwise compliant environment.
• Practical Focus: Concentrate on operational evidence (e.g., configuration baselines, system outputs, integrity checker logs) rather than solely on policy documents, as policies alone can be easily misrepresented.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.3.2 Audit log files are protected to prevent modifications by individuals.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.3.2 Examine system configurations and privileges and interview system administrators to verify that current audit log files are protected from modifications by individuals via access control mechanisms, physical segregation, and/or network segregation.

Purpose:
Without adequate protection, audit logs can be easily altered by malicious individuals, undermining their usefulness for investigations.

Good Practice:
Entities should attempt to prevent logs from being exposed in public-accessible locations.

title: Read access to audit log files is limited to those with a job-related need.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that file system permissions on audit log files (and associated directories) are set so that only a defined group of job-critical users (e.g., sysadmins or security team) have read access.
  WHY: Correct permission settings indicate that access is intentionally limited to those with a job-related need.
  HOW: Use file permission listing commands (e.g., ls -l on Linux or icacls on Windows) to review who has read access, ensuring only authorized users/groups are included.
  FAIL: If log files are accessible by broad user groups (like general users) or if the permissions are set to world-readable, this check fails.

• CHECK: Confirm that system configuration (e.g., access control lists, group policies, or security roles) is appropriately defined and enforced in audit log settings.
  WHY: Configurations or policies properly limit read access and demonstrate operational adherence to the control.
  HOW: Examine system configuration guides and active policies; on Windows, inspect Group Policy Objects related to log file access, and on Unix, examine PAM or sudoers configurations.
  FAIL: If configuration files allow non-critical staff or overly broad groups to read audit log files, it indicates improper control implementation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply document that access is limited without applying proper file-level permissions or account restrictions.
• Temporary accounts or shared credentials with elevated access might be used, which superficially meets the requirement on paper but fails operationally.
• Relying on administrative policies only (without monitoring or automated enforcement) can allow non-job-related users to gain access during misconfigurations.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review file system permissions on audit logs.
   WHY: Confirms only authorized users have the ability to read logs.
   HOW: Run OS-level commands (e.g., ls -l for Linux, icacls for Windows) and verify that the file owners/groups match the limited set of job-related roles.
   FAIL: Evidence of non-authorized user read permissions or unnecessarily broad access modes (e.g., 644 or world-readable settings).

2. CHECK: Audit system configuration and access control settings.
   WHY: Ensures that policies and settings enforce read restrictions in an active system state.
   HOW: Examine system configuration files, Group Policy Objects (for Windows), SELinux/AppArmor settings (for Linux), and/or custom audit management tools used to enforce log file permissions.
   FAIL: If configurations show default or permissive settings that allow broader access than required.

3. CHECK: Perform user account reviews and verify role-based access.
   WHY: User account audits ensure that only necessary, job-related accounts have read access to logs.
   HOW: Compare the list of users with log file privileges against HR or role definitions to confirm alignment with job-related necessity.
   FAIL: Presence of accounts that do not clearly meet the role description for audit log access (e.g., generic accounts, non-critical staff, or shared credentials).

4. CHECK: Interview system administrators regarding processes for onboarding/offboarding and periodic reviews of audit log access.
   WHY: This confirms that organizational procedures actively monitor and restrict access, preventing unauthorized exceptions over time.
   HOW: Ask for evidence of review logs, training records, or administrative change logs that confirm scheduled review cycles for audit log access permissions.
   FAIL: Lack of documented review procedures or inconsistent evidence of periodic access recertification.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcut: Directly check audit logs for evidence of unauthorized read attempts or configuration anomaly alerts indicating that broader access was once granted.
• Critical Detail: Ensure that access control settings are not just defined in a policy document, but are actually enforced in the live environment. Automated configuration management and validation tools can help provide this evidence.
• Shortcut: Compare system user groups explicitly defined in access control lists for audit logs against HR records or infrastructure documentation to quickly pinpoint any misalignments.
• Note: Temporary elevation of privileges (used during maintenance windows) should be carefully monitored and logged; verify that such authorizations are time-bound and automatically reverted.

In summary, by cross-verifying file-level permissions, system configuration, actual user roles, and operational review processes, you can confirm that read access to audit log files is indeed limited strictly to those whose job function necessitates it.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.3.1 Read access to audit logs files is limited to those with a job-related need.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.3.1 Interview system administrators and examine system configurations and privileges to verify that only individuals with a job-related need have read access to audit log files.

Customized Approach Objective:
Stored activity records cannot be accessed by unauthorized personnel.

Purpose:
Audit log files contain sensitive information, and read access must be limited to those with a valid business need. This includes audit log files stored on originating systems and elsewhere.

title: Audit logs record details for each auditable event including user identification, event type, date/time, result, origination, and affected data.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology to verify compliance with PCI DSS req-10.2.2.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Audit log files clearly show entries that include user IDs, event types, timestamps, results (e.g., success or failure), the origin (e.g., source IP or system identifier), and details of the affected resource (such as file name, system component, or data identifier).
• Technical Artifacts: Extracted samples from log files, centralized logging configuration files, and audit policies that list required log elements. Automated dashboards showing live feed log details can also serve as evidence.
• Operational Patterns: Regular generation of logs for each auditable event, consistent formatting across entries, and evidence that log reviews or automated alerts are in place.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations may include these fields in log templates without ensuring that dynamic data (e.g., actual user IDs or source IPs) are captured. They might also retain logs in a format that is not easily searchable or may mask values with generic placeholders.
• Surface-level Implementation: Simply logging generic messages such as "User action performed" instead of detailed records, or logging only successful events while ignoring failures.
• Technical Gaps: Missing integration between critical systems, incomplete retention of logs for all required events, or having audit logs that are not secured against tampering.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that audit logs contain the complete data elements (User ID, Event Type, Date/Time, Result, Origination, Affected Data).
WHY: Ensures that the log records provide a complete forensic trail for identifying who did what, when, where, and the outcome.
HOW: Review several sampled log entries directly from system log files or centralized logging solutions. Confirm each entry contains the required fields and check varied events (e.g., login attempts, system access, changes to sensitive data).
FAIL: If log records show missing fields, generic placeholders, or inconsistent record formats.

CHECK: Inspect the configuration of audit logging mechanisms to verify settings automatically capture all auditable events.
WHY: Proper configuration indicates that the logging system is designed to capture all necessary details without manual interventions.
HOW: Examine system or application configuration files, audit policy documents, and logging rules. Interview system administrators to demonstrate that logging is automatically triggered on relevant events.
FAIL: If configuration files lack specification for certain required fields or if manual steps are needed to capture events.

CHECK: Validate the operational process by reviewing change management activity for log configuration, ideally showing that updates are periodically verified and logs are monitored.
WHY: Regular maintenance and review assure that the log system remains aligned with compliance requirements and hasn’t been altered to omit data.
HOW: Request and review evidence such as change logs, update records, and interviewing security personnel regarding periodic log reviews. Confirm that log management procedures are followed as per documented processes.
FAIL: Lack of recent change records or documented log reviews, or if interviews reveal that the process is ad-hoc.

CHECK: Test logging by simulating both successful and failed events to ensure that all required fields are recorded in real time.
WHY: A live test demonstrates the real-time effectiveness of log recording, ensuring dynamic data capture as events occur.
HOW: Create controlled test events (e.g., successful and failed login attempts) and then review the generated audit log entries for completeness. Verify that details like user identity, source, and affected resource appear correctly.
FAIL: If simulated events result in incomplete or inconsistent log entries that do not capture all required details.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Always start by directly accessing raw log files rather than relying solely on dashboards or summarized reports; dashboards may mask incomplete data capture.
• Critical Indicators: Pay special attention to event timestamps and origin data. A mismatch here often indicates that log correlations might fail during incident analysis.
• Details Matter: Detailed identification of the affected data or resource is paramount, as vague descriptions can hinder breach investigation significantly.
• Automation Over Documentation: Automated log parsing and alerting rules provide stronger evidence of ongoing compliance than scattered policy documents.
• Complementary Checks: Look for evidence that logs are also protected (e.g., write-once media or secure logging channels) to ensure post-event integrity, even though that isn’t the primary focus of this requirement.

This methodology focuses on checking that log entries are not only present but contain complete and dynamically generated details that align with the PCI DSS requirement for effective auditing and forensic analysis.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.2 Audit logs record the following details for each auditable event:
- User identification.
- Type of event.
- Date and time.
- Success and failure indication.
- Origination of event.
- Identity or name of affected data, system component, resource, or service.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.2 Interview personnel and examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that all elements specified in this requirement are included in log entries for each auditable event (from 10.2.1.1 through 10.2.1.7).

Purpose:
By recording these details, a potential compromise can be quickly identified with sufficient detail to facilitate follow-up on suspicious activities.

Customized Approach Objective:
Sufficient data is captured to identify who, what, when, where, and how for each event.

title: Audit logs capture all creation and deletion of system-level objects.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: System audit logs show clear entries logging every creation and deletion event of system-level objects. Configuration files or system settings indicate that audit logging for these events is enabled.
• Technical Artifacts: Audit log configurations (e.g., settings in auditd.conf on Linux or Group Policy settings on Windows) that specify monitoring for system-level object changes. Log files from SIEM solutions and operating system logs capturing timestamps, object names, operations performed, and the user/process responsible.
• Operational Patterns: Regular, timestamped entries that correlate with object modification events. Consistency in log retention policies and a pattern over time showing that both creation and deletion are being recorded and monitored.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations might enable logging at a superficial level without ensuring the logs capture all required events, e.g. missing specific flags for system-level events. They might also rotate logs too frequently or redirect logs to unmonitored locations.
• Surface-level Implementations: Setting audit logging without properly filtering or analyzing logs, resulting in noisy logs that fail to highlight actual events. Organizations could simply enable logging in configuration but fail to test if logs are actually generated for system-level object changes.
• Technical Gaps: Failure to include logs from out-of-band channels, missing integration with a SIEM, or not covering all necessary object types. Misconfiguration or non-operational logging due to permissions or incorrect configuration may also occur.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify Audit Log Configuration Settings for System-Level Object Changes
   WHY: Proper configuration ensures that events for creation and deletion are enabled at the source.
   HOW: Review system configuration files (e.g., auditd.conf, audit.rules, or relevant Windows audit policies) to confirm that rules are set to capture system-level object modifications. Compare with known good configuration baselines.
   FAIL: If configuration files do not list the relevant rules or if they reference non-existent rules, this indicates misconfiguration.

2. CHECK: Examine Recent Audit Logs for Event Details
   WHY: Actual log entries provide proof that both creation and deletion events have been recorded.
   HOW: Extract log samples from the past period and look for entries related to system-level object creation and deletion (e.g., file or registry key changes, service modifications). Use filtering queries in SIEM or grep on audit logs to identify relevant events.
   FAIL: Absence of such records or entries that are too generic or lack detail indicates a failure to capture the intended events.

3. CHECK: Test Event Generation and Logging
   WHY: Controlled event generation validates that the system actively logs the intended operations.
   HOW: Conduct a test by creating and then deleting a non-critical system-level object (or a simulated event) and verify that the audit logs capture both actions with complete detail (timestamp, user, object, operation).
   FAIL: If the test actions do not appear in the audit logs or if essential details (user/process, timestamp) are missing, the logging mechanism is ineffective.

4. CHECK: Evaluate Log Integrity and Retention Controls
   WHY: Ensuring logs are tamper-proof and retained for sufficient duration is essential for post-incident investigation.
   HOW: Review log retention policies and configurations in both system and central log repository (SIEM). Check file permissions and integrity monitoring controls.
   FAIL: If logs can be easily altered, or if the retention period is too short to cover forensic investigations, this is a red flag.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use established SIEM queries to filter for creation/deletion events – if queries yield consistent results, it is a strong sign that logging is active.
• Critical Indicator: Look at the granularity of logged events. A poor implementation might collect only high-level events without details about the creator, timestamp, or affected object.
• Key Detail: Verify that the rules cover all aspects of system-level objects (files, services, registry keys on Windows, etc.). Missing any category may lead to unmonitored attack surfaces.
• Remember: Automated tests (e.g., controlled event generation) are preferred over just a review of documentation; they show operational effectiveness rather than paper compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1.7 Audit logs capture all creation and deletion of system-level objects.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1.7 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that creation and deletion of system level objects is captured.

Purpose:
Malicious software, such as malware, often creates or replaces system-level objects on the target system to control a particular function or operation. Logging these events helps determine whether modifications were authorized.

Customized Approach Objective:
Records of alterations that indicate a system has been modified from its intended functionality are captured.

title: Audit logs capture initialization of new audit logs and changes to audit log activity status such as starting, stopping, or pausing.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Audit logs contain clear entries showing events when a new log file is initialized, along with distinct markers for audit log start, stop, or pause activities. Look for specific event codes or messages (e.g., 'log initialized', 'audit logging stopped') in centralized monitoring systems.
   - Technical Artifacts: Configuration files and system settings that explicitly list the events to be captured, and SIEM dashboards or alerting systems that depict these activities in real-time. Log playback interfaces with time-synchronized records help confirm operational status.
   - Operational Patterns: Routine creation of new log files or changes in log status often coincide with system maintenance and tests. Consistent, timestamped entries reflecting normal business operations confirm that these logs are active and monitored.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faking Compliance: Organizations might configure the system to log actions while manually editing audit logs or logs might be written to alternative locations, thus bypassing centralized collection. Simply having a policy or static configuration file does not guarantee active monitoring.
   - Surface-Level Failures: Setting up the configuration file to include these events while the underlying software fails to capture or forward events due to improper permissions or misconfigured agents. Example: logs capturing only administrative actions without test validations.
   - Technical Gaps: Inadequate timestamping, lack of secure time synchronization, or missing event identifiers can allow malicious actors to manipulate or delete log entries without detection.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Verify audit log configuration files and settings for logging of events such as initialization, start, stop, and pause of audit logs.
   WHY: These configurations are the first line of evidence that the system is intended to capture the required events.
   HOW: Inspect system and audit log configuration (e.g., syslog, Windows Event Log configurations, or application-specific settings) to ensure that log initialization and state changes are set to be recorded; confirm that filters or triggers are in place for these events.
   FAIL: Configuration settings do not list the required event types or have been altered to exclude certain critical events.

   CHECK: Examine centralized audit log data from SIEM or log management system for entries indicating the initialization of new audit logs and changes in log activity (start, stop, pause).
   WHY: Direct evidence from active logs confirms that the control is functioning as expected during routine operations.
   HOW: Query the SIEM for event types associated with log initialization and state changes; cross-reference timestamps and event IDs with documented events to ensure coverage.
   FAIL: Absence or underrepresentation of such events in the audit records, or evidence of manually inserted/modified logs.

   CHECK: Conduct a controlled simulation by purposely triggering a change in audit log activity status (if allowed in a test environment) such as pausing and restarting logging, then verify that these events are captured correctly.
