   WHY: A live test demonstrates operational effectiveness and ensures that the logging mechanism captures dynamic changes.
   HOW: In a controlled segment of the environment, issue commands to pause, stop, and restart the audit logs, then immediately review log records to validate that each action was captured with appropriate detail and timestamps.
   FAIL: The test actions do not generate corresponding audit log entries, or the entries lack the detail necessary for forensic investigation.

   CHECK: Review time synchronization (NTP) and secure logging practices to ensure that the recorded events are properly timestamped and cannot be easily altered.
   WHY: Accurate and tamper-evident time stamps are critical to reliably correlate events related to audit log state changes.
   HOW: Validate the configuration of time synchronization services and inspect audit logs for consistency in timestamping; review any cryptographic or checksum-based mechanisms that protect log integrity.
   FAIL: Inconsistent timestamps, misconfiguration of NTP services or absence of integrity checks lead to potential evidence tampering.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Focus on automation and real-time monitoring dashboards. Verify alerts for abnormal changes in audit log status which often indicate bypass attempts.
   - Critical Indicator: Pay particular attention to any log gaps or delays between event time and captured time – these can be red flags for log tampering.
   - Detail Matters: Ensure that the logs not only indicate that an event occurred, but also capture who initiated the change and from what source/IP address; missing details may indicate inadequate logging or deliberate manipulation.
   - Watch for Outliers: Rare or unexpected times when an audit log is reinitialized without a corresponding maintenance window may indicate an attempt to hide malicious activity.
   - Do Not Rely on Documentation Alone: Actual system logs and real-time evidence are the most reliable indicators of proper implementation. Documentation can be prepared post-factum and may not reflect the true operational status.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1.6 Audit logs capture the following:
- All initialization of new audit logs, and
- All starting, stopping, or pausing of the existing audit logs.

Customized Approach Objective:
Records of all changes to audit log activity status are captured.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1.6 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that all elements specified in this requirement are captured.

Purpose:
Turning off or pausing audit logs before performing illicit activities is common practice for malicious users who want to avoid detection. Initialization of audit logs could indicate that a user disabled the log function to hide their actions.

title: Audit logs capture all changes to identification and authentication credentials including new account creation, elevation of privileges, and changes to administrative accounts.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
--------------------------------------------------
• Visible Evidence: Actual audit logs that clearly list events such as creation of new user accounts, changes or deletions to administrative accounts, and privilege escalations. Look for event timestamps, user IDs, source IP addresses, and action descriptions in the logs.
• Technical Artifacts: Configurations from logging systems (e.g., syslog, Windows Event Viewer, SIEM dashboards) that include rules specific to account and privilege changes. Look for filters or event IDs that map to account creation, privilege elevation, etc.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent log generation without apparent gaps or manual interventions; evidence of alerts/notifications generated by the system when critical changes occur; secure and centralized log storage indicating an active audit mechanism.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
--------------------------------------------------
• Faking Compliance: Organizations may show documentation or static configuration screenshots that claim all events are captured, yet the actual logs may miss key event types. For example, only changes to certain accounts or periodic snapshots rather than real-time logging.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Configuring logs to capture events but failing to include detailed metadata (e.g., user context, timestamp, source IP) required to correlate suspicious activities.
• Frequent Technical Gaps: Not covering all categories – often missing logs for privilege escalation or omission in capturing events if an administrative account is modified. Logs may exist but if they are not sent to a central SIEM or are kept locally, tampering is easier.

VALIDATION STEPS
--------------------------------------------------
CHECK: Verify Audit Log Configuration Settings
WHY: Ensures that the logging mechanism is explicitly configured to record account creations, privilege escalations, and administrative changes.
HOW: Review system or SIEM configuration files and logging rules to confirm inclusion of event IDs/filters for new account creation, changes to admin accounts, and privilege changes. Use queries/filtering in the SIEM to extract these events.
FAIL: Absence of specific rules or filters for key event identifiers; only generic logs without reference to authentication credential changes.

CHECK: Examine Actual Audit Log Data for Relevant Events
WHY: Real evidence of control implementation is found in the logs themselves, demonstrating that events are captured in practice.
HOW: Query the audit logs for key activities (e.g., event types or messages indicating account creation, modifications to administrative privileges, etc.). Validate details such as timestamp, actor, and the nature of the change.
FAIL: No or sparse events on account modifications, missing details, or significant time gaps where such changes would have been expected.

CHECK: Test the Logging of a Controlled Change
WHY: Performing a controlled action and verifying its log ensures the logging configuration is operational and real-time.
HOW: Coordinate with system administrators to create a test scenario (e.g., create a test account or elevate privileges for a test account) and then immediately check if the event is logged with complete details.
FAIL: The test action does not appear in the logs or appears incomplete or tampered with, indicating configuration gaps or logging failures.

CHECK: Verify Secure Storage and Centralization of Logs
WHY: Audit logs must be protected from tampering; centralized storage provides additional evidence of operational logging and resilience against local modifications.
HOW: Review the log management setup to ensure logs are forwarded to a centralized, secure SIEM or log server with restricted access. Check retention policies and verify blockchain or write-once-read-many (WORM) records if applicable.
FAIL: Logs are stored locally without evidence of backups or centralization, making them vulnerable to tampering or deletion.

CHECK: Cross-Reference Alerts or Incident Reports
WHY: Alerts generated from these audit logs or subsequent incident reports help verify that the logs are not just collected but actively monitored.
HOW: Ask for evidence of alerts/notifications triggered by such events and review incident response records to validate that actions on identification and authentication credentials trigger proper alerts.
FAIL: No record of alerts or notifications related to credential changes, which might indicate either improper monitoring or possible suppression of key events.

EXPERT TIPS
--------------------------------------------------
• Shortcut Verification: Use automated queries in the SIEM by correlating event IDs for account creation and privilege modification to quickly surface issues.
• Critical Indicators: Pay special attention to logs where administrative accounts are modified. Inconsistent timestamp patterns or missing user context are red flags.
• Details that Matter: Ensure that logs include immutable audit trails. Check for integrity verification mechanisms (e.g., digital signatures or forward-only logs) to prevent tampering.
• Additional Check: Review the retention period of logs and ensure logs older than the minimum retention period are archived appropriately.
• Practical Insight: Manual test events (e.g., a controlled privilege escalation) provide immediate evidence; if those events aren’t captured, there’s likely a gap in technical configuration or monitoring design.

Overall, the analysis centers on verifying that both the logging configuration and real-time operation capture all critical changes to authentication credentials, making it extremely difficult for malicious users to bypass or tamper with the logs.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1.5 Audit logs capture all changes to identification and authentication credentials including, but not limited to:
- Creation of new accounts.
- Elevation of privileges.
- All changes, additions, or deletions to accounts with administrative access.

Customized Approach Objective:
Records of all changes to identification and authentication credentials are captured.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1.5 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that changes to identification and authentication credentials are captured in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
Logging changes to authentication credentials provides residual evidence of activities. Malicious users may attempt to manipulate authentication credentials to bypass them or impersonate a valid account.

title: Audit logs capture all invalid logical access attempts.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Audit log repositories (e.g., SIEM, OS logs, application logs) contain entries flagged as invalid access attempts.
  WHY: This shows that the system is actively capturing events where users have attempted but failed to log in, fulfilling the control’s mandate.
  HOW: Look for entries with error codes, login failure messages, or tags that indicate an invalid attempt. Review sample logs from key systems.
  FAIL: No entries exist or logs only capture successful accesses, or the invalid attempts are filtered out.

• CHECK: Configuration of log management tools and audit settings show inclusion of events from authentication systems.
  WHY: Proper configuration ensures that systems are set to record these events automatically as part of their logging policies.
  HOW: Inspect configuration files, artifact settings, or SIEM rules where event types are defined. Confirm that both recognized user failures and potential brute force attacks are flagged.
  FAIL: Only a subset of events is captured, or configuration settings for invalid access attempts are missing.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may fake compliance by enabling logging but configuring filters that exclude invalid access entries, effectively hiding failed attempts from view.
• Another common gap is using generic logging without specific rules for invalid attempts, so events blend with other noise, making true invalid login tracking ineffective.
• Surface-level implementations include logging only at the application level but ignoring underlying OS or network authentication logs.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Retrieve audit log samples from critical systems (servers, applications, network devices) and search for keywords like "invalid login", "failed authentication", or error codes associated with invalid access attempts.
  WHY: Direct evidence from live logs confirms the system is capturing these events in real-time.
  HOW: Use SIEM query tools or manual log inspection to verify recent entries corresponding to invalid login attempts.
  FAIL: No matching log entries for known test failures or absence of variations in error messages.

2. CHECK: Review and verify the audit log configuration files (or system settings) that specify which events are recorded.
  WHY: Ensuring that invalid access events are explicitly configured for capture is crucial to compliance.
  HOW: Examine system configuration files (e.g., /etc/rsyslog.conf, application config files) and SIEM rule settings pre-defined to log authentication failures.
  FAIL: Configuration lacks specific rules to capture invalid access attempts or misconfigures severity levels causing logs to be ignored.

3. CHECK: Conduct a controlled test by initiating deliberate invalid login attempts on a test account and observe if the events are logged.
  WHY: Practical validation by simulation demonstrates the logging process in a live environment.
  HOW: Trigger one or more invalid logins at known time stamps, then query the audit logs to verify the events match the actions executed.
  FAIL: Test attempts do not appear in the audit logs, or they are recorded with insufficient detail (e.g., missing timestamp, source IP).

4. CHECK: Verify the correlation between multiple systems by checking if centralized logging (e.g., SIEM) aggregates invalid access attempts from different sources.
  WHY: This confirms that the logging mechanism is not isolated and invalid attempts across the infrastructure are captured.
  HOW: Review SIEM dashboards or aggregated log reports for events from various authentication endpoints and check consistency of event recording.
  FAIL: Logs are siloed without central correlation, missing data from non-standard sources.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Begin with an automated SIEM query for the past month for keywords associated with invalid access. Analyze trends rather than isolated events.
• Critical Indicator: Lack of diversity in log sources (e.g., only OS logs and no application logs) is a red flag that the implementation might be incomplete.
• Expert Detail: Examine log timestamp accuracy and source IP details since these are vital during forensic analysis; missing data here often indicates poor implementation.
• Remember: Focusing on live log data (especially simulated failed logins) minimizes the risk of relying solely on potentially outdated configuration documents, ensuring operational effectiveness is validated.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements (from PDF page 244):
10.2.1.4 Audit logs capture all invalid logical access attempts.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures (from PDF page 244):
10.2.1.4 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that invalid logical access attempts are captured.

(Table on PDF page 245):
Records of all invalid access attempts are captured. Guidance: Invalid login attempts may be an indication of an unauthorized user’s attempts to “brute force” or guess a password.

title: Audit logs capture all access to audit logs.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Configured audit systems (e.g., syslog, SIEM, or auditd) show rules that trigger recording when any user accesses audit logs. The audit log files themselves contain entries that reflect attempts to view, modify, or delete the log records. 
• Technical artifacts: System configuration files (such as auditd.conf or centralized logging appliance settings) that explicitly mention monitoring of audit log directories and files. Correlation rules in SIEM systems or log parsers that identify events generated from accessing audit logs. 
• Operational patterns: Regular patterns of log entries that include metadata (user IDs, timestamps, hostnames) indicating when audit logs were read, changed, or deleted. Such events should be consistently recorded even during routine administrative actions.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake compliance: Organizations may configure policies stating that audit log access is monitored but neglect to enable the actual audit rule. They might generate superficial log entries that only mark log file creation without capturing subsequent accesses. 
• Surface-level implementations: Relying solely on operating system-level file permissions without explicit audit rule entries can lead to gaps where access is not explicitly captured. 
• Technical gaps: Failing to monitor log access by third-party applications or remote administration tools; missing out on capturing both read and administrative actions on the logs.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify audit daemon (e.g., auditd) or logging appliance configuration to confirm audit rules are in place to monitor access to audit log files.
WHY: Configuration evidence proves the mechanism to record access events has been intentionally implemented.
HOW: Review the audit configuration file (e.g., /etc/audit/audit.rules) or centralized logging configuration for entries that mention the audit log directory and file paths (e.g., /var/log/audit/*). Look for rules that capture read, write, and attribute change events.
FAIL: If audit rules do not include references to audit log files or are missing altogether.

CHECK: Inspect actual audit log entries for records that indicate access events to log files.
WHY: Live event data confirms that the control is operational and that attempts to access audit logs create traceable events.
HOW: Query the audit logs (using tools like ausearch or reviewing SIEM console) for event IDs or messages that reference audit log file accesses (e.g., file open, file read, file modification events). Verify that these events capture user identification, time stamps, and activity details.
FAIL: No recorded events pertaining to access of audit logs, or missing critical metadata such as user or timestamp.

CHECK: Perform a controlled simulation of an audit log access event and verify its audit trail.
WHY: Simulated operations are the most direct evidence of an active control capturing the required events.
HOW: With proper authorization, have an administrator or auditor execute a controlled read or modification of an audit log file. Immediately review the audit logs to check if an entry was created that details this access event, including the correct actor, time, and nature of the operation.
FAIL: No corresponding audit entry created or entry lacks critical information such as the actor’s identity or activity type.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcut: Use automated query tools (e.g., ausearch, splunk queries) that specifically look for keywords like “audit.log” or “open” events on log file paths to quickly identify access events.
• Critical indicator: Absence of event correlation in SIEM across audit log access events often signals misconfiguration; check for any anomaly where administrative actions are not captured.
• Detail matter: Ensure that both successful and unsuccessful attempts to access audit logs (e.g., attempts that fail due to proper use of file permissions) are logged, as missing error events may indicate incomplete coverage.
• Validate timestamp synchronization between the log server and auditing services to ensure the recorded times accurately reflect the event timeline.

By following this methodology, auditors can confidently verify that audit log access events are captured accurately and comprehensively, thereby reducing the risk of undetected tampering.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1.3 Audit logs capture all access to audit logs.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1.3 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that access to all audit logs is captured.

Purpose:
Malicious users often attempt to alter audit logs to hide their actions. A record of access allows an organization to trace any inconsistencies or potential tampering of the logs to an individual account. Having logs identify changes, additions, and deletions to the audit logs can help retrace steps made by unauthorized personnel.

title: Audit logs capture all actions taken by any individual with administrative access, including any interactive use of application or system accounts.
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology to validate that audit logs capture all actions taken by individuals with administrative access, including any interactive use of application or system accounts.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes active audit policies configured to log administrative commands, interactive sessions, and their associated details (timestamps, session IDs, command history) on servers and workstations.
• Technical artifacts may be found in audit configuration files (e.g., /etc/audit/audit.rules for Linux, Group Policy settings for Windows) and log management systems (SIEM) that aggregate and correlate administrative account actions.
• Operational patterns include continuous log generation during administrative sessions, real-time alerting for actions using elevated privileges, and periodic reviews demonstrating that these logs are actively used for forensic investigations.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes fake compliance by simply enabling logs without ensuring they capture detailed interactive commands or administrative commands. For example, they may log only login/logout events instead of every command executed.
• Surface-level implementations might only capture actions from the primary administrative account but not for additional system or application accounts with elevated rights. Incomplete configuration or disabled detailed logging (e.g., session recording) might be present.
• Automated scripts that run administrative tasks may bypass interactive logging if logs are filtered at the log aggregation layer or if the logging system is improperly configured.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Audit Configuration Settings for Elevated Accounts
WHY: Ensures the technical settings include detailed logging for all actions taken by privileged users.
HOW: Examine audit configuration files (e.g., /etc/audit/audit.rules, Windows Audit Policy settings) to check for explicit inclusion of administrative accounts and interactive session commands. Confirm settings such as -a always,exit -F uid=0 (or equivalent) are configured.
FAIL: If configuration files only log minimal events (e.g., session start/end) or omit key privileged actions, this indicates noncompliance.

CHECK: Inspect Log Records from Administrative Sessions
WHY: Direct evidence from the logs shows that detailed administrative actions are captured in real time.
HOW: Sample audit logs from a recent administrative session and verify entries include session initiation, command execution, and command parameters. Search for interactive use of application or system accounts within your SIEM or logging repository.
FAIL: Absence of detailed command execution records or timestamps in logs reduces the effectiveness of audit trails.

CHECK: Correlate Privileged Account List with Logged Events
WHY: Confirm that all known administrative and system accounts are actually generating logs in the audit system.
HOW: Cross-reference a current list of privileged users/administrative accounts (from directory services or system management tools) with corresponding entries in the audit log repository over a representative period.
FAIL: If there are accounts that should be logging actions but have no corresponding records, then the control has significant gaps.

CHECK: Validate Interactive Session Logging Mechanisms
WHY: Ensures that interactive sessions (e.g., remote shell sessions, RDP, SSH) are completely captured, including the commands run during those sessions.
HOW: Recreate an administrative interactive session in a controlled environment and verify that all steps are appropriately logged by the system (e.g., recording keystrokes or command-line operations).
FAIL: Missing or incomplete interactive session logs indicate that the logging mechanism is not fully effective.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include using SIEM query templates to filter log entries by known administrative accounts and timeframes, which quickly highlights the detail level of logged events.
• Critical red flags include logs that only capture session start and termination, lacking the granular detail of the commands executed in between.
• Pay close attention to the integration between the audit logs and incident response tools – without real-time monitoring of these logs, the control’s operational value diminishes.
• In environments with automation, ensure that logs from automated tasks using elevated access are distinctly marked from interactive administrative use, as ambiguous logging might hide potential misuse.

By following these steps, you strengthen assurance that your audit logs effectively capture all pertinent administrative activity.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1.2 Audit logs capture all actions taken by any individual with administrative access, including any interactive use of application or system accounts.

Customized Approach Objective:
Records of all actions performed by individuals with elevated privileges are captured.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1.2 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that all actions taken by any individual with administrative access, including any interactive use of application or system accounts, are logged.

Purpose:
Accounts with increased access privileges, such as the "administrator" or "root" account, have the potential to significantly impact the security or operational functionality of a system. Without a log of the activities performed, an organization cannot trace any issues resulting from an administrative mistake or misuse of privilege back to the specific action and account.

title: Audit logs capture all individual user access to cardholder data.
methodology:
Control: Audit logs capture all individual user access to cardholder data.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Configuration screens or system setting panels that explicitly list audit logging settings for cardholder data access, including user identifiers, timestamps, and accessed resources.
   • Technical Artifacts: Audit log files, SIEM data streams, database configuration files, and file integrity configuration records demonstrating logging of every access event.
   • Operational Patterns: Regularly captured log entries with details about individual user actions, automated log collection and aggregation, and alerts based on anomalous access patterns.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake or Incomplete Logging: Organizations might configure basic logging that only captures login events without recording the specific actions performed on cardholder data.
   • Surface-Level Implementation: Logs might exist but do not contain key fields (e.g., individual user identifiers, specific cardholder data elements), making them ineffective for forensic purposes.
   • Technical Gaps: Custom applications where logging was hard-coded and not integrated with central audit logging systems or using non-immutable log stores that are easily altered or deleted.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify audit log configuration includes settings explicitly for recording individual user access to cardholder data.
   WHY: Ensures that the logging mechanism is configured to capture detailed user activity concerning cardholder data.
   HOW: Review configuration files/settings in application environments, database servers, and security appliances. Confirm that each setting specifies logging for actions on cardholder data.
   FAIL: If configurations are absent, generic, or do not list cardholder data access as a distinct event.

   CHECK: Retrieve and examine a sample of audit log entries from the SIEM or centralized logging system.
   WHY: Direct evidence from the logs proves that user activities are recorded with necessary granularity (user ID, timestamp, accessed data).
   HOW: Use log analysis tools or automated queries to pull entries that relate to cardholder data access. Look for clear attribution to an individual user.
   FAIL: Logs missing user identifiers or showing aggregated data that cannot be linked to individual actions.

   CHECK: Validate that audit logs are securely stored, access-controlled, and retained per policy.
   WHY: This demonstrates that not only are events captured, but they are also protected from tampering and available for later review.
   HOW: Inspect the log management and retention policy, review system permissions, and test for write-once or secure archiving measures in the SIEM or log repository.
   FAIL: If log storage is decentralized with lax permission settings or if retention settings are insufficient for forensic investigations.

   CHECK: Confirm that audit logs include key data elements (e.g., user identifier, timestamp, action performed, and result) for every access to cardholder data.
   WHY: These details are critical for establishing an audit trail that provides accountability for data access.
   HOW: Cross-reference a few live or historical transactions in the application with corresponding log entries to ensure all required data points are captured.
   FAIL: If any critical information is missing or if the logs only capture minimal event data that does not credibly link actions to specific users.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated log search and correlation tools within the SIEM to quickly filter events related to cardholder data. Regularly schedule these queries to identify gaps.
   • Critical Indicator: Pay attention to discrepancies between the number of access transactions recorded in application logs and those appearing in audit logs – this is a red flag for incomplete capture.
   • Detail Matters Most: Ensure logs are immutable or protected with digital signatures where possible. A log’s integrity is as important as its completeness.
   • Holistic View: Look at log collector server configurations and confirm that all relevant systems (databases, applications, file servers) feeding cardholder data have proper logging enabled.

This methodology ensures that you are not only verifying the existence of policies but also assessing the practical, technical, and operational effectiveness of the audit log controls for capturing all individual user access to cardholder data.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1.1 Audit logs capture all individual user access to cardholder data.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1.1 Examine audit log configurations and log data to verify that all individual user access to cardholder data is logged.

Customized Approach Objective:
Records of all individual user access to cardholder data are captured.

title: Audit logs are enabled and active for all system components and cardholder data.
methodology:
Control ID: req-10.2.1 – Audit logs are enabled and active for all system components and cardholder data.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Configuration Evidence: System settings, configurations, or console screenshots showing that audit logging is enabled (e.g., syslog, Windows Event Logs, database audit trails).
• Technical Artifacts: Log files, log rotation scripts, and collection metadata from monitoring tools that prove continuous logging.
• Operational Patterns: Alerts or dashboards that indicate logs are actively monitored and stored, including evidence of periodic reviews or automated archival.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Enabled Flags: Settings changed to “enabled” without actual log collection due to misconfigurations or inadequate logging agents.
• Incomplete Coverage: Only critical systems or cardholder data environments are marked, leaving gaps in less scrutinized systems.
• Documentation without Activity: Policies exist on paper, but actual logs are not being collected or are rotated/discarded too quickly.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify System Configurations and Settings
   WHY: Confirm that audit logging is explicitly enabled for all relevant components and data stores.
   HOW: Review configuration files, logging settings in the OS, DBMS, and network devices (e.g., check syslog.conf, Windows Event Viewer, database audit settings); confirm that log collection parameters are set.
   FAIL: If configurations indicate logging is disabled or if the settings are not applied consistently across components.

CHECK: Examine Actual Log Files
   WHY: Active logs provide observable evidence that logging is continuously taking place.
   HOW: Pull sample logs from various systems (servers, databases, network devices) and validate timestamps, events recorded, and log completeness.
   FAIL: Absence of logs, logs with outdated timestamps, or incomplete event records indicate non-active logging.

CHECK: Assess Log Management Process and Tools
   WHY: Automated mechanisms (like centralized log collection and SIEM integration) ensure logs are used effectively.
   HOW: Inspect the log aggregation tool’s configuration and dashboards; verify logs are centralized, and automated processes (alerts, archival) are functioning.
   FAIL: If logs are scattered, not integrated with SIEM or monitoring tools, or if automated alerts and archival are missing.

CHECK: Interview System Administrators
   WHY: Direct inquiries can provide verification for how logs are maintained, monitored, and protected.
   HOW: Ask for details on log retention policies, how frequently logs are reviewed, and steps taken when anomalies are detected.
   FAIL: Vague or inconsistent answers that do not align with observed configurations or artifacts.

CHECK: Validate End-to-End Logging from Cardholder Data Systems
   WHY: Ensures that audit logs specifically cover systems handling sensitive cardholder data.
   HOW: Identify endpoints processing cardholder data and confirm that audit logging is engaged on these systems through configuration reviews and log examinations.
   FAIL: Discovery of systems processing cardholder data without configured or functional logging.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated scripts to parse log directories for recent file modifications; this can quickly surface inactive log sources.
• Critical Indicator: Logs with uniform timestamps (all logs older than current date) are a red flag that logging might be enabled in config only but not in practice.
• Detail: Confirm that log retention periods meet your policy and compliance requirements; check for anomalies in log rotation schedules which can indicate poor implementation.
• Remember: Focus on actual evidence from production systems over policy documents – operational evidence is hard to fake and directly shows control effectiveness.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
10.2.1 Audit logs are enabled and active for all system components and cardholder data.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.2.1 Interview the system administrator and examine system configurations to verify that audit logs are enabled and active for all system components.

title: Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 10 are documented, assigned, and understood.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible role allocation matrices, charts, or tables that list personnel responsible for Requirement 10 activities. These are often found in internal policies or dedicated role documents.
• Technical artifacts such as updated organizational charts, role acknowledgment records, email distributions of roles, and training logs that confirm personnel awareness.
• Operational patterns include recurring evidence of personnel performing the specified log reviews, audit trails, and periodic re-certification of roles.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes rely solely on high-level policy documents without connecting them to daily operational evidence—i.e., printed policies that are never enforced or updated.
• Some may present static architectures or outdated org charts as proof, bypassing verification of the current personnel actually performing Requirement 10 activities.
• Role documents might be produced, but without employee acknowledgment or regular reviews, resulting in a superficial demonstration of compliance.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the existence of current documentation that explicitly lists roles and responsibilities for all activities in Requirement 10.
   WHY: This demonstrates that the organization has formally assigned responsibilities.
   HOW: Obtain a copy of the policy or role matrix document, and check the effective date, the assigned personnel, and any update logs. Cross-reference with the current organizational chart.
   FAIL: No current documentation exists or the document is outdated unnecessary, missing personnel names or did not receive a recent review.

2. CHECK: Interview a sample of personnel who are designated to perform Requirement 10 activities.
   WHY: Direct confirmation ensures that they understand their day-to-day responsibilities beyond what’s stated on paper.
   HOW: Ask selected individuals to describe their specific tasks related to log reviews and monitoring. Verify if they recall training or have recent communication acknowledging these responsibilities.
   FAIL: Personnel are unclear or contradictory in their descriptions, indicating the role assignment wasn’t effectively communicated.

3. CHECK: Inspect evidence of role acknowledgment or periodic review mechanisms, such as signed acknowledgments, training records, or audit logs confirming task execution.
   WHY: This shows that roles are not only documented but are actively maintained and reviewed over time.
   HOW: Look for documentation of regular review meetings or signed forms where employees confirm their responsibility, and check corresponding logs or audit trails showing active involvement.
   FAIL: Lack of employee acknowledgment forms, outdated training records, or absence of any periodic review trace.

4. CHECK: Validate the integration of role responsibilities with operational monitoring (e.g., automated alerts, log review schedules).
   WHY: This proves the documented role is translated into effective, observable operational practices.
   HOW: Review system configurations or monitoring tools that reference role-specific alerting or logging duties. Correlate these with the documented roles.
   FAIL: No link between the role documentation and automated monitoring controls, suggesting a disconnect between policy and practice.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Ask for the most recent review meeting minutes regarding Requirement 10 activities. If personnel can directly cite these, it’s a strong indicator of an active and enforced role assignment.
• Watch for red flags such as generic role descriptions without clear task segmentation or dated documentation with no revision history.
• Do not rely solely on downloaded PDFs of policies—confirm that these policies are actively referenced in daily operations, and tie them to system monitoring and alerts to demonstrate effectiveness.
• Critical detail: Ensure employee interviews reference specific examples from actual day-to-day tasks; vague or scripted answers are a strong indicator of superficial implementation.
content:
10.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 10 are documented, assigned, and understood.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
10.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 10 are documented and assigned.
10.1.2.b Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 10 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as defined and are understood.

Customized Approach Objective:
Day-to-day responsibilities for performing all the activities in Requirement 10 are allocated. Personnel are accountable for successful, continuous operation of these requirements.

Guidance:
Purpose:
If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, personnel may not be aware of their day-to-day responsibilities and critical activities may not occur.

Good Practice:
Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents. As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities.

title: All security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 10 are documented, kept up to date, in use, and known to all affected parties.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documents exist in a formal repository with version histories and update logs showing periodic reviews. These logs are often found in document management systems or internal websites where revision dates are printed on the document.
• Security policies are referenced in staff training records or periodic communications, indicating active use and distribution among affected parties.
• Operational personnel can reference and discuss the policies and procedures, showing that the documents are not only available but actively utilized in day-to-day operations.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may superficially maintain policy documents with outdated revision dates or without documented change logs, giving the appearance of compliance without real updates.
• Some entities may rely solely on a central repository listing without evidence of training, resulting in policies that are not effectively disseminated to all staff.
• Compliance may be faked with static, boilerplate documents that do not reflect recent changes in processes or technology, thus failing the operational relevance test.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verification of Document Versioning and Last Updated Dates
WHY: Ensures that policies are not static and are kept up to date reflecting current operational practices.
HOW: Access the document management system, identify the latest revision dates, and compare with known change events or review cycles. Look for a version history log in each policy document.
FAIL: Missing revision history, outdated revision dates, or evidence that policies have not been updated following changes in external/internal environments.

CHECK: Review of Change Control and Approval Process
WHY: Validates that updates to policies are tracked via an established change management process.
HOW: Examine change control logs, meeting minutes, or email records that document policy reviews and updates. Confirm that amendments are formally approved and recorded.
FAIL: Absence of documented change controls, ad hoc updates without traceable approvals, or inconsistent revision practices.

CHECK: Examination of Training and Awareness Records
WHY: Verifies that all affected personnel are informed about and understand the documented policies and procedures.
HOW: Request and review training schedules, attendance records, or corporate communications that include policy distributions. Interview a sample of staff to gauge their familiarity with the policies.
FAIL: Lack of evidence that training was conducted, low attendance figures, or staff inability to articulate policy procedures.

CHECK: Interviews with Operational Personnel
WHY: Confirms that the policies are actually in use within day-to-day operations and not merely documented for compliance purposes.
HOW: Conduct targeted interviews with personnel from areas affected by Requirement 10. Ask them to describe how the policies are applied in their work routines. Cross-reference their descriptions with the documented procedures.
FAIL: Staff unaware of policy details, inconsistent practices, or reliance on outdated procedures.

CHECK: Evidence of Accessibility and Distribution Mechanisms
WHY: Ensures that the policies are well-distributed and easily accessible to all affected parties.
HOW: Verify that policies are available on the company's intranet or through an internal portal, and check for any posted updates or memos that indicate when policies were distributed. Look for digital timestamps and access logs if available.
FAIL: Policies are stored in restricted locations with limited access, or there are no clear mechanisms for distribution and notification.

EXPERT TIPS
• Look for integration between the document management system and the training records system. Good implementations will have linked processes.
• If policies are only available as PDF files with no dynamic update mechanism, it raises a red flag regarding timely updates.
• When interviewing staff, focus on specifics: if they can mention recent changes or provide examples, that is a strong indicator of effective dissemination.
• Pay close attention to the change logs – incomplete or infrequent updates, especially in a rapidly evolving environment, indicate potential compliance failures.
• Verify that policies are not only accessible but referenced in job-specific procedures or operational checklists, which is a strong sign that they’re being actively applied.

By following these steps, auditors can efficiently determine whether the control is not just documented on paper but actively enforced and updated in real operational contexts.
content:
10.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 10 are:
- Documented.
- Kept up to date.
- In use.
- Known to all affected parties.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 10 are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
Requirement 10.1.1 is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout Requirement 10. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in Requirement 10, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated.

Good Practice:
It is important to update policies and procedures as needed to address changes in processes, technologies, and business objectives.

title: Processes and mechanisms for logging and monitoring all access to system components and cardholder data are defined and understood.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that logging mechanisms are actively capturing all access events to system components and the CDE.
  WHY: Visible records (log files, audit trails, SIEM events) demonstrate that user and system activities are being monitored in real time.
  HOW: Physically inspect log management configurations on devices, SIEM dashboards, and system audit trails for consistent, timestamped events covering access attempts and actions on both system components and cardholder data.
  FAIL: Absence of recent logs or records that show gaps in logging events, such as missing typical administrative or access attempt logs.

• CHECK: Confirm that logging processes are fully integrated with alerting mechanisms and incident response workflows.
  WHY: Seamless integration proves that the organization not only collects logs but also actively monitors and responds to anomalies.
  HOW: Observe alert logs or SIEM-triggered notifications for sensitive or suspicious activities and validate that they follow through with documented incident response procedures.
  FAIL: If alerts are enabled only in documentation or are not visible in the SIEM event stream, or if response workflows are never triggered (i.e., only theoretical in policies).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• A common evasion is generating logs only at critical times or on selective endpoints, thereby neglecting broader logging of all access. Superficial modifications in system configuration may rely solely on static policies which don’t translate into active monitoring.
• Organizations might use outdated or ineffective log retention settings, meaning the logs exist but are not searchable or actionable. Similarly, settings that fail to capture contextual data (e.g., user identity or session details) can lead to gaps in forensic investigation.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Audit the log configurations on a random sample of system components and servers in the CDE.
   WHY: This confirms that logging is properly configured across the entire environment.
   HOW: Review configuration files and SIEM integrations for consistency in settings. Check if event types such as access, error, and exceptional conditions are being recorded with appropriate metadata (timestamp, user ID, source IP). 
   FAIL: Detected systems missing log configuration files or inconsistent settings (e.g., some components not feeding into SIEM at all).

2. CHECK: Validate the effectiveness of the alerting mechanisms by simulating or reviewing historical triggered events.
   WHY: It demonstrates that logs are not only collected, but are also actively monitored and acted upon.
   HOW: Request evidence from the incident response team on how they reviewed alerts from logging sources, or simulate access events and confirm that alerts are generated and escalated in the SIEM.
   FAIL: No traceable alert responses or alert logs indicating that suspicious log events are either ignored or not recognized.

3. CHECK: Examine log retention and integrity controls.
   WHY: Ensuring logs are kept for an appropriate period and are protected from tampering is critical for both real-time monitoring and forensic investigations.
   HOW: Verify retention policies in log management systems and conduct random integrity checks (e.g., log checksum validation). Verify that logging servers have restricted access controls.
   FAIL: If logs are purged too quickly, or if access controls on log repositories are weak allowing tampering without triggering alerts.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Focus on high-risk systems first (e.g., administrative servers, authentication systems) as these are often the target of bypass attempts.
• Critical indicator: Inconsistent timestamps or lack of contextual data (user IDs, IP addresses) across logs suggest poor implementation or possible intentional evasion.
• Note: Automated evidence from SIEM dashboards with real-time alerts is harder to fake than static documentation. Always cross-reference event logs with physical system configurations.
• Additional check: Validate time synchronization (e.g., NTP) on all logging systems to ensure consistency across the data which is essential for accurate event correlation.

By following the above steps, you ensure that logging and monitoring processes for access to system components and cardholder data are both implemented and effective, rather than relying solely on documented policy assertions.
content:
Processes and mechanisms for logging and monitoring all access to system components and cardholder data are defined and understood.

Logging mechanisms and the ability to track user activities are critical in preventing, detecting, or minimizing the impact of a data compromise. The presence of logs on all system components and in the cardholder data environment (CDE) allows thorough tracking, alerting, and analysis when something does go wrong. Determining the cause of a compromise is difficult, if not impossible, without system activity logs.

This requirement applies to user activities, including those by employees, contractors, consultants, and internal and external vendors, and other third parties (for example, those providing support or maintenance services).

These requirements do not apply to user activity of consumers (cardholders).

Refer to Appendix G for definitions of PCI DSS terms.

title: Personnel in POI environments receive training to detect and report device tampering.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Training records, logs, and schedules showing that personnel in POI (Point of Interaction) environments have attended sessions on device tampering detection and reporting. Posters, brochures, or on-screen reminders in POI areas that remind staff of tampering indicators and procedures. 
   • Technical Artifacts: Copies of training materials (presentations, handbooks, e-learning modules) that explicitly cover verifying third-party identities, device replacement protocols, and suspicious behaviors. Training attendance data paired with assessment results.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular refreshers or simulated exercises indicating that training is recurrent and responsive. Evidence of actual reporting activity (ticket logs or incident response records) where suspicious activities were noted and acted upon by trained personnel.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply produce generic training materials or outdated presentations that mention tampering without practical, actionable instructions.
   • Superficial evidence such as a printed agenda without any records of actual training sessions or interactive evaluations may be used to fake compliance.
   • Failing to update training materials to cover emerging methods of device tampering or not conducting refresher sessions that keep personnel alert.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that training materials include explicit instructions on verifying third-party identity, device replacement verification, and recognizing suspicious behavior.
   WHY: Ensures the content is aligned with the requirement and gives personnel the necessary knowledge to detect tampering.
   HOW: Review a representative sample of training slide decks and handbooks; confirm inclusion of topics on verifying credentials and reporting procedures.
   FAIL: Training materials that are generic or omit specific procedures (e.g., steps to verify repair personnel) indicate non-compliance.

   CHECK: Examine training attendance records and assessment results.
   WHY: Demonstrates that personnel have actively participated and passed training modules relevant to device tampering.
   HOW: Access training logs, attendance sheets, and if available, evaluation scores or test results; correlate with POI staff rosters.
   FAIL: Lack of verified training evidence or missing participation records for key personnel.

   CHECK: Interview a sample of personnel in POI environments regarding their ability to detect and report tampering.
   WHY: Direct interviews reveal whether training has been effectively absorbed and whether personnel can recall critical procedures.
   HOW: Conduct spot interviews or a structured questionnaire asking about steps to handle unverified maintenance personnel and suspicious activities.
   FAIL: Responses that are vague, outdated, or incorrect regarding procedures indicate ineffective or inadequate training.

   CHECK: Review incident reports or logs for any reported cases of suspicious behavior related to device tampering and subsequent actions taken.
   WHY: Operational evidence of detection and reporting reinforces that training is actively applied in practice.
   HOW: Analyze incident response logs, help desk records, or security monitoring incident tickets to confirm that suspicious incidents were reported and handled as per procedures.
   FAIL: Absence of any tampering-related incident records (or evidence of mishandling incidents) may indicate that training either didn’t occur or is not being applied.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Check if the training materials include scenario-based examples or interactive segments; these are hard to fake and indicate deeper engagement.
   • Critical Indicator: Ensure training content reflects current threats from spoofed or unauthorized maintenance personnel and is regularly updated.
   • Practical Detail: Look for evidence of periodic refresher training rather than one-off sessions—the recency of training sessions is a strong indicator of operational effectiveness.
   • Verification Shortcut: Use targeted questions during interviews that solicit specific procedures (e.g., "What’s the first step if an unknown technician appears at your device?") to quickly gauge the depth of training absorption.
   • Note: Do not rely solely on formal policies or documented procedures; active, measurable evidence (training logs, interactive exercises, and firsthand employee feedback) is key in verifying real-world compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.5.1.3 Training is provided for personnel in POI environments to be aware of attempted tampering or replacement of POI devices, and includes:
- Verifying the identity of any third-party persons claiming to be repair or maintenance personnel, before granting them access to modify or troubleshoot devices.
- Procedures to ensure devices are not installed, replaced, or returned without verification.
- Being aware of suspicious behavior around devices.
- Reporting suspicious behavior and indications of device tampering or substitution to appropriate personnel.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.5.1.3.a Review training materials for personnel in POI environments to verify they include all elements specified in this requirement.
9.5.1.3.b Interview personnel in POI environments to verify they have received training and know the procedures for all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
Criminals will often pose as authorized maintenance personnel to gain access to POI devices.

Good Practice:
Personnel training should include being alert to and questioning anyone who shows up to do POI maintenance to ensure they are authorized and have a valid work order. Additionally, all third parties requesting access should be verified, and procedures should be in place to validate the legitimacy of new or replacement devices.

Additional Examples (from continuation):
Suspicious behavior includes attempts by unknown persons to unplug or open devices, and mechanisms should be in place for reporting such behavior.

title: The frequency and type of POI device inspections are defined by the entity's risk analysis.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for a comprehensive risk analysis document that explicitly outlines the frequency and type of periodic inspections for POI devices. This document should reference all elements required by Requirement 12.3.1. Additionally, there should be a scheduled plan or calendar indicating inspection intervals and the procedures to follow.
• Technical Artifacts: Review inspection logs and device maintenance records that align with the risk analysis guidance. Automated reminders or integrated dashboards (if available) that enforce the inspection schedule are a strong indicator.
• Operational Patterns: Regular, documented inspections with consistent intervals and detailed reports. Interviews with operational staff should reveal that inspection practices are tailored based on location factors (attended vs. unattended) and device criticality.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may produce generic risk analysis documents that simply cite compliance without actual linkage to POI device-specific risk factors. They might submit templated inspection logs that are not updated with real-time results or fail to capture deviations from the approved schedule.
• Surface-Level Implementations: A company might develop an inspection schedule that is not adjusted to reflect changes in device location or operational context, resulting in check-box compliance rather than adaptive risk management.
• Common Technical Gaps: Missing granular details in inspection logs (e.g., timestamps, inspector identification, environmental changes) or lack of integration between the risk analysis outcomes and the inspection scheduling system.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the targeted risk analysis document for POI devices includes defined inspection frequency and type.
   WHY: It ensures that the inspection schedule is based on a thorough risk evaluation aligned with Requirement 12.3.1.
   HOW: Review the risk analysis document and cross-check with the risk analysis criteria in Requirement 12.3.1; look for explicit mention of inspection intervals based on device location, operation, or nature.
   FAIL: Absence of specific frequency details or generic risk analysis that does not address POI device aspects.

2. CHECK: Review periodic inspection logs and maintenance records for POI devices.
   WHY: These logs are the operational evidence that the inspection frequencies and types are executed as defined in the risk analysis.
   HOW: Compare inspection dates, documented procedures, and outcomes against the scheduled intervals and documented risk analysis; validate consistency and completeness of logs.
   FAIL: Incomplete or inconsistent logs that do not match the defined risk analysis frequency, or if logs are missing detailed inspection outcomes.

3. CHECK: Interview operational personnel responsible for POI device inspections.
   WHY: Personnel insights confirm that the inspection practices align with the risk analysis and that adjustments are made in a live environment when needed.
   HOW: Ask inspectors about how their inspection frequency and type are determined and request examples where the risk analysis influenced the inspection schedule; pose scenario-based questions about attended versus unattended environments.
   FAIL: If personnel are unable to provide clear explanations or if practices differ from the documented risk analysis.

4. CHECK: Validate integration between risk analysis outcomes and inspection scheduling (if automated systems are used).
   WHY: Automation that feeds risk criteria into scheduling systems minimizes human error and ensures continuous compliance.
   HOW: Check system configurations, automated alerts or dashboards that reflect the inspection schedule; verify that any dynamically changing risk factors (such as location or environmental changes) are incorporated.
   FAIL: If automated tools are misconfigured or if the scheduling system does not reflect updated risk assessments.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Quickly reviewing the risk analysis document’s table of contents and section on POI devices can reveal if the risk analysis was suitably targeted – generic risk analysis documents are a red flag.
• Critical Indicator: Consistent and detailed inspection logs with timestamps and inspector credentials are strong indicators of a robust process, while missing dates or generic 'OK' notes are a sign of poor implementation.
• Detail Matters: Ensure that the risk analysis document factors in different environmental conditions (attended vs. unattended devices) and that inspection logs reflect these variations through noted conditions during inspections.
• Look for Integration: Automated alerts or calendars that derive inspection schedules from risk analysis parameters are less prone to manipulation compared to manual logs.

This methodology prioritizes evaluating operational practices over mere documentation and emphasizes evidence that cannot be easily manufactured, thereby ensuring that the control is not just theoretically compliant but practically effective.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.5.1.2.1 The frequency of periodic POI device inspections and the type of inspections performed is defined in the entity's targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.

Customized Approach Objective:
POI devices are inspected at a frequency that addresses the entity’s risk.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.5.1.2.1.a Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for the frequency of periodic POI device inspections and type of inspections performed to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
9.5.1.2.1.b Examine documented results of periodic device inspections and interview personnel to verify that the frequency and type of POI device inspections performed match what is defined in the entity's targeted risk analysis conducted for this requirement.

Guidance:
Purpose: Entities are best placed to determine the frequency of POI device inspections based on the environment in which the device operates.
Good Practice: The frequency of inspections will depend on factors such as the location of a device and whether the device is attended or unattended.

title: POI device surfaces are periodically inspected to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes physically marked POI devices with tamper-evident seals, secure marker pen indications, or labels that are regularly compared against a baseline photograph.
• Technical artifacts such as inspection logs, digital photographs with timestamps, and electronic checklists that record inspection dates and findings.
• Operational patterns include documented periodic inspection schedules, random unannounced checks, and maintenance records confirming that all devices have been reviewed for anomalies such as broken casing, altered serial numbers, or unauthorized attachments.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might post procedural documents or checklists without actually performing regular physical inspections. They may produce retrospective logs that are updated inaccurately.
• Superficial implementations include having a single periodic visual check record without correlating it to actual physical changes on devices.
• Technical gaps often arise when digital photographs or log entries are not time-stamped or are not securely stored, potentially allowing forgery or post-inspection alterations.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review the physical POI devices for tamper-evident seals or markers and compare them against the established baseline (e.g., historical photographs or catalog images).
   WHY: Baseline images and physical markers provide tangible evidence that the device has not been altered since its last approved state.
   HOW: Physically inspect a sample of devices, compare with archival images or markers, and verify that there are no signs of unexpected attachments, broken casing, or altered serial numbers.
   FAIL: Evidence of any unauthorized changes or mismatches between current and baseline images/markers indicates non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Analyze inspection logs and digital records (e.g., timestamped photographs, electronic checklists) that document periodic checks.
   WHY: Timestamped and securely stored records prove that inspections have not only been scheduled but also executed on time.
   HOW: Obtain a set of digital logs and verify that the timestamps are consistent with the inspection schedule; check for any gaps or retroactive alterations in the logs.
   FAIL: Missing timestamps, signs of retroactive log entries, or inconsistent intervals between inspections indicate control failure.

3. CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for the inspections and observe the inspection process in action.
   WHY: Direct observation and interviews verify that employees understand and consistently apply the inspection procedures.
   HOW: Conduct structured interviews to assess knowledge of inspection procedures, and request a demonstration or observe an unscheduled inspection to confirm practices align with documented procedures.
   FAIL: Inadequate responses, lack of familiarity with procedures, or observable divergence from documented practices will signal potential gaps in implementation.

4. CHECK: Validate that any discrepancies noted during inspections trigger a documented escalation and remediation process.
   WHY: A prompt response to detected issues is crucial to mitigate risks from tampering or unauthorized modifications.
   HOW: Ask for incident records or follow-up reports on any detected anomalies, verifying that corrective actions were implemented and documented.
   FAIL: Absence of any follow-up or recorded remediation efforts after detecting a discrepancy may suggest the control is not working effectively.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use historical data and trend analysis to quickly spot irregularities in the inspection schedules or repeated minor discrepancies that may indicate ongoing tampering attempts.
• Emphasize the importance of cross-verification: compare digital logs against physical evidence to check for consistency.
• Pay close attention to whether the digital artifacts (photos/logs) are protected from tampering. Signs of post-inspection modification or lack of integrity controls (e.g., secure timestamping) are critical indicators of weak implementation.
• Confirm that the inspection process includes random spot-checks; scheduled inspections alone can be more easily gamed.
• In your interviews, ask for specific examples of how deviations were handled to assess the operational robustness of the process.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.5.1.2 POI device surfaces are periodically inspected to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution.

Customized Approach Objective:
Point of interaction devices cannot be tampered with, substituted without authorization, or have skimming attachments installed without timely detection.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.5.1.2.a Examine documented procedures to verify processes are defined for periodic inspections of POI device surfaces to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution.
9.5.1.2.b Interview responsible personnel and observe inspection processes to verify:
- Personnel are aware of procedures for inspecting devices.
- All devices are periodically inspected for evidence of tampering and unauthorized substitution.

Guidance (from continuation on next page):
Examples and additional inspection methods include comparing current device appearance (e.g., via photographs or secure marker pen indications) to detect tampering, checking for unexpected attachments, missing labels, broken casing, or changes to serial numbers and other external markings.

title: The list and records of POI devices are kept up to date.
methodology:
Below is the detailed practical methodology for validating that the list and records of POI devices are kept up to date:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
   - Visible Evidence: Updated, current inventory list of POI devices, either in an automated device-management system or maintained as regularly updated electronic/paper records.
   - Technical Artifacts: System logs or change logs that record device additions, removals, location updates, and decommissions. Evidence of automated inventory pulls or reconciliation reports.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular reviews or reconciliations between physical device presence and documented records; periodic audits confirming updates coincide with actual events like device handovers or decommissioning.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
   - Faked Compliance: Organizations may produce a static list that rarely changes, even when devices are added or relocated, thus not reflecting real-time changes.
   - Surface-level Implementations: Relying only on policy documentation without operational verification; using outdated spreadsheets that are not integrated with actual device management processes.
   - Technical Gaps: Absence of change logs, incomplete documentation when devices are temporarily off-site, or records maintained only on paper and not cross-verified against digital records.

3. VALIDATION STEPS:
   CHECK: Verify that the inventory list of POI devices records key attributes (unique identifier, current location, assignment details) and is updated when changes occur.
   WHY: A properly maintained record confirms that device life-cycle changes (new addition, relocation, decommissioning) are tracked consistently.
   HOW: Interview staff responsible for maintaining records, review the latest version of the list, and cross-reference recent device management system logs for updates.
   FAIL: Records show outdated or missing entries, or discrepancies between physical inventory and documented records.

   CHECK: Confirm the existence and use of automated change logs or device-management system reports that log device status changes.
   WHY: Automated logs provide an auditable and less easily manipulated trail of updates, reducing the risk of manual error.
   HOW: Access system dashboards or logs to identify timestamps when devices were added, relocated, or decommissioned. Validate by matching a sample of log entries with known events.
   FAIL: Inconsistent logs or a lack of audit trails, which indicates that the list may not be reliably updated.

   CHECK: Reconcile a physical spot-check of POI devices with the inventory records (especially for mobile or 'on-the-road' devices assigned to personnel).
   WHY: A direct comparison between physical devices and record entries provides concrete proof of real-time accuracy and upkeep.
   HOW: Select a sample of facilities or personnel, then physically verify the presence and current location of POI devices against the documented list.
   FAIL: Any discrepancies between the physical check and the records point to implementation gaps or delays in updating the list.

   CHECK: Examine the process for updating records when a device's status changes (e.g., during decommissioning or relocation) and verify that procedures are followed consistently.
   WHY: A clear, standardized update process reduces errors and ensures that changes are captured promptly.
   HOW: Review documented procedures and validate with actual recent changes. Ask personnel for an explanation of their update processes and check if there’s evidence of training on this process.
   FAIL: Lack of standardized procedures or inconsistent follow-up after status changes.

4. EXPERT TIPS:
   - Shortcut: Always cross-check the digital logs with physical on-site device inventories, as automated systems are less likely to be manipulated compared with manually updated records.
   - Critical Indicator: Frequent reconciliation mismatches or lack of real-time system updates are strong signals of poor control over the inventory process.
   - Detail Matters: Ensure that the list not only captures static device attributes but also dynamic elements like current location and assigned personnel, particularly for on-the-road devices. Look for evidence that these details are maintained actively rather than as a one-time effort.
   - Additional Insight: Interviews with both the device management team and operational staff can reveal if there are delays in communication and update processes, which can be a subtle indicator of compliance issues.

This methodology emphasizes obtaining observable and auditable evidence rather than relying solely on documented policies, ensuring robust, operationally effective control validation.
content:
Customized Approach Objective:

9.5.1.c Interview personnel to verify the list of POI devices is updated when devices are added, relocated, decommissioned, etc.

Additional Guidance:
The identity and location of POI devices is recorded and known at all times.
Good Practice: The method for maintaining a list of devices may be automated (for example, a device-management system) or manual (for example, documented in electronic or paper records). For on-the-road devices, the location may include the name of the personnel to whom the device is assigned.
Examples: Methods to maintain device locations include identifying the address of the site or facility where the device is located.

title: An up-to-date list of POI devices is maintained.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: An easily accessible, centrally maintained inventory system (or spreadsheet/database) that includes all POI devices with details like make, model, serial number, and location. Look for audit trails or change logs indicating regular updates.
   • Technical Artifacts: System logs showing updates to the device list, automated inventory software reports, and backup copies of the device register. Evidence that the list is integrated with maintenance or asset management systems strengthens the compliance posture.
   • Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled inventory reconciliation meetings, visible signage of device locations that match listed inventory, and documented procedures used by technicians to update the device register when changes occur.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Superficial Compliance: Some organizations may submit a static, outdated list from a one-time exercise rather than a dynamic register. They might also include required elements but fail to update it after device moves or replacements.
   • Incomplete Implementations: Only providing a policy document that states the process exists without showing evidence of actual, up-to-date records. In some cases, the list might be maintained in non-secure, unversioned documents that are rarely updated.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing audit logs or metadata showing the timing of updates. Records that lack device unique identifiers (like serial numbers) or correct location details would signal a gap.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the Completeness of the POI Device List
      WHY: This shows that the list contains all devices with required attributes (make/model, location, unique ID).
      HOW: Obtain the current POI device list, review entries for completeness, and check that each entry includes make/model, device serial number (or other identifier), and location. Confirm that no devices are missing based on facility walkthroughs.
      FAIL: If any POI device lacks essential details or if known devices are not included in the list.

   CHECK: Examine Update Mechanisms and Audit Trails
      WHY: Evidence of active maintenance (timestamps, personnel changes, version control) confirms the list is kept current.
      HOW: Review system logs or change history associated with the POI device list. Interview asset management personnel to ensure regular updates and reconciliation events, and check if there are automated notifications for inventory discrepancies.
      FAIL: If there are no timestamps, change records, or if update procedures appear to be one-off rather than routine.

   CHECK: Conduct a Physical Verification Walk-through
      WHY: Observing POI devices in their stated locations helps validate that the list accurately reflects actual installations.
      HOW: Randomly select a sample of devices from the list and physically verify their location, make/model, and serial numbers. Compare these observations with the list entries.
      FAIL: If any sampled device is missing, in a different location, or has mismatched recorded details.

   CHECK: Validate Integration with Inventory Management Systems (If Applicable)
      WHY: An integrated system often reduces human error and maintains consistency across updates.
      HOW: Identify if the POI device list is part of a broader inventory management system. Check the configuration, synchronization schedules, and controls in place to automatically update the list from deployment records.
      FAIL: If the list appears isolated or there is evidence of manual updates only, increasing the risk of outdated information.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Use asset management tools or CMDB queries to generate reports and cross-check the POI device list automatically versus physical or network scan data.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay attention to the regularity of update timestamps and supporting audit logs. Inconsistent or absent metadata is a strong indicator of poor ongoing compliance.
   • Detail Focus: Ensure the list includes detailed unique identifiers such as serial numbers, which are less likely to be fabricated. Also review maintenance records to confirm periodic reconciliation of physical locations with documented entries.
   • Interviewing Personnel: Brief discussions with the IT asset manager or team members responsible for updating the list can quickly reveal if proper controls and procedures are in place or if the list is tick-box documentation.
