By following these practical steps, you can validate that an up-to-date list of POI devices is actively maintained, is accurate, and is integrated with routine operational practices, thus ensuring operational compliance with req-9.5.1.1.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.5.1.1 An up-to-date list of POI devices is maintained, including:
• Make and model of the device.
• Location of device.
• Device serial number or other methods of unique identification.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.5.1.1.a Examine the list of POI devices to verify it includes all elements specified in this requirement.
9.5.1.1.b Observe POI devices and device locations and compare to devices in the list to verify that the list is accurate and up to date.

Purpose:
Keeping an up-to-date list of POI devices helps an organization track where devices are supposed to be and quickly identify if a device is missing or lost.

title: POI devices that capture payment card data are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible and up-to-date inventories of POI devices that detail device serial numbers, models, locations, and responsible personnel.
• Photographic evidence or physical labels on POI devices indicating tamper-evident seals and inspection stamps.
• Maintenance logs and inspection reports showing periodic physical checks for tampering or unauthorized substitution.
• Training records demonstrating that staff responsible for POI device management have received targeted security awareness and tampering detection training.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may maintain a static list of POI devices that is not regularly updated as devices are added, moved, or decommissioned.
• Falsely documented inspection logs without supporting evidence (e.g., photos or signed inspection reports) can give the appearance of compliance.
• Inadequate training records or cursory on-the-job instructions that aren’t formalized may be used to fake compliance.
• Relying solely on system-based controls (e.g., automated alerts) without physical verification of tamper evidence.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the Inventory List of POI Devices
WHY: A current, complete inventory proves that the organization tracks all payment-capturing devices, enabling accountability and supporting physical inspections.
HOW: Request the latest POI inventory and cross-reference with the physical site. Check for device serial numbers, models, and assigned locations.
FAIL: Missing devices, outdated records, or discrepancies between the physical environment and the documented list indicate non-compliance.

CHECK: Review Physical Inspection Reports and Photographic Evidence
WHY: Regular and documented physical inspections ensure that devices are frequently checked for tampering or substitution.
HOW: Examine a sample of recent inspection reports including dates, inspector signatures, and any photographic evidence capturing tamper-evident seals or anomalies.
FAIL: Lack of supporting photos, missing inspection logs for recent periods, or vague report narratives suggest superficial checks.

CHECK: Interview and Verify Training Records for Personnel
WHY: Well-trained personnel are critical to recognizing and acting on signs of tampering, reducing the risk of unnoticed substitution or modification.
HOW: Check training records and conduct interviews with staff responsible for device inspections to confirm specific tampering detection training.
FAIL: Inadequate training programs, missing training records, or staff unaware of tampering indicators point to a weak control implementation.

CHECK: Observe Physical Devices in-situ
WHY: Direct on-site observation provides tangible evidence of tamper-evident measures and proper physical security of POI devices.
HOW: Physically inspect a sample of POI devices to verify that they display tamper-evident seals, physical safeguards, and are not substituted with unauthorized devices.
FAIL: Devices lacking proper seals, or signs of tampering (or reapplication of tamper-evident labels without inspection oversight) indicate control failure.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Look for irregularities in tamper-evident seals or mismatched serial numbers between the inventory and the actual device; these are red flags.
• Confirm that inspection schedules are frequently reevaluated and that deviations are documented and addressed.
• Do not rely solely on documentation—physical and operational evidence is critical in verifying that the control is effectively applied.
• Use a combination of automated records (e.g., updated inventory systems) and manual physical checks to gain a full picture.
• Pay attention to environmental controls around POI devices; secure physical placement can further reduce substitution risks.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.5.1 POI devices that capture payment card data via direct physical interaction with the payment card form factor are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution, including the following:
• Maintaining a list of POI devices.
• Periodically inspecting POI devices to look for tampering or unauthorized substitution.
• Training personnel to be aware of suspicious behavior and to report tampering or unauthorized substitution of devices.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.5.1 Examine documented policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined that include all elements specified in this requirement.

title: Electronic media with cardholder data are destroyed when no longer needed.
methodology:
Below is a complete validation methodology for verifying that electronic media containing cardholder data is destroyed when no longer needed (PCI DSS req-9.4.7):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible traces of a media destruction process such as scheduled destruction sessions, physical destruction tools (e.g., shredders, degaussers), or dedicated secure wiping software running on devices.
   • Operational logs or system audit trails that record secure deletion or destruction events; for physical media, records of chain-of-custody and certificates of destruction.
   • Interviews and direct observation where personnel can explain and demonstrate the actual destruction process.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Relying on generic file deletion (e.g., using the operating system’s delete function) which does not actually remove data.
   • Documenting a media destruction policy without showing evidence of operational enforcement or verifiable destruction logs.
   • Using ad hoc or manual deletion methods that do not meet industry-accepted standards, and then later producing digitally altered records that claim compliance.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review the media destruction policy and supporting records.
   WHY: A documented policy confirms that the organization acknowledges the need for secure destruction, but must be supported by evidence of execution.
   HOW: Request and review documented policies and recorded chain-of-custody logs, destruction certificates, or digital logs that include timestamps, media identifiers, and destruction methods used.
   FAIL: Absence of destruction certificates, incomplete logs, or generic ‘deletion’ records with no technical details.

   CHECK: Observe the media destruction process or obtain demonstrable evidence of secure deletion operations.
   WHY: Direct observation or recorded evidence (e.g., video or process logs) ensures the process is active and per the defined standards.
   HOW: Schedule a walkthrough of the destruction process, interview responsible staff, and review machine logs or secure deletion software outputs verifying that the method (e.g., secure wiping, degaussing, physical destruction) renders cardholder data unrecoverable.
   FAIL: If employees cannot convincingly demonstrate the process, or if only policy documents are available without any operational evidence.

   CHECK: Validate technical artifacts such as system-generated logs from secure deletion software or audit trails from degaussing/physical destruction operations.
   WHY: Automated logs are less likely to be fabricated and confirm real-time operational effectiveness.
   HOW: Request and review automated audit logs that record deletion commands, the specific algorithms used (e.g., DoD standard wipes), and confirmations that data cannot be recovered. Cross-check with inventory records to ensure all media have been processed per policy.
   FAIL: If logs are missing, contain gaps, or only document deletion commands without confirming the irrecoverability of the data.

   CHECK: Cross-reference media inventory with destruction records.
   WHY: Ensuring that every piece of media previously holding cardholder data has a corresponding destruction record reduces the risk of orphan devices.
   HOW: Obtain an inventory of electronic media (such as drives, tapes, or SSDs) and verify that each item has an associated destruction certificate or log entry showing that it was destroyed or rendered unrecoverable.
   FAIL: If there are any media entries lacking matched destruction records or if discrepancies exist between the inventory and destruction logs.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus on automated logs and chain-of-custody documents rather than relying solely on policy documents – these artifacts are more difficult to fake.
   • Critical Indicator: Pay close attention to any records that simply state ‘deleted’ without technical confirmation that the data has been irrecoverable. Ensure the use of industry-accepted destruction methods.
   • Detail Matter: Validate that destruction processes are not only documented but are periodically and independently reviewed; review dated logs to ensure the process is current.
   • Additional Note: While physical observation is ideal, if remote auditing is performed, request detailed video walkthroughs or screenshots of the secure deletion processes.

This methodology focuses on verifying actual operational implementation of electronic media destruction, ensuring that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed after media disposal.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.4.7 Electronic media with cardholder data is destroyed when no longer needed for business or legal reasons via one of the following:
- The electronic media is destroyed.
- The cardholder data is rendered unrecoverable so that it cannot be reconstructed.

Customized Approach Objective:
Cardholder data cannot be recovered from media that has been erased or destroyed.

Applicability Notes:
These requirements for media destruction when that media is no longer needed for business or legal reasons are separate and distinct from PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1, which is for securely deleting cardholder data when no longer needed per the entity's cardholder data retention policies.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.4.7.a Examine the media destruction policy to verify that procedures are defined to destroy electronic media when no longer needed for business or legal reasons in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.
9.4.7.b Observe the media destruction process and interview responsible personnel to verify that electronic media with cardholder data is destroyed via one of the methods specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
If steps are not taken to destroy information contained on electronic media when no longer needed, malicious individuals may retrieve information from the disposed media, leading to a data compromise.

Good Practice:
The deletion function in most operating systems allows deleted data to be recovered, so instead, a dedicated secure deletion function or application should be used to make data unrecoverable.

Examples:
Methods for securely destroying electronic media include secure wiping in accordance with industry-accepted standards for secure deletion, degaussing, or physical destruction (such as grinding or shredding hard disks).

title: Hard-copy materials with cardholder data are destroyed when no longer needed.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that physical areas contain designated, locked 'to-be-shredded' containers for hard-copy media with cardholder data.
  WHY: The existence of secure storage containers shows that the organization segregates materials pending destruction, which is a prerequisite for controlled disposal.
  HOW: Visit the facility and directly inspect the storage area for locked containers; review vendor contracts for secure storage if outsourced.
  FAIL: Absence of secure storage containers or use of unsecured drop-off points indicates non-compliance.

• CHECK: Confirm that destruction devices (e.g., cross-cut shredders, incinerators) are operational and documented.
  WHY: Operational shredding or incineration with the appropriate controls ensures that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed.
  HOW: Observe a live destruction process or request demonstration logs, inspect machine maintenance records, and review receipts/certificates from third-party destruction vendors.
  FAIL: Outdated equipment, lack of maintenance logs, or evidence of non cross-cut methods suggest the control is not effectively implemented.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may have a documented media destruction policy but fail to enforce secure storage or proper destruction.
• Falsification of logs or demonstration of a mock process without real-time evidence can superficially satisfy auditors.
• Some implement temporary holding areas that are not actually secure, thereby exposing cardholder data before destruction.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Inspect the physical environment to verify that hard-copy materials containing cardholder data are stored in secure, lockable containers designated for destruction.
   WHY: Direct evidence of secure storage and segregation prevents unauthorized access to cardholder data.
   HOW: Conduct a physical walk-through of the storage area, and check that access is controlled (e.g., through locks or restricted entry).
   FAIL: Unsecured storage containers or access-controlled areas without a lock indicate a potential risk.

2. CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for media destruction to confirm that they follow procedures for cross-cut shredding, incineration, or pulping.
   WHY: Staff testimony and process observation validate that the documented procedures are actively enforced.
   HOW: Request a demonstration of the destruction process if possible, and ask specific questions regarding frequency, method, and record-keeping of destruction events.
   FAIL: Inconsistent responses or lack of familiarity with the procedures suggest lax enforcement.

3. CHECK: Review and trace documented logs and certificates of media destruction (internal records or third-party vendor reports).
   WHY: Verified records demonstrate that destruction is auditable and traceable to prevent the recovery of cardholder data.
   HOW: Obtain and analyze media destruction logs, including dates, destruction methods, and personnel involved, ensuring they match observed practices.
   FAIL: Missing, incomplete, or outdated logs indicate that the destruction process might not be reliably implemented.

4. CHECK: Verify that the destruction methods employed (e.g., cross-cut shredder settings, incineration conditions) meet industry standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-88 Guidelines).
   WHY: The effectiveness of the destruction method directly impacts the risk of data reconstruction from remnants.
   HOW: Compare the technical specifications of the equipment or service provider's procedures with industry standards and guidelines; review any certifications or audit reports regarding these mechanisms.
   FAIL: Use of equipment or practices that do not meet the prescribed standards (e.g., strip-cut shredding instead of cross-cut) is a red flag.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Prioritize on-site verification over purely paper-based documentation. Look for real-time evidence of both secure storage and destruction methods.
• Check for any discrepancies between documented procedures and visible operational behavior; if destruction logs are inconsistent or incomplete, it is a strong indicator of ineffective implementation.
• Review maintenance records for destruction devices to ensure reliable operation; a well-maintained device is critical for assuring that destruction methods achieve non-reconstructable results.
• If outsourcing, confirm that service level agreements (SLAs) and destruction certificates are current and align with the organization’s internal policies.
• Focus on the physical integrity and security of the environment before destruction, as any lapses in storage could compromise data even if the destruction method is robust.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.4.6 Hard-copy materials with cardholder data are destroyed when no longer needed for business or legal reasons, as follows:
- Materials are cross-cut shredded, incinerated, or pulped so that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed.
- Materials are stored in secure storage containers prior to destruction.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.4.6.a Examine the media destruction policy to verify that procedures are defined to destroy hard-copy media with cardholder data when no longer needed for business or legal reasons in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.
9.4.6.b Observe processes and interview personnel to verify that hard-copy materials are cross-cut shredded, incinerated, or pulped such that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed.
9.4.6.c Observe storage containers used for materials that contain information to be destroyed to verify that the containers are secure.

Customized Approach Objective:
Cardholder data cannot be recovered from media that has been destroyed or which is pending destruction.

Applicability Notes:
These requirements for media destruction when that media is no longer needed for business or legal reasons are separate and distinct from PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1, which is for securely deleting cardholder data when no longer needed per the entity's cardholder data retention policies.

Purpose:
If steps are not taken to destroy information contained on hard-copy media before disposal, malicious individuals may retrieve information from the disposed media, leading to a data compromise.

Good Practice:
Consider "to-be-shredded" containers with a lock that prevents access to its contents or that physically prevent access to the inside of the container.

Further Information:
See NIST Special Publication 800-88, Revision 1: Guidelines for Media Sanitization.

title: Electronic media inventories are performed at least once every 12 months.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for regularly dated electronic media inventory logs stored in a secure system or physical binder. The records should show periodic reviews (every 12 months) and include details like media identifiers, locations, and custody details.
   • Technical Artifacts: System-generated audit logs, ticketing system entries or automated reports showing the inventory completion dates. Active tracking systems or databases that flag assets containing cardholder data can also be used for compliance evidence.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular, timetabled updates to the inventory, coupled with management reviews and corrective action entries when discrepancies are found, are practical signs that the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might simply reuse the previous year’s inventory documents without verifying the current state, or they may have logs generated manually without audit trails, making it easier to backdate activities.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Some companies may have inventory checklists that are filled out as a formality, without cross-referencing against actual, live assets. Merely having procedure documents or policies without routine operational monitoring is a common pitfall.
   • Technical Gaps: Lack of integration between the inventory system and incident management systems can help mask discrepancies or missing updates. Logs might be incomplete or altered if proper access controls aren’t present.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of electronic media inventory logs with timestamps showing a review at least once every 12 months.
   WHY: Consistent timestamped logs indicate that inventories are conducted periodically and are backed by operational procedures.
   HOW: Review sample inventory logs for a minimum of 12 months, ensuring that the dates align with the annual cycle. Look for signatures or electronic audit trails that corroborate these entries.
   FAIL: Inventory logs missing for any period of 12 months or with backdated entries lacking verification evidence indicate non-compliance.

   CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for performing the electronic media inventories.
   WHY: Direct interviews can confirm that actual operations are taking place, beyond what the documentation indicates.
   HOW: Ask staff to describe the inventory process, the frequency of updates, and how discrepancies are handled. Request evidence that inventory rounds were planned and executed as per the documented procedure.
   FAIL: Inability of staff to provide clear and recent examples of completed inventories or details on corrective actions points to a likely control failure.

   CHECK: Validate technical logs from any automated inventory systems or asset tracking tools.
   WHY: Automated systems reduce the chances of manual error or intentional bypass; consistent logs provide objective evidence of periodic inventory verifications.
   HOW: Access system-generated reports or audit trails in the inventory management system. Look for entries that consistently occur within each 12-month period and confirm that they include analyses of media containing cardholder data.
   FAIL: Gaps or irregularities in the automated logs, or evidence of log manipulation, indicate that the control is not being properly enforced.

   CHECK: Cross-reference inventory records with asset management data to ensure all electronic media are accounted for.
   WHY: This independent check reinforces the accuracy of the inventory logs and demonstrates a higher level of operational rigor.
   HOW: Compare the list of electronic media from the inventory logs with asset registers or other related databases. Identify any discrepancies or missing items and check for documented resolution actions.
   FAIL: A high number of unaccounted-for media or unresolved discrepancies between systems is a strong indicator of control weakness.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Focus on the timeliness and consistency of automated log entries. A reliable system will have immutable audit trails that can’t be easily faked.
   • Critical Indicators: Look for a complete audit trail including updates, review approvals, and corrective actions for any discrepancies. Mismatches or inconsistent data points are red flags.
   • Key Details: Ensure that logs include not only dates but also responsible personnel identifiers and a clear audit path. Incomplete logs or those lacking a chain of custody signal potential implementation issues.
   • Best Practice Check: If possible, simulate a review cycle by asking for a recent run-through of the process, ensuring control adherence in both planned inventory cycles and ad hoc reviews.

By validating these points, you ensure that the electronic media inventory control is not just documentarily compliant, but operationally effective.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.4.5.1 Inventories of electronic media with cardholder data are conducted at least once every 12 months.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.4.5.1.a Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined to conduct inventories of electronic media with cardholder data at least once every 12 months.
9.4.5.1.b Examine electronic media inventory logs and interview personnel to verify that electronic media inventories are performed at least once every 12 months.

Customized Approach Objective:
Media inventories are verified periodically.

Guidance - Purpose:
Without careful inventory methods and storage controls, stolen or missing electronic media could go unnoticed for an indefinite amount of time.

title: Inventory logs of all electronic media with cardholder data are maintained.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-9.4.5: Inventory logs of all electronic media with cardholder data are maintained.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • There are visible, regularly updated inventory logs (electronic or printed) listing all electronic media (hard drives, USB sticks, tapes, etc.) that store cardholder data.
   • Technical artifacts include database records, asset management systems, or centralized logging systems which cross-reference electronic media assets with cardholder data.
   • Operationally, staff responsible for media management can demonstrate scheduled reviews and reconciliations of inventories, and there are changes tracked over time.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes simply produce a static document or outdated spreadsheets that list assets without evidence of updates or process controls.
   • Failing to keep an integrated inventory that’s automatically updated can allow discrepancies. For example, relying only on paper logs or periodic manual updates which might not reflect current state.
   • Technical implementations without proper cross-checks (e.g., asset tracking without validation from system logs) can create gaps where new media with cardholder data are not captured.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Existence and Update Frequency of Electronic Media Inventory Logs
   WHY: A current, well-maintained log clearly demonstrates that inventories are actively managed.
   HOW: Review the latest inventory log (electronic or paper format) and verify its date; cross-reference it with internal change logs to ensure it’s updated regularly.
   FAIL: Logs that are outdated, incomplete, or missing date stamps indicate non-compliance.
   
   CHECK: Correlation Between Inventory Logs and Media in Use
   WHY: Matching inventory records against actual media ensures that stored media with cardholder data are thoroughly tracked.
   HOW: Select at least three media items from the inventory log, then physically or virtually verify their existence and contents on the system. Use asset management tools or scripts to list currently detected devices and compare with the documented inventory.
   FAIL: Significant discrepancies between physical assets found and the log signal ineffective inventory management.
   
   CHECK: Interview and Operational Process Verification
   WHY: Direct confirmation that responsible personnel understand and follow inventory procedures indicates live operational enforcement.
   HOW: Interview personnel responsible for maintaining the inventory. Ask for a quick demo of how a new media device is logged. Check if there are structured procedures integrated into IT operational workflows and training records supporting this process.
   FAIL: Inability of personnel to explain or demonstrate the process is indicative of superficial or non-operational procedures.
   
   CHECK: Automated Evidence of Log Maintenance
   WHY: Automated processes are less likely to be subject to human error or non-compliance faking.
   HOW: Identify any scripts, scheduled tasks, or asset management software that automatically logs new electronic media. Validate configuration files and execution logs to confirm that automated collection is active and complete.
   FAIL: Absence of automation, outdated scripts, or irregular scheduling of automated tasks indicates potential manual process inefficiencies.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Ask for a recent audit report (internal or external) that discusses electronic media inventories; discrepancies or repeated findings indicate poor implementation.
   • Critical Indicators: Look for irregularities such as devices labeled as retired or replaced, yet still found in logs, or vice versa. Look for timely updates in the audit trail.
   • Detail to Note: The link between inventory management systems and change management processes is crucial – media that have been newly installed, decommissioned, or repurposed must trigger automatic inventory updates. Missing these details often point to manual or incomplete controls.

This methodology emphasizes direct, current, and automated evidence over static, easily fabricated documentation. It focuses on ensuring that the processes are integrated into live operational environments, which is the core of demonstrating true compliance with req-9.4.5.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.4.5 Inventory logs of all electronic media with cardholder data are maintained.

Customized Approach Objective:
Accurate inventories of stored electronic media are maintained.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.4.5.a Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined to maintain electronic media inventory logs.
9.4.5.b Examine electronic media inventory logs and interview responsible personnel to verify that logs are maintained.

title: Management approval is required for moving media with cardholder data outside the facility.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes physical and electronic logs tracking media movement events. Look for signed management approval forms, authorization emails, or digital signatures that clearly identify accountable personnel.
• Technical artifacts that demonstrate compliance are offsite media tracking logs with timestamps, personnel identifiers, and descriptions of the media moved out, as well as audit trails from information asset management systems.
• Operational patterns include consistent recorded approvals for each instance of media leaving the facility, using a documented chain of custody that records pre-move risk assessments and post-move receipting.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simulate compliance by using generic or batch email approvals that don’t verify individual media movements.
• A common superficial implementation is maintaining a policy document stating management approval is needed without an operationalized process tied to actual media movement events.
• Frequently missed technical gaps include lack of unique identifiers on media movement records, incomplete approval logs, or absence of follow-up audits on offsite media events.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Review offsite media tracking logs and verify the presence of management approval entries (with names, timestamps, and specific media details).
WHY: This confirms that every instance of media moving outside the facility is coupled with a recorded, accountable management approval.
HOW: Request a sample list of media moved offsite, review corresponding log entries, and match them with management approval evidence (electronic or paper-based). Automated systems may also provide logs with audit trails.
FAIL: If records show missing approval fields, generic batch approvals, or if specific instances lack individual attributed approvals, the control is not properly implemented.

CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for media handling and verify they understand and follow the proper procedures for obtaining management approval.
WHY: Direct interviews help confirm the operational enforcement of policies rather than mere documentation. They also reveal if employees use workarounds that undermine the control.
HOW: Conduct structured interviews with a random selection of staff who manage sensitive media, and ask them to demonstrate the approval process with recent examples.
FAIL: If staff are unable to recall or demonstrate specific approval steps, or if they report informal or ad hoc practices, this indicates a potential gap in enforcement.

CHECK: Conduct a walkthrough of the process for moving media, from request to approval to transfer, including reviewing any automated IT alerts or software logs used to trigger management approval.
WHY: Observing the full process in action ensures the control is integrated into daily operations and not just theoretical or documented without practical enforcement.
HOW: Arrange and witness the process during a routine media move, or simulate a request and trace it through the system, ensuring all steps are recorded with proper management authorization.
FAIL: Inconsistencies in the process flow, manually overridden steps, or missing triggers for management approval represent gaps in control.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Cross-reference approval logs with physical media inventories to detect any discrepancies. A missing log for a recorded physical item is a red flag.
• Look for evidence of periodic review or reconciliation by internal audit teams, which can help catch anomalies in approval processes.
• Verify that approval records include sufficient detail (e.g., media description, destination, purpose) as scant records might indicate a superficial compliance effort.
• Pay attention to any indications that the media has been moved without a documented approval; this is a critical indicator of control breakdown.
• Ensure that approvals are granted by persons with true management authority — check job roles or organizational charts if available during interviews.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:

9.4.4 Management approves all media with cardholder data that is moved outside the facility (including when media is distributed to individuals).

Customized Approach Objective:
Media cannot leave a facility without the approval of accountable personnel.

Applicability Notes:
Individuals approving media movements should have the appropriate level of management authority to grant this approval. However, it is not specifically required that such individuals have "manager" as part of their title.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
9.4.4.a Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined to ensure that media moved outside the facility is approved by management.
9.4.4.b Examine offsite media tracking logs and interview responsible personnel to verify that proper management authorization is obtained for all media moved outside the facility (including media distributed to individuals).

title: Media with cardholder data sent outside the facility is secured and tracked.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for physical or electronic shipping logs, including courier receipts and tracking screenshots that clearly indicate when media containing cardholder data was shipped from the facility. Check for secure evidence such as scanned shipping documents, signed delivery confirmations, or digital logs from a courier service’s tracking system.
• Technical Artifacts: Evidence includes export logs from secure courier APIs, offsite tracking logs with detailed metadata (shipment dates, tracking numbers, destination addresses, and personnel responsible), and system audit logs that automatically record media shipments. Look for integration between the facility’s shipment process and third-party tracking systems.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly updated offsite logs, periodic cross-validation between internal shipping logs and courier records, and consistent personnel interviews confirming adherence to a defined, repeatable process for securing and tracking media leaving the facility.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might rely solely on paper-based logs that are manually created and not regularly audited. They could use non-trackable shipping methods and simply “log” the action after the fact without any integration or reconciliation with actual courier data.
• Superficial Implementations: Some may have policies or procedures documented without consistent operational evidence. For example, they might only have an offsite log template filled with placeholder information rather than actual shipment details, or they may use couriers with no valid tracking system.
• Technical Gaps: Missing automated log generation from courier tracking systems and absence of a centralized repository that correlates shipment reports with internal requests can indicate weakness in real tracking.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify shipment logs for media sent outside the facility
WHY: Automated logs from the shipment system are less prone to human error and manipulation, ensuring the media is tracked accurately.
HOW: Request and review digital or electronic logs showing dates, tracking IDs, destination details, and courier service information. Cross-reference these logs with courier receipts or data from the courier’s API.
FAIL: Absence of digital logs or reliance solely on handwritten logs which do not correlate with external data from couriers.

CHECK: Confirm use of secure courier services
WHY: The use of a courier with a robust tracking system directly correlates with the requirement to secure media.
HOW: Interview shipping personnel and inspect evidence such as service contracts with secure couriers, receipts, and tracking numbers that indicate media movement outside the facility.
FAIL: Evidence that standard postal services or non-trackable methods are used, or documented tracking numbers do not match what the courier system provides.

CHECK: Validate offsite tracking logs include full shipment details
WHY: Detailed tracking logs ensure that if media is lost or misdirected, its location and movement history can be audited.
HOW: Examine offsite tracking records for completeness including shipment dates, detailed content description, responsible personnel, and current location statuses. Check for consistency with internal records.
FAIL: Offsite logs missing critical details (e.g., tracking numbers, shipment date, or destination) or containing generic placeholders rather than specific data.

CHECK: Cross-check physical evidence with operational records
WHY: Matching physical documentation (e.g., courier receipts) with recorded electronic logs confirms the integrity and consistency of the tracking process.
HOW: Select a sample of media shipments, then validate consistency between physical courier receipts, email confirmations, and electronic logs. Confirm that the shipment events are synchronously recorded in both internal and external systems.
FAIL: Significant discrepancies between physical documentation and internal electronic records, or records that appear retroactively filled in with no supporting evidence.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on samples where high-risk cardholder data is involved. Automated logs and system integrations are harder to fake compared to manual logs, so prioritize evidence from electronic systems.
• Critical Indicator: Inconsistencies between the internal shipping log and courier tracking data are a strong sign of poor implementation. Make sure details such as tracking numbers and timestamps match.
• Detail to Note: Confirm that offsite tracking logs consistently include all required details (shipment date, location, person responsible, and tracking number) – lacking any one of these elements can undermine the control.
• Practical Check: When possible, integrate log analysis tools that flag discrepancies or missing data from the shipment process, ensuring real-time compliance monitoring.

This methodology ensures that the media containing cardholder data sent outside the facility is not only logged but secured via a trusted, verifiable process that maintains a comprehensive audit trail.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

**9.4.3** Media with cardholder data sent outside the facility is secured as follows:
- Media sent outside the facility is logged.
- Media is sent by secured courier or other delivery method that can be accurately tracked.
- Offsite tracking logs include details about media location.

### Customized Approach Objective

Media is secured and tracked when transported outside the facility.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**9.4.3.a** Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined for securing media sent outside the facility in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

**9.4.3.b** Interview personnel and examine records to verify that all media sent outside the facility is logged and sent via secured courier or other delivery method that can be tracked.

**9.4.3.c** Examine offsite tracking logs for all media to verify tracking details are documented.

### Guidance
Purpose: Media may be lost or stolen if sent via a non-trackable method such as regular postal mail. The use of secure couriers to deliver any media that contains cardholder data allows organizations to use their tracking systems to maintain inventory and location of shipments.

title: Media with cardholder data is classified according to its sensitivity.
methodology:
Below is the expert methodology for validating that media with cardholder data is classified according to its sensitivity:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
   • Visible evidence includes official classification labels or markings on physical media (e.g., tapes, disks) and visible data in asset registers that indicate the sensitivity level.
   • Technical artifacts include automated tagging in inventory systems, digital asset management records, and document management system metadata that enforces classification.
   • Operational patterns include routine scans or audits where sensitivity classification is verified, and employees following clear labeling and handling procedures consistently.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
   • Organizations might simply document classification policies without applying them consistently. For example, they may have a policy document but lack evidence of actual tagging or handling processes in practice.
   • A surface-level implementation might involve generic labels (e.g., simply 'Confidential') without clear sensitivity tiers defined relative to cardholder data specifics.
   • Critical technical gaps include media that is re-purposed or archived without updated classification, or systems where classification metadata isn’t maintained or regularly reviewed.

3. VALIDATION STEPS:
   CHECK: Review documented procedures for media classification and verify they align with the sensitivity of cardholder data.
   WHY: This demonstrates that the organization has defined a process that should be followed for classification based on sensitivity.
   HOW: Inspect internal policy documents, classification matrices, and training materials provided to staff, then verify that the process covers both new and legacy media.
   FAIL: Absence of a detailed classification matrix or procedures that address the sensitivity nuances of cardholder data.

   CHECK: Examine physical media and electronic asset registers for evidence of sensitivity classification labels or markings.
   WHY: Visible physical or digital markings provide concrete evidence that classification is actively applied in practice.
   HOW: Select a sample of media items from storage logs or asset inventories and check for clear sensitivity classification labels. Confirm that these labels match the defined classification scheme.
   FAIL: If media items either lack classification labels or have inconsistent or generic labels that do not reflect sensitivity levels.

   CHECK: Inspect media logs, storage records, and change records to ensure that reclassification and updates are documented appropriately.
   WHY: Logs provide an audit trail that shows ongoing control and enforcement of the classification process over time.
   HOW: Use automated queries within the asset management system to review logs for changes in classification during media lifecycle events. Check if periodic reviews are documented.
   FAIL: Absence of dynamic logging, outdated records, or evidence that classification was not updated when media was re-purposed or archived.

   CHECK: Conduct interviews or observe operational procedures with personnel responsible for handling sensitive media to validate that classification is actively used.
   WHY: Hands-on processes and awareness among workers is a strong indicator that policy translates into practice rather than being merely documented.
   HOW: Ask operators to explain how they classify media, what labeling processes they follow, and request a demonstration of how classification metadata is updated in the system.
   FAIL: Inability of staff to explain classification process or demonstrate its practical application, indicating potential gaps between policy and real execution.

4. EXPERT TIPS:
   • Look for consistency between the documented classifications in policies and the actual markings on physical or digital media. A mismatch highlights superficial compliance.
   • Prioritize evidence from operational logs and live demonstrations over static documentation—they are much harder to fake.
   • Technical indicators such as automated classification tagging within asset management or data discovery tools are strong signals that the process is automated and unlikely to be manually manipulated.
   • Sample a diverse range of media (in-house servers, backups, portable drives) to ensure the control is uniformly applied.
   • Critical detail: The sensitivity classification must not only be clear but also integrate with subsequent protective measures (like encryption or access controls), reinforcing the overall security framework.

This combined practical and technical approach will provide a robust assessment that the control for classification of media with cardholder data is not only on paper but actively enforced in day-to-day operations.

## Defined Approach Requirements

9.4.2 All media with cardholder data is classified in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

9.4.2.a Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined for classifying media with cardholder data in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.

9.4.2.b Examine media logs or other documentation to verify that all media is classified in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.

## Customized Approach Objective

Media are classified and protected appropriately.

title: The security of offline media backup locations is reviewed at least annually.
methodology:
Below is the expert methodology for validating control req-9.4.1.2, which ensures that the security of offline media backup locations is reviewed at least annually.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes dated review logs, meeting minutes, or site visit reports that clearly document a review of offline backup locations at least once every 12 months.
   • Technical artifacts such as system-generated audit logs, ticketing records for physical access audits, and secure storage inspection reports serve as proof of periodic evaluation.
   • Operational patterns include recurring calendar entries or workflow tasks scheduled on an annual basis that trigger review of the backup location’s physical and environmental controls.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply recycle old documentation with outdated dates to suggest compliance without a genuine, current review process.
   • Fake compliance can occur when the documented procedure exists on paper but field interviews or system logs indicate that reviews are not diligently followed.
   • Superficial implementations often involve check-box exercises where review records are filled in without the critical, actual physical inspection or verification of environmental/physical security controls.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Obtain and review the documented procedure for annual security reviews of offline media backup locations.
   WHY: This verifies that formal guidelines exist and that an annual review cycle is mandated.
   HOW: Look for clear procedures in the organization’s internal security policy repository, including a schedule, scope, and responsible personnel; then check if the procedure references the backup locations with cardholder data.
   FAIL: Absence of a documented procedure, outdated procedures, or procedures that do not explicitly require review at least every 12 months.

   CHECK: Inspect logs, meeting minutes, or inspection reports that evidence an annual security review of the offline media backup locations.
   WHY: These artifacts demonstrate that reviews were actually conducted and not just documented for compliance.
   HOW: Request and review inspection reports or meeting records from physical site visits, verifying dates and signatures. Cross-check these against the annual schedule.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete records, mismatched dates, or lack of verifiable review evidence.

   CHECK: Interview key personnel responsible for the offline backup storage locations and security reviews.
   WHY: Verbal confirmation and detailed responses provide insight into the operational effectiveness of the review process.
   HOW: Prepare targeted questions regarding the timing of the last review, the criteria used during the review, and any findings or remediation actions taken. Compare responses with available documentation.
   FAIL: Inconsistent answers, inability to recall recent reviews, or lack of understanding of the review process.

   CHECK: Correlate the scheduling of reviews with the calendar and system reminders or automated workflows.
   WHY: Ensures that the process isn’t ad hoc but is systematically enforced.
   HOW: Check system configurations (for example, security management tools) that set reminders for annual reviews and verify if the alerts and incident follow-up reports align with the control's timeline.
   FAIL: Absence of such automated reminders or workflows, indicating a high risk for oversight.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • A quick verification shortcut is to verify digital signatures or authenticated timestamps on review documents – these are hard to fake and provide a reliable audit trail.
   • Critical details include the specificity of the backup location(s) covered by the review; a generic checklist that does not mention physical security aspects may be an indicator of a superficial review.
   • Pay attention to the depth of discussion during personnel interviews – vagueness or inability to point to specific evidence typically indicates poor operational enforcement.
   • Ensure that the review covers both physical security (e.g., controlled access, environmental hazards) and procedural elements (e.g., logging, incident management).

By following the above validation methodology, you can effectively verify that the control is not only documented on paper but also actively and effectively implemented in practice.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

9.4.1.2 The security of the offline media backup location(s) with cardholder data is reviewed at least once every 12 months.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

9.4.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined for reviewing the security of the offline media backup location(s) with cardholder data at least once every 12 months.

9.4.1.2.b Examine documented procedures, logs, or other documentation, and interview responsible personnel at the storage location(s) to verify that the storage location’s security is reviewed at least once every 12 months.

## Customized Approach Objective

The security controls protecting offline backups are verified periodically by inspection.

title: Offline media backups with cardholder data are stored in a secure location.
methodology:
Control: PCI-DSS Req-9.4.1.1 – Offline media backups with cardholder data are stored in a secure location.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: A designated secure storage area (e.g., locked room, safes, or physically restricted facility) for backup media. Evidence of access control measures such as key or badge access logs at the facility.
   - Technical Artifacts: Physical access logs, CCTV recordings, alarm systems, and chain-of-custody records demonstrating restricted access. Inventory logs detailing each backup media item along with movement history.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular audits and periodic reconciliations of offline media, review of access logs by security personnel, and physical inspections confirming that backups are stored at off-site or alternate secure locations.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faked Compliance through Documentation: Organizations may produce policies or procedural documents that state media are secured while not maintaining any operational evidence or physical controls in the storage area.
   - Surface-Level Implementations: Backup media stored in areas labeled as secure but without robust physical security (e.g., unlocked storage closets or easily accessible rooms).
   - Common Technical Gaps: Lack of detailed access logs, no chain-of-custody records, and insufficient evidence that the backup media storage area is physically separated from general access areas.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Physical Inspection of the Storage Facility
   WHY: Direct observation of controlled access areas (locked doors, alarm systems, badges) verifies that backup media are truly stored in a secure location.
   HOW: Conduct an on-site walkthrough of the storage location; confirm the presence of physical security measures and restricted entry points. Verify that the area is not accessible to unauthorized personnel.
   FAIL: Unsecured access points, visible signs of tampering, or a general lack of physical security measures.

   CHECK: Review Physical Access and Security Logs
   WHY: Access logs and CCTV recordings provide objective operational evidence that only authorized personnel access the secure area.
   HOW: Examine recent physical access logs and CCTV footage; interview personnel responsible for monitoring access; confirm that logs reflect controlled and periodic access.
   FAIL: Missing, incomplete, or inconsistent logs; evidence of unauthorized or frequent unsupervised access.

   CHECK: Validate Chain-of-Custody and Inventory Records for Backup Media
   WHY: These records demonstrate an established process for tracking and protecting cardholder data backups.
   HOW: Review sample chain-of-custody records and inventory lists; ensure they are maintained consistently, reviewed regularly, and are reconciled with the stored items.
   FAIL: Gaps or inconsistencies in records; lack of periodic reconciliation between the documented inventory and physical media present.

   CHECK: Interview and Verify Operational Procedures
   WHY: Conversations with responsible personnel can reveal whether documented procedures are effectively implemented and followed in practice.
   HOW: Interview the storage facility manager and backup media custodians; ask about procedures for access control, incident response, and periodic audits of stored media.
   FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly explain procedures, or evidence that procedures are only theoretical and not operationalized.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Ask for recent security audit reports or internal review findings regarding the secure storage of backup media. These often summarize physical inspection outcomes and log review results.
   - Critical Indicator: The presence of a robust chain-of-custody process and properly maintained access logs is a strong sign of operational effectiveness.
   - Important Detail: Verify that backup media is stored off-site or at a facility with documented physical security controls; look for evidence of periodic audits and reconciliations as a sign of ongoing diligence.
   - Watch Out: Ensure that the security controls are not just on paper. Real evidence like access logs, camera feeds, and inventory reconciliations matter most in practice.

This approach ensures that you validate the control not only through policy documentation but also by examining tangible, operational evidence that offline media backups containing cardholder data are indeed stored securely.
content:
### Defined Approach Requirements

**9.4.1.1** Offline media backups with cardholder data are stored in a secure location.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**9.4.1.1.a** Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined for physically securing offline media backups with cardholder data in a secure location.

**9.4.1.1.b** Examine logs or other documentation and interview responsible personnel at the storage location to verify that offline media backups are stored in a secure location.

### Purpose

If stored in a non-secured facility, backups containing cardholder data may easily be lost, stolen, or copied for malicious intent.

### Good Practice

For secure storage of backup media, a good practice is to store media in an off-site facility, such as an alternate or backup site or commercial storage facility.

title: All media with cardholder data is physically secured.
methodology:
Below is a detailed methodology for validating that all media with cardholder data is physically secured:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Clearly secured storage areas (locked rooms, cabinets, safes) used exclusively for media containing cardholder data.
   • Visual evidence such as signage indicating restricted access and physical barriers (e.g., access-controlled doors).
   • Operational logs showing access events (e.g., keycard logs, visitor logs) to the media storage areas.
   • CCTV footage covering critical physical entry points to storage areas.
   
2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may rely solely on documentation (e.g., written procedures and policies) without showing actual physical security controls in place.
   • Use of superficial measures such as labels on an unlocked cabinet without effective locking or monitoring mechanisms.
   • Periodic security reviews that are not consistently enforced or monitored, resulting in outdated access lists or inactive CCTV systems.
   • Reluctance to restrict physical access to multiple storage areas, assuming that informal practices are sufficient.
   
3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Physically verify access to media storage areas. 
     WHY: Direct observation of secure storage (locked rooms/cabinets) demonstrates implementation of physical controls.
     HOW: Perform a site walk—inspect storage areas where cardholder data media is kept; check for locks, access control systems, and restricted signage.
     FAIL: If media is stored in unsecured areas (open rooms, desks) or if access control devices (locks, keycards) are missing or non-functional.
   
   • CHECK: Review access logs for storage areas containing cardholder data.
     WHY: Logged and monitored access events confirm that only authorized personnel have accessed sensitive media.
     HOW: Request recent electronic or paper logs (e.g., from keycard systems, visitor logs) and cross-verify with authorized personnel lists. 
     FAIL: Absence of logs or evidence showing unrestricted access (e.g. generic entries, missing details on user identity) indicates weakness.
   
   • CHECK: Examine physical security monitoring measures.
     WHY: CCTV or alarm systems provide an independent confirmation that physical areas are monitored.
     HOW: Inspect if CCTV covers critical entry points and verify at least that the review process and retention policy for recordings is in place.
     FAIL: Inadequate camera coverage, absence of monitoring, or lack of review logs are red flags.
   
   • CHECK: Validate the destruction procedures for media with cardholder data.
     WHY: Secure destruction procedures (e.g. shredding, degaussing) ensure that unused or obsolete media is irrecoverable.
     HOW: Review records of media destruction events including witness logs, certificates of destruction, and physical evidence (e.g. locked destruction bins).
     FAIL: Missing destruction logs, evidence of improper media disposal, or storage of media past retention periods indicate a non-compliant process.
   
4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: In many cases, a simple door inspection and a review of the lock type can quickly indicate whether proper physical controls exist. If keys or card access systems seem outdated or easy to bypass (e.g. default codes), it’s an immediate flag.
   • Critical Indicator: Always cross-check the physical evidence with operational logs. If logs are missing or inconsistent with the physical state (e.g. logs show no access events while the area is active), that's a red flag.
   • Focus on Implementation Gaps: Pay close attention to areas where backup or temporary storage might be used. Often, organizations secure primary storage but overlook secondary locations such as portable devices or off-site backups.
   • Detailing Inspections: Ask to see recent physical security audit results or penetration tests focused on storage areas. This will further validate the robustness of the physical security controls.

This methodology emphasizes direct observation and correlation of physical security controls with operational evidence, ensuring that compliance is more than just a documented procedure—it is actively enforced and verifiable.
content:
# 9.4 Media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed.

### Defined Approach Requirements

**9.4.1** All media with cardholder data is physically secured.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**9.4.1.** Examine documentation to verify that the procedures defined for protecting cardholder data include controls for physically securing all media.

### Purpose

Controls for physically securing media are intended to prevent unauthorized persons from gaining access to cardholder data on any media. Cardholder data is susceptible to unauthorized viewing, copying, or scanning if it is unprotected while it is on removable or portable media, printed out, or left on someone’s desk.

### Customized Approach Objective

Media with cardholder data cannot be accessed by unauthorized personnel.

title: Visitor logs are maintained to record visitor activity in the facility and sensitive areas.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Printed or digital visitor logs physically posted and stored in reception areas and sensitive zones. Logs that are clearly filled out with visitor name, organization, date/time, and personnel authorizing access.
   • Technical Artifacts: Digital log files with timestamp metadata; access control system integration records; retention time stamps confirming logs are archived for at least three months.
   • Operational Patterns: Routine updates on logs; observable process where a guard or front desk operator registers visitor details in real-time and retains logs in a secure, tamper-resistant manner.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Relying solely on static or templated log sheets that are not actively filled with dynamic visitor data, or using outdated logs that have not been updated to meet the three-month retention rule.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Logs that are merely filed away without active review, or logs that record only partial data (e.g., missing authorization details or exact date/time stamps).
   • Technical Gaps: Inconsistencies in digital logs (e.g., missing metadata) or logs visible only for certain areas of the facility while sensitive areas are not covered.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Inspect Visitor Log Contents
     WHY: Ensures logs record all required fields (visitor’s name, organization, date/time, and authorizing personnel).
     HOW: Physically review a sample of logs at facility reception and sensitive areas; verify that each entry includes complete information.
     FAIL: Missing fields, generic placeholders, or inconsistent entry formats.

   CHECK: Verify Log Retention Practices
     WHY: To confirm logs are maintained for at least three months as required.
     HOW: Examine storage practices; review archive logs to spot entries older than three months or interview custodians responsible for log retention.
     FAIL: Logs overwritten or destroyed before the three-month period; no archival evidence.

   CHECK: Interview Personnel Responsible for Log Maintenance
     WHY: Understand operational procedures and confirm that logs are actively monitored and updated.
     HOW: Conduct interviews with reception/security staff regarding the process for recording visitor data and retaining logs. Ask about incident scenarios and data review practices.
     FAIL: Personnel unable to explain clear procedures or indicate that logging is a formality with little oversight.

   CHECK: Cross-reference Digital and Physical Logs (if applicable)
     WHY: Validates the operational effectiveness and synchrony between manual and electronic systems.
     HOW: Compare log entries from an electronic system with physical logs; check for time discrepancies or missing records.
     FAIL: Inconsistencies between systems, outdated or unsynchronized logs.

   CHECK: Observe Access Control Integration (if implemented)
     WHY: Confirms that visitor logs are integrated with other security systems (e.g., badge deactivation systems) enhancing operational security.
     HOW: Review access control system logs and verify that deactivation or badge return events correlate with visitor log exit times.
     FAIL: Absence of correlation or documentation to support integrated security processes.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Start by checking the most visible log locations (reception and main sensitive areas) and follow the paper trail to digital archives if available. 
   • Critical Indicator: Uniformity and real-time updating of logs are key – logs with irregular entries or post-dated entries may signal a weak process. 
   • Detail Matters: Ensure that the name of the personnel authorizing access is legible and verifiable (e.g., through employee ID), as this distinctly ties the visitor’s access to accountable actions.
   • Beware of Complacency: A log existing does not equal effective monitoring – check if there is evidence of regular reviews or audits of these logs.
   • Confirm Integration: If the facility uses digital access systems, confirm the automated deactivation or alert mechanisms are in place, reducing risks from outdated visitor credentials.
content:
## Defined Approach Requirements

### 9.3.4 Visitor logs are used to maintain a physical record of visitor activity both within the facility and within sensitive areas, including:
* The visitor’s name and the organization represented.  
* The date and time of the visit.  
* The name of the personnel authorizing physical access.  
* Retaining the log for at least three months, unless otherwise restricted by law.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 9.3.4.a Examine the visitor logs and interview responsible personnel to verify that visitor logs are used to record physical access to both the facility and sensitive areas.

### 9.3.4.b Examine the visitor logs and verify that the logs contain:  
* The visitor’s name and the organization represented.  
* The personnel authorizing physical access.  
* Date and time of visit.

### 9.3.4.c Examine visitor log storage locations and interview responsible personnel to verify that the log is retained for at least three months, unless otherwise restricted by law.

## Guidance

### Purpose  
Ensuring that visitor badges are returned or deactivated upon expiry or completion of the visit prevents malicious persons from using a previously authorized pass to gain physical access into the building after the visit has ended.

### Purpose  
A visitor log documenting minimum information about the visitor is easy and inexpensive to maintain. It will assist in identifying historical physical access to a building or room and potential access to cardholder data.

### Good Practice

When logging the date and time of visit, including both in and out times is considered a best practice, since it provides helpful tracking information and provides assurance that a visitor has left at the end of the day. It is also good to verify that a visitor’s ID (driver’s license, etc.) matches the name they put on the visitor log.

title: Visitor badges or identification are surrendered or deactivated before visitors leave the facility.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Check that physical visitor badges or electronic credentials have a process documented and are physically surrendered (e.g., drop-box, badge reader deactivation logs) upon exit. Visible signage and logs at reception areas support adherence.
   • Technical Artifacts: Obtain deactivation logs from badge management systems or visitor management systems. Review timestamps that correlate visitor exit with badge deactivation. Additionally, review camera footage confirming badge return.
   • Operational Patterns: Observe that the front-desk routinely collects visitor badges and that the deactivation process is enforced rather than relying on manual logging only.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations might only document a policy for badge surrender or deactivation without actively enforcing it. Photos or one-off testimonials may be provided rather than continuous evidence.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Relying on unverified sign-in logs where visitors self-report exit can be insufficient. In some cases, badges are simply marked as expired post-visit without a real-time mechanism or verification steps.
   • Technical Gaps: Lack of integration between physical badge collection and the automated update of the badge status in the system, or poor audit trail on the actual deactivation moment.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that visitor badges or IDs are physically surrendered before visitors leave.
   WHY: This proves that the organization actively ensures no badge is reused once the visit is complete.
   HOW: Interview front desk staff and review operational procedures; inspect physical drop zones or collection bins; check for an audit trail of badge returns.
   FAIL: If badges remain issued at departure or there is no collection record, compliance has likely failed.

   CHECK: Review system logs for real-time deactivation of visitor badges.
   WHY: Automated logs show timely deactivation, indicating operational control beyond paper procedures.
   HOW: Access the electronic visitor management system and extract logs; confirm that exit timestamps align with badge deactivation events.
   FAIL: Gaps in the deactivation timeframe or missing log entries indicate a failure in enforcing the process.

   CHECK: Cross-check visitor records with physical evidence from surveillance or manual logs.
   WHY: Multiple data sources help confirm that badges are not reused and are deactivated before the visitor leaves.
   HOW: Compare a sample set of visitor entry/exit logs with recorded camera footage; verify consistency between the recorded exit event and system deactivation.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies between physical evidence and system logs raise concerns about the control’s effectiveness.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Directly ask for and examine a report from the badge management system focusing on status changes; an absence of detailed deactivation logs can indicate poor implementation.
   • Critical Indicator: Note if there’s any instance where a badge remains active past the visitor’s departure; reactivation events are a red flag.
   • Practical Detail: Ensure that any digital or manual processes have timestamps and, where possible, correlate these with independent surveillance or trusted logs to prevent back-dating or manual manipulation.

This methodology ensures a focus on actual operational practices and observable artifacts rather than relying solely on documented policies, reducing the likelihood of simulated compliance.
content:
### 9.3.3 Visitor badges or identification are surrendered or deactivated before visitors leave the facility or at the date of expiration.

### Customized Approach Objective

Visitor identification or badges cannot be reused after expiration.

title: Procedures for authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE are implemented.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes clearly marked visitor badges that distinguish visitors from personnel, logs of visitor entries and exits, and visible escorting of visitors in the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE).
• Technical artifacts include visitor management system logs, digital records with time-stamped visitor badge assignments (with expiration details), and video surveillance recordings showing visitor escort practices.
• Operational patterns include consistent enforcement of escorting policies, regular auditing of badge issuance and expiration, and observable procedures at access points.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes produce documented procedures without actual operational enforcement – i.e., they have a paper trail but lack observable evidence in practice.
• Visitor badges may be generic or nearly identical to employee badges, making it difficult to distinguish between authorized personnel and visitors.
• Some systems log visitor information only superficially (without expiration details) or lack real-time updates, thereby faking adherence to the expiration requirement.
• Supervisory oversight may be nominal, with escorts occasionally not present, yet staff may assert compliance during interviews.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review and compare documented visitor access procedures with actual on-site practices.
WHY: Ensures that the documented procedures are in active use and that visitor authorization, identification, and escort requirements are met in practice.
HOW: Interview responsible security personnel, observe visitor reception areas and entry points, and match documentation against observed visitor logs and procedures.
FAIL: If procedures are documented but not visible in practice (e.g., visitors are unescorted or badges are not visibly distinct).

CHECK: Verify the visitor badge issuance process and the expiration mechanism for visitor identification.
WHY: To confirm that visitor badges are not only issued but also designed to expire, preventing prolonged or unauthorized access.
HOW: Inspect current visitor badges for expiry markings, access the digital log from the visitor management system to check validity periods, and observe badge collection/expiry at exit points.
FAIL: Badges are generic, lack expiration information, or logs do not show accurate expiration data.

CHECK: Directly observe visitor interactions within the CDE, especially focusing on escorting practices.
WHY: Physical observation reviews actual enforcement of the escort policy, ensuring visitors do not roam unescorted.
HOW: Schedule unannounced visits to the CDE, observe compliance at entry/exit points, and interview on-ground personnel about escort processes.
FAIL: Instances of visitors not being accompanied or corroboration of inconsistent enforcement of the escort requirement.

CHECK: Confirm that the physical or digital systems in place differentiate visitor badges from personnel badges.
WHY: The clear visual distinction is critical for personnel to quickly identify and monitor visitor movement within the CDE.
HOW: Inspect the design and issuance records of visitor badges, and review internal guidelines for badge appearance. Additionally, compare these with staff badges to ensure distinct visual differences.
FAIL: Visitor and employee badges are nearly identical, reducing the effectiveness of visual monitoring.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Rely on a combination of automated logs (from visitor management systems) and real-life observations to avoid relying solely on documentation that can be easily faked.
• Interview several staff members across shifts; if only some are aware or if their responses vary, it may indicate poor implementation.
• Check time-stamped records against actual observed visitor activity to detect any discrepancies in enforcement.
• Look for evidence of training or refresher courses provided to staff responsible for escorting visitors – recurring training gaps can signal operational weaknesses.
• A quick shortcut is to verify unescorted visitor instances on video surveillance for a random selection of entries, which is a strong indicator of the control’s practical enforcement.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements

9.3.2 Procedures are implemented for authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE, including:
- Visitors are authorized before entering.
- Visitors are escorted at all times.
- Visitors are clearly identified and given a badge or other identification that expires.
- Visitor badges or other identification visibly distinguishes visitors from personnel.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

9.3.2.a Examine documented procedures and interview personnel to verify procedures are defined for authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

9.3.2.b Observe processes when visitors are present in the CDE and interview personnel to verify that visitors are:
- Authorized before entering the CDE.
- Escorted at all times within the CDE.

9.3.2.c Observe the use of visitor badges or other identification to verify that the badge or other identification does not permit unescorted access to the CDE.

9.3.2.d Observe visitors in the CDE to verify that:
- Visitor badges or other identification are being used for all visitors.
- Visitor badges or identification easily distinguish visitors from personnel.

9.3.2.e Examine visitor badges or other identification and observe evidence in the badging system to verify visitor badges or other identification expires.

## Guidance

### Purpose
Visitor controls are important to reduce the ability of unauthorized and malicious persons to gain access to facilities and potentially to cardholder data.

Visitor controls ensure visitors are identifiable as visitors so personnel can monitor their activities, and that their access is restricted to just the duration of their legitimate visit.

### Definitions
Refer to Appendix G for the definition of "visitor."  

## Customized Approach Objective

Requirements for visitor access to the CDE are defined and enforced. Visitors cannot exceed any authorized physical access allowed while in the CDE.

title: Physical access to sensitive areas for personnel is controlled and revoked upon termination.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible signs include access control panels at sensitive area entrances displaying authorized personnel names or badges.
• Technical artifacts such as access logs for card readers and key issuance records.
• Consistent physical presence of security personnel at entry points and an observable procedure during shift changes (e.g., scanning badges, deactivating access for terminated personnel).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might show outdated access control lists or incomplete deactivation records that look compliant but haven’t been updated promptly.
• Superficial documentation exists without evidence of real-time log monitoring, leaving terminated personnel’s access active in the system.
• Processes marked on paper policies where keys or cards are assumed returned but aren’t physically checked at termination.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Inspect access log systems and verify deactivation entries for terminated users
