   WHY: Observing a live session ensures that MFA is operational and not just documented in policies or configuration.
   HOW: Initiate a remote access attempt from an off-premises location using valid credentials; verify that, after successful input of the primary factor, an additional challenge (such as OTP, push notification, or biometric verification) is presented and must be successfully completed.
   FAIL: If the additional authentication challenge isn’t triggered or if only a single factor is required, the check fails.

4. CHECK: Interview IT/security personnel responsible for remote access management to understand the process for integrating new remote access points and how MFA is enforced across different technologies.
   WHY: Personnel can provide insights into operational practices, potential exceptions, and how consistently the MFA control is applied across all platforms.
   HOW: Request a walkthrough or demonstration of the remote access management process, ask for change management records demonstrating MFA implementation verification for both new and legacy systems.
   FAIL: Inconsistent practices, undocumented exceptions, or inability of staff to clearly articulate MFA enforcement often indicate compliance gaps.

EXPERT TIPS
• When verifying MFA, always check for integration between the authentication infrastructure (like RADIUS, SAML, or OAuth providers) and all remote access endpoints – misconfigurations here are common pitfalls.
• Beware of systems that only enable MFA in test environments or pilot projects; ensure the validation covers both production and any active remote access nodes.
• Logs tend to be the most tamper-resistant evidence; focus on obtaining logs from centralized authentication services as these are harder to manipulate post-facto.
• If SMS is used as an MFA factor, require additional context on fallback mechanisms; SMS-based methods can be weak if not supplemented with more robust factors.
• Prioritize automated checks using security monitoring tools over static policy reviews, as they more reliably confirm the dynamic enforcement of MFA.

By following these validation steps, auditors can reliably verify that MFA is effectively implemented for all remote access, ensuring that no remote connection is possible with a single factor and thereby reducing the risk of unauthorized access to critical systems.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.4.3 MFA is implemented for all remote access originating from outside the entity’s network that could access or impact the CDE.

Customized Approach Objective:
Remote access to the entity’s network cannot be obtained by using a single authentication factor.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.4.3.a Examine network and/or system configurations for remote access servers and systems to verify MFA is required in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.
8.4.3.b Observe personnel and third parties connecting remotely to the network and verify that multi-factor authentication is required.

Guidance:
Purpose: Requiring more than one type of authentication factor reduces the probability that an attacker can gain access since multiple factors must be compromised.

Applicability Notes:
The requirement applies to all remote access to networks with access to the CDE and is recommended for all remote access scenarios.

title: Implement MFA for all non-console access
methodology:
Control ID: req-8.4.2 – Implement MFA for all non-console access

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Login screens for non-console access (VPNs, remote desktop gateways, web applications) showing an MFA challenge, such as OTP, push notification, or hardware token prompts.
• Technical artifacts: Configuration files from identity providers and network devices that enforce MFA policies; audit logs showing use of second factors during authentication sessions; system logs from authentication brokers.
• Operational patterns: Consistent MFA prompts whenever a user initiates non-console access, with logs capturing successful and failed MFA attempts, and an absence of sessions initiated solely with a single factor.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake documentation: Organizations might claim MFA is implemented, but underlying systems or network access configurations might be using legacy configurations that only require MFA on a subset of connections.
• Surface-level implementations failing in practice: Deploying MFA for only some protocols or having test accounts exempted, resulting in gaps that can be exploited by attackers.
• Technical gaps: Misconfigured identity provider integrations or partial rollout, where some network segments or remote access solutions don’t enforce MFA even though policies exist.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Review network and system configurations that manage non-console access.
WHY: Correct configuration files or system settings that mandate MFA prove the mechanism is in place at the technical level.
HOW: Inspect the settings on VPN gateways, remote desktop services, and web application firewalls, and review identity provider configurations for explicit MFA enforcement; use automated scripts where available to extract these settings.
FAIL: Missing MFA configuration directives, or policies that allow fallback to single-factor authentication.

CHECK: Observe live login sessions to the CDE.
WHY: Watching real-time authentication processes and validation logs reveals whether MFA is actively enforced during non-console access.
HOW: Coordinate with operations to monitor a sample remote login attempt (or review screen recordings, if available) ensuring that an MFA prompt appears and is successfully completed before granting access.
FAIL: Instances where users gain access without encountering an MFA prompt, or if bypass mechanisms are used.

CHECK: Analyze audit and event logs from authentication systems.
WHY: Logs provide automated, tamper-evident evidence that MFA challenges are consistently issued and processed during non-console access attempts.
HOW: Use log aggregation tools to filter events for MFA-related activity; verify that logs contain entries indicating successful MFA completions and any failed attempts that required further review.
FAIL: Sparse or absent logging of MFA events, or evidence that some access records don't include MFA events.

CHECK: Test account validation for non-console access using MFA.
WHY: Simulated access attempts using test accounts can confirm the operational integrity of the MFA configuration across all applicable access points.
HOW: Initiate controlled access sessions with test credentials and verify that MFA is required and correctly processed; cross-check results with configured policies.
FAIL: Test accounts bypassing the MFA challenge or using alternative, less secure access methods.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query the identity provider’s API for policy enforcement results rather than solely relying on static documentation. Look for logs with real timestamps to validate active enforcement.
• Critical indicator: Ensure there are zero exceptions in the rule set. Even a single non-compliant access point can undermine the overall security posture. Cross-check network diagrams to avoid potential backdoor routes that aren’t covered by the MFA configuration.
• Detail matters: Verify integration points between the MFA system and all applicable non-console access gateways. Poor or incomplete integration is often a telltale sign of a control that is present in theory but ineffective in practice.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.4.2 MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.4.2.a Examine network and/or system configurations to verify MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE.
8.4.2.b Observe personnel logging in to the CDE and examine evidence to verify that MFA is required.

Customized Approach Objective:
Access into the CDE cannot be obtained by the use of a single authentication factor.

Guidance:
Purpose: Requiring more than one type of authentication factor reduces the probability that an attacker can gain access, as multiple factors must be compromised.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement does not apply to certain system or application accounts; MFA must be applied to each type of access as specified.

title: Implement MFA for non-console administrative access
methodology:
Below is the validation methodology for PCI-DSS Req-8.4.1 to implement MFA for all non-console access into the CDE for users with administrative privileges.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visual evidence in login attempts: When administrative users access CDE systems, the login pages or clients should display an MFA prompt (e.g., push notifications, OTP screens, or hardware token prompts).
   • Technical artifacts such as system configuration files or firewall rules showing integration with MFA service providers can serve as evidence. For example, logs from the authentication server demonstrating multi-factor challenges.
   • Operational patterns include audit logs that record MFA authentication events for every non-console access attempt by administrative accounts.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might disable MFA on certain administrative accounts or system maintenance accounts during peak use, then re-enable it, thus showing fake compliance on paper.
   • Some may place MFA only at the portal front-end while back-end services or direct API calls bypass the additional authentication factor.
   • Configuration settings in the network or authentication server might appear to be enabled but are not correctly enforced due to misconfigurations or outdated systems.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that all administrative user accounts in the CDE have MFA configured and enforced for non-console access.
     WHY: Ensures that no administrative access bypasses MFA, protecting the CDE from unauthorized access.
     HOW: Review and query the identity management system; check automated policies (using system logs and configuration management databases) and conduct test logins simulating non-console access, observing MFA invocation.
     FAIL: Any test login without an MFA prompt or accounts without proper MFA configurations are indications of non-compliance.

   • CHECK: Examine network and system configuration settings that enforce MFA for remote access.
     WHY: This confirms that MFA is implemented not just as a policy but embedded in the technical control mechanisms.
     HOW: Inspect configurations on VPN, remote desktop gateways, or administrative portals. Validate integration settings with the MFA provider, and verify through change logs that these settings are current.
     FAIL: Absence of MFA-based challenge configurations or evidence that outdated configurations are active indicates insufficient implementation.

   • CHECK: Audit logs review for the past 90 days showing MFA events for administrative access.
     WHY: Consistent logging of MFA events indicates operational enforcement and monitoring of MFA controls.
     HOW: Query centralized logging systems for authentication event logs (e.g., event IDs, MFA challenge responses, etc.) for administrative users accessing the CDE. Verify that each administrative login event includes an MFA challenge.
     FAIL: Missing logs or gaps in the MFA events for non-console administrative access suggest that MFA is not consistently enforced.

   • CHECK: Direct observation of administrative personnel attempting login into the CDE using non-console methods.
     WHY: Live testing and observation validate that MFA is actively required, not simply deployed in configuration.
     HOW: Set up scheduled observation sessions or simulated remote access sessions, ensuring that administrative users must complete an MFA challenge before gaining access.
     FAIL: Instances where administrative users bypass MFA, have fallback logins without MFA, or there is no challenge in place during the observation are direct indicators of a control gap.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Prioritize the audit of authentication logs over policy documents because logs provide real-time evidence of MFA enforcement.
   • In many cases, misconfigurations occur in integration points (like SSO gateways or API endpoints) – focus on those endpoints to detect loopholes.
   • Use automated tools to simulate login attempts under various scenarios. This helps quickly determine if any backdoor exists that bypasses MFA.
   • Check for any recently added administrative accounts without proper MFA assignment – these are common oversight points.
   • It is crucial that the test covers both scheduled access and emergency or break-glass accounts, as neglecting these can leave significant vulnerabilities.

This detailed methodology focuses on checking real-time operational evidence rather than simply relying on documented policies and ensures that the MFA control for non-console administrative access is both technically enforced and operationally monitored.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.4.1 MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE for personnel with administrative access.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.4.1.a Examine network and/or system configurations to verify MFA is required for all non-console access into the CDE for personnel with administrative access.
8.4.1.b Observe administrator personnel logging into the CDE and verify that MFA is required.

Guidance:
Requiring more than one type of authentication factor reduces the probability that an attacker can gain access as multiple factors must be compromised.

title: Ensure authentication factors are individually assigned
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
- Visible evidence includes system configurations where multi-factor authentication setup is documented for individual user credentials rather than group-shared tokens.
- Technical logs and audit trails show individual mapping of authentication artifacts (such as smart cards, certificates) to unique user accounts.
- Observed physical controls may include user-specific PIN pads or biometric scanners integrated with smart card readers.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
- Organizations sometimes create generic account templates that are then used by multiple individuals, effectively sharing authentication tokens.
- Some implementations merely require password plus token, but the token isn’t bound to a user, or is copied and reused across users.
- Configuration that logs a “card identifier” without linking it to an individual may be misinterpreted as simply mapping to a card, while in practice it could be shared.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify unique binding of tokens/smart cards to individual user accounts in the authentication system.
WHY: Ensures that authentication tokens cannot be used by anyone except the designated user, thereby preventing sharing.
HOW: Review system configurations, user profiles, and the mapping between tokens and accounts in a centralized directory; cross-check logs for user-specific authentications.
FAIL: Presence of generic tokens or identifiers assigned to multiple users.

CHECK: Inspect audit logs for abnormal patterns indicating shared usage of authentication factors (e.g., same token used from different locations or at overlapping times).
WHY: Consistent individual use should align with geographically and temporally isolated access patterns.
HOW: Use automated log analysis to filter authentication events by token ID and review their origin timestamps and associated IP addresses.
FAIL: A token used for accessing systems from different users’ locations or concurrently by multiple users.

CHECK: Interview security personnel for process verification that issuance and decommissioning of tokens/smart cards are tied to individual user onboarding/offboarding.
WHY: Interviews reveal whether standard operational procedures enforce individual-based token assignment and control.
HOW: Ask for demonstrations of the token issuance process and review training materials / SOPs relating to token management.
FAIL: Procedures indicate token sharing or non-specific assignments to groups rather than individuals.

CHECK: Evaluate physical access controls on systems using tokens (e.g., verifying that card readers require a PIN or biometric validation).
WHY: Logical and physical measures ensure that even if a token is physically possessed by someone else, they will not be able to authenticate as the intended user.
HOW: Inspect device configurations and physical hardware interfaces; attempt to verify pairing between a token and its user via test authentication procedures if allowed.
FAIL: Lack of secondary verification (PIN/biometric) on token-based systems, meaning the token alone is sufficient for access.

EXPERT TIPS:
- Shortcut: Directly query the authentication system's logs for issuance history and re-assignments. Checking whether any token was reassigned to multiple users can quickly indicate mismanagement.
- Critical Indicator: The ability to challenge a token (or card) for additional authentication (PIN/biometric) is a strong indicator of individual assignment; if missing, the control is weak.
- Detail to Check: Confirm that the user entitlement management process clearly logs the association of tokens with individual users during onboarding. This review should be automated or heavily supported by real-time logs if possible.
- Remember: Written policies are not enough; always back up with system configuration and evidence of operational enforcement to avoid cases where generic credentials are widely reused.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.11 Where authentication factors such as physical or logical security tokens, smart cards, or certificates are used:
- Factors are assigned to an individual user and not shared among multiple users.
- Physical and/or logical controls ensure only the intended user can use that factor to gain access.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.11.a Examine authentication policies and procedures to verify that procedures for using authentication factors such as physical security tokens, smart cards, and certificates are defined and include all elements specified in this requirement.
8.3.11.b Interview security personnel to verify authentication factors are assigned to an individual user and not shared among multiple users.
8.3.11.c Examine system configuration settings and/or observe physical controls, as applicable, to verify that controls are implemented to ensure only the intended user can use that factor to gain access.

Guidance:
Purpose: If multiple users can use authentication factors such as tokens, smart cards, and certificates, it may be impossible to identify the individual using the authentication mechanism.

Good Practice:
Having physical and/or logical controls (for example, a PIN, biometric data, or a password) to uniquely authenticate the user of the account will prevent unauthorized users from gaining access through shared authentication factors.

Customized Approach Objective:
An authentication factor cannot be used by anyone other than the user to which it is assigned.

title: Enforce periodic change or dynamic analysis for service provider passwords
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology for verifying compliance with the PCI DSS req-8.3.10.1 control for service provider passwords. This control requires that when passwords/passphrases are the only factor used for customer user access, either they are rotated at least every 90 days or the account security posture is dynamically analyzed in real time for access decisions.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Screenshots or printouts of system configuration settings showing a 90-day password expiration policy, or dashboards/logs indicating active dynamic analysis (e.g., risk scores, behavior analytics triggers, real-time session monitoring).
• Technical Artifacts: Configuration files (LDAP, Active Directory, PAM configurations), scheduler logs for automatic password resets, dynamic risk assessment logs, and API logs from dynamic access control systems.
• Operational Patterns: Regular automated password reset events recorded in system logs, continuous updates indicating active monitoring of user behavior, and alerts generated when anomalies are detected.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations could simply update policy documentation or screenshots without actually enforcing password changes in the live environment. They might put placeholder settings or have configurations that are not enforced on all accounts.
• Surface-Level Failures: Implementing password expiration on a subset of accounts or failing to verify the actual timestamp of the most recent password change. Similarly, deploying dynamic analysis tools without real-time integration of access controls is a common oversight.
• Technical Gaps: Missing synchronization between password expirations and account activity logs, or using static reports that do not capture real-time dynamic decisions.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Password Expiration Policy Configuration
WHY: This confirms if the system is set to enforce a password change at least once every 90 days for customer user accounts.
HOW: Inspect system or directory configuration settings (e.g., Active Directory, LDAP, or equivalent authentication service). Check for attributes such as 'maxPasswordAge' and survey a sample of account creation/change timestamps to ensure compliance.
FAIL: If the policy configuration indicates no expiration, a longer duration than 90 days, or if account timestamps do not reflect regular changes.

CHECK: Review Automated Password Change Logs
WHY: Automated logs provide evidence that the password reset mechanism is operational and not merely documented.
HOW: Access server/system logs that track password change events. Verify that password change events are occurring on a periodic basis (90 days or less) and correlate password change timestamps with policy configurations.
FAIL: Absence of such logs, outdated logs, or inconsistencies between expected periodic changes and the logged events.

CHECK: Validate Dynamic Analysis Integration (if used as an alternative)
WHY: Ensuring real-time analysis of account security posture demonstrates an alternative form of control to mitigate the risk associated with static passwords.
HOW: Examine the logs or dashboards of the dynamic access control solution. Look for risk scores, alerts, or decision logs that indicate real-time analysis during authentications. Interview administrators for insight into operational use of these controls.
FAIL: Lack of integration between authentication events and dynamic analysis logs, or if the dynamic tool is present but not actively involved in access decisions.

CHECK: Analyze System Alerts & Incident Response Logs
WHY: Incidents related to failed or compromised authentication demonstrate that controls are active and that defenses are promptly triggered.
HOW: Review incident logs and alert notifications for evidence that irregular account activity (e.g., high-risk login events) was detected and responded to. Confirm that these events align with either enforced password rotation or dynamic analysis alerts.
FAIL: If such logs show no correlation with the expected periodic changes or if alerts regarding risk indications from dynamic analysis are missing.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated configuration scanning tools to extract and report password policy settings directly from the authentication management systems. Compare these results with the system’s security logs for consistency.
• Indicator: Pay close attention to the actual timestamp data, not just documented periodic settings. The consistency between the configured policy and observed password change events is critical.
• Detail: For dynamic analysis, verify that automated risk scoring is integrated into the authentication flow (ideally enforced by zero-trust controls). Manual reviews of dashboard snapshots can often be deceived by infrequent refreshes—real-time API queries may reveal gaps.
• Best Practice: Double-check that every customer account is subject to the enforced control, as exceptions can lead to gaps in overall security posture.

This methodology centers on verifying operational effectiveness—tracking both configuration parameters and real operational outcomes—to ensure the control isn’t merely documented but is actively safeguarding customer access in the field.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.10.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access (i.e., in any single-factor authentication implementation) then either:
- Passwords/passphrases are changed at least once every 90 days, OR
- The security posture of accounts is dynamically analyzed, and real-time access to resources is automatically determined accordingly.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.10.1 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access, inspect system configuration settings to verify that passwords/passphrases are managed in accordance with ONE of the elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
Using a password/passphrase as the only authentication factor provides a single point of failure if compromised. Therefore, in these implementations, controls are needed to minimize how long malicious activity could occur via a compromised password/passphrase.

Good Practice:
Passwords/passphrases that are valid for a long time without a change provide malicious individuals with more time to break the password/phrase. Periodically changing passwords offers less time for a malicious individual to crack a password/passphrase and less time to use a compromised password.

Dynamically analyzing an account's security posture is another option that allows for more rapid detection and response to address potentially compromised credentials.

Further Information:
For information about using dynamic analysis to manage user access to resources, refer to NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture.

Customized Approach Objective:
Passwords/passphrases for service providers’ customers cannot be used indefinitely.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Until this requirement is effective on 31 March 2025, service providers may meet either Requirement 8.3.10 or 8.3.10.1.

title: Provide service provider password guidance for customer user access
methodology:
Below is the detailed validation methodology for verifying compliance with req-8.3.10, which requires that service providers provide password guidance for customer user access to cardholder data when only single-factor authentication is used.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Published communications: Customers see clear, easily accessible guidance on password management (e.g., on login pages, help sections, or in welcome emails).
   • Configuration artifacts: The service provider’s password policy documentation and related customer-facing guidance outlines specific intervals for mandatory password changes and defines conditions for initiating changes (e.g., suspected compromise).
   • Operational evidence: Logs or records showing periodic dissemination of password guidance (e.g., notifications or customer alerts) and support communications during password reset events.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Relying solely on internal policy documents that are not publicly visible to customers creates a facade of compliance even though the guidance is not operationally communicated.
   • Static screenshots of old help pages that haven’t been updated or lack detailed change conditions might hide ineffective implementations.
   • Superficial implementation where guidance exists in documentation but is not reflected in actual system communications (e.g., alerts missing or misleading content on the user login interface).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the publicly accessible customer portal or login page includes clear password guidance.
   WHY: Visible instructions that advise customers on how and when to change their passwords demonstrate the guidance is active.
   HOW: Access the customer login or help pages; review linked documentation; confirm that the instructions detail periodic password changes and conditions (e.g., after suspected breaches).
   FAIL: The login portal and associated help materials lack any guidance regarding periodic changes or change conditions.
   
   CHECK: Examine a representative sample of customer communication (emails, notifications) for guidance on password management.
   WHY: Actual communication to customers is direct evidence that the guidance is actively provided rather than merely documented internally.
   HOW: Request and review copies of emails, system notifications, or support messages sent to customers regarding password resets or security updates; check that the communications mention both periodic changes and when changes are mandated.
   FAIL: Communications are absent or generic and do not include the specific change criteria defined in the control.
   
   CHECK: Review the service provider’s published password policy and customer-facing security guidance documentation.
   WHY: The documentation should explicitly state both the requirement for periodic changes and the circumstances triggering a mandatory password change.
   HOW: Access the current version of the password policy on the service provider’s website; verify that it covers periodic password changes and conditions (e.g., after a breach, account inactivity, etc.).
   FAIL: The policy only mentions security practices in broad terms or omits key details such as change intervals and trigger events.
   
   CHECK: Validate that the guidance is integrated into onboarding materials or support processes in a manner that engages customers proactively.
   WHY: Integration with onboarding means that the guidance is an integral part of the customer experience rather than an afterthought.
   HOW: Interview support personnel or review onboarding documentation to assess whether and how they inform customers about password change requirements during account setup.
   FAIL: Onboarding and support materials do not mention any specific password change intervals or the conditions under which a password should be updated.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use an incognito browser or ask as a new customer to verify that the guidance is visible and current on public-facing pages – this minimizes reliance on potentially outdated internal documents.
   • Check that guidance is both comprehensive (covering periodic changes and specific triggers) and clearly understandable by non-technical users. Overly technical language might indicate that the guidance is not tailored for customer understanding.
   • Pay attention to the frequency of updates in both the published policy and customer communications; infrequent updates might reveal a static or outdated process that could fail in real conditions.
   • When assessing communications, a cross-check between the stated policy and any incident-driven communications (e.g., following a breach) can be a strong indicator of true operational adherence.

This step-by-step practical methodology ensures that compliance is verified by actual public and operational evidence, not just internal policy statements.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.10 Additional requirement for service providers only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access to cardholder data (i.e., in a single-factor authentication implementation), then guidance is provided to customer users including:
- Guidance for customers to change their user passwords/passphrases periodically.
- Guidance as to when, and under what circumstances, passwords/passphrases are to be changed.

Customized Approach Objective:
Passwords/passphrases for service providers’ customers cannot be used indefinitely.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
This requirement does not apply to accounts of consumer users accessing their own payment card information.
This requirement for service providers will be superseded by Requirement 8.3.10.1 once 8.3.10.1 becomes effective.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.10 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access to cardholder data, examine guidance provided to customer users to verify that the guidance includes all elements specified in this requirement.

Guidance:
Purpose: Using a password/passphrase as the only authentication factor provides a single point of failure if compromised.

Good Practice:
Passwords/passphrases that are valid for a long time without a change provide malicious individuals with more time to break the password/phrase. Periodically changing passwords offers less time for a malicious individual to crack a password/passphrase and less time to use a compromised password.

title: Manage single-factor authentication usage
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying compliance with the requirement to manage single-factor authentication usage through either periodic password changes (at least every 90 days) or dynamic risk analysis for real-time access decisions:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence: Actual system configuration screens (or settings in directory services/OS management tools) showing password expiration policies set for accounts using password-only authentication, or apparent integration of risk-based controls (e.g., real-time monitoring dashboards, API integrations with dynamic risk scoring systems).
   - Technical artifacts: Configuration files, scripts, or managed policy settings that enforce a maximum 90-day lifespan on passwords/passphrases, along with logs of password change events. Alternatively, logs and audit trails from risk analysis systems that document real-time risk evaluations during authentication events.
   - Operational patterns: Regular, automated password change events recorded in security logs, or observable processes where authentication requests trigger real-time scoring and risk-based granting/denial of access. Evidence that these configurations are routinely maintained and monitored over time.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faking compliance by maintaining policy documents that state a 90-day password change requirement without implementing system settings to enforce it.
   - Superficial implementation where password change events are recorded, but are manually updated or infrequently audited, thus potentially bypassing the actual 90-day rule.
   - Configurations that only target high-privilege accounts, while leaving lower-level accounts to operate outside of the requirement, or deploying risk analysis tools that are not properly integrated or fail to log decisions.
   - Relying solely on documentation or screenshots of configurations, without automated evidence or analysis of actual log data.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Password Expiration Configuration
   WHY: This confirms that single-factor authentication accounts are enforced to change their passwords (or passphrases) at least every 90 days.
   HOW: Access system or directory service configuration settings (e.g., in Active Directory, Linux PAM settings, or application configuration files) and inspect the specific attributes for password expiration. Ensure that the maximum password age is set to 90 days or less.
   FAIL: If settings show no expiration value, are manually overridden, or if the lifespan exceeds 90 days.

   CHECK: Review Authentication Logs for Password Change Events
   WHY: Operational evidence of periodic password changes confirms that the control is actively enforced.
   HOW: Obtain and examine system or application logs that track authentication events, with a focus on password change timestamps. Look for a consistent interval (ideally no more than 90 days) across a representative sample of user accounts.
   FAIL: Absence of password change records within the required time window or irregular intervals inconsistent with policy.

   CHECK: Examine Integration and Logs for Dynamic Risk Analysis (if implemented as an alternative)
   WHY: Confirms that accounts relying on a single authentication factor are subject to real-time risk assessments that can mitigate prolonged use of compromised credentials.
   HOW: Inspect system configuration settings and integration logs to confirm that a dynamic risk analysis system is operational. Review logs for correlation between authentication attempts and real-time risk decisions (e.g., additional challenges triggered or access delays based on risk scores).
   FAIL: No configuration evidence of integration with a risk analysis solution, or missing/empty logs that should indicate real-time risk evaluations.

   CHECK: Sample Account Property Validation
   WHY: Ensures that in-scope systems and accounts uniformly adhere to control requirements rather than selectively applying patches.
   HOW: Randomly select multiple user accounts from different systems. Validate through system queries or management consoles whether each account’s credentials are configured with either a password expiration setting or are recorded in logs as subject to dynamic risk analysis.
   FAIL: Discovery of accounts not bound by either control method or records that suggest exceptions without documented risk compensations.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Use automated scripts or tools (such as PowerShell for Windows or shell scripts for Linux/UNIX) to query system configurations and account attributes. This reduces reliance on static screenshots and manual inspections.
   - Cross-reference configuration settings with real-time logs: discrepancies between intended policy and actual event logs are strong indicators of poor implementation.
   - Pay extra attention to legacy systems: older applications may still be using manual password reset processes that don’t integrate with automated logging, making them vulnerable.
   - Validate that any dynamic risk analysis system is both integrated and properly logging decisions, as a misconfigured or silent-failing system can give a false sense of compliance.
   - Verify across different systems and account types to ensure consistency. A control that only applies to high-profile accounts but not the broader user base is a red flag.

This methodology emphasizes verification against observable configurations and operational log evidence while being alert to common implementation pitfalls. The focus is on actionable, automatable checks and expert-driven shortcuts that reveal the effectiveness of the control in practice.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.9 If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for user access (i.e., in any single-factor authentication implementation) then either:
• Passwords/passphrases are changed at least once every 90 days, OR
• The security posture of accounts is dynamically analyzed, and real-time access to resources is automatically determined accordingly.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.9 If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for user access, inspect system configuration settings to verify that passwords/passphrases are managed in accordance with ONE of the elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
Access to in-scope system components that are not in the CDE may be provided using a single authentication factor, such as a password/passphrase, token device or smart card, or biometric attribute. Where passwords/passphrases are employed as the only authentication factor for such access, additional controls are required to protect the integrity of the password/passphrase.

Good Practice:
Passwords/passphrases that are valid for a long time without a change provide malicious individuals with more time to break the password/phrase. Periodically changing passwords offers less time for a malicious individual to crack a password/passphrase and less time to use a compromised password. Using a password/passphrase as the only authentication factor provides a single point of failure if compromised.

Customized Approach Objective:
An undetected compromised password/passphrase cannot be used indefinitely.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement does not apply to in-scope system components where MFA is used.
This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
This requirement does not apply to service providers’ customer accounts but does apply to accounts for service provider personnel.

title: Document and communicate authentication policies and procedures
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Confirm that a formal authentication policy document exists and is current. 
  WHY: The document serves as tangible evidence that guidance on strong password selection, factor protection, non-reuse of passwords, and procedures for when a compromise is suspected are formally communicated.
  HOW: Inspect the version-controlled policy document repository; verify that the policy includes sections on selecting strong factors, protecting credentials, avoiding reuse, and breach response procedures. 
  FAIL: Policy document is missing, outdated, or does not cover all key areas.

• CHECK: Verify that the authentication policy has been distributed to all users.
  WHY: Distribution ensures that all employees are aware of the necessary authentication practices as specified.
  HOW: Review system logs of policy distribution emails, intranet access history, or training attendance records; conduct random sampling of user interviews asking if they recall the content.
  FAIL: No distribution record, low user awareness, incomplete or inconsistent communication across the user base.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may merely upload a policy document on an internal website without monitoring user acknowledgement or effective dissemination.
• They might distribute outdated or boilerplate guidance that does not address the dynamic risk landscape. 
• Using only documentation without practical training or periodic reminders can also mask non-operational compliance.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Examine the authentication policy document for explicit inclusion of:
   - Guidance on strong authentication factors (e.g., password complexity, length, randomization).
   - Instructions not to reuse passwords or passphrases.
   - Steps to protect authentication factors (e.g., not sharing them, secure storage).
   - Procedures to change passwords upon any suspicion of compromise and guidance on incident reporting.
   WHY: The inclusion of these elements directly meets the control’s defined approach criteria.
   HOW: Compare the document content against the checklist in the requirement; map each key item to its related policy section.
   FAIL: Any missing or vague guidance on these topics indicates non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Verify evidence of distribution and communication of the policy.
   WHY: Authentic communication is required for operational compliance.
   HOW: Inspect distribution logs (email announcements, intranet postings, learning management system records) and interview a sample of users.
   FAIL: Lack of distribution records or user recollection of having received and acknowledged the policy.

3. CHECK: Review training records or user interview responses regarding familiarity and understanding of the authentication procedures.
   WHY: Regular training and affirmation from personnel is necessary to ensure that policy knowledge translates into practice.
   HOW: Validate that training sessions (or communication sessions) include content on the authentication procedures; interview employees to gauge their comprehension on how to protect their credentials.
   FAIL: Employees are unaware of the procedure details or cannot articulate the policy's key components.

4. CHECK: Validate that there are mechanisms for periodic policy review and updates as authentication threats evolve.
   WHY: An outdated policy may become ineffective, even if initially compliant.
   HOW: Check for documented reviews, update logs, and management review minutes that indicate periodic updates and communications.
   FAIL: Absence of documented review cycles or reactive updates only after incidents.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Randomly select a set of users and ask them to recite key policy elements—this often reveals if the distribution was superficial or impactful.
• Tip: Ensure that evidence of distribution is not solely digital; physical training attendance or interactive sessions provide stronger proof.
• Critical Indicator: Look for evidence of active management support (e.g. leadership sign-off on the policy and records of when it was last communicated), which is essential for user buy-in.
• Detail: Check whether the guidance includes context-specific examples (like what qualifies as a strong password), as generic advice may indicate poor implementation.
• Note: Be wary of “one-off” notification emails that are not followed up with periodic reminders—effective communication should be iterative.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.8 Authentication policies and procedures are documented and communicated to all users including:
- Guidance on selecting strong authentication factors.
- Guidance for how users should protect their authentication factors.
- Instructions not to reuse previously used passwords/passphrases.
- Instructions to change passwords/passphrases if there is any suspicion or knowledge that the password/passphrases have been compromised and how to report the incident.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.8.a Examine procedures and interview personnel to verify that authentication policies and procedures are distributed to all users.
8.3.8.b Review authentication policies and procedures that are distributed to users and verify they include the elements specified in this requirement.
8.3.8.c Interview users to verify that they are familiar with authentication policies and procedures.

Guidance:
Purpose: Communicating authentication policies and procedures to all users helps them to understand and abide by the policies.

Good Practice:
Guidance on selecting strong passwords may include suggestions to help personnel select hard-to-guess passwords that do not contain dictionary words or personal information.

Additional Good Practice guidance is provided regarding protecting authentication factors and changing passwords in case of compromise.

title: Prohibit reuse of the last four passwords/passphrases
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for system configuration screens (e.g., in Active Directory, Linux PAM, or custom application settings) that display the password history requirement (set to at least the last four passwords).
   • Technical Artifacts: Extract configuration files, scripts, or policy management dashboards that show a parameter like “passwordHistory” or equivalent set to 4. In automated systems, check logs for rejected password change attempts where a reused password triggered an error.
   • Operational Patterns: Real-world password change attempts by users should result in a rejection message when trying to use an old password; periodic audit logs should confirm enforcement of the rule.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance: Organizations might update policy documents or guidelines but neglect to configure systems properly or may limit enforcement to only certain user groups.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Some systems may display the correct settings in a GUI without enforcing enforcement at the back end. Others might use a temporarily stored password history in non-production systems or fail to propagate changes across all application servers.
   • Technical Gaps: Omitting configuration in legacy systems or custom applications that do not support automated password history checks, thereby leaving endpoints vulnerable to password reuse.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify system configuration settings reflect the password history limit (e.g., history count set to at least 4).
   WHY: This proves the system is configured to remember and compare at least the last four passwords to prevent reuse.
   HOW: Extract and review configuration files or policy settings in the password management system (e.g., Active Directory Group Policy, PAM configuration, or custom application settings). Use automated scripts to query password policy parameters if available.
   FAIL: If configuration does not include a parameter for password history or if it’s set to a value less than 4, the control implementation has failed.

   CHECK: Test password change functionality by attempting to reuse one of the last four passwords.
   WHY: A functional test confirms operational enforcement rather than just documented compliance.
   HOW: Create a test account or use a controlled test environment, change the password four times, and then attempt to revert to one of the previous four. Verify that the system rejects the reuse attempt with a proper error message.
   FAIL: If the system accepts a password that was used within the last four changes, it fails to enforce the control.

   CHECK: Review audit logs for events related to password change attempts, especially those that denote rejection due to password reuse.
   WHY: Log entries provide evidence that the control is actively monitoring and enforcing the rule in real time.
   HOW: Use log analysis tools or manually inspect security/event logs for entries indicating password change failures due to reuse. Ensure these logs are maintained and timestamped.
   FAIL: Absence of log entries for rejected password reuse attempts or logs that show acceptance of reused passwords indicate a failure of the control.

   CHECK: Interview system administrators and/or review system update records for evidence that the password policy was updated in line with this control requirement.
   WHY: Direct confirmation from the system custodians strengthens the evidence that the control is actively maintained.
   HOW: Ask administrators to provide records or change logs demonstrating when and how the password history control was implemented and verified in any custom or legacy systems.
   FAIL: Inability to provide supporting administrative evidence or records of configuration changes is an indicator that the control may not be fully in place.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: On platforms like Active Directory, quickly validate by running command-line utilities (e.g., net accounts) that show password policy details, including history depth.
   • Critical Indicator: Pay close attention to discrepancies between policy documentation and actual system configuration; a system that only superficially enforces these settings is vulnerable.
   • Important Detail: Ensure that the configuration applies uniformly across all systems and that custom applications have integrated password history checking, not just offloading to external authentication mechanisms. 
   • Be Aware: In cloud environments or hybrid systems, verify that all endpoints and backup authentication mechanisms also honor the password reuse restrictions to prevent bypasses.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.7 Individuals are not allowed to submit a new password/passphrase that is the same as any of the last four passwords/passphrases used.

Customized Approach Objective:
A previously used password cannot be used to gain access to an account for at least 12 months.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.7 Examine system configuration settings to verify that password parameters are set to require that new passwords/passphrases cannot be the same as the four previously used passwords/passphrases.

Purpose (from table continuation):
If password history is not maintained, the effectiveness of changing passwords is reduced, as previous passwords can be reused over and over. Requiring that passwords cannot be reused for a period reduces the likelihood that passwords that have been guessed or brute-forced will be reused in the future.

Additional note:
Passwords or passphrases may have previously been changed due to suspicion of compromise or because the password or passphrase exceeded its effective use period, both of which are reasons why previously used passwords should not be reused.

title: Minimum password/passphrase complexity requirements
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for PCI DSS req-8.3.6 – Minimum password/passphrase complexity requirements:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence: Review administrative consoles or system settings where password policies are configured. For instance, screenshots of system configuration pages (e.g., Active Directory, Linux PAM configuration, or application-specific settings) showing a minimum length set to 12 characters (or eight if limited) and validations for both numeric and alphabetic characters.
   • Technical artifacts: Extract configuration files (such as /etc/login.defs, /etc/pam.d/common-password, Windows Group Policy settings, or application config files) which state the password complexity, length rules, and associated enforcement logic.
   • Operational patterns: Check for logs or change history records that indicate enforcement of these rules – such as rejected password change attempts or error messages indicating complexity issues.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake compliance: Organizations might document these policies in internal guidelines, yet the actual system configuration could be misconfigured. In some cases, a policy might state 12-character requirements while the technology only supports an 8-character length.
   • Surface-level implementation: Simply having a policy that is not enforced at the technical level (e.g., password complexity is only validated on UI forms but not applicable via API or command-line utilities).
   • Technical gaps: Not checking all entry points (e.g., administrative interfaces vs. self-service password resets) may result in bypassing the complexity requirements. Some systems may allow legacy accounts with weaker configurations until the account is reset.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify system configuration files and Group Policy settings
         WHY: This demonstrates that the intended complexity settings (minimum 12 characters where supported, containing numeric and alphabetic characters) are declared in the system
         HOW: Extract and review configuration files or Group Policy objects from systems that manage authentication. Cross-check settings against PCI DSS requirements, ensuring a documented minimum length of 12 characters (or eight if systems do not support more) and rules checking for both numeric and alphabetic characters.
         FAIL: If configuration files or policy screens either do not exist or have values that deviate from the standard (e.g., only numeric or alphabetic checks, or a lesser minimum length without documented fallback).

   • CHECK: Test password creation/change flow
         WHY: Actual enforcement can only be verified by attempting to set passwords that are intentionally weak
         HOW: Create test accounts or attempt password changes using passwords that intentionally break one or more rules (such as: less than required length, missing numeric characters, or missing alphabetic characters). Verify that the system rejects these attempts with clear error messaging.
         FAIL: Acceptance of weak passwords or missing error feedback confirms that the enforcement mechanism is not effective.

   • CHECK: Review system logs for enforcement feedback
         WHY: Operational logs provide evidence that password policy enforcement is active during account creation and modification
         HOW: Identify relevant logs (e.g., security event logs on Windows, /var/log/auth.log on Linux) for events corresponding to password change attempts. Look for audit trails that record rejected password attempts due to complexity violations.
         FAIL: Absence of logging or evidence that password attempts do not trigger proper audit events suggests a gap in operational enforcement.

   • CHECK: Verify end-to-end application behavior
         WHY: Many systems treat password complexity validation differently on diverse interfaces (e.g., web UI vs. API)
         HOW: Test the password change process on all available interfaces (web portal, mobile apps, APIs). Ensure that each interface enforces the same complexity rules. Document any interfaces that allow non-compliant passwords.
         FAIL: If one or more interfaces accept passwords that do not meet the complexity criteria, the control is not fully implemented.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated configuration assessment tools (e.g., policy scanners, custom scripts) to check registry settings on Windows or configuration files on Linux for password policy directives.
   • Critical indicator: Discrepancies between documented policies and live configuration are a red flag—look for exceptions or legacy configurations especially in ad-hoc administrative interfaces.
   • Detail matters: Ensure that both the minimum length and the dual requirement for numeric and alphabetic characters are enforced. If special characters or case mixing are recommended (even if not required by PCI DSS at this moment), note this as an extended best practice.
   • Insider hint: Validate that all new account creations, password resets, and password change mechanisms (including API and CLI methods) are subject to the same validation rules to prevent policy bypass.

These steps provide a comprehensive approach to confirm that password/passphrase complexity requirements are not just documented but are actively enforced across all authentication entry points.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.6 If passwords/passphrases are used as authentication factors to meet Requirement 8.3.1, they meet the following minimum level of complexity:
• A minimum length of 12 characters (or IF the system does not support 12 characters, a minimum length of eight characters).
• Contain both numeric and alphabetic characters.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.6 Examine system configuration settings to verify that user password/passphrase complexity parameters are set in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose:
Strong passwords/passphrases may be the first line of defense into a network since a malicious individual will often first try to find accounts with weak, static, or non-existent passwords. If passwords are short or easily guessable, it is relatively easy for a malicious individual to find these weak accounts and compromise a network under the guise of a valid user ID.

Good Practice:
Password/passphrase strength is dependent on password/passphrase complexity, length, and randomness. Passwords/passphrases should be sufficiently complex, so they are impractical for an attacker to guess or otherwise discover its value. Entities can consider adding increased complexity by requiring the use of special characters and upper- and lower-case characters, in addition to the minimum standards outlined by this requirement. Additional complexity increases the time required for offline brute force attacks of hashed passwords/passphrases.

Another option for increasing the resistance of passwords to guessing attacks is by comparing proposed password/passphrases to a bad password list and having users provide new passwords for any passwords found on the list.

Customized Approach Objective:
A guessed password/passphrase cannot be verified by either an online or offline brute force attack.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement is not intended to apply to:
• User accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
• Application or system accounts, which are governed by requirements in section 8.6.

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Until 31 March 2025, passwords must be a minimum length of seven characters in accordance with PCI DSS v3.2.1 Requirement 8.2.3.

title: Initial or reset passwords are unique and must be changed immediately upon first use.
methodology:
Control: 8.3.5 – Initial or reset passwords are unique and must be changed immediately upon first use.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: User provisioning and password reset workflows that require unique, non-default passwords. During first login, users encounter a prompt to change their password.
   - Technical Artifacts: System configuration settings enforcing password change on first login; automated audit logs showing initial login events where password change flag is set; unique token or temporary password generation records stored in secure logs.
   - Operational Patterns: Routine account creation and password reset testing logs; evidence that temporary passwords cannot be reused; periodic review of authentication events where password changes post-reset are enforced.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faking Compliance: Organizations might document the policy but use static default passwords across multiple accounts or manually reset them without true enforcement of a change on first use.
   - Surface-level Failures: Relying solely on policy documentation rather than checking automated enforcement; using generic temporary passwords that are predictable; failing to log the enforcement events accurately.
   - Technical Gaps: Absence of system-level enforcement (i.e., not implementing a forced password change prompt), or settings where password change is optional instead of mandatory after a reset.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the system enforces a mandatory password change prompt upon first login or after a password reset.
   WHY: Ensures that even if a temporary or default password is set initially, it cannot be used beyond the first login, preventing unauthorized default credential reuse.
   HOW: Observe a new or reset user account session; check system configuration and logs for the flag that forces a password change; confirm that authentication via temporary password is blocked after the initial login.
   FAIL: If the system allows continued use of the initial temporary password, or if manual intervention is required, then the control is not effectively implemented.

   CHECK: Review audit and event logs to confirm that initial login events include a password change flag and that all temporary passwords are unique per user.
   WHY: Automated logging demonstrates that the mechanism is not only present but is also used consistently, providing an evidence trail.
   HOW: Use log management tools to search for password change events; match account creation or reset timestamps with password change events; ensure logs show unique temporary password issuance for different user accounts.
   FAIL: If logs show missing or generic events, or if temporary passwords appear to be reused across accounts, it indicates a bypass of the intended control.

   CHECK: Test account provisioning and password reset workflows to ensure the generated temporary password is not easily guessable or reused.
   WHY: Verifies that the system generates truly unique and random temporary passwords that are immediately invalidated after use.
   HOW: Create a new account and perform a reset on an existing account; examine the temporary password details (if permitted); check against configuration to ensure they are not following a default or predictable pattern.
   FAIL: If temporary passwords follow a predictable or common pattern (like default values), the control fails in creating genuinely unique credentials.

   CHECK: Validate the configuration settings in the authentication system that enforce immediate password change on login after account creation or reset.
   WHY: Automated settings confirm that the control isn’t just process-based, but also built into the system’s security configuration.
   HOW: Access system configuration interfaces or management consoles; verify parameters/options that demand password change on first login are enabled; cross-check with change management logs for verification.
   FAIL: If configuration settings are misconfigured or the enforced flag is missing, then the process can be bypassed.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut Verification: Directly query the system configuration database or use vendor CLI commands to extract settings related to initial password enforcement. This avoids relying solely on user interviews or documentation.
   - Critical Indicators: Look for repeated default or common temporary password patterns in log data; these are signs that the password generation mechanism may be flawed.
   - Focus on Enforcement: Don’t just check policy documents – ensure that every new account or reset password case triggers a forced change. Automated and technical evidence from administration consoles or audit logs is key.
   - Cross-Verification: Correlate account creation/reset event logs with authentication logs to make sure the “change on first use” flag isn’t being overridden or ignored in practice.
   - Attention to Log Overhead: Regularly review log aggregation outputs to ensure the event logs are complete and tamper-evident, as these are your best indicators that the control is actively enforced.

This methodology provides practical, step-by-step instructions for verifying that initial or reset passwords are unique and immediately changed upon first use, ensuring both system and operational enforcement above superficial compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.5 If passwords/passphrases are used as authentication factors to meet Requirement 8.3.1, they are set and reset for each user as follows:
- Set to a unique value for first-time use and upon reset.
- Forced to be changed immediately after the first use.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.5 Examine procedures for setting and resetting passwords/passphrases and observe that they are performed in accordance with these requirements.

Customized Approach Objective:
Ensures that initial or reset credentials are not vulnerable to unauthorized use.

title: Excessive invalid authentication attempts result in user account lockout.
methodology:
Control: Excessive invalid authentication attempts result in user account lockout

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: System configuration interfaces (admin consoles, security dashboards) display lockout settings with thresholds (≤10 attempts) and lockout durations (≥30 minutes or until identity confirmation).
   • Technical Artifacts: Configuration files, registry settings, or policy definitions that include lockout thresholds and durations; logs indicating account lockouts after repeated failed logins.
   • Operational Patterns: Automated logs showing account lockouts triggered following ~10 invalid login attempts; monitoring alerts when lockouts occur.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might simply include the appropriate settings in configuration files without enforcing them in the authentication logic, or might disable enforcement in non-test environments.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Systems that reset the failure count before reaching 10 attempts under specific conditions, or allow administrative unlocking immediately without an actual delay.
   • Technical Gaps: Not logging the lockout events accurately, or using defaults that bypass the proper lockout duration requirement (e.g., locking out only temporarily during off-peak hours).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the authentication mechanism enforces a maximum of 10 invalid login attempts before lockout.
   WHY: This ensures that brute force attempts are mitigated by limiting the number of guess attempts.
   HOW: Review system configuration settings and policy documentation; trigger a controlled test with a test account, attempting 10 invalid logins in sequence. Check logs for a lockout entry after the 10th attempt.
   FAIL: If the account remains active past 10 invalid attempts, or if the system resets the counter improperly without locking out the account.

   CHECK: Verify that locked accounts remain locked for a minimum of 30 minutes, or until the user’s identity is confirmed.
   WHY: This requirement enforces an adequate delay between login attempts after lockout, preventing continuous attempts.
   HOW: Review system policy rules for lockout duration; perform a controlled test by intentionally triggering a lockout and measuring the time before you are permitted to attempt another login; examine logs to validate that the lock duration is correctly enforced.
   FAIL: If the account automatically resets/re-enables before 30 minutes and without a deliberate identity-confirmation event.

   CHECK: Inspect logs and alerts for evidence of consistent lockout events following failed login attempts.
   WHY: Regular occurrence and logging of lockout events demonstrate that the control is actively enforced in operational settings.
   HOW: Utilize automated log analysis tools to filter for lockout events; verify the timestamp correlation with multiple invalid attempts; confirm that logs include information on both the triggering event and lockout duration.
   FAIL: Absence of consistent log entries for lockouts, or logs showing lockout overrides occurring frequently.

   CHECK: Confirm that the account lockout mechanism cannot be bypassed by administrative or debugging settings in production.
   WHY: Ensuring that enforcement mechanisms cannot be easily disabled is critical for protecting live systems.
   HOW: Interview system administrators; review change logs for any recent modifications to lockout configurations; attempt to audit/run a simulated attack that tests if the lockout bypass is effective.
   FAIL: Discovery of configuration anomalies or exceptions that allow bypassing the lockout mechanism without triggering security alerts.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Observe system dashboards or SIEM feeds for frequent lockout indications – a sudden absence might indicate that the mechanism is improperly configured or disabled.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay attention to logs that mix successful logins immediately following a burst of failed attempts; this might indicate that lockout policies are only superficially configured.
   • Detail Matters: Verify not only the configuration settings but also confirm that enforcement code paths (in the authentication back-end) actually respect these settings, as misaligned configurations can lead to failing the control.
   • Testing in Production vs. Lab: Ensure that testing is done on a production-similar environment to avoid scenarios where test accounts are treated differently from actual user accounts.

This methodology focuses on automated evidence and direct controls verification, minimizing reliance on written procedures alone.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
8.3.4 Invalid authentication attempts are limited by:
- Locking out the user ID after not more than 10 attempts.
- Setting the lockout duration to a minimum of 30 minutes or until the user’s identity is confirmed.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
8.3.4.a Examine system configuration settings to verify that user accounts are locked out after not more than 10 invalid logon attempts.
8.3.4.b Examine system settings to verify that locked accounts remain locked for at least 30 minutes or until identity confirmation.

Customized Approach Objective:
Prevents brute force attempts to guess authentication factors.

Applicability Notes:
Not intended for point-of-sale terminal accounts.

title: User identity is verified before any authentication factor is modified.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes user workflows and UI prompts that require additional verification (e.g. secret questions, callback procedures) before allowing an authentication factor to be changed. 
• Technical artifacts such as system logs or audit trails showing a recorded step for verifying user identity prior to any modifications of authentication data.
• Operational patterns that include periodic testing or alerts on identity verification failures during live session monitoring.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simulate the process through documentation without enforcing real-time identity checks; for example, a form that merely includes a checkbox for identity verification. 
• Surface-level implementations may simply send an email confirmation that can be intercepted, rather than a robust knowledge-based or callback verification method. 
• Technical gaps occur when the verification step is only performed during initial enrollment and not repeated during modification events.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review the system’s modification workflow for authentication factors.
WHY: Ensures that there is an enforced user identity verification step before any authentication factor modification is allowed.
HOW: Interview operational staff, examine system design documents, and review UI requirements to see that a verification control (e.g., secret Q&A, callback, or pre-established phone number matching) is integrated. 
FAIL: Absence of any additional prompt or the process shows a simple unverified modification request process.

CHECK: Inspect system logs and audit trails for modification events involving authentication factors.
WHY: Automated logs provide evidence of the identity verification step being executed before modification.
HOW: Query logs to verify that every modification event in authentication factors has an associated log entry detailing a verification check (e.g., callback initiated, secret answer validated).
FAIL: Logs display modifications without corresponding identity verification events.

CHECK: Perform a live test in a controlled environment by requesting an authentication factor change.
WHY: A live test not only checks for procedures but also operational effectiveness.
HOW: Follow the procedure as an authorized user and note that the system requests a verification mechanism (e.g., additional phone confirmation) before processing the change; then, simulate an attempt by an unauthorized individual to see if the process fails.
FAIL: The test allows a change without additional identity verification or easily bypassable verification steps.

CHECK: Examine configuration settings and integration points with multi-factor authentication systems.
WHY: Verifying configuration ensures that identity verification is not bypassed and is integrated into authentication modifications.
HOW: Review configuration management databases (CMDBs) and code repositories for implementation code that enforces the verification step, and test the control via API calls if available.
FAIL: Missing or misconfigured integration that does not cross-reference identity before authentication changes.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Look for logs that mark a “verification success” event; missing markers are a red flag.
• Critical indicators include mismatches between documented procedures and logs, and processes that do not require a second factor for changes. 
• In practice, ensure that the verification control is not simply a checkbox in the UI but invokes a robust, verifiable mechanism that cannot be easily simulated via automation.
• Details matter: Validate that the process forces human interaction (callback or knowledge question) rather than just an automatic email confirmation which could be intercepted.
• Always ensure that audit trails are immutable and independently verified to avoid falsification.
content:
Table Content:
Defined Approach Requirements: 8.3.3 User identity is verified before modifying any authentication factor.
Customized Approach Objective: Unauthorized individuals cannot gain system access by impersonating an authorized user.
Defined Approach Testing Procedures: 8.3.3 Examine procedures for modifying authentication factors and observe that user identity is verified prior to modification.
Purpose: Prevents social engineering attacks where an imposter might request a change in authentication factors.
Good Practice: Verification methods may include secret questions, knowledge-based verification, or callback procedures.
Examples: Using a previously established phone number for verification.

title: Authentication factors are protected using strong cryptography.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes updated system configurations and active deployment of cryptographic protocols for data in transit (e.g., TLS settings) and data at rest (e.g., encrypted databases or storage volumes).
• Technical artifacts such as network capture logs (showing encrypted transport layers) and database encryption settings (e.g., encryption keys configuration) demonstrate compliance.
• Operational patterns include consistent use of cryptographic libraries, timely certificate updates, and cryptography-specific auditing events in SIEM logs.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim compliance by simply deploying a TLS layer without validating proper cipher suite configurations (which might be weak) or ensuring that stored data is also encrypted.
• Surface-level implementations include using outdated protocols (e.g., SSL, early TLS versions) or weak encryption algorithms that meet the letter of policy but not the spirit, e.g., DES or RC4.
• Often missed details include not enforcing encryption on all endpoints, or neglecting to encrypt temporary storage and backups.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify encryption in transit settings for authentication factors
WHY: Ensures all authentication factors are never transmitted in cleartext and protected by strong, modern TLS configurations.
HOW: Capture network traffic during authentication exchanges using a packet sniffer (e.g., Wireshark) and verify that data is encrypted using modern protocols (TLS 1.2 or higher) with strong cipher suites.
FAIL: Detect cleartext transmission, usage of outdated protocols or weak cipher suites (e.g., SSL or early TLS).

CHECK: Confirm encryption at rest for authentication factors in repositories
WHY: Guarantees stored credentials remain unreadable even if storage is breached.
HOW: Review system configurations and audit logs to confirm that stored authentication factors are encrypted using robust algorithms (e.g., AES-256). Check databases, flat files, and backup storage for encryption metadata.
FAIL: Evidence of unencrypted files or use of weak encryption algorithms for stored credentials.

CHECK: Review system configuration and vendor documentation
WHY: Validates that system components are configured to enforce encryption policies consistently.
