   WHY: Automation minimizes human error in managing user privileges and ensures consistency in enforcing need-to-know policies across all systems.
   HOW: Review system integration setups (e.g., API connections between the HR system and the access control system) and verify automated provisioning processes with sample transactions.
   FAIL: Manual processes without automation, or discrepancies between provisioning and actual access configuration, indicate weak control reliability.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Directly query active sessions or real-time access logs during system audits to observe access control behavior in actual operation rather than relying solely on documentation.
• Critical Indicator: Pay extra attention to system components such as legacy applications or cloud services that might not be fully integrated into central access control systems.
• Details Matter: Ensure that periodic role reviews are evidenced; mere one-time configurations often hide drift in the access controls over time.

Overall, real compliance is demonstrated not just by policies or documentation, but by automated, verifiable enforcement across every system component that aligns with the need-to-know principle.
content:
7.3.1 An access control system(s) is in place that restricts access based on a user’s need to know and covers all system components.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.3.1 Examine vendor documentation and system settings to verify that access is managed for each system component via an access control system(s) that restricts access based on a user’s need to know and covers all system components.

Customized Approach Objective
Access rights and privileges are managed via mechanisms intended for that purpose.

Purpose
Without a mechanism to restrict access based on user’s need to know, a user may unknowingly be granted access to cardholder data. Access control systems automate the process of restricting access and assigning privileges. Restricting privileged access with an access control system reduces the opportunity for errors in the assignment of permissions to individuals, applications, and systems.

title: User access to query cardholder data repositories is restricted.
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for ensuring that user access to query cardholder data repositories is properly restricted, as per PCI DSS req-7.2.6.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of well-defined role-based access controls (RBAC) in system configurations and access management solutions.
• Technical artifacts include database or application logs showing that only authorized administrator accounts are performing direct, unfiltered queries; configuration files and stored procedures that limit access to cardholder data.
• Operational patterns observed include automated role assignments, routine review of query logs, and alerts for any ad hoc queries by non-administrators.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply document policies without enforcing them in the systems; for example, having all users with extended privileges instead of limiting query commands to admin roles.
• Some might rely on superficial configuration settings but fail to correctly implement stored procedures and role restrictions, or they may grant broad access for convenience.
• Frequent gaps include failure to disable direct ad hoc query access for non-admin accounts, or missing rigorous audit/logging of query actions to detect abuse.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) configuration
WHY: Ensures that only designated administrator roles have the ability to perform direct queries on cardholder data repositories.
HOW: Review system configuration settings and access control lists (ACLs) in the database and/or application. Check that stored procedures or controlled interfaces are used by non-admin users, and confirm that any direct ad hoc query capability is restricted to authorized administrative accounts.
FAIL: If non-administrative accounts are found with permissions to perform unfiltered database queries or access CHD repositories directly.

CHECK: Analyze Database and Application Logs for Query Activities
WHY: Logs provide real-time evidence that only responsible administrators are executing direct queries on cardholder data.
HOW: Utilize automated tools to extract and analyze query logs, verifying that all direct queries against CHD repositories are issued by defined administrative accounts. Look for exceptions where non-admin accounts are generating direct queries.
FAIL: Discovery of logs showing ad hoc queries performed by non-authorized users indicating bypass of role restrictions.

CHECK: Review the Implementation of Stored Procedures or Controlled Query Interfaces
WHY: Confirms that programmatic methods are employed to restrict data query actions to only allowed operations based on roles and least privileges.
HOW: Inspect the database objects (like stored procedures, views) and associated permissions to ensure that they do not permit unfiltered access. Verify that only the administrator accounts have override capabilities while all other roles rely on controlled methods.
FAIL: Finding that stored procedures or controlled access methods are not enforced, or that direct table access is permitted beyond the boundaries defined by policy.

CHECK: Interview Personnel and Validate Operational Processes
WHY: Personnel interviews can confirm that the process for granting access and reviewing privileges aligns with the documented policies and that there is a clear distinction between administrator duties and everyday user access.
HOW: Interview IT and database security administrators to verify that the policy is actively enforced. Request a walkthrough of the process for access provisioning and de-provisioning, and confirm evidence of periodic reviews and audits.
FAIL: If interviews reveal a lack of awareness, contradictory access practices, or evidence that role assignments deviate from documented procedures.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Focus on automated log review tools that correlate account roles with query actions. Anomalies in log patterns often signal that the control has been bypassed or is too permissive.
• Critical Indicator: The presence and frequency of routine, unfiltered query actions executed outside of scheduled maintenance or administrative windows suggests poor implementation.
• Final Check: Look for exception reports in periodic audits that flag any non-admin query events. Consistently missing or vague audit trails are a red flag for ineffective control enforcement.

Using these steps ensures that the control not only exists on paper but is effectively enforced, and any potential evasions are detected and addressed promptly.
content:
7.2.6 All user access to query repositories of stored cardholder data is restricted as follows:
- Via applications or other programmatic methods, with access and allowed actions based on user roles and least privileges.
- Only the responsible administrator(s) can directly access or query repositories of stored CHD.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.6.a Examine policies and procedures and interview personnel to verify processes are defined for granting user access to query repositories of stored cardholder data, in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.6.b Examine configuration settings for querying repositories of stored cardholder data to verify they are in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Customized Approach Objective
Direct unfiltered (ad hoc) query access to cardholder data repositories is prohibited, unless performed by an authorized administrator.

Applicability Notes
This requirement applies to controls for user access to query repositories of stored cardholder data.
See Requirements 7.2.5 and 7.2.5.1 and 8.6.1 through 8.6.3 for controls for application and system accounts.

Purpose
The misuse of query access to repositories of cardholder data has been a regular cause of data breaches. Limiting such access to administrators reduces the risk of such access being abused by unauthorized users.

Definitions
"Programmatic methods" means granting access through means such as database stored procedures that allow users to perform controlled actions to data in a table, rather than via direct, unfiltered access to the data repository by end users (except for the responsible administrator(s), who need direct access to the database for their administrative duties).

Good Practice
Typical user actions include moving, copying, and deleting data. Also consider the scope of privilege needed when granting access. For example, access can be granted to specific objects such as data elements, files, tables, indexes, views, and stored routines. Granting access to repositories of cardholder data should follow the same process as all other granted access, meaning that it is based on roles, with only the privileges assigned to each user that are needed to perform their job functions.

title: Access for application and system accounts is reviewed periodically.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for documented review schedules, meeting minutes with management sign-off, and tickets or logs indicating periodic account review sessions.
   • Technical Artifacts: Evidence in configuration management or identity governance systems showing timestamps, reviewer IDs, and decisions related to application/system account access. Risk analysis documents (based on Req 12.3.1) that define the review frequency are also key indicators.
   • Operational Patterns: Recurring automated alerts or reports for account reviews; periodic notifications to responsible personnel for account access evaluation.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply fill out review checklists without performing an actual analysis or update, thereby producing dated reports that do not reflect current account statuses.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on manual attestations or static policy documents that are not periodically validated against actual account configurations or automated system reports.
   • Technical Gaps: Inconsistent integration between the risk analysis (Req 12.3.1) and the operational review process; missing audit trails indicating when access was reviewed and remediated.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of formal policies and procedures defining the periodic review process for application and system accounts.
   WHY: This ensures that there is a documented, repeatable process standardized across the organization.
   HOW: Inspect policy documents, review risk analysis records (referencing Req 12.3.1), and match them with operational schedules for reviews.
   FAIL: Absence of clear review guidelines or a mismatch between policy frequency and documented review dates.

   CHECK: Assess the documented evidence and artifacts of periodic reviews including logs, ticketing systems entries, and management sign-offs.
   WHY: Demonstrates that the review process has been executed in practice and validates that access rights were actually evaluated periodically.
   HOW: Examine logs, meeting minutes, audit trails, or tickets indicating dates, reviewers, and corrective actions taken for inappropriate access.
   FAIL: Incomplete, missing, or stale evidence that does not align with the risk analysis’ defined frequency.

   CHECK: Interview responsible personnel to confirm that reviews are conducted actively and any remediation steps are addressed promptly.
   WHY: Interviews provide qualitative assurance that the process is embedded in operational activities, not just in documentation.
   HOW: Prepare questions on how and when reviews are performed, what criteria are used, and how issues or excessive privileges are remediated; cross-check with actual review evidence.
   FAIL: Personnel are unable to describe the operational process or provide details that contradict the provided documentation.

   CHECK: Confirm that management formally acknowledges that application and system account privileges are appropriate following each review.
   WHY: Management acknowledgment is key in ensuring that reviews carry accountability and that any deficiencies are escalated and remediated.
   HOW: Look for formal sign-off documents or email trails from management confirming the completed reviews and actions taken on detected issues.
   FAIL: Lack of management sign-offs or evidence pointing to bypassed or cursory acknowledgment of the review outcomes.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Quick Verification Shortcut: Check if the risk analysis document (Req 12.3.1) explicitly ties a review frequency to application and system accounts. If absent, it’s a red flag.
   • Critical Indicator: Consistent and time-stamped logs entries that clearly show the review process was not a one-time event, but recurring per the defined interval.
   • Detail Focus: Pay close attention to how remediation and management acknowledgments are documented. Vague terminology or missing timestamps may indicate poor implementation.
   • Cross-Verification: Validate that the schedule in the review logs and automated alerts aligns with the frequency defined in the risk analysis.
   • Additional Insight: Use automated scripts to extract and review audit logs to ensure process integrity—a practice that rarely gets faked compared to manual attestation.
content:
7.2.5.1  All access by application and system accounts and related access privileges are reviewed as follows:
- Periodically (at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1).
- The application/system access remains appropriate for the function being performed.
- Any inappropriate access is addressed.
- Management acknowledges that access remains appropriate.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.5.1.a  Examine policies and procedures to verify they define processes to review all application and system accounts and related access privileges in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.5.1.b  Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for the frequency of periodic reviews of application and system accounts and related access privileges to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.

7.2.5.1.c  Interview responsible personnel and examine documented results of periodic reviews of system and application accounts and related privileges to verify that the reviews occur in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose
Regular review of access rights helps to detect excessive access rights remaining after system functions change, or other application or system modifications occur. If excessive rights are not removed when no longer needed, they may be used by malicious users for unauthorized access.

Customized Approach Objective
Application and system account privilege assignments are verified periodically by management as correct, and nonconformities are remediated.

Applicability Notes
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Application and system accounts are assigned and managed based on least privilege.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Documentation and screenshots showing configurations of application and system accounts, with details on their privileges. Evidence such as system audit logs indicating account usage and controls (e.g., restricted login times or allowed hosts) can be observed.
   • Technical Artifacts: Configuration files, system settings (e.g., Linux /etc/sudoers, Windows Local/Security Policy, account group memberships), and automated reports listing account privileges. Tools that enumerate effective permissions and compare them against a defined baseline are key artifacts.
   • Operational Patterns: Routine reviews, monitoring alerts for unauthorized privilege escalation, and periodic account audits that demonstrate active management and enforcement of least privilege.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might maintain policy documents that describe least privilege without enforcing them practically. They can create user accounts with minimal configurations on paper, but then assign them unnecessary elevated privileges in system settings.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Mere role definitions in documentation without periodic reviews, or providing accounts with embedded higher privileges (e.g., adding application accounts to administrators groups) that are not immediately visible without deep technical checks.
   • Technical Gaps: Failing to restrict accounts to specific systems or processes; accounts may have inherited privileges from group memberships that are not directly tied to the application. Lack of automated account reviews that can highlight drift from the least privilege model is a frequent oversight.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Account Privilege Assignments
   WHY: Confirms that account privileges strictly match what is required for operational purposes, enforcing the principle of least privilege.
   HOW: (1) Extract a sample list of application/system accounts from the identity management system; (2) Review group memberships and direct privileges; (3) Compare the defined permissions in configuration management with the granting of privileges.
   FAIL: Discovery of accounts with privileges beyond what the application or system needs (e.g., membership in local or domain administrator groups) indicates non-compliance.

   CHECK: Review Restriction Configurations
   WHY: Ensures that accounts are limited to specific systems or processes, reducing the risk of broader privileges.
   HOW: (1) Inspect configuration settings to determine if accounts are restricted by machine, time, or network; (2) Validate against documented controls such as host restrictions or allowed application contexts; (3) Check automated configurations in platforms like Active Directory or configuration management systems.
   FAIL: If accounts are allowed generic logins without restrictions or are configured on multiple systems without approval, the control is failing.

   CHECK: Analyze Audit Logs and Monitoring Reports
   WHY: Regular monitoring is a practical indicator that least privilege is enforced and any deviations are actively detected.
   HOW: (1) Perform a log review focusing on account usage, including unexpected elevated privileges or anomalous login attempts; (2) Verify alerts or reports generated by security monitoring tools on privilege escalations; (3) Interview operations teams to validate regular reviews and escalation processes.
   FAIL: Inadequate logging, absence of review, or unaddressed alerts regarding privilege escalation imply ineffective enforcement.

   CHECK: Confirm Process and Technical Controls Through Interviews
   WHY: Interviews with application owners and system administrators can reveal gaps between documented policies and real-world implementations.
   HOW: (1) Ask technical staff to demonstrate how they manage and verify least privilege assignments; (2) Request to see recent examples of account reviews and adjustments following audits; (3) Validate that there is a feedback loop between policy documentation and technical enforcement.
   FAIL: If there is a disconnect between what is documented and the actual steps taken, or if staff are unaware of proper configurations, compliance is likely superficial.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Automatically extract effective privilege settings using established scripts or configuration management tools. Compare against a known good baseline for anomalies.
   • Critical Indicators: Watch for service accounts that have not been reviewed recently, or that show signs of excessive privileges. Hidden inherited rights from group memberships are common red flags.
   • Details that Matter: Focus on both the explicit privileges set on each account and the indirect permissions inherited through group associations or system defaults. Documentation consistency with technical settings is key.

This methodology emphasizes real-world checks on the operational enforcement of least privilege, ensuring that application and system account privileges are tightly controlled and consistently reviewed.
content:
7.2.5
All application and system accounts and related access privileges are assigned and managed as follows:
- Based on the least privileges necessary for the operability of the system or application.
- Access is limited to the systems, applications, or processes that specifically require their use.

Customized Approach Objective
Access rights granted to application and system accounts are limited to only the access needed for the operability of that application or system.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.5.a
Examine policies and procedures to verify they define processes to manage and assign application and system accounts and related access privileges in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.5.b
Examine privileges associated with system and application accounts and interview responsible personnel to verify that application and system accounts and related access privileges are assigned and managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Guidance

Purpose
It is important to establish the appropriate access level for application or system accounts. If such accounts are compromised, malicious users will receive the same access level as granted to the application or system. Therefore, it is important to ensure limited access is granted to system and application accounts on the same basis as to user accounts.

Good Practice
Entities may want to consider establishing a baseline when setting up these application and system accounts including the following as applicable to the organization:
- Making sure that the account is not a member of a privileged group such as domain administrators, local administrators, or root.
- Restricting which computers the account can be used on.
- Restricting hours of use.
- Removing any additional settings like VPN access and remote access.

title: All user accounts and related access privileges are reviewed periodically.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for records of scheduled reviews (e.g., meeting minutes, approved review reports, automated logs) that detail periodic checks of user accounts including third-party/vendor accounts. Evidence of signed management acknowledgment forms or workflows confirming access validity is important.
• Technical Artifacts: Check automated reports from Identity and Access Management (IAM) systems that list last review timestamps, change logs capturing modifications and remediation actions, and system audit trails. Look for change request records that capture nonconformities and their resolution.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent six-month review cycle documented in calendar invites, recurring tasks within change management systems, and integration of periodic reviews into continuous monitoring dashboards.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations may reuse outdated review reports or generic policy statements without actual evidence of recent account reviews. They might generate one-off reports without consistent timelines.
• Surface-level Implementations: Relying solely on documentation or static snapshots rather than real, automated logging and remediation evidence. Reviews that are tick-box exercises without clear evidence tied to system alerts or remediation actions.
• Technical Gaps: Omitting third-party/vendor or cloud service accounts, incomplete account inventories, or failure to capture remediation actions in an automated system.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review the log of periodic review reports for user accounts (including third-party/vendor accounts) executed within the past six months.
WHY: These reports confirm that the organization adheres to the required six-month review frequency and covers all account types.
HOW: Access the IAM dashboard or designated repository where review reports are stored; verify date stamps and scope of accounts.
FAIL: Absence of recent automated reports or reliance on outdated documentation indicates noncompliance.

CHECK: Verify documented remediation actions for any nonconforming or inappropriate access privileges identified during the reviews.
WHY: This shows that the periodic reviews are not merely perfunctory, but actively lead to corrective actions.
HOW: Inspect remediation work orders, change management logs, or IT service management records that tie nonconformities with timely corrective actions.
FAIL: Lack of clear, traceable remediation evidence suggests that inappropriate accesses are not being addressed.

CHECK: Interview responsible personnel to confirm the operational process for account reviews and how responsibilities are delineated.
WHY: Direct interviews ensure that personnel can describe the technical process and verify that reviews are operational, not just documented.
HOW: Ask questions regarding the scheduling, tools used, how discrepancies are handled, and request demonstration of active review mechanisms.
FAIL: Inability of staff to explain the process or discrepancies between their explanation and available artifacts indicates poor implementation.

CHECK: Cross-check system logs for account creation, modification, and deletion timelines to ensure they align with review cycle findings.
WHY: Validates that changes to account privileges are posted and tracked continuously between reviews, not just retroactively documented every six months.
HOW: Analyze IAM or security logs, focusing on event timelines and correlating them with review report dates; look for anomalies or signs of batch processing of reviews.
FAIL: Discrepancies in logs or absence of continuous monitoring signals that reviews are not integrated into the operational environment.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Use automated IAM reporting features to extract a comprehensive list of all account types and their last reviewed dates. Tools that integrate alerting for overdue reviews are a strong indicator of operational maturity.
• Indicator of Poor Implementation: If the review process only covers standard user accounts while ignoring third-party, cloud, or vendor accounts, expect compliance gaps.
• Critical Details: Ensure that remediation evidence is corroborated by both documented policies and observable technical actions (such as account deactivation logs or privilege changes) to avoid reliance on self-attested compliance.
• Tip on Consistency: Regularly sample accounts across different systems to verify that the review process is uniformly applied rather than selectively implemented in isolated areas.

This methodology emphasizes the need for verifying the operational effectiveness of the periodic reviews, ensuring that the process is automated, observable, and results in tangible remediation actions rather than merely existing in policy documents.
content:
7.2.4  All user accounts and related access privileges, including third-party/vendor accounts, are reviewed as follows:
- At least once every six months.
- To ensure user accounts and access remain appropriate based on job function.
- Any inappropriate access is addressed.
- Management acknowledges that access remains appropriate.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.4.a Examine policies and procedures to verify they define processes to review all user accounts and related access privileges, including third-party/vendor accounts, in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.4.b Interview responsible personnel and examine documented results of periodic reviews of user accounts to verify that all the results are in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Customized Approach Objective
Account privilege assignments are verified periodically by management as correct, and nonconformities are remediated.

Applicability Notes
This requirement applies to all user accounts and related access privileges, including those used by personnel and third parties/vendors, and accounts used to access third-party cloud services.

See Requirements 7.2.5 and 7.2.5.1 and 8.6.1 through 8.6.3 for controls for application and system accounts.
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

title: Required privileges are approved by authorized personnel.
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for the control 'Required privileges are approved by authorized personnel.'

1. CHECK: Verification of documented approval records for privilege assignments
   WHY: If each privilege assignment has a corresponding documented approval by authorized personnel, it ensures that access isn’t arbitrarily granted and aligns with job requirements.
   HOW: a) Extract a sample of user IDs and their assigned privileges from system directories or access management tools (e.g., Active Directory, IAM systems).
        b) Obtain approval records (e.g., signed email confirmations, ticketing system approvals, or formal approval forms) and cross-reference them against these assignments.
        c) Verify that approvers are listed as authorized personnel according to policy.
   FAIL: If there are privilege assignments missing corresponding approval records or if the approving personnel do not have proper authorization, this indicates a failure.

2. CHECK: Comparison of technical artifact logs with documented approval evidence
   WHY: Automated logs and system audit trails from identity management systems help confirm that the approved changes took place as documented, indicating operational effectiveness beyond static policies.
   HOW: a) Review the system’s change logs or audit trails that record when privilege changes are made.
        b) Identify entries where privilege changes occurred, then match the change records with the documented approval evidence.
        c) Confirm that the date, user, and changer information align with the approval documentation.
   FAIL: If logs indicate privilege updates without corresponding documented approvals or if dates and personnel do not match, this shows a bypass of the control.

3. CHECK: Interview and process walkthrough with authorized personnel and system administrators
   WHY: Engaging with the personnel who execute and review privilege assignments offers insights that documentation alone might miss, such as informal workarounds or de facto practices.
   HOW: a) Conduct interviews with personnel responsible for the privilege review process to understand how approvals are captured and verified.
        b) Request a demonstration of the process (for example, how a new privilege is documented and subsequently logged in the system).
        c) Ask for examples of recent changes and trace them through all steps of the approval process.
   FAIL: If personnel cannot clearly articulate the process or if steps appear to be bypassed, it indicates potential weakness in the control’s implementation.

Expert Tips:
- Verify that criteria for 'authorized personnel' are clearly defined and consistently applied. Look for approval records that include job titles or roles which match the authorization guidelines.
- Be wary of controls that rely solely on manual document submissions, as they can be easily faked; seek corroborating technical evidence in system logs.
- Pay special attention to outlier cases (for example, temporary accounts or contractors) to ensure that all privilege assignments are supported with proper authorization.
- Automation is key: Prefer evidence from integrated identity management or ticketing systems that automatically enforce and log the necessary approvals.
- When in doubt, trace a random sample of at least 5-10% of privilege assignments to detect recurring issues.

This methodology moves beyond policies to verify that operational practices are robust, and that technical artifacts match the documented approval processes, ensuring real, effective implementation.
content:
7.2.3 Required privileges are approved by authorized personnel.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.3.a Examine policies and procedures to verify they define processes for approval of all privileges by authorized personnel.

7.2.3.b Examine user IDs and assigned privileges, and compare with documented approvals to verify that:
• Documented approval exists for the assigned privileges.
• The approval was by authorized personnel.
• Specified privileges match the roles assigned to the individual.

Guidance

Purpose
Documented approval (for example, in writing or electronically) assures that those with access and privileges are known and authorized by management, and that their access is necessary for their job function.

Customized Approach Objective
Access privileges cannot be granted to users without appropriate, documented authorization.

title: Access is assigned to users based on job classification and least privilege.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Actual role definitions, access control matrices, and PAM system configurations that explicitly tie user accounts to job classifications and enforce least privilege. Screenshots or system views showing role-based access assignments are critical.
• Technical Artifacts: System logs reflecting provisioning changes, automated role assignment records, and periodic access review reports. Evidence from privileged account management tools (if used) is also essential.
• Operational Patterns: Regular reviews and audits of user access, documented processes for periodic re-certification of privileges, and interviews that confirm that users receive only the access required for their job roles.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations may draft comprehensive policies without actual enforcement. A common evasion is to show approved policies while actual system configurations grant broader privileges.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Creating roles that nominally match job classifications without rigorous periodic review or bypassing the policy via manual changes that are not logged.
• Technical Gaps: Failing to integrate PAM tools properly, not automating revocation when job roles change, or having outdated user-to-role mappings that remain in the system.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify role-based access assignments in actual system configurations
WHY: This check confirms that users are only assigned the roles (and associated privileges) matching their job classification and no more.
HOW: Examine system access control lists, review RBAC configurations, and compare user account details against documented job roles. Use automated scripts to extract role assignments and cross-check them with HR records or job descriptions.
FAIL: If users are found with roles that exceed or deviate from their job requirements, or if role assignment evidence cannot be correlated with job classifications.

CHECK: Inspect technical control artifacts such as access control matrices and log records from PAM/RBAC tools
WHY: These artifacts provide objective proof that least privilege enforcement is active and that changes in access rights are properly tracked.
HOW: Retrieve and analyze recent log excerpts or audit trails that show user provisioning, role changes, and periodic reviews. Ensure these logs are automated and tamper-resistant.
FAIL: Missing logs, incomplete records, or discrepancies between documented policy and actual configurations indicate failure.

CHECK: Conduct interviews with access management personnel
WHY: Direct interviews can reveal whether privileged access is truly allocated based on job roles and if procedures are followed in practice.
HOW: Ask questions about the process for assigning, reviewing, and revoking access. Confirm that multi-level approval processes are in place and that access is strictly tied to job functions.
FAIL: Inconsistent responses, reliance on manual workarounds, or a lack of clarity in the process points to non-compliance.

CHECK: Execute automated scripts to analyze audit logs for anomalies such as unauthorized privilege escalation
WHY: Continuous monitoring helps verify that the system effectively enforces least privilege and that deviations are quickly detected.
HOW: Run scripts to scan logs for events indicating ad hoc access changes or deviations from the predefined role assignments. Compare the frequency of such events against expected patterns.
FAIL: Absence of regular audit logs, untimely detection of privilege changes, or high frequency of anomalies signal ineffective control.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Leverage automated log parsers and centralized monitoring dashboards to quickly spot misconfigurations or over-privileged accounts.
• Critical Indicators: Look for undocumented manual access changes and mismatches between HR records and system assignments; these are prime indicators of poor implementation.
• Details That Matter: The alignment between the provisioning system, the PAM tool (if deployed), and the documented role definitions is critical. Discrepancies here often reveal systemic issues.
• Beware of One-Off Exceptions: Temporary elevation of privileges should be clearly documented with automatic revocation. Any exception to the least privileged model may signal deeper compliance risks.
content:
7.2.2 Access is assigned to users, including privileged users, based on:  
- Job classification and function.  
- Least privileges necessary to perform job responsibilities.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.2.a Examine policies and procedures to verify they cover assigning access to users in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.2.b Examine user access settings, including for privileged users, and interview responsible management personnel to verify that privileges assigned are in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.2.c Interview personnel responsible for assigning access to verify that privileged user access is assigned in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Guidance

Purpose
Assigning least privileges helps prevent users without sufficient knowledge about the application from incorrectly or accidentally changing application configuration or altering its security settings. Enforcing least privilege also helps to minimize the scope of damage if an unauthorized person gains access to a user ID.

Good Practice
Access rights are granted to a user by assignment to one or several functions. Access is assigned depending on the specific user functions and with the minimum scope required for the job. When assigning privileged access, it is important to assign individuals only the privileges they need to perform their job (the "least privileges"). For example, the database administrator or backup administrator should not be assigned the same privileges as the overall systems administrator. Once needs are defined for user functions (per PCI DSS requirement 7.2.1), it is easy to grant individuals access according to their job classification and function by using the already-created roles. Entities may wish to consider use of Privileged Access Management (PAM), which is a method to grant access to privileged accounts only when those privileges are required, immediately revoking that access once they are no longer needed.

title: An access control model is defined with appropriate access and least privileges.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Look for an official, current Access Control Policy that specifies roles, responsibilities, and access rights based on job functions and the least privileges principle.
• Identify technical artifacts such as role-based access control (RBAC) configurations in the identity and access management (IAM) system, including role definitions that align with business needs and job classifications.
• Observe operational patterns like routine reviews of role assignments, access logs showing adherence to least privileges, and separation of duties in processes (e.g., distinct roles for system administration and audit functions).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might rely solely on outdated or overly generalized documentation that does not reflect the actual system configurations.
• Surface-level implementations might involve pre-defined roles that are not regularly updated, leading to accumulation of excess privileges over time.
• Technical gaps include failing to enforce separation of duties or not aligning IAM configurations with the documented procedures, thereby allowing easy circumvention via administrative overrides.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK 1: Verify Documented Access Control Policy & Procedures
WHY: An up-to-date policy that defines role-based access and least privilege requirements is foundational.
HOW: Interview key personnel and review the official documentation to confirm that it covers granting access based on job classifications, specified access rights, and separation of duties.
FAIL: Missing or outdated policies, or policies that do not address least privilege or separation of duties.

CHECK 2: Review IAM Role Configurations and Access Rights
WHY: Direct examination of IAM settings shows how the policy is practically implemented, ensuring users are granted only the rights needed for their roles.
HOW: Access the IAM system or request a role-and-access matrix export. Verify roles, permissions, and their alignment with documented job functions and the least privilege principle.
FAIL: Discrepancies between the documented roles and the IAM system settings (e.g., roles with excessive permissions or a mismatch in access rights).

CHECK 3: Validate Separation of Duties and Cross-Role Assignment
WHY: Effective implementation prevents conflicts of interest and fraud by ensuring that critical tasks such as configuration/administration and audit are not controlled by a single entity.
HOW: Review the role assignment charts and audit logs. Confirm that roles with high-level access (like system administrators) do not overlap with audit or change approval functions, and that multiple individuals are involved in end-to-end critical processes.
FAIL: Single individuals holding access that spans multiple sensitive areas, or lack of role segregation in critical functions.

CHECK 4: Automated Log & Configuration Monitoring
WHY: Continuous monitoring is essential to ensure that role assignments and access modifications follow the defined access control model.
HOW: Validate that automated tools are in place (e.g., SIEM and configuration management software) to log access changes and to trigger alerts when privilege deviations occur.
FAIL: Absence of monitoring tools, irregular or missing logs, or no alert mechanisms for suspicious permission changes.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Prioritize a real-time review of IAM system screenshots or extracts over static policy documents to confirm live implementations.
• Interviews should probe for recent changes in role configurations and adherence to the separation of duties, as these areas are high-risk for non-compliance.
• Check that the controls are not only designed but also regularly tested and adjusted to reflect evolving business needs and access requirements, ensuring dynamic enforcement of the least-privilege principle.
content:
7.2.1 An access control model is defined and includes granting access as follows:
• Appropriate access depending on the entity’s business and access needs.
• Access to system components and data resources that is based on users’ job classification and functions.
• The least privileges required (for example, user, administrator) to perform a job function.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.2.1.a Examine documented policies and procedures and interview personnel to verify the access control model is defined in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

7.2.1.b Examine access control model settings and verify that access needs are appropriately defined in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose
Defining an access control model that is appropriate for the entity’s technology and access control philosophy supports a consistent and uniform way of allocating access and reduces the possibility of errors such as the granting of excessive rights.

Good Practice
A factor to consider when defining access needs is the separation of duties principle. This principle is intended to prevent fraud and misuse or theft of resources. For example, 1) dividing mission-critical functions and information system support functions among different individuals and/or functions, 2) establishing roles such that information system support activities are performed by different functions/individuals (for example, system management, programming, configuration management, quality assurance and testing, and network security), and 3) ensuring security personnel administering access control functions do not also administer audit functions.
In environments where one individual performs multiple functions, such as administration and security operations, duties may be assigned so that no single individual has end-to-end control of a process without an independent checkpoint. For example, responsibility for configuration and responsibility for approving changes could be assigned to separate individuals.

title: Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 7 are documented and understood.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence: Formal documentation exists (policies, procedures, or a dedicated roles/responsibilities assignment document such as a RACI matrix) that outlines who is responsible for activities under Requirement 7. 
   - Technical artifacts: Versions of the responsibility assignment matrix, access control matrices, or system logs indicating role assignments for actions performed in line with Requirement 7. 
   - Operational patterns: Regular review cycles, employee trainings or acknowledgments (signed, digital or otherwise) that confirm personnel understand their tasks and responsibilities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations may simply produce outdated or generic documentation that is not tailored to activities in Requirement 7. 
   - Policies may state roles exist, but detailed job-specific activities or responsibilities are missing or ambiguously defined, making it unclear who is accountable. 
   - Personnel interviews could be coached or responses rehearsed, providing an illusion of proper role assignment without real operational understanding.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of formal documentation listing roles and responsibilities for activities covered in Requirement 7.
   WHY: This provides tangible evidence that a structured approach to access control and assigned responsibilities is in place.
   HOW: Review policy documents, procedure manuals, and RACI matrices; check revision dates and update logs to ensure current relevance.
   FAIL: Absence of a dedicated document or the presence of outdated/generic documentation that doesn’t specifically address Requirement 7 responsibilities.

   CHECK: Interview selected personnel whose roles include performing activities under Requirement 7.
   WHY: Direct conversations confirm that roles have been communicated and that personnel understand their specific duties.
   HOW: Conduct interviews and ask specific questions about daily tasks, responsibilities, and how they relate to the access control model (e.g., RBAC or ABAC). Verify whether they have acknowledged their roles via signed acknowledgments or meeting minutes.
   FAIL: Personnel are unable to clearly articulate their responsibilities or if several employees suggest different role definitions than what is documented.

   CHECK: Cross-check documentation against actual assignments in system configurations or access control management tools.
   WHY: This ensures that the documented roles match the implemented access control settings.
   HOW: Compare the roles/responsibilities matrix from documentation with system user roles in administration consoles, user provisioning systems or access logs.
   FAIL: Misalignment where documented roles do not match system configurations or access rights, indicating a gap between policy and practice.

   CHECK: Look for evidence of continuous review and update processes for roles and responsibilities.
   WHY: Regular reviews indicate the organization’s commitment to keeping the documentation accurate and operationally effective.
   HOW: Review audit logs, meeting minutes, or training schedule records that include discussions or updates regarding these roles, ensuring that changes to business functions are reflected.
   FAIL: No records of periodic reviews or evidence that roles and responsibilities haven’t been revisited since initial documentation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Verification Shortcut: Quickly verify the validity of the RACI or access control matrix under Requirement 7. Check the document’s latest revision date and confirm that it aligns with the current organizational structure. 
   - Critical Indicator: Inconsistent or vague definitions in interviews (such as ambiguous phrasing like 'handle accesses') or mismatches between documented roles and system roles signal weak implementation. 
   - Real-World Detail: Over-reliance on centralized documentation without updated growth (e.g. forgotten changes due to acquisitions or departmental restructures) is a red flag. 
   - Always cross-reference employee interview data with system provisioning records. Automated reports from identity management systems can quickly highlight discrepancies.

This methodology aims to ensure that roles and responsibilities under Requirement 7 are not only documented, but actively understood and enforced. Real, up-to-date evidence across documentation, personnel interviews, and system configurations proves that compliance is more than just theoretical.
content:
7.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 7 are documented, assigned, and understood.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

7.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 7 are documented and assigned.

7.1.2.b Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 7 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as and are understood.

Guidance

Purpose
If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, personnel may not be aware of their day-to-day responsibilities, and critical activities may not occur.

Good Practice
Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents. As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities.

Examples
A method to document roles and responsibilities is a responsibility assignment matrix that includes who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed (also called a RACI matrix).

7.1.2 (continued)

Definitions
Key elements of an access control model include:
- Resources to be protected (the systems/devices/data to which access is needed).
- Job functions that need access to the resource (for example, system administrator, call-center personnel, store clerk), and
- Which activities each job function needs to perform (for example, read/write or query).

Once job functions, resources, and activities per job functions are defined, individuals can be granted access accordingly.

Examples
Access control models that entities can consider include role-based access control (RBAC) and attribute-based access control (ABAC). The access control model used by a given entity depends on their business and access needs.

title: All security policies and operational procedures in Requirement 7 are defined and maintained.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Updated and version-controlled policy documents readily accessible via an internal repository. Training records, meeting minutes, or email acknowledgements showing the dissemination of the policies to affected personnel. 
• Technical artifacts: Digital document management systems (e.g., SharePoint or Git repositories) listing revision history and update timestamps. Change management logs linking policy updates to process or technology changes. 
• Operational patterns: Scheduled periodic reviews with documented minutes; employee interviews demonstrating awareness of the policies; automated distribution systems that log policy acknowledgements.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply upload a static set of documents without evidence of periodic review or updates. 
• Fake compliance can occur when policies exist on paper but are not actively communicated or enforced (e.g., lack of employee training records).
• Surface-level implementations include missing revision histories, non-integrated change management systems, or having documents that are outdated compared to actual practices.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify existence and currency of policy documentation.
WHY: Versioned and timestamped documents in an accessible repository demonstrate active maintenance.
HOW: Access the document management system and review metadata (revision history, update dates) against known process change events.
FAIL: Documents lack revision history, show outdated timestamps, or are stored in inaccessible locations.

CHECK: Interview a sample of affected personnel regarding their awareness and understanding of these policies.
WHY: Demonstrated employee knowledge confirms that the policies are not only documented but actively disseminated and in use.
HOW: Conduct brief interviews or focus groups asking for key policy details and compare responses against the documented policies.
FAIL: Employees are unable to correctly describe policies or recall recent updates.

CHECK: Review distribution and training records linked to these policies.
WHY: Documentation of distributions, such as email notifications or sign-off forms, proves that affected parties have been made aware of the current versions.
HOW: Inspect email logs, training session attendance registers, and sign-off forms stored within the centralized system.
FAIL: Absence of distribution trails, missing or outdated training records, or a lack of acknowledgement receipts.

CHECK: Assess change management logs for evidence that procedures were updated in response to process or technology shifts.
WHY: Correlating policy updates with documented change requests confirms that documents are maintained in line with operational changes.
HOW: Review configuration management or change request logs and compare change dates with document modification timestamps.
FAIL: Change logs do not link to document updates, or documentation remains static despite known operational changes.

CHECK: Verify that a review schedule is established and adhered to, with documented evidence.
WHY: A scheduled review process indicates that the organization continuously revalidates and updates policies as needed.
HOW: Obtain the policy review calendar and corresponding meeting minutes or progress reports demonstrating adherence.
FAIL: No documented schedule exists, or review meeting minutes and records are absent or infrequent.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Check digital file metadata for update timestamps to quickly determine if policies are maintained.
• Critical indicator: If employees cannot confidently describe key policy elements, it’s a strong sign that policies are either not disseminated or not actively enforced.
• Note: Ensure that evidence of change management (such as linking change tickets to document revisions) is present and easily traceable.
• Detail: Focus on observable, automated evidences like system logs and metadata rather than solely relying on static policy documents, as these are harder to fake and provide real insight into operational effectiveness.
content:
7.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 7 are:
• Documented.
• Kept up to date.
• In use.
• Known to all affected parties.

Customized Approach Objective
Expectations, controls, and oversight for meeting activities within Requirement 7 are defined and adhered to by affected personnel. All supporting activities are repeatable, consistently applied, and conform to management’s intent.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
7.1.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 7 are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose
Requirement 7.1.1 is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout Requirement 7. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in Requirement 7, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated.

Good Practice
It is important to update policies and procedures as needed to address changes in processes, technologies, and business objectives. For this reason, consider updating these documents as soon as possible after a change occurs and not only on a periodic cycle.

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

title: Test data and test accounts are removed from system components before entering production.
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for controlling the removal of test data and test accounts before production deployment:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Deployed production systems that have no accounts or data with naming patterns like 'test', 'qa', 'dummy', etc. Artifacts include screenshots of active user accounts, database entries, file systems, and system configuration logs.
   • Technical Artifacts: Automated scans output, deployment scripts with cleanup routines, version control history showing removal steps, and audit logs confirming execution of test data cleanup processes.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular enforcement documented by continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines that include hooks or scripts for data/account removal, and periodic internal audits that confirm no test elements exist.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations may produce policies stating that test data and accounts are removed without verifying that production systems are actually cleansed. They might show documentation or static screen grabs from an earlier cleanup without current evidence.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Merely renaming dummy accounts or moving test data to less obvious locations, rather than complete removal. Some teams might override automated routines during time crunches.
   • Technical Gaps: Overlooking embedded test accounts in legacy systems or scripts, incomplete deletion routines in deployment scripts, or leaving temporary test data that was not properly purged.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK 1: Verify Removal Policies and Procedures
     WHY: Reviewing documented procedures ensures there is an established process that mandates test data and test account removal before production.
     HOW: Examine official policies and process documents; interview relevant stakeholders (release managers, QA, and DevOps teams) to confirm that these processes are well understood and enforced.
     FAIL: Absence of documented procedures or interviewees unable to describe the process.

   • CHECK 2: Inspect Production System Configurations and Accounts
     WHY: Direct observation of production systems substantiates that no test accounts or data remain.
     HOW: Run automated scans or manual reviews on production servers, databases, and application configurations to search for accounts or data with indicators (e.g., names including 'test', 'qa', or 'dummy').
     FAIL: Presence of accounts or data with these naming patterns or other test-related identifiers in production.

   • CHECK 3: Review Deployment Scripts and Logs for Cleanup Activities
     WHY: Automated removal in deployment processes is essential for ensuring consistent test-data removal.
     HOW: Examine version control and deployment logs for evidence of scripts executing test data and account removal routines; check timestamps and change-history for the cleanup steps during production deployments.
     FAIL: No automated cleanup routines in the scripts or missing/partial log entries indicating removal activity.

   • CHECK 4: Interview Key Operational Personnel
     WHY: Personnel interviews provide insight into the actual process, helping to validate that the documented procedure is followed in practice.
     HOW: Conduct interviews with system release managers, DevOps engineers, and QA teams who can detail the process from staging to production and confirm that cleanup routines are executed.
     FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly describe or evidence of routine test-data removal during production rollouts.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated scanning tools configured to detect common test account name patterns; these tools can quickly flag potential remnants in production environments.
   • Critical Indicator: A production system with any residual test accounts or data is a significant red flag and indicates a high risk of unauthorized access or information leakage.
   • Detail That Matters: Ensure that the logs and scripts not only indicate the existence of cleanup tasks but also demonstrate successful execution (e.g., timestamps, completion status). Automated routines that are manually overridden or inconsistently applied are a clear sign of non-compliance.
   • Best Practice: Validate that the cleanup process is integrated within the CI/CD pipeline for consistent enforcement rather than relying solely on manual interventions shortly before production releases.

This methodology emphasizes operational evidence over mere documentation to verify that test data and test accounts are effectively removed from system components before entering production.
content:
| **Requirements and Testing Procedures** | **Guidance** |
|-----------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Defined Approach Requirements**       |                     |
| 6.5.6 Test data and test accounts are removed from system components before the system goes into production.  |                     |
| **Defined Approach Testing Procedures** |                     |
| 6.5.6.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for removal of test data and test accounts from system components before the system goes into production.  | **Purpose**  |
| 6.5.6.b Observe testing processes for both off-the-shelf software and in-house applications, and interview personnel to verify test data and test accounts are removed before a system goes into production.  | This data may give away information about the functioning of an application or system and is an easy target for unauthorized individuals to exploit to gain access to systems. Possession of such information could facilitate compromise of the system and related account data.  |
| 6.5.6.c Examine data and accounts for recently installed or updated off-the-shelf software and in-house applications to verify there is no test data or test accounts on systems in production.  |                     |
| **Customized Approach Objective**       |                     |
| Test data and test accounts cannot exist in production environments. |                     |

title: Live PANs are not used in pre-production environments outside of the CDE.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
----------------------------------
• Visible evidence includes pre‐production environments that are clearly segregated from the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) with testing databases populated using synthetic or masked data. 
• Technical artifacts include database snapshots, data sanitization scripts, logs from data scrubbing tools, and configuration files that delineate which environments are allowed to access live data.
• Operational patterns include regular reviews of data sets in pre-production, documented processes for data masking, and system audits that verify no live PANs appear in non-CDE systems.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
-------------------------
• Some organizations might simply clone production environments and rely on manual data scrubbing without automated controls, risking residual live PANs.
• Surface-level compliance may include having policies on paper that mandate test data usage, but enforcement might be weak or inconsistently applied.
• A common technical gap is the failure to routinely scan pre-production databases for live PAN patterns, giving a false sense of security if only documentation is reviewed.

VALIDATION STEPS
----------------
CHECK: Verify that pre-production environments do not contain live PANs.
WHY: This demonstrates adherence to the PCI DSS requirement by ensuring that actual cardholder data is not exposed outside the protected CDE.
HOW: 1. Review technical configuration documents and network diagrams to confirm pre-production environments are segregated from the CDE unless appropriately secured. 2. Run automated scripts to scan databases in the pre-production environment for valid PAN patterns (using regex for 16-digit numbers, Luhn algorithm checks) and compare against test PAN numbers. 3. Interview system administrators and test personnel to validate the routine use of synthetic or masked card data.
FAIL: Discovery of any databases or logs with patterns resembling live PANs (that pass Luhn checks and match known card issuer formats) indicates failure.

CHECK: Examine the data sanitization or masking procedures applied to production clones that might be used in pre-production.
WHY: This proves that even if production snapshots are used, they are subjected to controls that strip or mask live PANs effectively.
HOW: 1. Obtain the documented procedure for data masking/sanitization. 2. Verify the existence and execution of data masking tools by examining logs and change management records. 3. Inspect a sample of cloned data set in the pre-production environment for residual live PAN data.
FAIL: Evidence of incomplete or inconsistent application of masking procedures or documentation discrepancies post-cloning indicate non-compliance.

CHECK: Validate the segregation controls between the CDE and pre-production environments.
WHY: This ensures that if live PANs are inadvertently introduced, they remain within the confines of the properly secured CDE environment.
HOW: 1. Review network segmentation policies and architecture diagrams. 2. Verify firewall rules and access control lists that restrict data flow between the CDE and pre-production. 3. Check event logs for unauthorized access attempts.
FAIL: Any misconfiguration in network segmentation or evidence of cross-environment data sharing can be indicative of a breach in process.

CHECK: Inspect audit logs and monitoring alerts for any incidents related to live PAN exposure in non-CDE environments.
WHY: Continuous monitoring and incident response are key markers of operational control.
HOW: 1. Access audit trail reports in both production and pre-production environments. 2. Look for alerts that flag live PAN usage or anomalous data access patterns. 3. Correlate these findings with any remediation actions taken.
FAIL: Absence of automated detection logs or records of ignoring/failing to remediate such incidents signals a weak control.

EXPERT TIPS
-----------
• Focus on the actual data content: Automated scans using both format and algorithmic (Luhn algorithm) checks are less likely to be faked than policy reviews.
• Verify recent snapshots or clones – timing is critical since older snapshots might have been properly cleansed while recent ones might contain residual live data.
• Watch for “shadow” environments that might not be covered by formal policies. In practice, undocumented test systems often become a hiding place for live PANs.
• Ensure that the verification covers both technical and operational controls. Policies without implementation can be misleading.
• Look for evidence of regular periodic reviews (e.g., quarterly scans) and proper documentation of remediation efforts to spot patterns of repeated failures.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements

### 6.5.5 Live PANs are not used in pre-production environments, except where those environments are included in the CDE and protected in accordance with all applicable PCI DSS requirements.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 6.5.5.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for not using live PANs in pre-production environments, except where those environments are in a CDE and protected in accordance with all applicable PCI DSS requirements.

### 6.5.5.b Observe testing processes and interview personnel to verify procedures are in place to ensure live PANs are not used in pre-production environments, except where those environments are in a CDE and protected in accordance with all applicable PCI DSS requirements.

### 6.5.5.c Examine pre-production test data to verify live PANs are not used in pre-production environments, except where those environments are in a CDE and protected in accordance with all applicable PCI DSS requirements.

## Customized Approach Objective

Live PANs cannot be present in pre-production environments outside the CDE.

## Guidance

### Purpose
Use of live PANs outside of protected CDEs provides malicious individuals with the opportunity to gain unauthorized access to cardholder data.

### Definitions
Live PANs refer to valid PANs (not test PANs) issued by, or on behalf of, a payment brand. Additionally, when payment cards expire, the same PAN is often reused with a different expiry date. All PANs must be verified as being unable to conduct payment transactions or pose fraud risk to the payment system before they are excluded from PCI DSS scope. It is the responsibility of the entity to confirm that PANs are not live.

title: Roles and functions are separated between production and pre-production environments to ensure accountability.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence includes documented proofs of strictly separated user accounts, access lists, and role assignments for production vs pre-production environments.
   - Technical artifacts include production system access logs, documented role-based access control (RBAC) configurations, change management records, and monitoring alerts that verify only approved changes are deployed.
   - Operational patterns such as periodic reviews of account privileges, audit logs capturing change requests and approvals, and enforced dual control/peer review in production environments indicate the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations may simply state in policies that roles are separated without enforcing technical restrictions, e.g., maintaining shared accounts or not truly segregating environments.
   - Superficial implementations include using the same credentials for multiple environments or having manual, non-auditable processes that can be easily manipulated.
   - Technical gaps frequently observed include lack of enforced least privilege, unreviewed privilege escalation, or bypassing change management controls (e.g., undocumented emergency deployments).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify separate user access control lists for production and pre-production environments.
   WHY: Distinct account lists ensure that individuals are restricted to only the appropriate environment, supporting accountability and segregation.
   HOW: Review system configuration management tools or RBAC settings, extract access lists from production and pre-production, and compare them for overlaps.
   FAIL: Overlapping user accounts or shared credentials across environments indicate a failure of this control.

   CHECK: Inspect change management logs to verify that changes in production were approved by an independent reviewer.
   WHY: Independent review and approval create accountability and ensure that only validated changes are deployed in production.
   HOW: Access logs and change management system records to see approval workflows, timestamps, and independent authorizations documenting production deployments.
   FAIL: Missing audit trails, shared accounts executing both change requests and approvals, or evidence of unapproved emergency changes signal control breakdown.

   CHECK: Interview personnel and perform targeted sampling of production vs pre-production system activities.
   WHY: Direct observation and interviews help validate operational practices beyond what’s documented, ensuring role separation is enforced daily.
   HOW: Conduct interviews with system administrators, developers, and compliance officers; ask for specific examples of process usage during recent changes; and correlate with system usage logs.
   FAIL: Inconsistent narratives, inability to demonstrate processes in practice, or observed unauthorized access to production environments reveal weaknesses.

   CHECK: Validate technical segregation through network configurations or environment isolation.
   WHY: Ensuring that production and pre-production environments are not inter-connected minimizes unauthorized lateral movements and misconfigurations.
   HOW: Examine network diagrams, firewall rules, or access control policies; verify that production and pre-production are separated and that only approved pathways exist for deployment.
   FAIL: Inadequate network segmentation or overlapping infrastructure components indicate a violation of the separation intent.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Use automated tools to extract and compare user role assignments across environments. Gaps in scripts or control reports often hint at manual workarounds that bypass proper separation.
   - Critical indicator: Review the change management system for dual approvals and evidence that no single person can both request and approve a production change. A single technician handling both roles is a clear red flag.
   - Detail Matters: Look for subtle gaps in logging that might allow role creep; verify that audit logs are tamper-evident and cover both environments to ensure consistency.
   - Insight: If environments are limited in personnel, check that procedural controls mimicking role separation are rigorously enforced and documented with independent oversight; otherwise, policy alone is insufficient.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements

**6.5.4** Roles and functions are separated between production and pre-production environments to provide accountability such that only reviewed and approved changes are deployed.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**6.5.4.a** Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for separating roles and functions to provide accountability such that only reviewed and approved changes are deployed.

**6.5.4.b** Observe processes and interview personnel to verify implemented controls separate roles and functions and provide accountability such that only reviewed and approved changes are deployed.

## Purpose
The goal of separating roles and functions between production and pre-production environments is to reduce the number of personnel with access to the production environment and account data and thereby minimize risk of unauthorized, unintentional, or inappropriate access to data and system components and help ensure that access is limited to those individuals with a business need for such access. The intent of this control is to separate critical activities to provide oversight and review to catch errors and minimize the chances of fraud or theft (since two people would need to collude in order to hide an activity). Separating roles and functions, also referred to as separation or segregation of duties, is a key internal control concept to protect an entity's assets.

## Customized Approach Objective

Job roles and accountability that differentiate between pre-production and production activities are defined and managed to minimize the risk of unauthorized, unintentional, or inappropriate actions.

## Applicability Notes

In environments with limited personnel where individuals perform multiple roles or functions, this same goal can be achieved with additional procedural controls that provide accountability. For example, a developer may also be an administrator that uses an administrator-level account with elevated privileges in the development environment and, for their developer role, they use a separate account with user-level access to the production environment.

title: Pre-production environments are separated from production through enforced access controls.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes network diagrams and documented architecture that distinctly show physical or logical separation between pre-production (e.g., development, testing, UAT) and production systems.
• Technical artifacts such as firewall configurations, VLAN tags, ACLs, and access control policies that clearly enforce separation between these environments.
• Operational patterns include distinct authentication logs and access records showing that production systems are only accessed by authorized personnel and that pre-production systems are isolated.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply maintain separate documentation without implementing effective technical controls (e.g., using dangling policies or default network segmentation that are not enforced by access controls).
• Relying solely on naming conventions or user self-certification to claim environment separation without robust firewall or network segmentation.
• Implementing VLANs without robust ACLs or oversight can appear compliant on paper while still permitting unintended traffic.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review network and security configuration documentation for explicit mapping of pre-production vs. production environments.
WHY: This documentation should provide not only architectural intent but also evidence that the environments have been intentionally segmented.
HOW: Examine network diagrams, data flow charts, and published configuration standards; match these against live configurations.
FAIL: If the diagrams or documentation show overlapping networks or if they do not clearly delineate the security controls between environments.

CHECK: Verify firewall and ACL configurations that enforce network segmentation between pre-production and production systems.
