WHY: Access controls at the network level must block unauthorized communications between environments, preventing pre-production vulnerabilities from affecting production.
HOW: Use configuration management systems to extract firewall rules, perform configuration reviews, and, if possible, run tests (or review logs) to confirm blocked access attempts between the environments.
FAIL: If firewall rules are too permissive, lack proper segmentation, or do not target the correct IP ranges or VLAN segments.

CHECK: Audit user access controls for systems in both environments and confirm distinct credentials and permissions are enforced.
WHY: Properly enforced access restrictions indicate that users cannot inadvertently or maliciously cross-access from pre-production to production systems.
HOW: Review access control lists, authentication logs, and system privileges. Confirm that users are strictly segregated by environment (e.g., separate AD groups, role-based controls).
FAIL: If users have overlapping accounts or if audit logs show cross-access incidents between environments.

CHECK: Test connectivity (using controlled scans or internal pentesting) between production and pre-production systems to confirm separation.
WHY: Active attempts to traverse from one environment to another reveal if the enforced access controls are functioning effectively in a live scenario.
HOW: In a controlled testing environment, attempt to access pre-production resources from production systems (or vice versa) and verify that appropriate blocks are triggered (logging attempts and denied connections).
FAIL: If connectivity or unauthorized access is successful or if logs do not record the block attempts.

EXPERT TIPS
• In many cases, poor implementations are indicated by controls that only exist on paper. Always validate the live settings using automated tools and direct log analysis.
• Critical indicators include overlapping IP address ranges, misconfigured ACLs, or firewall rules that are not actively monitored or improved in change management processes.
• Verify that change management tools confirm that any modifications affecting separation (e.g., new servers or services) are reviewed with respect to environment tagging and access restrictions.
• Use automated network scanners and log analyzers to capture real-time evidence of enforcement; this minimizes the chance of superficial compliance.

This multi-step approach ensures that both documentation and technical enforcement are reviewed, providing assurance that pre-production environments are effectively segregated from production through enforced access controls.
content:
## Requirements and Testing Procedures

### Defined Approach Requirements

**6.5.3** Pre-production environments are separated from production environments and the separation is enforced with access controls.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**6.5.3.a** Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for separating the pre-production environment from the production environment via access controls that enforce the separation.

**6.5.3.b** Examine network documentation and configurations of network security controls to verify that the pre-production environment is separate from the production environment(s).

**6.5.3.c** Examine access control settings to verify that access controls are in place to enforce separation between the pre-production and production environment(s).

### Customized Approach Objective

Pre-production environments cannot introduce risks and vulnerabilities into production environments.

### Purpose

Due to the constantly changing state of pre-production environments, they are often less secure than the production environment.

### Good Practice

Organizations must clearly understand which environments are test environments or development environments and how these environments interact on the level of networks and applications.

### Definitions

Pre-production environments include development, testing, user acceptance testing (UAT), etc. Even where production infrastructure is used to facilitate testing or development, production environments still need to be separated (logically or physically) from pre-production functionality such that vulnerabilities introduced as a result of pre-production activities do not adversely affect production systems.

title: Post-change systems are verified to comply with applicable PCI DSS requirements.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Post-change verification logs and evidence of system re-assessments immediately following significant change events.
  WHY: This proves that after a change, systems are re-evaluated against PCI DSS requirements to ensure proper controls are applied and documented.
  HOW: Review automated change management systems and system audit logs for entries showing post-change validations, screenshots, or scan reports that include system configuration details. Verify that network and data-flow diagrams were updated where changes occurred.
  FAIL: Absence of entries or reliance solely on pre-change configurations without post-change assessments.

• CHECK: Updated configuration standards and system inventories after significant changes.
  WHY: The updated documentation demonstrates integration of the new or changed systems into the PCI environment, with configurations meeting PCI DSS standards.
  HOW: Compare system inventories, configuration baselines, and updated network diagrams before and after a noted significant change. Verify changes were documented and approved per change management policy.
  FAIL: Missing updates in configuration baselines or network diagrams and lack of install logs.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes simply attach a signed memo or add a clause in change management documentation without re-scanning systems or verifying actual settings.
• A superficial update of documentation without real operational testing or system scanning often fails to catch misconfigurations or missing controls (e.g., not installed FIM, outdated scans).
• Reliance on manual confirmation rather than automated checks can create gaps in evidence.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify that a formal post-change test or scan (e.g., vulnerability scan, configuration integrity check) was conducted on each significant change.
  WHY: This shows independent testing beyond policy paperwork to validate the control is in effect.
  HOW: Retrieve reports from automated vulnerability management tools and FIM or anti-malware scan schedules associated with the changed systems. Cross-reference the changes with the scan logs.
  FAIL: No corresponding scan logs or test reports for newly changed systems.

• CHECK: Interview technical personnel responsible for implementing changes to confirm that verification of PCI DSS controls is part of the change process.
  WHY: It provides insight that the process is not just documented but actively practiced.
  HOW: Conduct interviews and ask for walk-throughs of change management procedures, and look for evidence of independent review by security or compliance teams.
  FAIL: Personnel cannot demonstrate or articulate how the post-change validations are practically performed.

• CHECK: Audit configuration management tools for automated checks that flag deviations from PCI DSS configurations post-change.
  WHY: Automated tools reduce the likelihood of human error and detect non-compliance in real time.
  HOW: Review system configuration management logs and alerts, and trace any remediation activities or error corrections following automated scans.
  FAIL: Lack of automated alerts or remediation records, or mismatches between tool configurations and actual system settings.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• TIP: Focus on systems that have experienced substantial modifications, such as changes to network segments, addition of new services, or updates to firewall policies, as these areas are prone to misconfigurations.
• TIP: Look for automated system validation integrators that combine change management data with real-time compliance scanning; these integrations are less likely to be faked.
• TIP: Verification of control effectiveness should include not only the existence of documentation but also the presence of historical audit trails showing the iterative process of post-change validation.
• TIP: Ensure that evidence shows a clear timeline linking the change event, the post-change scan or review, and the update of system documentation. Gaps in these timelines are a red flag.

Summary:
The validation methodology focuses on confirming that significant changes trigger a sequence of technical assessments, system re-scans, and updated documentation. By checking for both human and automated evidence, and identifying when organizations merely retrofit documentation without operational verification, this approach ensures that post-change systems are reliably compliant with applicable PCI DSS requirements.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements  
### 6.5.2  
Upon completion of a significant change, all applicable PCI DSS requirements are confirmed to be in place on all new or changed systems and networks, and documentation is updated as applicable.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures  
### 6.5.2  
Examine documentation for significant changes, interview personnel, and observe the affected systems/networks to verify that the entity confirmed applicable PCI DSS requirements were in place on all new or changed systems and networks and that documentation was updated as applicable.

## Purpose  
Having processes to analyze significant changes helps ensure that all appropriate PCI DSS controls are applied to any systems or networks added or changed within the in-scope environment, and that PCI DSS requirements continue to be met to secure the environment.

## Good Practice  
Building this validation into change management processes helps ensure that device inventories and configuration standards are kept up to date and security controls are applied where needed.

## Examples  
Applicable PCI DSS requirements that could be impacted include, but are not limited to:  
- Network and data-flow diagrams are updated to reflect changes.  
- Systems are configured per configuration standards, with all default passwords changed and unnecessary services disabled.  
- Systems are protected with required controls—for example, file integrity monitoring (FIM), anti-malware, patches, and audit logging.  
- Sensitive authentication data is not stored, and all account data storage is documented and incorporated into data retention policy and procedures.  
- New systems are included in the quarterly vulnerability scanning process.  
- Systems are scanned for internal and external vulnerabilities after significant changes per Requirements 11.3.1.3 and 11.3.2.1.

## Customized Approach Objective  
All system components are verified after a significant change to be compliant with the applicable PCI DSS requirements.

## Applicability Notes  
These significant changes should also be captured and reflected in the entity’s annual PCI DSS scope confirmation activity per Requirement 12.5.2.

title: Changes to system components are made securely following established change control procedures.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Existence and usage of formal, documented change control procedures that incorporate the reason for, description of, security impact analysis, testing results, approval signatures/electronic approvals, and rollback procedures.
  WHY: This documentation ensures that every change has been carefully planned, assessed for security implications, and authorized by designated personnel. It also exhibits that an operational framework exists to guide secure changes.
  HOW: Verify that the change control policy documents include specific sections for change rationale, security impact assessment, testing outcomes, and rollback instructions. Check that recent changes in production have corresponding ticket records or electronic approvals.
  FAIL: Absence of a detailed change record, missing authorization fields, or only ad hoc approvals without documented procedures indicates a failure of implementation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may produce superficial or outdated policy documents with little evidence that the procedures are actually executed.
• Process documentation without linking to corresponding change tickets or logs may be faked.
• Predominant reliance on manual records without behind-the-scenes audit trails or system logs may not prove operational effectiveness.
• Some changes might bypass formal testing procedures by patching ad hoc, justified as emergency changes but lacking complete security impact reviews.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review a sample of recent production change tickets to verify they include documented change reasons, security impact assessments, and explicit authorization (with signatures/electronic approval logs).
   WHY: These records prove that the change control process is actively followed and that each change has been evaluated for security impacts.
   HOW: Pull a sample (e.g., last 10 changes) and correlate each with the documented procedure; check for testing records and rollback plans. Use automated tools to extract logs if available.
   FAIL: If change records are missing the required detail or lack linkage to approval information or testing results.

2. CHECK: Verify that testing documentation includes verification that the change did not adversely affect system security, and that rollback procedures were tested or simulated.
   WHY: Testing records and rollback exercise results demonstrate that the environment can safely revert in case of failure, ensuring continuous secure posture.
   HOW: Locate evidence of testing results for changes and confirm that any issues were documented along with corrective actions. Interview system administrators if needed to confirm rollback procedures.
   FAIL: If testing documentation is absent or if rollback steps are designed but never validated through tests or simulations.

3. CHECK: Inspect automated change management tools/logs to ensure that changes in production are recorded with time stamps and audit trails demonstrating compliance with established procedures.
   WHY: Automated logs are harder to manipulate than paper trails and provide a clear, timestamped history of changes and approvals.
   HOW: Use log analysis tools to pull data on changes, compare with change ticket records, and look for discrepancies or missing entries.
   FAIL: Gaps in automation logs or mismatches between ticket data and system logs indicate poor implementation.

4. CHECK: Evaluate how security impact is documented for bespoke and custom software changes and confirm that they are tested for compliance with security requirements (e.g., Requirement 6.2.4).
   WHY: This ensures that custom code changes do not introduce vulnerabilities and comply with the organization’s broader security requirements.
   HOW: Review change records specific to custom software with focus on security test outputs and approval endorsements from knowledgeable personnel.
   FAIL: If custom changes proceed without the required security test results, or the documentation deviates from prescribed guidelines.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: If reviewing a sample of change tickets, look directly for digital signatures or electronic logs as they are more robust than handwritten notes.
• Critical Indicator: Incomplete security impact analysis in a majority of tickets is a red flag that changes may inadvertently compromise security.
• Detail to Note: The presence of rollback and failure response procedures is crucial – absence or incomplete rollback instructions are common oversights.
• Observational Hint: Always correlate automated logs with manual records; discrepancies between the two indicate possible non-adherence to formal procedures.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## 6.5 Changes to all system components are managed securely.

### Defined Approach Requirements

6.5.1 Changes to all system components in the production environment are made according to established procedures that include:
- Reason for, and description of, the change.
- Documentation of security impact.
- Documented change approval by authorized parties.
- Testing to verify that the change does not adversely impact system security.
- For bespoke and custom software changes, all updates are tested for compliance with Requirement 6.2.4 before being deployed into production.
- Procedures to address failures and return to a secure state.

### Customized Approach Objective

All changes are tracked, authorized, and evaluated for impact and security, and changes are managed to avoid unintended effects to the security of system components.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

6.5.1.a Examine documented change control procedures to verify procedures are defined for changes to all system components in the production environment to include all elements specified in this requirement.

6.5.1.b Examine recent changes to system components and trace those changes back to related change control documentation. For each change examined, verify the change is implemented in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

### Purpose

Change management procedures must be applied to all changes—including the addition, removal, or modification of any system component—in the production environment. It is important to document the reason for a change and the change description so that relevant parties understand and agree the change is needed. Likewise, documenting the impacts of the change allows all affected parties to plan appropriately for any processing changes.

### Good Practice

Approval by authorized parties confirms that the change is legitimate and that the change is sanctioned by the organization. Changes should be approved by individuals with the appropriate authority and knowledge to understand the impact of the change. Thorough testing by the entity confirms that the security of the environment is not reduced by implementing a change and that all existing security controls either remain in place or are replaced with equal or stronger security controls after the change. The specific testing to be performed will vary according to the type of change and system component(s) affected. 

For each change, it is important to have documented procedures that address any failures and provide instructions on how to return to a secure state in case the change fails or adversely affects the security of an application or system. These procedures will allow the application or system to be restored to its previous secure state.

title: Payment page scripts are managed to ensure authorization and integrity.
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-6.4.3: Payment page scripts are managed to ensure authorization and integrity.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: A maintained and up-to-date inventory of all payment page scripts accompanied by business or technical justification. Verification that scripts loaded on the payment page include integrity mechanisms (such as cryptographic hashes, Subresource Integrity (SRI) attributes, or digital signatures).
   • Technical Artifacts: Screenshots or exports of the script inventory system; configuration files or metadata that show valid hash values or digital certificates; logged approvals or change management tickets for script updates.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular reviews of script inventory, audit logs demonstrating integrity verifications, and monitoring alerts when a script is updated or a checksum mismatch occurs.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Inventories: Organizations may provide a static list pulled from documentation without a dynamic process, or include scripts that aren’t actually in use on the payment page. In some cases, the inventory is maintained as a paper record that isn’t reconciled with live configurations.
   • Superficial Integrity Measures: Using SRI attributes without verifying if they are continuously updated, or relying on manual approvals without automated checksum validations. Organizations might claim authorized scripts without enforcing cryptographic validation.
   • Missing Authorization Controls: Some setups might list authorized scripts symbolically (e.g., in a white list) but not implement runtime checks to prevent unauthorized code injection.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify the inventory and approval records for payment page scripts.
     WHY: A current inventory with documented business justification directly proves that the organization is managing and monitoring scripts.
     HOW: Interview staff responsible for script management, review the live script inventory tool or database, and match the list against scripts loaded on the actual payment page. Check for documented change management tickets that approved each entry.
     FAIL: If the inventory is missing, outdated, or lacks clear business/technical justification, or if the list does not match the active scripts on the page.

   • CHECK: Validate the integrity protection measures for each script.
     WHY: Integrity controls (e.g., SRI, digital signatures, cryptographic hashes) ensure that scripts have not been altered in transit or on the server, proving operational effectiveness of the control.
     HOW: Examine the source code of the payment page to see if SRI attributes or similar integrity mechanisms are implemented and verify that the hash values match known good ones from the inventory records. Alternatively, inspect deployment configurations for automated signing processes.
     FAIL: The absence of integrity-check attributes, mismatches between expected and computed hash values, or the use of non-automated integrity checks.

   • CHECK: Assess the authorization mechanism for new or updated scripts.
     WHY: Ensures that only properly approved scripts are allowed to execute in the consumer browser, preventing unauthorized code execution.
     HOW: Review system configurations and deployment pipelines to verify that there is an enforced process (e.g., change management workflows, digital signing of scripts) requiring explicit authorization. Interview staff and review audit logs to confirm that any change to a script was accompanied by an approval record.
     FAIL: Evidence that scripts can be or have been deployed without undergoing a formal approval process or where audit trails do not exist.

   • CHECK: Review monitoring and logging related to script integrity.
     WHY: Continuous monitoring and logging of script execution or integrity validation provides proof that controls remain operational over time.
     HOW: Check SIEM or logging system configurations for alerts triggered by mismatched checksums or unauthorized script changes on the payment page. Validate that logs are preserved and reviewed regularly.
     FAIL: An absence of active monitoring or logs that would capture integrity breaches, or logs that show repeated anomalies without corrective action.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Look for automated reconciliation between the script inventory and the live environment. Manual lists alone are easy to manipulate and can be outdated.
   • Pay attention to evidence of real-time integrity monitoring (for example, continuous hash recalculations and alerts) rather than periodic spot checks.
   • Insist on evidence from the production environment (such as live site source code and log files) rather than relying solely on policy documents or management interviews.
   • Validate that any external scripts (even from trusted third parties) are documented and follow similar integrity checks.
   • When reviewing logs, verify that alerts have been resolved and that there is a clear process for remediating failed checks, as persistent failures can indicate underlying control weaknesses.

This methodology emphasizes observable and technical evidence extracted directly from the production environment, reducing the risk of compliance being faked or documented inaccurately.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements

### 6.4.3  All payment page scripts that are loaded and executed in the consumer’s browser are managed as follows:
- A method is implemented to confirm that each script is authorized.
- A method is implemented to assure the integrity of each script.
- An inventory of all scripts is maintained with written business or technical justification as to why each is necessary.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

### 6.4.3.a Examine policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined for managing all payment page scripts that are loaded and executed in the consumer's browser, in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

### 6.4.3.b Interview responsible personnel and examine inventory records and system configurations to verify that all payment page scripts that are loaded and executed in the consumer’s browser are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

## Purpose

Scripts loaded and executed in the payment page can have their functionality altered without the entity’s knowledge and can also have the functionality to load additional external scripts (for example, advertising and tracking, tag management systems). Such seemingly harmless scripts can be used by potential attackers to upload malicious scripts that can read and exfiltrate cardholder data from the consumer browser.

Ensuring that the functionality of all such scripts is understood to be necessary for the operation of the payment page minimizes the number of scripts that could be tampered with. Ensuring that scripts have been explicitly authorized reduces the probability of unnecessary scripts being added to the payment page without appropriate management approval. Where it is impractical for such authorization to occur before a script is changed or a new script is added to the page, the authorization should be confirmed as soon as possible after a change is made. Using techniques to prevent tampering with the script will minimize the probability of the script being modified to carry out unauthorized behavior, such as skimming the cardholder data from the payment page.

## Customized Approach Objective

Unauthorized code cannot be executed in the payment page as it is rendered in the consumer’s browser.

title: An automated technical solution continually detects and prevents web-based attacks for public-facing web applications.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Review network architecture diagrams and firewall/WAF configurations showing the solution installed in front of public-facing web applications.
  WHY: This verifies the automated solution is actively positioned to inspect inbound traffic for web-based attacks.
  HOW: Examine device inventories, network diagrams, and configuration management records that list WAF or web application security appliances in the DMZ.
  FAIL: Absence of diagrams or evidence that the solution is deployed directly in front of public-facing applications.

• CHECK: Inspect audit logs and event records from the technical solution to confirm that attack detection, prevention actions, or alerting events are being generated.
  WHY: Evidence of active logging and incident capture indicates the solution is actively detecting and mitigating or alerting on threats.
  HOW: Retrieve recent logs from the WAF and correlate them with known test events or benign attacks to verify detection and reporting.
  FAIL: Logs are missing, incomplete, or only contain low-severity events (i.e., no actual test or live attack events captured).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply enable a WAF in alert mode rather than actively blocking attacks, thereby faking compliance if alert reviews are not timely.
• Some organizations may continue to use outdated rule sets or disable automatic updates; documentation could be updated but the solution remains ineffective against recent threats.
• Relying solely on vendor-generated reports or periodic manual reviews without continuous monitoring can be a superficial implementation.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Confirm solution placement by reviewing detailed network configuration and verifying that the public-facing traffic flows through the WAF or equivalent automated solution.
   WHY: This validates the strategic deployment necessary for real-time protection.
   HOW: Use network scanning tools and review firewall policies to trace traffic paths to public applications.
   FAIL: Evidence shows traffic bypassing the security control or mixed deployment with no clear segregation.

2. CHECK: Verify that the system is actively running and dynamically updated by checking update logs and rule-set revision histories from the management console.
   WHY: A running, updated system is crucial for defending against evolving web-based threats.
   HOW: Access the WAF management interface, review the automatic update logs, and inspect change control documentation.
   FAIL: Outdated or stale rule sets, disabled automated updates, or long gaps in update logs indicate non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Assess automated detection and prevention actions by simulating benign attacks (with prior authorization) or reviewing historical logs for blocked web-based attack signatures.
   WHY: Simulated or historical evidence confirms that the solution not only logs but actively detects and mitigates real threats.
   HOW: Conduct controlled tests or review incident response records where alerts triggered immediate investigation or automated blocking.
   FAIL: No evidence of responses to attack patterns or only alerts without any automated block/mitigation actions.

4. CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for incident management and monitoring to confirm that generated alerts are promptly investigated and resolved.
   WHY: Ensuring operational effectiveness is as important as technical configuration, verifying that alerts lead to timely response.
   HOW: Ask incident response teams about past alerts, response times, and how the solution integrates within the organizational security operations center (SOC).
   FAIL: Personnel report delays in response or reliance on manual processing without automated mitigation workflows.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Focus on real-time logs and live traffic reports during your assessment; these provide more reliable evidence than static documentation.
• Critical Indicator: A solution running solely in alert mode with no active blocking may indicate poor operational posture if manual follow-ups are delayed or inconsistent.
• Note: Verify that the update mechanism (e.g., automatic signature updates) is not disabled, as outdated signatures substantially reduce the effectiveness of web attack mitigation.
• Detail: Confirm that configuration settings are regularly audited against evolving threat intelligence to ensure the solution is not only compliant on paper but effective against current threats.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Defined Approach Requirements

**6.4.2** For public-facing web applications, an automated technical solution is deployed that continually detects and prevents web-based attacks, with at least the following:
- Is installed in front of public-facing web applications and is configured to detect and prevent web-based attacks.
- Actively running and up to date as applicable.
- Generating audit logs.
- Configured to either block web-based attacks or generate an alert that is immediately investigated.

## Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**6.4.2** For public-facing web applications, examine the system configuration settings and audit logs, and interview responsible personnel to verify that an automated technical solution that detects and prevents web-based attacks is in place in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

## Purpose
Public-facing web applications are primary targets for attackers, and poorly coded web applications provide an easy path for attackers to gain access to sensitive data and systems.

## Good Practice
When using automated technical solutions, it is important to include processes that facilitate timely responses to alerts generated by the solutions so that any detected attacks can be mitigated. Such solutions may also be used to automate mitigation, for example rate-limiting controls, which can be implemented to mitigate against brute-force attacks and enumeration attacks.

title: Public-facing web applications are protected against attacks through assessment and remediation.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for control req-6.4.1:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence of recurring vulnerability assessments (manual or automated) for public‐facing web applications with documented schedules, scan reports, and remediation logs.
   • Technical artifacts such as detailed assessment reports from specialized application security tools or third‐party testing reports that cover common attack vectors (as defined in Req 6.2.4).
   • For the automated solution approach, visible evidence includes a properly configured Web Application Firewall (WAF) with active audit logs, version/update records, and alert management processes.
   • Operational patterns: Regular re-assessments following remediation, alerts triggering immediate investigation, and periodic review of WAF configuration.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might produce outdated or one-off scan reports that do not reflect continuous assessment or post-remediation re-evaluation.
   • Falsifying compliance by performing superficial internal scans that omit key external attack vectors and specialized security expertise.
   • Installing a WAF without ensuring it is actively monitored, properly configured, or generating actionable alerts can give the appearance of compliance without genuine protection.
   • Relying solely on policy documents rather than showing evidence of effective operational processes.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Validate the ongoing vulnerability assessment process for the public-facing web application.
   WHY: Regular assessments (at least annually and after significant changes) prove that vulnerabilities are being actively identified and tracked.
   HOW: Review recent assessment reports, schedules, and evidence (such as third-party penetration test results or automated scan logs); interview responsible personnel to confirm that the scans include all common attack types.
   FAIL: Absence of dated reports, lack of post-remediation re-assessment evidence, or use of generic internal scans not performed by specialized entities.
   
   CHECK: Verify effective vulnerability remediation and re-evaluation procedures.
   WHY: Demonstrating that vulnerabilities are not only identified but also ranked (per requirement 6.3.1), corrected, and then re-tested confirms the operational effectiveness of the control.
   HOW: Examine remediation records linked to previously identified vulnerabilities, check for follow-up assessment logs that confirm re-evaluation, and validate that vulnerability ranking criteria are applied consistently.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete remediation records, no evidence of re-evaluation after remediation, or records that don’t align with defined risk ranking requirements.
   
   CHECK: Confirm the configuration and operation of the automated technical solution (e.g., WAF) that detects and prevents web-based attacks.
   WHY: An effectively installed and maintained WAF shows continuous protection with real-time monitoring and alert generation, meeting the alternative approach in the control.
   HOW: Inspect the WAF configuration settings (ensuring it’s placed in front of all public-facing applications), review its audit logs and alerts, and verify that it’s up-to-date and actively blocking or alerting on suspicious traffic. Interview operational staff regarding their alert response process.
   FAIL: Evidence of outdated configurations, inactive logs, alerts not monitored, or test results that indicate the WAF is bypassable by common attack vectors.
   
   CHECK: Validate the incident response or alert-handling process for automated attack detection.
   WHY: Ensuring that alerts are immediately investigated confirms that the automated solution is not just generating logs but also supports timely remediation of attacks.
   HOW: Request evidence (e.g., incident tickets, follow-up reports) that show how alerts from the WAF or similar solutions are triaged and remediated; interview personnel to confirm response times and protocols.
   FAIL: No documented alert handling process, repeated delays in addressing triggered alerts, or missing records of incident investigations related to web-based attacks.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Look for technical evidence (e.g., recent scan timestamps, audit log entries, and configuration snapshots) rather than relying solely on policy documents.
   • Validate that assessment reports are generated by recognized specialized security tools or qualified third-parties. Check for consistency in vulnerability severity rating and remediation evidence.
   • For automated solutions, perform a test (if possible) by simulating known attack patterns to verify that the WAF or detection system reacts as expected.
   • Shortcuts include a quick review of the active log files and system update records, combined with brief interviews of operational staff to confirm they are aware of and actively manage alerts.
   • Critical indicators of poor implementation include outdated scan dates, missing re-assessment evidence, or alerts that are generated but never resolved.

Overall, the focus should be on observable, technical, and operational evidence rather than just scripted policies. The goal is to ensure that the control actively protects public-facing web applications by both detecting vulnerabilities promptly and ensuring that identified threats are remediated effectively.
content:
## 6.4 Public-facing web applications are protected against attacks.

### Defined Approach Requirements

6.4.1 For public-facing web applications, new threats and vulnerabilities are addressed on an ongoing basis and these applications are protected against known attacks as follows:
- Reviewing public-facing web applications via manual or automated application vulnerability security assessment tools or methods as follows:
  - At least once every 12 months and after significant changes.
  - By an entity that specializes in application security.
  - Including, at a minimum, all common software attacks in Requirement 6.2.4.
  - All vulnerabilities are ranked in accordance with requirement 6.3.1.
  - All vulnerabilities are corrected.
  - The application is re-evaluated after the corrections.

**OR**  
- Installing an automated technical solution(s) that continually detects and prevents web-based attacks as follows:
  - Installed in front of public-facing web applications to detect and prevent web-based attacks.
  - Actively running and up to date as applicable.
  - Generating audit logs.
  - Configured to either block web-based attacks or generate an alert that is immediately investigated.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

6.4.1 For public-facing web applications, ensure that either one of the required methods is in place as follows:
- If manual or automated vulnerability security assessment tools or methods are in use, examine documented processes, interview personnel, and examine records of application security assessments to verify that public-facing web applications are reviewed in accordance with all elements of this requirement specific to the tool/method.

**OR**  
- If an automated technical solution(s) is installed that continually detects and prevents web-based attacks, examine the system configuration settings and audit logs, and interview responsible personnel to verify that the automated technical solution(s) is installed in accordance with all elements of this requirement specific to the solution(s).

### Purpose  
Public-facing web applications are those that are available to the public (not only for internal use). These applications are primary targets for attackers, and poorly coded web applications provide an easy path for attackers to gain access to sensitive data and systems.

### Good Practice  
Manual or automated vulnerability security assessment tools or methods review and/or test the application for vulnerabilities. Common assessment tools include specialized web scanners that perform automatic analysis of web application protection.

When using automated technical solutions, it is important to include processes that facilitate timely responses to alerts generated by the solutions so that any detected attacks can be mitigated.

### Examples  
A web application firewall (WAF) installed in front of public-facing web applications to check all traffic is an example of an automated technical solution that detects and prevents web-based attacks (for example, the attacks included in Requirement 6.2.4). WAFs filter and block non-essential traffic at the application layer. A properly configured WAF helps to prevent application-layer attacks on applications that are improperly coded or configured.

title: All system components are protected from known vulnerabilities by timely installation of applicable security patches/updates.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence includes centralized patch management console logs, change management/ticketing records, and automated vulnerability scanning outputs that show up-to-date patch status across system components.
• Technical artifacts like detailed patch inventories, system audit logs showing patch installations, and screenshots from patch management tools provide direct confirmation.
• Regular, observable patterns include routine scans with minimal high-risk vulnerabilities, consistent patch application within defined timeframes, and system admin alerts highlighting patch rollouts.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply document that a patching policy exists without ensuring that patches are truly applied. They might present out-of-date screenshots or reports that don’t reflect the current state.
• Some may schedule patch updates but fail to actually install them on critical components—relying on process documentation rather than tangible evidence from system logs.
• Technical gaps include misconfigured patch management tools, manual patch application without synchronization, or incomplete vulnerability scan results that hide unpatched systems.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review centralized patch management logs (automated system logs) for evidence of timely installation of critical patches.
   WHY: These logs show the actual installation dates, providing proof against the one-month timeline for critical vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Extract logs from the patch management console; correlate patch release dates from vendor advisories with installation timestamps on representative critical systems.
   FAIL: If logs indicate delays beyond the required timelines or missing patch entries for critical systems, this check fails.

2. CHECK: Compare vulnerability scan reports with patch inventory records to verify that known vulnerabilities do not appear on critical systems.
   WHY: Up-to-date scans provide an independent, automated verification that systems are not exposed to known vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Run a current vulnerability scan on a sample set of systems; cross-check findings against the patch inventory and vendor patch release notes.
   FAIL: Presence of high-risk vulnerabilities or outdated patch levels in the scan reports indicates non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Examine change management/ticketing records for patch-related activities to confirm that patches were deployed within the mandated timeframe.
   WHY: Change tickets provide an audit trail that helps corroborate patch management logs and reveal response timing for vulnerable components.
   HOW: Select a sample of high-risk patch events; review the associated tickets and approval records to ensure installation occurred within one month of release.
   FAIL: Tickets with delayed or missing patch deployment notes suggest a lack of operational rigor.

4. CHECK: Interview system administrators and review configuration settings in patch automation tools.
   WHY: Direct interviews and configuration reviews help validate that processes are actively managed and supported by automated tools, reducing human error.
   HOW: Ask administrators how patch prioritization is handled, check that the automation settings align with critical patch timelines, and review any exception logs for deviations.
   FAIL: Inconsistent responses or misconfigurations in the automation settings indicate potential gaps in the effective implementation of the control.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts: Focus on automated log exports and live vulnerability scanning dashboards which are less likely to be manipulated and provide real-time status.
• Critical indicators: Pay attention to discrepancies between patch release dates and installation logs; any out-of-date scan reports, particularly on high-risk systems, are red flags.
• Details that matter: Ensure that the patch management process covers all relevant systems (including virtualized and cloud components) and that the risk ranking process for vulnerabilities is actively maintained to prioritize patching appropriately.
In summary, practical validation should integrate automated system evidence with process verification, ensuring that patch management is both documented and demonstrably effective in protecting all system components from known vulnerabilities.
content:
6.3.3 All system components are protected from known vulnerabilities by installing applicable security patches/updates as follows:
• Patches/updates for critical vulnerabilities (identified according to the risk ranking process at Requirement 6.3.1) are installed within one month of release.
• All other applicable security patches/updates are installed within an appropriate time frame as determined by the entity’s assessment of the criticality of the risk to the environment as identified according to the risk ranking process at Requirement 6.3.1.

**Customized Approach Objective**
System components cannot be compromised via the exploitation of a known vulnerability.

---

**Defined Approach Testing Procedures**

6.3.3.a Examine policies and procedures to verify processes are defined for addressing vulnerabilities by installing applicable security patches/updates in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

6.3.3.b Examine system components and related software and compare the list of installed security patches/updates to the most recent security patch/update information to verify vulnerabilities are addressed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

**Purpose**
New exploits are constantly being discovered, and these can permit attacks against systems that have previously been considered secure. If the most recent security patches/updates are not implemented on critical systems as soon as possible, a malicious actor can use these exploits to attack or disable a system or gain access to sensitive data.

**Good Practice**
Prioritizing security patches/updates for critical infrastructure ensures that high-priority systems and devices are protected from vulnerabilities as soon as possible after a patch is released. An entity’s patching cadence should factor in any re-evaluation of vulnerabilities and subsequent changes in the criticality of a vulnerability per Requirement 6.3.1. For example, a vulnerability initially identified as low risk could become a higher risk later. Additionally, vulnerabilities individually considered to be low or medium risk could collectively pose a high or critical risk if present on the same system, or if exploited on a low-risk system that could result in access to the CDE.

6.3.3 (continued)
It is recommended that the entity complete a targeted risk analysis (TRA) according to PCI DSS Requirement 12.3.1 to document the frequency of installing all other applicable security patches/updates. This TRA would include consideration of the entity’s assessment of the criticality of the risk to their environment as identified in the risk ranking process at Requirement 6.3.1.

Examples
An example time frame for installation of patches/updates could be 60 days for high-risk vulnerabilities and 90 days for others, as determined by the entity’s assessment of risk.

title: Maintain an inventory of software components for vulnerability management.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• An active and regularly updated inventory list that includes both bespoke/custom software and all incorporated third-party components (e.g., libraries, platforms, services) used for building those applications.
• Evidence from automated software composition analysis (SCA) or application discovery tools showing periodic scans and reconciliation with the inventory.
• Change logs or patch management records indicating that vulnerabilities identified in the inventory are tracked and remediated.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Relying solely on a static list stored in a document or spreadsheet that is not regularly updated, lacking integration with automated discovery tools.
• Providing evidence of an inventory that only covers well-known systems while neglecting custom or lesser-known components, thus creating blind spots.
• Faking compliance by showing outdated snapshots from automated tools without demonstrating continuous updates or integration into patch management workflows.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify the inventory list includes all bespoke, custom software, and their third-party dependencies.
   WHY: This confirms that all relevant software components are tracked for vulnerability management.
   HOW: Review the master inventory record; cross-reference with software build documents and deployment records. Check for entries covering libraries, frameworks, and integrations.
   FAIL: Missing components, especially third-party libraries or custom integrations, indicate a non-comprehensive inventory.

2. CHECK: Confirm integration of automated discovery or software composition analysis tools with the inventory.
   WHY: Automated tools ensure that the inventory is current and reflective of the actual deployed environment.
   HOW: Inspect tool configurations and review output logs; validate that scan results are reconciled with the maintained inventory. Interview technical staff to confirm tool usage and periodic scans.
   FAIL: Absence of automated tool evidence or mismatch between tool findings and the documented inventory suggests potential gaps.

3. CHECK: Validate change management or patch management logs to determine if vulnerabilities linked to listed components are being actively managed.
   WHY: This ensures that the inventory is not just maintained, but actively used in the vulnerability and patch management cycle.
   HOW: Trace a sample of inventory items with known vulnerabilities to their corresponding remediation or patch deployment records. Check change tickets and patch logs.
   FAIL: Lack of linkage between inventory items and documented remediation actions implies the inventory is not operationally effective.

4. CHECK: Interview technical and operational personnel to confirm routine reviews and updates of the inventory.
   WHY: Personnel confirmation provides validate that the process is embedded in regular operations.
   HOW: Conduct interviews with staff responsible for vulnerability management, asking about frequency of updates, integration with CI/CD pipelines, and processes for adding new software components.
   FAIL: Inability of staff to clearly describe the update process or inconsistent practices indicates poor implementation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Leverage automated scans from SCA tools and compare timestamps; a recent scan with full coverage is a strong indicator that the inventory is actively maintained.
• Critical indicator: A dynamic inventory that interfaces with patch management systems (triggering alerts or automated updates) is a sign of mature implementation.
• Detail that matters: Look for evidence that the inventory covers all layers of software (from application code to third-party services) and is periodically reconciled with actual deployments, not just static documentation.
• Watch out for superficial compliance: If the inventory has not been updated in months or lacks integration with remediation processes, it’s likely a check-the-box exercise rather than effective risk management.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
6.3.2 An inventory of bespoke and custom software, and third-party software components incorporated into bespoke and custom software is maintained to facilitate vulnerability and patch management.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
6.3.2.a Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that an inventory is maintained and used to identify and address vulnerabilities.
6.3.2.b Examine software documentation, including for bespoke and custom software that integrates third-party components, and compare it to the inventory.

Customized Approach Objective
Known vulnerabilities in third-party software components cannot be exploited in bespoke and custom software.

Purpose
Maintaining an exhaustive inventory enables effective vulnerability and patch management, reducing the risk posed by outdated or insecure software components.

Good Practice
The inventory should cover all payment software components and dependencies, including platforms, third-party libraries, services, and other functionalities. Consider the use of software composition analysis tools and application discovery tools.

Applicability Notes
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be fully required during PCI DSS assessments.

title: Identify and manage security vulnerabilities proactively.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of subscriptions or integrations with industry-recognized vulnerability feeds (e.g., alerts from international/national CERTs, vendor bulletins, CVE databases) visible in security dashboards or alerting systems.
• Artifacts such as automated vulnerability scan logs, incident tickets, and risk ranking reports that show vulnerabilities being identified, categorized (e.g., high, critical), and tracked.
• Observable operational patterns like regular review meetings, documented vulnerability remediation timelines, and integration with change management processes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might only maintain a static list of vulnerabilities without active monitoring of live feeds, which can misrepresent robust vulnerability management.
• Reliance solely on periodic manual scans without continuous automated feeds can lead to outdated risk assessments.
• Policies that exist on paper but do not reflect true integration with operational systems or lack detailed risk prioritization methods may indicate superficial compliance.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify subscription and integration with recognized external vulnerability feeds.
WHY: Continuous updates from industry sources prove proactive monitoring and identification of new threats.
HOW: Review configurations in security information and event management (SIEM) systems, inspect alerting dashboards, and check system logs for incoming CERT/vulnerability alerts.
FAIL: Absence of automated alerts, outdated feed entries, or manual entries with no evidence of live system integration.

CHECK: Confirm the existence and operational effectiveness of a formal risk ranking process.
WHY: A well-documented, objective risk ranking directly supports prioritization of remediation for high-risk vulnerabilities.
HOW: Examine risk assessment reports, documentation of the methodology (including CVSS, vendor scores, impact analysis), and verify that high/critical issues are promptly flagged in vulnerability management tools.
FAIL: Evidence that risk rankings are not consistently applied or that the process is missing rigorous, objective criteria.

CHECK: Validate that vulnerabilities for bespoke, custom, and third-party software are actively included in the vulnerability management framework.
WHY: Comprehensive protection requires that all software types are monitored and risk-assessed to prevent overlooked security gaps.
HOW: Inspect integration logs, review incidents related to custom or third-party applications, and interview personnel regarding how these systems are tracked and prioritized.
FAIL: A gap in recorded vulnerabilities for specialized or non-standard systems, or a process solely directed at mainstream vendor software.

CHECK: Check for integration with change and risk management processes.
WHY: Effective vulnerability management must coincide with change control to ensure prompt remediation and evidence of continual review.
HOW: Review change management logs, incident response plans, and cross-reference with vulnerability tracking records to ensure correlations between newly identified vulnerabilities and system changes.
FAIL: Disconnects between change management records and vulnerability assessments, or documentation that does not reflect coordinated risk management efforts.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Look for automated dashboards that display real-time vulnerability feeds and risk trending. They are hard to fake compared to static documents.
• Critical Indicator: Inconsistent methodologies or delayed remediation times are red flags. Check if risk rankings are periodically recalibrated in light of new threat intelligence.
• Detail to Watch: Ensure that bespoke and third-party applications are not an afterthought; these are frequently neglected and are common targets for sophisticated attacks.
• Verifier Hint: Automated scans with detailed reporting that include timestamps and linkage to remediation tickets are a strong indicator of active, ongoing vulnerability management rather than an exercise in compliance documentation.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
6.3.1 Security vulnerabilities are identified and managed as follows:
- New security vulnerabilities are identified using industry-recognized sources for security vulnerability information, including alerts from international and national CERTs.
- Vulnerabilities are assigned a risk ranking based on industry best practices and potential impact.
- Risk rankings identify, at a minimum, all vulnerabilities considered to be high-risk or critical to the environment.
- Vulnerabilities for bespoke and custom, and third-party software are covered.

Customized Approach Objective
New system and software vulnerabilities that may impact the security of cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data are monitored, cataloged, and risk assessed.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
6.3.1.a Examine policies and procedures for identifying and managing security vulnerabilities to verify that processes are defined as specified.
6.3.1.b Interview personnel, examine documentation, and observe processes to verify that security vulnerabilities are managed in accordance with this requirement.

Purpose
Classifying vulnerabilities by risk (e.g., critical, high, medium, or low) helps prioritize remediation efforts to reduce potential exploitation.

Good Practice
Use a formal and objective methodology for risk ranking vulnerabilities, ensuring that high-risk vulnerabilities are promptly addressed. Consider external alerts, CVSS scores, vendor classifications, and the overall impact on critical systems.

Applicability Notes and Further Guidance
This control also encompasses guidance on integrating vulnerability management with other risk and change management processes, and may include additional practices such as bug bounty programs and monitoring of external sources.

title: Apply software engineering techniques to prevent common software attacks.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying that software engineering techniques are actively used to prevent common software attacks:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence of integrated static (SAST) and dynamic application security testing (DAST) in the CI/CD pipelines. For example, logs or dashboards showing recurring scans that target injection flaws, buffer issues, or misconfigured cryptographic libraries.
   • Documented secure coding standards and code review checklists that explicitly address common attack categories (injection, business logic, access control, etc.).
   • Records of threat modeling sessions and code reviews that focus on identifying and mitigating high-risk vulnerabilities.
   • Training materials and sign-offs from developers indicating that secure coding practices are a mandatory part of their development lifecycle.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply document secure development practices without actual enforcement in code reviews or automated tests.
   • Relying solely on annual training or periodic manual reviews, rather than continuous integration of automated testing and continuous monitoring.
   • Faking compliance by only producing policies or guidance documents that are never practically applied during the software development process.
   • Neglecting to update scanning rules or tools, resulting in obsolete automated security checks that do not catch current attack vectors.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify CI/CD pipeline integration for automated security testing (SAST/DAST).
     WHY: Automated tools in the development pipeline actively scan for injection flaws and other coding errors, proving that security testing is not just documented but operational.
     HOW: Request logs, dashboard screenshots, or automated report outputs from the CI/CD pipeline that detail recent scanning activities and remediation of vulnerabilities.
     FAIL: Absence of scanner integration or evidence that scans are run infrequently or not at all.

   • CHECK: Review secure coding standards and evidence of recurring threat modeling sessions and code reviews.
     WHY: Consistent threat modeling and use of secure coding guidelines demonstrate proactive mitigation of common vulnerabilities.
     HOW: Interview development leads and access internal documentation showing regular threat assessments, secure design documents, and code review records that reference secure coding practices.
     FAIL: Documents that are outdated, show minimal engagement, or reveal that security is an afterthought in the development lifecycle.

   • CHECK: Examine training records and developer attestations on secure coding practices.
     WHY: Regular training confirms that developers are aware of the risks and adopt engineering techniques to mitigate them.
     HOW: Validate records from HR systems or learning management systems detailing scheduled secure coding trainings, along with quiz scores or attendance, and, if possible, sample feedback on how training impacted code quality.
     FAIL: Training records are missing, sporadic, or indicate merely a perfunctory review of policies rather than actual engagement.

   • CHECK: Validate testing artifacts that target business logic and input validation weaknesses (manual penetration test reports, bespoke testing scripts for injection).
     WHY: Evidence from testing activities shows that controls targeting common attacks (like SQL injection, XSS, CSRF) are practically being tested and verified.
     HOW: Review recent penetration testing or vulnerability assessment reports focusing on input validation, error handling, and cryptography practices. Look for documented risk findings and remediation actions taken related to these issues.
     FAIL: Reports that highlight recurring unmitigated vulnerabilities or solely generic test cases not targeting the common attack scenarios specified in the control.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Prioritize evidence from automated security tools feeding directly into the development cycle. If integration is missing, this is a red flag.
   • Critical indicators of poor implementation include patchy or outdated training records and ad hoc threat modeling that only surfaces when a vulnerability is already exploited.
   • Ensure that any secure coding checklist or review process is actually enforced in the code repository’s pull request workflow; lack of real-time, documented security review is a major gap.
   • Always correlate the presence of security policies and documented procedures with observable, recurring phenomenon in CI/CD metrics or test artifacts – policies alone aren’t sufficient to infer working controls.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
6.2.4 Software engineering techniques or other methods are defined and in use by software development personnel to prevent or mitigate common software attacks and related vulnerabilities in bespoke and custom software, including but not limited to the following:

- Injection attacks, including SQL, LDAP, XPath, or other command, parameter, object, fault, or injection-type flaws.
- Attacks on data and data structures, including attempts to manipulate buffers, pointers, input data, or shared data.
- Attacks on cryptography usage, including attempts to exploit weak, insecure, or inappropriate cryptographic implementations, algorithms, cipher suites, or modes of operation.
- Attacks on business logic, including attempts to abuse or bypass application features and functionalities through manipulation of APIs, communication protocols, client-side functionality, or other system functions. This includes cross-site scripting (XSS) and cross-site request forgery (CSRF).
- Attacks on access control mechanisms, including attempts to bypass or abuse identification, authentication, or authorization mechanisms, or weaknesses in their implementation.
- Attacks via any "high-risk" vulnerabilities identified as defined in Requirement 6.3.1.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
6.2.4 Examine documented procedures and interview responsible software development personnel to verify that software engineering techniques or other methods are defined and in use to prevent or mitigate all common software attacks as specified in this requirement.

Purpose
Detecting or preventing common coding errors early in the development process reduces the risk of vulnerabilities in production. This approach is known as "shifting security left." 

Good Practice
Ensure that code is developed with a focus on preventing common software attacks, including the use of both automated and manual testing practices.

title: Conduct manual code reviews with external review and management approval.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes code review meeting minutes, sign-off sheets, or digital approval logs that show the review was conducted by a personnel other than the author, and that management approval was obtained.
• Technical artifacts such as annotated code review checklists, commit messages with review comments, and source code repository logs that document review cycles.
• Operational patterns include consistent pairing of code changes with documented review steps, external reviewer comments, and audit trails in the version control system demonstrating independent verifications.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply attach a preformatted review checklist or memos post release instead of conducting an actual review.
• Relying solely on automated tools that simulate review comments without a human review can be a surface-level approach that does not meet the “manual review” requirement.
• Some entities might have the code author also provide review signatures or rotate internal personnel in a way that does not preserve true independence.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Confirm that documented procedures for manual code reviews require that reviewers be independent of the code author and possess secure coding expertise.
   WHY: This ensures that reviews are conducted by knowledgeable and independent personnel and not merely as a formality.
   HOW: Interview development and QA personnel, review the formal software development process documentation, and check for specific references to external or non-originating reviewers.
   FAIL: Absence of documented criteria or process guidelines that explicitly mandate independent review and management sign-off indicates a failure.

2. CHECK: Verify that evidence from the version control system or code repository shows review annotations from personnel other than the author.
   WHY: Code repository logs or commit comments provide automated, tamper-resistant evidence of actual review events.
   HOW: Analyze commit logs, pull request reviews, or specific code review tools to confirm that independent reviewers have logged comments, and that management approvals are recorded before production deployment.
   FAIL: Evidence where the same individual is listed both as the author and the reviewer, or missing management approval timestamps, weakens compliance.

3. CHECK: Review sample change records of bespoke or custom software code to trace through the manual review process.
   WHY: Direct sample evidence shows that mandatory manual reviews are applied consistently prior to production release.
   HOW: Select a sample of recent changes and verify the presence of annotated review checklists, independent reviewer signatures, and management approval statements within the change history.
   FAIL: Missing review evidence, lack of external reviewer annotations, or missing approval confirmation on records, signifies non-compliance.

4. CHECK: Interview management to understand how they approve code changes and oversee the code review process.
   WHY: Management involvement is a critical component, so their active oversight confirms the review's authority and thoroughness.
   HOW: Conduct interviews with management responsible for code releases to verify that they review summaries of manual code review reports and that there is an established approval process prior to production release.
   FAIL: Inability to produce management review records or vague processes that don’t demonstrate clear managerial oversight indicates poor implementation.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Look for automated alerts from version control systems that flag unauthorised merges or omissions in the review process. These are often integrated into CI/CD pipelines.
• Critical indicator: Consistent evidence of external reviewers (not simply rotating duties among the same small team) and clearly recorded management approvals are key quality indicators.
• Detail matters: Ensure that the process documentation and sample evidence include specifics such as reviewer names, dates, and detailed review comments rather than generic check-lists. This granularity is less likely to be fabricated and confirms operational effectiveness.
• Additional shortcut: Compare code release dates with review meeting dates to ensure they logically precede production deployment. Discrepancies can indicate manual review steps have been bypassed.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
6.2.3.1 If manual code reviews are performed for bespoke and custom software prior to release to production, code changes are:
• Reviewed by individuals other than the originating code author, and who are knowledgeable about code-review techniques and secure coding practices.
• Reviewed and approved by management prior to release.

Customized Approach Objective
The manual code review process cannot be bypassed and is effective at discovering security vulnerabilities.

Applicability Notes
Manual code reviews can be conducted by knowledgeable internal or third-party personnel. The reviewer must be independent of the code author.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
6.2.3.1.a Examine documented software development procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify that processes are defined for manual code reviews in accordance with the requirement.
6.2.3.1.b Examine evidence of changes to bespoke and custom software and interview personnel to verify that manual code reviews were conducted as required.

Purpose
Having code reviewed by someone other than the original author minimizes the possibility of logic or security errors reaching production.

Good Practice
Utilize formal review methodologies and checklists, and monitor reviewer workload to ensure quality.

title: Review bespoke and custom software prior to release.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes a clearly defined and documented code review process accompanied by logs or subscriptions in version control systems (e.g., GitHub/GitLab merge/pull request histories showing detailed reviews and sign-offs).
• Technical artifacts include formal code review checklists, automated/static analysis scan reports, audit trails in change management or ticketing systems that document review approvals, and records of remediation for any found vulnerabilities.
• Operational patterns indicate that every bespoke or custom software release is tied to a mandatory review process with traceable approvals from designated reviewers, evidence of manual code reviews supplementing automated scans, and feedback loops for security findings.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations sometimes produce documentation that describes a code review process but in practice, reviews are either superficial or only performed in an ad hoc manner without adherence to secure coding guidelines.
• Some may rely solely on automated code scans without manual verification, or they might retrofit reviews after a release rather than as a pre-release requirement.
• Surface-level implementations include using generic checklist tick-boxes without evidence of thorough review or failure to capture reviewer comments and corrective actions in an auditable manner.
• Technical gaps often include missing integration between version control, automated tools, and review tracking, as well as inconsistent enforcement of review as part of the release process.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Examine the version control merge/pull request history for bespoke and custom software.
WHY: This offers observable and automated evidence that code changes went through peer reviews before release.
HOW: Access the repository logs, look for tag markers indicating review completion, and verify that reviewer comments and approval are attached.
FAIL: No merge requests or absence of reviewer sign-offs indicate the process is either non-automated or bypassed.

CHECK: Review documented code review and secure coding procedures and compare these against actual process execution.
WHY: This confirms that the formalized procedures are integrated into daily development practices.
HOW: Interview development leads, correlate documented procedures with operational evidence in ticketing systems and repository logs, and check for consistency in review checklists.
FAIL: Discrepancies between documented procedures and operational evidence, or lack of adherence by development teams, signal a breakdown in implementation.

CHECK: Verify automated static code analysis scan reports and check integration with manual review steps.
WHY: A dual approach of both automated and manual reviews is essential to catch both common and emerging vulnerabilities.
HOW: Gather scan reports, validate that the reports correlate with changes in releases, and look for records of follow-up remediation efforts on identified vulnerabilities.
FAIL: Missing automation evidence, or reports that show known defect patterns without corresponding manual review commentary, indicate an ineffective process.

CHECK: Sample a few change tickets for bespoke/custom software and trace the entire review history from submission to approval.
WHY: This real-life tracking exercise confirms that secure coding guidelines were embedded into the release process for each change.
HOW: Choose recent change tickets, review audit logs for documentation of code review feedback, approvals, any identified issues, and the subsequent corrections.
FAIL: Tickets lacking detailed review steps or evidencing rushed approvals without feedback suggest a faked compliance process.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Look directly in version control and code repository systems; they provide hard-to-fake, timestamped evidence of the review process.
• Pay close attention to the quality and depth of reviewer comments – superficial comments may indicate a checkbox approach rather than a thorough review.
• Validate that the process isn’t simply reliant on automated tools; ensure manual reviews are present and are effectively linked to the code changes.
• Always cross-reference multiple sources (documentation, automated logs, and interviews) to confirm operational effectiveness rather than mere policy existence.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements
6.2.3 Bespoke and custom software is reviewed prior to being released into production or to customers, to identify and correct potential coding vulnerabilities, as follows:
- Code reviews ensure code is developed according to secure coding guidelines.
- Code reviews look for both existing and emerging software vulnerabilities.
- Appropriate corrections are implemented prior to release.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
6.2.3.a Examine documented software development procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify that processes are defined that require all bespoke and custom software to be reviewed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.
6.2.3.b Examine evidence of changes to bespoke and custom software to verify that the code changes were reviewed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Customized Approach Objective
Bespoke and custom software cannot be exploited via coding vulnerabilities.

Applicability Notes
This requirement for code reviews applies to all bespoke and custom software (both internal and public facing), as part of the system development lifecycle. Public-facing web applications are also subject to additional controls, as defined at PCI DSS Requirement 6.4.

Purpose
Security vulnerabilities in bespoke and custom software are commonly exploited by malicious individuals to gain network access and compromise account data.

Good Practice
Consider both manual and automated code reviews to detect vulnerabilities efficiently.

title: Train software development personnel in secure development annually.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Look for a formal training calendar/schedule, training materials (presentations, handouts, online modules), training certificates, and attendance records that explicitly reference secure development topics tailored to the languages and frameworks in use.
   - Technical Artifacts: Records from a Learning Management System (LMS), logs from training sessions including quizzes or assessments, calendar invites, and automated reminders that track annual completion. Check meeting minutes or internal communication replicas that confirm discussions around secure development topics.
