title: Enable and retain audit logs for the anti-malware solution.
methodology:
Below is the detailed validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-5.3.4 – 'Enable and retain audit logs for the anti-malware solution.'

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: An active anti-malware dashboard showing recent logs, scan histories, and update events. Administrative interfaces that allow review of log retention settings and timestamps are present.
   • Technical Artifacts: Config files from the anti-malware solution listing audit log parameters (e.g. logging level, retention duration of at least 12 months, etc.), centralized log server entries, and exported logs with clear timestamps of anti-malware events.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular automated scans, timely updates, and correlated log entries that match known malware event patterns. Reconciled records between monitoring systems and anti-malware logs indicating consistent archival over time.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Operators might simply enable logging in configuration files without ensuring that logs are actually generated, transmitted, or stored in a secure location outside of local volatile memory.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on policy documentation without verifying that system settings enforce a 12-month retention period, or that log encryption and integrity checks are implemented.
   • Technical Gaps: Inadequate monitoring of log generation; logs stored on the same system vulnerable to compromise; anti-malware solutions not configured to report detailed events (e.g., false-positives lack granularity).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that the anti-malware configuration file or admin interface shows logging is enabled with retention settings aligning with Requirement 10.5.1 (at least 12 months).
     WHY: This confirms that the anti-malware not only logs its actions but also is set to retain them for the required period.
     HOW: Locate the configuration file or admin console; inspect the parameter controlling log retention; compare to PCI-DSS specified requirements.
     FAIL: Missing or incorrect retention period settings or if logs are not being persisted for the required timeframe.

   • CHECK: Examine the centralized log management system or SIEM for records of anti-malware logs covering the past 12 months.
     WHY: Demonstrates that logs are being forwarded and retained securely, providing traceability of anti-malware actions.
     HOW: Query the centralized logging solution for records tagged with anti-malware events; verify timestamps across a 12-month period.
     FAIL: Incomplete historical logs, missing log entries, or logs older than expected retention period.

   • CHECK: Validate audit trails showing anti-malware events (updates, scans, detection events) that correspond with expected operational activities.
     WHY: Confirms that real events are being logged and that the anti‐malware solution is actively monitoring and reporting its actions.
     HOW: Cross-reference operational activity schedules (e.g., daily scans) with log timestamps; check for consistency and integrity in event records.
     FAIL: Inconsistencies between scheduled operations and logged events or absence of expected scan/update records.

   • CHECK: Test the anti-malware solution by simulating a benign malware incident to confirm that an event is logged and retained correctly.
     WHY: Provides practical evidence that when a real event occurs, the system logs it per requirements.
     HOW: Introduce a test file recognized by the solution, then review the logs for an event with correct timestamps and details.
     FAIL: No log entry is generated for the test event, or the log lacks critical details (e.g., timestamp, event type).

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use automated queries in your SIEM to filter by anti-malware events over the previous 12 months. If your solution supports logging APIs, perform a direct REST API query to validate active log collection settings.
   • Critical Indicators: Consistent timestamp consistency across logs, proper linking of log events to anti-malware actions, and correct archival to a secure, tamper-evident location.
   • Key Details: Look for fields in the logs that indicate not only the occurrence of scans but also the success/failure status, update events, and error conditions (if any). Missing details or truncated logs should prompt a further audit.
   • Practical Reminder: Make sure the review includes both local anti-malware logs and those forwarded to central logging to spot any deliberate removal or tampering of local logs.
content:
| Requirements and Testing Procedures | Guidance |
| --- | --- |
| **Defined Approach Requirements** | **Purpose** |
| 5.3.4 Audit logs for the anti-malware solution(s) are enabled and retained in accordance with Requirement 10.5.1. | It is important to track the effectiveness of the anti-malware mechanisms—for example, by confirming that updates and scans are being performed as expected, and that malware is identified and addressed. Audit logs also allow an entity to determine how malware entered the environment and track its activity when inside the entity’s network. |
| **Defined Approach Testing Procedures** |  |
| 5.3.4 Examine anti-malware solution(s) configurations to verify logs are enabled and retained in accordance with Requirement 10.5.1. |  |
| **Customized Approach Objective** |  |
| Historical records of anti-malware actions are immediately available and retained for at least 12 months. |

title: Enable automatic scanning for removable electronic media.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence: System configuration panels or management consoles showing settings for automatic scan triggers when a removable electronic media device (like USB, flash drive, etc.) is inserted.
   • Technical artifacts: Anti-malware configuration files, Group Policy or OS event logs that record scan initiations upon device insertion, and screenshots of anti-malware tool settings that indicate scanning modes for removable media.
   • Operational patterns: Repeated log entries demonstrating that scanning occurs in real time or continuously when peripheral devices are mounted, as well as observable alerts or notifications triggered by media insertion.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Superficial compliance: Organizations may simply document that scans should occur without actually enabling/remotely logging the events or linking the scan trigger to the actual removable media connection events.
   • Surface-level implementations: Some environments may only schedule periodic scans instead of real-time automatic scan configuration which does not satisfy the requirement.
   • Technical gaps: Missing evidence of integration between OS-level event hooks and the anti-malware software; scanning might only occur manually, or logging is disabled, so legitimate automatic scanning is not verifiable.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify anti-malware configuration settings to ensure automatic scanning or continuous behavioral analysis for removable media.
   WHY: Confirms that the solution is setup to detect and scan media at the time of insertion or mounting.
   HOW: Access the configuration interface (local or centralized management console) and review the settings or policies tied to media insertion events. Check for parameters such as "scan on device insertion" or similar.
   FAIL: No settings enabled for automatically triggering a scan on media insertion, or the property is set to manual.

   CHECK: Inspect system logs and scan result records for entries tied to removable media events.
   WHY: Provides operational evidence that the solution is actually performing the scanning during real events.
   HOW: Retrieve and analyze logs or alerts from the endpoint protection solution and OS log files; correlate timestamps with known removable media insert events.
   FAIL: No log entries or correlation between media insertion and scan initiation, or logs are consistently empty for device events.

   CHECK: Conduct a test by inserting a removable media device with a benign test file and observe if the scanning is triggered automatically.
   WHY: Practical test to ensure that the scanning mechanism is actively functioning rather than being only documented.
   HOW: Use a controlled benign test file (preferably in a sandboxed environment) on a removable device, insert it, and monitor the anti-malware console and logs for triggered scan activity.
   FAIL: The anti-malware does not trigger a scan on insertion or displays delays/omissions in scanning the inserted media.

   CHECK: Verify real-time integration between OS event notifications (or auto-run events) and the anti-malware scanning process on systems where removable media is allowed.
   WHY: Ensures that the anti-malware solution is proactively responding to hardware changes, not just set for scheduled scans.
   HOW: Check system event logs, tasks, or daemon settings that capture hardware events and ensure they are linked to the anti-malware action triggers.
   FAIL: No evidence of event-to-scan mapping, or the system only relies on periodic scanning without real-time responsiveness.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Focus on live system testing (inserting a removable device) and immediate log review rather than solely relying on policy documentation or screenshots that can be easily manipulated.
   • Critical Indicator: Logging mechanisms that capture the exact timing of both the insertion event and scan initiation are key. If the log shows a delay or absence of the scan trigger, the control is ineffective.
   • Detail Matter: Always validate that the anti-malware solution’s configuration is consistent across all endpoint machines and that the event triggers are not disabled by local policy overrides or user tampering.
   • Additional Consideration: Confirm that the continuous behavioral analysis (if implemented) is actively monitoring processes linked to executable code from removable devices, not merely relying on file signature scanning.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.3.3 For removable electronic media, the anti-malware solution(s):  
• Performs automatic scans when the media is inserted, connected, or logically mounted,  
OR  
• Performs continuous behavioral analysis of systems or processes when the media is inserted, connected, or logically mounted.

Customized Approach Objective

Malware cannot be introduced to system components via external removable media.

Applicability Notes

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.3.3.a Examine anti-malware solution(s) configurations to verify that, for removable electronic media, the solution is configured to perform at least one of the elements specified in this requirement.

5.3.3.b Examine system components with removable electronic media connected to verify that the solution(s) is enabled in accordance with at least one of the elements as specified in this requirement.

5.3.3.c Examine logs and scan results to verify that the solution(s) is enabled in accordance with at least one of the elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Portable media devices are often overlooked as an entry method for malware. Attackers will often pre-load malware onto portable devices such as USB and flash drives; connecting an infected device to a computer then triggers the malware, introducing new threats within the environment.

title: Define frequency of periodic malware scans via risk analysis.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: The entity’s risk analysis documents explicitly mention a defined frequency for periodic malware scans aligned with its risk posture. Additionally, documented scan schedules in the configuration or logs of the malware scanning system corroborate this frequency.
   - Technical Artifacts: Artifacts such as risk analysis assessments, scan schedules, system logs showing scan execution times, and change-management records demonstrating updates to the risk analysis when environmental risks change.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular execution of malware scans at intervals matching those defined in the risk analysis; periodic review meetings and updates in the risk analysis documentation indicating adaptation to evolving threats.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations might simply state a frequency in policy documents without ensuring the risk analysis process properly assesses the actual threat environment.
   - A surface-level implementation may involve having a schedule, but the actual frequency is not derived from or adjusted by comprehensive risk analysis. Automated scans may be running on a calendar schedule that is not scrutinized or updated over time.
   - Technical gaps include lacking proper review of risk assessment criteria or failure to update scan frequency when network or threat conditions change, leading to a static implementation that no longer reflects current risks.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that periodic malware scan frequency is explicitly defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis document.
     WHY: This demonstrates that the scanning frequency is not arbitrarily chosen, but based on a structured risk assessment.
     HOW: 1) Request and review the latest risk assessment report. 2) Check for a specific reference to malware scan frequency that aligns with the risk assessment elements from Requirement 12.3.1. 3) Validate the documentation date and ensure it is periodically updated.
     FAIL: Missing or generic risk analysis that does not specify the scanning frequency, or documentation that is outdated.

   CHECK: Corroborate the defined frequency with historical scan logs and malware scan result documentation.
     WHY: Matching the operational logs to the defined frequency confirms that the scans are being executed as per the risk analysis.
     HOW: 1) Extract scan execution logs from the malware scanner. 2) Compare timestamps against the frequency defined in the risk analysis. 3) Interview relevant personnel to confirm adherence to the defined schedule.
     FAIL: Inconsistencies between documented frequency and actual scan logs, or if personnel indicate deviations from the risk-based schedule.

   CHECK: Assess the risk analysis process for adherence to Requirement 12.3.1 elements.
     WHY: Ensuring every element of the threat and risk analysis methodology was followed confirms that the scan frequency selection is robust and tailored to risk exposure.
     HOW: 1) Review the risk analysis methodology section of the document. 2) Check for elements such as asset classification, threat likelihood, and impact analysis. 3) Verify that the process explicitly mentions periodic malware scan frequency determination.
     FAIL: A risk analysis process that omits key elements or does not clearly explain how the scan frequency was determined.

   CHECK: Validate evidence of periodic review and adjustments to the malware scanning frequency based on evolving risk conditions.
     WHY: This shows the organization actively uses risk analysis to update scan frequencies as threats evolve, rather than ignoring dynamic risk factors.
     HOW: 1) Check meeting minutes, change logs, or review reports where risk analysis outcomes and scan frequencies are adjusted. 2) Verify that change records match any adjustments in scan frequency in the malware scan configuration.
     FAIL: Lack of any review documentation or evidence of periodic updates to the risk analysis assessment or scan configuration.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Ask for a single instance of a scan schedule and immediately verify if the scheduled times match current risk analysis assertions; inconsistency here is a strong indicator of non-compliance.
   - Critical Detail: Look at the date stamps on risk analysis documents and scan logs. If these do not coincide with actual risk trigger events (e.g., after major network changes), the frequency may be outdated.
   - Tip: Validate that interviews of operational personnel align with documented evidence. Reluctance or vagueness in answers during interviews often signals that the risk analysis process is not fully ingrained in practice.
   - Focus on the process over the policy: Automate log checks where possible (for example using SIEM tools) to track scan execution intervals over time, reducing reliance on manual documentation review.

This methodology ensures that the frequency of periodic malware scans is not only documented but operationally enforced as part of a risk-based strategy and not merely a calendar-driven exercise.
content:
| Requirements and Testing Procedures       | Guidance                                                                                                                                                  |
|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Defined Approach Requirements**        |                                                                                                                                                          |
| 5.3.2.1 If periodic malware scans are performed to meet Requirement 5.3.2, the frequency of scans is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1. |                                                                                                                                                          |
| **Defined Approach Testing Procedures**  |                                                                                                                                                          |
| 5.3.2.1.a Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for the frequency of periodic malware scans to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1. | **Purpose**
Entities can determine the optimum period to undertake periodic scans based on their own assessment of the risks posed to their environments.                    |
| 5.3.2.1.b Examine documented results of periodic malware scans and interview personnel to verify scans are performed at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis performed for this requirement. |                                                                                                                                                          |
| **Customized Approach Objective**        | Scans by the malware solution are performed at a frequency that addresses the entity’s risk.                                                             |
| **Applicability Notes**                  | This requirement applies to entities conducting periodic malware scans to meet Requirement 5.3.2.
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment. |

title: Configure anti-malware solutions for periodic or real-time scans.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible signs include the installation of a dedicated anti‐malware application with a visible dashboard or system tray icon indicating active real-time scanning, scheduled scans, or behavioral monitoring. 
• Technical artifacts include configuration files or registry settings that specify scan frequency, logs of scheduled scans, and real-time alerts from the anti-malware solution. 
• Operational patterns include regular log entries documenting scan completions, updates to malware definitions, and alerts triggered by detected threats.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply enable the anti-malware solution during initial deployment and neglect periodic scans afterward. 
• Faking compliance by relying solely on a policy document rather than verifying if scans are run and logged can be a tactic to appear compliant.
• Outdated malware signatures or misconfigured scan schedules (e.g., scans scheduled at a time when systems are off) often fail in practice.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify active configuration of real-time scanning
WHY: Real-time scanning is essential for immediate threat detection, and its configuration indicates that malware won’t execute undetected.
HOW: Review the anti-malware application settings on workstations and servers to confirm real-time scanning is enabled. Check the application dashboard for alerts and real-time scan logs.
FAIL: Absence of real-time settings, disabled toggle, or missing logs for recent real-time events.

CHECK: Validate scheduled/periodic scan configuration
WHY: Scheduled scans provide comprehensive coverage, catching dormant or slow-acting malware that evades immediate detection.
HOW: Examine the scheduler settings, configuration files, or system management consoles for scheduled scanning tasks. Cross-reference with scan logs to ensure scans run as scheduled.
FAIL: Scan scheduler is misconfigured, disabled, or lacks corresponding log entries that prove execution.

CHECK: Review scan logs and historical results
WHY: Logs provide operational evidence that scans are occurring and are effective in detecting threats over time.
HOW: Access the centralized log repository or the anti-malware solution’s local logs. Verify timestamps, scan duration, and detection events; validate that scans cover entire file systems, including memory and boot sectors.
FAIL: Incomplete or missing logs, or logs that do not detail full scan scope for critical system components.

CHECK: Confirm periodic update of malware definitions/signature databases
WHY: Up-to-date signatures are critical for the detection of both known and emerging threats.
HOW: Inspect the update logs or configuration settings that indicate regular signature updates. Verify update frequency meets defined intervals (e.g., daily or hourly) and that the latest update date is acceptable.
FAIL: Evidence of outdated definitions or manual intervention required to update signatures.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include cross-checking automated alert systems with log timestamps to quickly spot gaps in scanning activities. 
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include disabled real-time scanning or recurring scan failures noted in logs, suggesting misconfigurations. 
• Details matter: ensure scans encompass all critical system files, temporary directories, and startup items; note that suboptimal configuration (e.g., excluding memory analysis) can allow sophisticated malware to bypass detection.
• Automated monitoring tools that consolidate anti-malware logs and send regular health-check alerts can provide a robust way to ensure ongoing compliance without manual log reviews.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.3.2 The anti-malware solution(s):  
• Performs periodic scans and active or real-time scans.  
OR  
• Performs continuous behavioral analysis of systems or processes.

Customized Approach Objective

Malware cannot complete execution.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.3.2.a Examine anti-malware solution(s) configurations, including an master installation of the software, to verify the solution(s) is configured to perform at least one of the elements specified in this requirement.

5.3.2.b Examine system components, including all operating system types identified as at risk for malware, to verify the solution(s) is enabled in accordance with at least one of the elements specified in this requirement.

5.3.2.c Examine logs and results to verify that the solution(s) is enabled in accordance with at least one of the elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Periodic scans can identify malware that is present, but currently inactive, within the environment. Some malware, such as zero-day malware, can enter an environment before the scan solution is capable of detecting it. Performing regular periodic scans or continuous behavioral analysis of systems or processes helps ensure that previously undetectable malware can be identified, removed, and investigated to determine how it gained access to the environment.

Good Practice

Using a combination of periodic scans (scheduled and on-demand) and active, real-time (on-access) scanning helps ensure that malware residing in both static and dynamic elements of the CDE is addressed. Users should also be able to run on-demand scans on their systems if suspicious activity is detected – this can be useful in the early detection of malware.

Scans should include the entire file system, including all disks, memory, and start-up files and boot records (at system restart) to detect all malware upon file execution, including any software that may be resident on a system but not currently active. Scan scope should include all systems and software in the CDE, including those that are often overlooked such as email servers, web browsers, and instant messaging software.

Definitions

Active, or real-time, scanning checks files for malware upon any attempt to open, close, rename, or otherwise interact with a file, preventing the malware from being activated.

title: Ensure anti-malware solutions are kept current via automatic updates.
methodology:
Below is a comprehensive methodology to validate that anti‐malware solutions are kept current via automatic updates:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes configuration screens or management console screens displaying that automatic updates are enabled.
• Technical artifacts include current update logs (timestamps, version numbers, definitions) from both endpoint devices and the centralized management console, as well as system event logs showing update events.
• Operational patterns such as routine, scheduled update events and automated alerts about signature status indicate that the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply configure the setting in the management console without verifying that endpoints are actually receiving updates. This can be faked by keeping default configuration documents without real-world evidence.
• Some may show outdated update logs that only refresh infrequently, or capture a one-time successful update instead of ongoing, automated behavior.
• A surface-level implementation might include manually downloading updates or having an update mechanism that is not centrally enforced across endpoints.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Auto-Update Configuration in Anti-Malware Settings
WHY: This confirms the system is set up to automatically fetch updates from the trusted source without requiring manual intervention.
HOW: Log into the anti-malware management console and each endpoint’s local configuration to check that the automatic update option is enabled and configured with the appropriate update interval and trusted update source.
FAIL: The setting is absent, set to manual update, or pointing to an untrusted/incorrect source.

CHECK: Review Update Logs on Endpoints and Central Management
WHY: Logs showing regular update activity, including the timestamp and update version/signature number, provide direct evidence that the solution is kept current.
HOW: Collect logs from both endpoints and the central management server. Verify that updates occur on a regular basis, that the latest definitions are being applied, and that timestamps correspond to recent updates.
FAIL: Logs are missing, outdated, or indicate that updates are not being automatically deployed regularly.

CHECK: Cross-Reference Update Registry/Timestamps on Key Files
WHY: Verification of file timestamps for the anti‐malware signatures and engine components confirms that automatic updates are actually taking effect on the system file level.
HOW: On a sample of endpoint systems, inspect the file properties or registry entries that record the last update time for malware definitions. Compare these timestamps against the expected update intervals.
FAIL: Timestamps are old or inconsistent across systems, suggesting that updates are not adequately deployed.

CHECK: Validate Update Distribution from a Central Location
WHY: When updates are staged and then distributed, ensuring that the central update repository reflects the latest definition files confirms trusted sourcing and propagation.
HOW: Interview administrators and inspect the central update repository. Validate that the repository is receiving automatic downloads from the trusted source and that distribution logs indicate push or pull events to endpoints.
FAIL: The repository contains outdated update files or lacks evidence of regular synchronization with the trusted source.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• A quick shortcut is to perform a timestamp check on a random set of endpoints to ensure that file modifications on signature files match the expected update schedule.
• Pay close attention to any error or warning messages in the logs that indicate failures in contacting the update source or in applying the updates.
• Don’t rely solely on management console settings – verify endpoint behaviors and actual file states to ensure that policies are effectively enforced.
• Look for signs of centralized reporting dashboards that continuously monitor update status across the enterprise. A lack thereof might indicate that automated update failures are not being detected promptly.
• A critical detail in practice is that even if automated update settings are enabled, network configurations (e.g., proxies, firewalls) may inadvertently block the update process – so ensure connectivity to the trusted update source is maintained.

This methodology provides both automated evidence (logs, timestamps) and observable conditions (configuration screenshots, centralized dashboards) that go beyond mere policy existence, focusing on operational effectiveness in keeping anti-malware solutions current.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.3.1 The anti-malware solution(s) is kept current via automatic updates.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

- 5.3.1.a Examine anti-malware solution(s) configurations, including any master installation of the software, to verify the solution is configured to perform automatic updates.
- 5.3.1.b Examine system components and logs, to verify that the anti-malware solution(s) and definitions are current and have been promptly deployed.

Customized Approach Objective

Anti-malware mechanisms can detect and address the latest malware threats.

Purpose

For an anti-malware solution to remain effective, it needs to have the latest security updates, signatures, threat analysis engines, and any other malware protections on which the solution relies.

Having an automated update process avoids burdening end users with responsibility for manually installing updates and provides greater assurance that anti-malware protection mechanisms are updated as quickly as possible after an update is released.

Good Practice

Anti-malware mechanisms should be updated via a trusted source as soon as possible after an update is available. Using a trusted common source to distribute updates to end-user systems helps ensure the integrity and consistency of the solution architecture.

Updates may be automatically downloaded to a central location—for example, to allow for testing—prior to being deployed to individual system components.

title: Define frequency of periodic evaluations for components not at risk.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documented targeted risk analysis that explicitly details the evaluation frequency for components identified as not at risk from malware.
• Records (logs, reports, tracked tickets) displaying periodic evaluations carried out at the defined intervals.
• Evidence from interviews with operational staff confirming adherence to the set evaluation schedule.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply include a generic risk analysis document that mentions evaluations without clearly correlating the frequency to non-risk systems.
• Providing outdated or templated documents that list frequencies without supporting evidence of actual, timely evaluations.
• Relying on annual reviews only, despite the risk analysis if the environment or technology requires more frequent evaluations.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that the targeted risk analysis explicitly defines the evaluation frequency for system components not at risk.
   WHY: A clear statement in the risk analysis confirms that the frequency is driven by a risk-based approach.
   HOW: Review the risk analysis documentation and cross-reference the defined frequency with the criteria specified in Requirement 12.3.1. 
   FAIL: Absence of a defined frequency or a generic statement that does not specifically address components not at risk.

2. CHECK: Confirm that documented periodic evaluation results for non-risk components are available and align with the stated frequency.
   WHY: Consistent documentation of evaluations proves the control is actively being executed as defined.
   HOW: Inspect evaluation reports, logs, or system audit records to verify that reviews occurred at the intervals specified in the risk analysis. 
   FAIL: Gaps, delays, or missing evidence that evaluations occurred at the prescribed intervals.

3. CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for conducting periodic evaluations and verify their awareness of re-evaluation frequencies for non-risk systems.
   WHY: Staff awareness and adherence underscore operational effectiveness beyond mere documentation.
   HOW: Ask detailed questions on how and when evaluations are conducted; check for discrepancies between the procedures and the documented schedule.
   FAIL: Inconsistent responses or indications that the practice deviates from the documented risk analysis frequency.

4. CHECK: Correlate risk analysis frequency with system complexity and the number of systems evaluated to ensure the chosen frequency is justifiable.
   WHY: The frequency should be optimal and risk-appropriate as determined by the entity’s unique environment.
   HOW: Review the complexity and inventory of systems along with the risk analysis rationale; consider if the evaluation intervals are realistically supportive of emerging risk conditions.
   FAIL: Mismatch between system environment complexity and the periodic evaluation frequency, indicating a potentially superficial risk analysis.

EXPERT TIPS
• Always cross-check the risk analysis documentation with independent evidence such as evaluation logs and personnel interviews; documentation alone can be easily faked.
• Look for signs of periodic evaluations being performed reactively rather than on a fixed schedule; delayed reports or outdated logs are a red flag.
• Pay particular attention to changes in system complexity or newly introduced systems that might require a re-assessment of the defined frequency.
• Validate that the risk analysis aligns with the elements of Requirement 12.3.1, ensuring a comprehensive risk-based approach is applied.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.2.3.1 The frequency of periodic evaluations of system components identified as not at risk for malware is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.

Customized Approach Objective

Systems not known to be at risk from malware are re-evaluated at a frequency that addresses the entity’s risk.

Applicability Notes

This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.2.3.1.a Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for the frequency of periodic evaluations of system components identified as not at risk for malware to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.

5.2.3.1.b Examine documented results of periodic evaluations of system components identified as not at risk for malware and interview personnel to verify that evaluations are performed at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis performed for this requirement.

Guidance

Entities determine the optimum period to undertake the evaluation based on criteria such as the complexity of each entity’s environment and the number of types of systems that are required to be evaluated.

title: Periodically evaluate system components not at risk for malware.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for PCI DSS req-5.2.3 – Periodically evaluate system components not at risk for malware.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: A maintained, current inventory of system components marked as not at risk for malware along with dated evaluation reports. Look for meeting minutes or logs that discuss periodic reviews and changes in threat landscape.
• Technical Artifacts: The documented list of systems not requiring anti-malware protection; change management records showing periodic evaluations; integration with threat intelligence feeds (e.g., vendor notices) demonstrating that the list is revisited regularly.
• Operational Patterns: Scheduled reviews (monthly, quarterly, etc.) reflected in automated workflows or calendar events; regular updates to configuration management systems; interviews with personnel confirming the active monitoring of malware threat trends.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations might produce a static list of systems without evidence of updating it in light of new threat trends. They may also claim periodic evaluations without substantiating through logs, minutes, or even vendor threat updates.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Simply having a documented policy without transactional evidence (like updated lists or automated alerts) or relying solely on annual reviews instead of continuous monitoring.
• Technical Gaps: Failing to demonstrate that the process considers evolving malware threats. For example, the list may be maintained, but there is no record of checking current vendor security notices or threat intelligence feeds.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify the existence of a documented list of system components identified as not at risk for malware.
WHY: This list is a primary artifact proving that the organization has selected specific systems deemed safe from malware threats.
HOW: Review configuration management databases or asset inventories and compare with anti-malware deployment records per Requirement 5.2.1; look for timestamps and revision logs.
FAIL: If the list is missing, outdated, or inconsistent with deployed anti-malware solutions, this check fails.

CHECK: Examine records and logs of periodic evaluations that review evolving malware threats for the listed systems.
WHY: Historical evaluation records confirm that the organization actively monitors and re-assesses threat conditions.
HOW: Look for meeting minutes, automated system logs (e.g., from SIEM or vulnerability management tools), and documented decisions tied to vendor security bulletins.
FAIL: Absence of such records or reviews that are not aligned with current threat intelligence indicates non-compliance.

CHECK: Interview technical personnel responsible for the evaluation process to confirm that reviews include identification of emerging malware threats.
WHY: Direct personnel interviews can reveal if the reviews are actively incorporated into operational decision-making rather than a document review exercise.
HOW: Prepare questions about how often evaluations are performed, what sources are used for threat intelligence, and how decisions are documented; corroborate answers with existing records.
FAIL: If personnel seem unaware of the process details or if their accounts do not match existing evidence, the control’s operational effectiveness is in doubt.

CHECK: Cross-reference system components in the maintained list with systems lacking anti-malware solutions (per Requirement 5.2.1) to verify consistency.
WHY: Alignment between the two records illustrates an accurate classification of systems and shows that only systems assessed as not at risk are exempt from anti-malware measures.
HOW: Retrieve these two data sets, perform a reconciliation check, and ensure all discrepancies are documented and justified.
FAIL: Mismatches between records suggest either an incorrect or an outdated classification process.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Ask for recent evidence (within the last few months) demonstrating that threat intelligence from trusted sources was incorporated. Quick checks include reviewing dashboards from vulnerability management tools or recent change logs that mention vendor notices.
• Critical Indicators of Poor Implementation: Look out for a static inventory with few updates, out-of-date meeting minutes, or personnel who are unaware of current threat trends impacting their systems.
• Details to Look For: Confirm that each evaluation has clear criteria and timelines and that there is an established mechanism to add anti-malware protection if a system’s risk profile changes.

This methodology ensures that verification is based on observable evidence and operational practices rather than merely relying on documented policies.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.2.3 Any system components that are not at risk for malware are evaluated periodically to include the following:  
• A documented list of all system components not at risk for malware.  
• Identification and evaluation of evolving malware threats for those system components.  
• Confirmation whether such system components continue to not require anti-malware protection.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.2.3.a Examine documented policies and procedures to verify that a process is defined for periodic evaluations of any system components that are not at risk for malware that includes all elements specified in this requirement.

5.2.3.b Interview personnel to verify that the evaluations include all elements specified in this requirement.

5.2.3.c Examine the list of system components identified as not at risk of malware and compare to the system components without an anti-malware solution deployed per Requirement 5.2.1 to verify that the system components match for both requirements.

Customized Approach Objective

The entity maintains awareness of evolving malware threats to ensure that any systems not protected from malware are not at risk of infection.

Applicability Notes

System components covered by this requirement are those for which there is no anti-malware solution deployed per Requirement 5.2.1.

Guidance

Purpose

Certain systems, at a given point in time, may not currently be commonly targeted or affected by malware. However, industry trends for malware can change quickly, so it is important for organizations to be aware of new malware that might affect their systems—for example, by monitoring vendor security notices and anti-malware forums to determine whether its systems might be coming under threat from new and evolving malware.

Good Practice

If an entity determines that a particular system is not susceptible to any malware, the determination should be supported by industry evidence, vendor resources, and best practices.
The following steps can help entities during their periodic evaluations:  
• Identification of all system types previously determined to not require malware protection.  
• Review of industry vulnerability alerts and notices to determine if new threats exist for any identified system.  
• A documented conclusion about whether the system types remain not susceptible to malware.  
• A strategy to add malware protection for any system types for which malware protection has become necessary.
Trends in malware should be included in the identification of new security vulnerabilities at Requirement 6.3.1, and methods to address new trends should be incorporated into the entity’s configuration standards and protection mechanisms as needed.

title: Ensure anti-malware solution detects and addresses all types of malware.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Anti-malware agents actively running on endpoints and network appliances, with visible logs showing detections and response actions (blocking, quarantining, or removal) across all malware categories.
• Evidence of multi-layered defense controls: host-based, network-based, and cloud/managed anti-malware services, each with up‐to‐date signature definitions and heuristic/sandboxing configurations.
• Operational patterns such as routine signature updates, auto-scans, and alerts integrated into SIEM tools that show anomaly detection and incident response actions.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply configure the anti-malware solution with basic signature scanning but neglect advanced techniques like sandboxing or behavioral analysis required for newer malware types.
• Falsely documented processes or outdated manuals that claim full detection without reflecting current system configurations or lacking integration with threat intelligence updates.
• Systems that generate logs without correlating true malware encounters (i.e., logs may be tampered with, or limited to a subset of endpoints) making it appear as if all threats are being consistently detected.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review anti‐malware agent configuration settings
   WHY: Ensures the solution is deployed with all detection modes enabled (signature-based, heuristic, sandboxing, machine learning) covering known malware types.
   HOW: Verify configuration files or management console settings against vendor documentation; check that policies are enforced to update signatures and scan in real time.
   FAIL: If settings are incomplete, outdated, or if advanced techniques (e.g., sandboxing) are not configured.

2. CHECK: Analyze system and centralized logs for malware detection and response events
   WHY: Operational evidence shows that the solution is actively detecting and mitigating malware incidents.
   HOW: Inspect logs in SIEM or endpoint management platforms for alerts, block notifications, and evidence of malware removal; cross-check timestamps, signatures, and event sources.
   FAIL: If logs are sparse, missing, or if there’s a discrepancy between expected detection events and reported activities.

3. CHECK: Conduct a controlled test using a safe test file (e.g., EICAR test string)
   WHY: Live testing validates that the anti‐malware actively detects and stops a known test signature.
   HOW: Deploy the EICAR file in a controlled environment and monitor the anti‐malware solution for an alert, its subsequent quarantine/block action, and SIEM reporting.
   FAIL: If the test file is not detected, or if no remediation action (removal or quarantine) is logged.

4. CHECK: Verify integration with network-based anti-malware controls and threat intelligence feeds
   WHY: Modern solutions rely on layered security; integration means that both endpoints and network boundaries receive updated intelligence and respond to malware threats.
   HOW: Confirm that logs from firewalls, IDS/IPS systems, or cloud anti-malware services reflect coordinated actions with host-based controls; review alert and incident management reports.
   FAIL: If integrated logs or correlations are missing, indicating siloed or incomplete control implementations.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include checking for automatic signature update schedules and reviewing real-time alerts across multiple devices.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation are outdated signature definitions, disabled scanning modes, or missing logs in centralized monitoring.
• Always cross-check configurations with vendor best practices and consider the specific risks posed by advanced malware (e.g., ransomware) by looking for evidence of machine learning and sandboxing being actively used.
• Don’t rely solely on documentation – live tests (like the EICAR test) and consistent audit trails in logs are key to proving effective implementation.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.2.2 The deployed anti-malware solution(s):
- Detects all known types of malware.
- Removes, blocks, or contains all known types of malware.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures  

5.2.2 Examine vendor documentation and configurations of the anti-malware solution(s) to verify that the solution:
- Detects all known types of malware.
- Removes, blocks, or contains all known types of malware.

Customized Approach Objective

Malware cannot execute or infect other system components.

Guidance

Purpose

It is important to protect against all types and forms of malware to prevent unauthorized access.

Good Practice

Anti-malware solutions may include a combination of network-based controls, host-based controls, and endpoint security solutions. In addition to signature-based tools, capabilities used by modern anti-malware solutions include sandboxing, privilege escalation controls, and machine learning.
Solution techniques include preventing malware from getting into the network and removing or containing malware that does get into the network.

Examples

Types of malware include, but are not limited to, viruses, Trojans, worms, spyware, ransomware, keyloggers, rootkits, malicious code, scripts, and links.

title: Deploy anti-malware solutions on all system components.
methodology:
Practical Signs of Implementation:
• CHECK: Verify that a current anti‐malware agent is installed and actively running on each system component. 
  WHY: Active and properly updated anti‐malware software on endpoints indicates operational defenses against malware. 
  HOW: Inspect system desktops, servers, and network devices via remote management or agent inventory tools to confirm presence and version details. 
  FAIL: Absence of any installed agent or agents showing outdated definitions, missing updates, or disabled status.

• CHECK: Look for consistent log entries and alert events related to malware detection and scanning from the anti‐malware solution.
  WHY: Such logs demonstrate that the solution is actively monitoring and mitigating malware threats in real time. 
  HOW: Query SIEM systems or local logging repositories for recurring anti‐malware events, detections, or scan results.
  FAIL: Lack of regular log entries, error messages related to scan failures, or disabled integrations with monitoring solutions.

Common Evasions/Bypasses:
• Organizations may deploy a basic or trial version of anti‐malware software that does not cover all system components, or disable functionality on non-critical endpoints.
• Faking compliance by providing static screenshots or reports that do not reflect the current real-time configuration and logs.
• Overlooking less common system components (such as IoT devices or specialized control systems) which might not have anti‐malware agents installed.

Validation Steps:
1. CHECK: Identify and sample a range of system components (servers, workstations, specialized devices) and verify anti‐malware installation through an automated asset inventory tool.
   WHY: A well-maintained inventory with verified installations confirms coverage across the environment.
   HOW: Use endpoint management tools to generate lists of systems with associated anti‐malware version data and update histories.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between inventory records and deployed agents, or indications of unprotected devices.

2. CHECK: Review centralized logs and SIEM alerts for malware detection events from the anti‐malware solutions over a defined period (e.g. 30 days).
   WHY: Operational logs provide dynamic, real-world evidence that solutions are active, updated, and responsive.
   HOW: Configure queries in the SIEM to filter for events tagged with malware detections and verify timestamp continuity and resolution actions.
   FAIL: Infrequent or non-existent logging events despite the presence of known risks, suggesting improper configuration or inactive solutions.

3. CHECK: For any system component lacking an anti‐malware solution, examine periodic risk evaluation reports (per Requirement 5.2.3) to confirm documented risk analysis.
   WHY: This ensures that exceptions are properly reviewed, justified, and are an accepted deviation from the norm.
   HOW: Access management’s approved evaluation records and verify that risk assessments systematically and regularly cover all system components not having anti‐malware agents.
   FAIL: Missing or outdated evaluation reports, or components listed without clear justification that they are not at risk.

Expert Tips:
• Shortcut: Prioritize automated collection of endpoint data and real-time log monitoring to minimize reliance on static documents, which can be manipulated.
• Critical Indicator: Ensure that the anti‐malware solution is set to auto-update definitions and that its engine is actively scanning in real time – manual scans alone are insufficient.
• Detail Focus: Check for coverage on less obvious components such as virtualized environments, IoT devices, and network equipment that can act as malware vectors.
• Best Practice: Cross-reference asset inventories with actual deployed agents to identify and quickly remediate any gaps.

These steps provide a practical and operationally focused methodology to verify that anti‐malware solutions are fully deployed and functioning as required.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.2.1 An anti-malware solution(s) is deployed on all system components, except for those system components identified in periodic evaluations per Requirement 5.2.3 that concludes the system components are not at risk from malware.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.2.1.a Examine system components to verify that an anti-malware solution(s) is deployed on all system components, except for those determined to not be at risk from malware based on periodic evaluations per Requirement 5.2.3.

Purpose

There is a constant stream of attacks targeting newly discovered vulnerabilities in systems previously regarded as secure. Without an anti-malware solution that is updated regularly, new forms of malware can be used to attack systems, disable a network, or compromise data.

Good Practice

It is beneficial for entities to be aware of "zero-day" attacks (those that exploit a previously unknown vulnerability) and consider solutions that focus on behavioral characteristics and will alert and react to unexpected behavior.

Definitions

System components known to be affected by malware have active malware exploits available in the real world (not only theoretical exploits).

Customized Approach Objective

Automated mechanisms are implemented to prevent systems from becoming an attack vector for malware.

5.2.1.b For any system components without an anti-malware solution, examine the periodic evaluations to verify the component was evaluated and the evaluation concludes that the component is not at risk from malware.

title: Document and assign roles and responsibilities for Requirement 5 activities.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documents such as a formal roles and responsibilities matrix (e.g., RACI) that specifically reference activities in Requirement 5 are available.
• Digital artifacts like access control logs or change management records that clearly show assignments for Requirement 5 duties.
• Evidence of training or acknowledgment forms confirming that personnel understand their roles in executing Requirement 5.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Relying solely on a high-level policy that mentions roles without detailed, specific assignments tied to Requirement 5 activities.
• Creating documentation that is outdated or not methodically communicated to the operational staff responsible for Requirement 5 activities.
• Temporary assignments that are not tracked, leaving gaps between documentation and day-to-day operations. 

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify the existence of a detailed, documented RACI matrix or role assignment list specific to Requirement 5 activities.
WHY: This directly shows that roles and responsibilities are clearly defined and assigned.
HOW: Access and review the latest version of the matrix or document, ensuring that each activity under Requirement 5 is mapped to specific individuals or teams.
FAIL: If the document is missing, vague, or lacks clear mapping to requirement activities, indicating non-compliance.

CHECK: Interview personnel responsible for executing Requirement 5 activities.
WHY: Direct interviews validate operational understanding and actual assignment as documented.
HOW: Contact a sample of staff, ask them to describe their specific responsibilities for Requirement 5, and confirm if they have acknowledged these responsibilities via signatures or training records.
FAIL: If interviewees are unaware of specific responsibilities or contradict the documented assignments.

CHECK: Review change management and training records related to roles/responsibilities for Requirement 5 activities.
WHY: Ongoing training and documented changes demonstrate that the role assignments are maintained and current.
HOW: Examine logs or records of recent training sessions, updates to the roles matrix, and any acknowledgment forms/signatures confirming receipt and understanding.
FAIL: Absence of updated training or change records suggests roles may not be effectively communicated or maintained.

CHECK: Validate duty assignments in relevant system configurations (e.g., access rights to change or review malware protection controls).
WHY: Technical artifacts provide automated evidence that operational responsibilities are enforced.
HOW: Check system permissions and configurations to confirm that individuals assigned to perform Requirement 5 tasks have the necessary system access aligned with their documented roles.
FAIL: Mismatches or excessive permissions indicate discrepancies between documented roles and implemented controls.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcuts: Use automated tools to extract role data from systems and cross-reference with documented matrices to quickly spot discrepancies.
• Be cautious of static documents that haven't been updated to reflect current operational changes; active change logs are more indicative of proper implementation.
• Critical indicator: Consistency between what is documented, what is communicated during interviews, and what is observed in system configurations is key. Any misalignment often signals a lack of true buy-in or ongoing management.
• Always verify that acknowledgment or training records have timestamps that coincide with changes in the documented roles to ensure they are not retroactive.
• In practice, testing the understanding with scenario-based interview questions can reveal if employees truly grasp their roles beyond what is written in policy documents.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 5 are documented, assigned, and understood.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 5 are documented and assigned.

5.1.2.b Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 5 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as documented and are understood.

Customized Approach Objective

Day-to-day responsibilities for performing all the activities in Requirement 5 are allocated. Personnel are accountable for successful, continuous operation of these requirements.

Guidance

Purpose

If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, networks and systems may not be properly protected from malware.

Good Practice

Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents. As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities.

Examples

A method to document roles and responsibilities is a responsibility assignment matrix that includes who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed (also called a RACI matrix).

title: Manage and maintain security policies and procedures for malware prevention.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Look for a centralized repository (electronic or physical) containing all documented malware prevention policies and operational procedures.
  WHY: A single source of truth indicates that policies are not only documented but are managed, maintained, and accessible to all affected parties.
  HOW: Access the repository and verify recent revision dates, version control logs, and distribution records. Check if the repository is accessible to all relevant personnel.
  FAIL: Absence of a central repository or documents with outdated revision dates that do not reflect recent changes in processes or technology.

• CHECK: Confirm that related training sessions and communications (emails, intranet announcements) accompany policy updates.
  WHY: Frequent, targeted training and communication ensure that policies are known to all affected parties and are actively in use.
  HOW: Request schedules for training sessions, review attendance logs, and survey a sample of personnel to assess their understanding of malware prevention procedures.
  FAIL: Lack of training records, infrequent policy communications, or feedback indicating that staff are unaware of the latest procedures.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may produce updated documentation that looks current on paper, but fail to communicate or enforce the changes with staff. This can be observed if training logs or staff interviews do not reflect the latest revisions.
• Some entities may simulate a change management process by merely altering document headers without substantive updates or by not following a structured review and approval process.
• A superficial check of a few documents without verifying the complete distribution or operational integration can lead to false compliance.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify Documented Evidence of Policy & Procedure Management
   WHY: Ensures that the malware prevention policies are not just written but are actively managed.
   HOW: Access the policy document repository; verify that each document includes a clear revision history, timestamps, and approval signatures. Check if the process for updating is linked to identified changes in business processes or technology.
   FAIL: Review reveals missing revision controls, outdated documents, or absence of documented maintenance procedures.

2. CHECK: Observe and Validate Staff Awareness & Training
   WHY: Staff comprehension confirms that policies are known and in use beyond mere documentation.
   HOW: Interview a cross-section of employees and IT security staff; review training logs and communication records (e.g. emails, intranet posts) about the malware prevention policies.
   FAIL: Interviews uncover that staff are unaware of critical procedures or recent updates, or that training/campaign records are missing or sporadic.

3. CHECK: Evaluate Change Management & Update Procedures
   WHY: A robust change management process is a key operational indicator that policies are maintained and updated as needed.
   HOW: Review change management logs, meeting minutes, or incident reports that triggered an update. Verify that the updated policies are distributed in a timely manner.
   FAIL: Inability to provide evidence of regular review cycles or records of updates following changes in the threat landscape or operational events.

4. CHECK: Automated Evidence of Policy Dissemination and Revision Control
   WHY: Automated systems (e.g. document management systems with audit trails) provide verifiable and tamper-resistant evidence of policy lifecycle management.
   HOW: Access audit logs from the document management system, search for alerts or notifications related to policy changes, and confirm that these logs match with published documents.
   FAIL: No audit logs found, inconsistencies between manual records and automated logs, or logs showing infrequent updates inconsistent with current risk profiles.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Begin by checking the document management system's audit trails; these provide immediate insight into how often and effectively the policies are updated.
• Critical Indicator: Consistent training records and staff knowledge serve as the real measure of policy enforcement; documentation alone is insufficient.
• Focus on the change management triggers – policies should be updated not on a fixed schedule alone, but in response to identifiable changes in technology, threat environment, or business processes.
• Look for evidence of proactive dissemination, such as automated notifications following an update, which strongly indicates operational robustness.
• When interviewing, ask for real incident examples where the updated malware prevention policies came into play, verifying practical application beyond paper compliance.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

5.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 5 are:
- Documented.
- Kept up to date.
- In use.
- Known to all affected parties.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

5.1.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 5 are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Requirement 5.1.1 is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout Requirement 5. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in Requirement 5, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated.

Good Practice

It is important to update policies and procedures as needed to address changes in processes, technologies, and business objectives. For this reason, consider updating these documents as soon as possible after a change occurs and not only on a periodic cycle.

Definitions

Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

title: PAN must be secured with strong cryptography whenever sent via end-user messaging technologies.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Screenshots or logs from messaging systems (e.g., email gateways, SMS platforms, chat applications) showing encrypted transmission settings (TLS versions, cipher suites, etc.).
   • Technical Artifacts: Configuration files or system settings that explicitly enforce strong cryptography (such as TLS 1.2+) for outgoing PAN messages. Network traffic captures (pcap files) demonstrating encryption in transit are also strong evidence.
   • Operational Patterns: Routine logs showing successful encrypted sessions, periodic cryptographic key rotations, and alerts for any fallback to weaker protocols.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might produce documented policies or screenshots of configuration settings without verifying that encryption is enforced in live environments.
   • Relying solely on vendor documentation or default settings without confirming the actual cryptographic algorithms in use (e.g., outdated TLS or SSL versions).
   • Overlooking end-to-end encryption nuances. For example, encrypting the transport layer but exposing PAN within application-level messages, or sending via channels that technically support encryption but are misconfigured.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Active Encryption Settings for End-user Messaging Systems
   WHY: Active system configurations that enforce strong cryptography prove that PAN is protected when transmitted via these channels.
   HOW: Review configuration files and system settings for email, SMS, or chat solutions; check for enforced TLS 1.2 or higher (or other proven cryptographic protocols) and approved cipher suites. Cross-reference with vendor documentation.
   FAIL: If configurations are absent, show use of outdated/insecure protocols (e.g., TLS 1.0/1.1) or if encryption settings are missing.

   CHECK: Capture and Analyze Live Network Traffic
   WHY: Observing encrypted sessions in real time confirms that PAN data is never sent in cleartext.
   HOW: Use packet-sniffing tools on a controlled test transmission using end-user messaging technology; verify that packets are encrypted (e.g., using TLS handshake details, strong cipher negotiation).
   FAIL: If the packet capture reveals unencrypted or weakly encrypted PAN transmissions.

   CHECK: Examine System and Security Logs for Cryptographic Operations
   WHY: Logs provide evidence of active encryption, including successful cryptographic handshakes and alerts for potential issues.
   HOW: Identify logs from messaging systems that record encryption handshake events, verify that encryption failures or fallbacks are absent, and review audit trails for encryption key usage and rotations.
   FAIL: If logs indicate repeated cryptographic errors, fallback to non-approved protocols, or lack of any encryption-related events.

   CHECK: Compare Policy Documentation Against Operational Implementation
   WHY: This ensures that documented processes are not merely theoretical and have been practically implemented and validated.
   HOW: Cross-reference documented security policies with actual system configurations, vendor documentation, and live test results to ensure consistency.
   FAIL: If there is a mismatch where the policy indicates strong encryption but technical evidence shows weak encryption or configuration gaps.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Focus on live traffic captures and real-time logging as these are difficult to fake compared to static documentation.
   • Critical Indicator: Look for negotiated cipher suite details; the presence of obsolete cipher suites or protocols (like SSL or early TLS) is a red flag.
   • Practical Detail: Ensure that both the transport layer and any intermediate messaging systems (such as email relays or SMS aggregators) adhere to the same cryptographic standards. Also, verify that encryption applies in both directions if applicable.
   • Consider the full transmission chain; even if the endpoint supports strong encryption, weak links in the intermediary systems can expose PAN.
   • Look at the certificate management process as expired or misconfigured certificates can force fallback to insecure communication modes.

This methodology centers on directly verifying operational encryption through configuration, live testing, and logging analysis, ensuring that PAN is secured with strong cryptography whenever sent via end-user messaging technologies.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
4.2.2 PAN is secured with strong cryptography whenever it is sent via end-user messaging technologies.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
4.2.2.a Examine documented policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined to secure PAN with strong cryptography whenever sent over end-user messaging technologies.

4.2.2.b Examine system configurations and vendor documentation to verify that PAN is secured with strong cryptography whenever it is sent via end-user messaging technologies.

Customized Approach Objective:
Cleartext PAN cannot be read or intercepted from transmissions using end-user messaging technologies.

Applicability Notes:
This requirement also applies if a customer, or other third party, requests that PAN is sent to them via end-user messaging technologies.
There could be occurrences where an entity receives unsolicited cardholder data via an insecure communication channel that was not intended for transmissions of sensitive data. In this situation, the entity can choose to either include the channel in the scope of their CDE and secure it according to PCI DSS or delete the cardholder data and implement measures to prevent the channel from being used for cardholder data.

Purpose:
End-user messaging technologies typically can be easily intercepted by packet-sniffing during delivery across internal and public networks.

Good Practice:
The use of end-user messaging technology to send PAN should only be considered where there is a defined business need and should be controlled through the Acceptable Use Policies for end-user technologies defined by the entity according to Requirement 12.2.1.

Examples:
E-mail, instant messaging, SMS, and chat are examples of the type of end-user messaging technology that this requirement refers to.

title: Wireless networks must use industry best practices to implement strong cryptography for authentication and transmission.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for verifying that wireless networks transmitting PAN or connected to the CDE implement strong cryptography for authentication and transmission.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Review the wireless access point (AP) or controller dashboards to confirm settings use strong encryption protocols (e.g., WPA2/3 with AES). Look for configuration panels showing no allowance for legacy encryption modes or insecure fallback options.
   - Technical Artifacts: Obtain configuration snapshots, system logs, and wireless security scanning reports which list encryption types negotiated during client authentication. Evidence of firmware or software settings specifying encryption strength and authentication methods is key.
   - Operational Patterns: Regularly updated AP configurations and logs showing exclusive use of modern encryption algorithms (e.g., absence of downgrade events) indicate control is active. Network monitoring that captures handshakes should reflect strong cryptographic algorithms in use.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations might simply enable WPA2 on the admin dashboard without disabling insecure fallback modes (like WEP or TKIP fallback). 
   - Surface-level compliance may be demonstrated by showing configuration policies without verifying live network traffic and handshake encryption details.
   - Technical gaps include missing evidence of enforced no-downgrade rules in firmware settings, older hardware that does not support modern protocols, or incomplete validation of encryption in vendor-specific configurations.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK 1: Verify AP/Controller Configuration Settings
   • WHY: Confirming that the wireless hardware is set to use only strong encryption (e.g., WPA2/3 with AES) and secure authentication prevents interception of cleartext PAN data.
   • HOW: Log into the wireless access point and controller management interfaces; review settings to ensure that only industry-standard protocols such as WPA2/3 are enabled. Check firmware release notes to validate that defaults enforce strong cryptography. Use automated configuration compliance tools if available.
   • FAIL: Presence of legacy protocols (e.g., WEP, TKIP) or available fallback options that allow downgrade to weaker encryption.

   CHECK 2: Perform a Live Wireless Traffic Capture
   • WHY: Observing the actual network handshakes and encrypted transmissions ensures that the control is actively protecting PAN data in transit.
   • HOW: Use a wireless network scanner or packet capture tool (e.g., Wireshark) in a controlled test environment. Capture the initial authentication handshakes and check that the encryption method is AES with no evidence of insecure fallback negotiations.
   • FAIL: If captured packets indicate use of non-industry-standard methods, insecure encryption algorithms, or evidence of negotiation fallback to weaker protocols.

   CHECK 3: Verify Policy Enforcement and No-Fallback Settings
   • WHY: Ensuring that both the configuration and operational behaviors prevent downgrade attacks is critical to prevent interception.
   • HOW: Review system and security logs for any instances of attempted fallback or downgrade negotiations. Confirm in the wireless device settings that policy restrictions explicitly disable fallbacks to less secure protocols.
   • FAIL: Logs indicating fallback attempts or configuration settings that permit insecure modes even if labeled otherwise.

   CHECK 4: Cross-Validate with Vendor Documentation and Firmware
   • WHY: Matching the implementation against vendor documentation ensures that cryptography is correctly configured per industry best practices and that any unique secure configurations are not overlooked.
   • HOW: Compare the observed settings and firmware versions with vendor security guidelines and release documentation. Confirm that encryption settings meet, or exceed, recommended standards.
   • FAIL: Mismatches between documented industry best practices and actual configurations, or use of outdated firmware known to have insecure defaults.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Verification Shortcut: Focus on obtaining screenshots or live sessions of AP configurations and logs that show active enforcement of strong cryptography—these are harder to fake than static policies.
   - Critical Indicator: Downgrade negotiation events or fallback success messages in logs are red flags that the configuration is not fully enforced.
   - Detail Focus: Pay attention to firmware version details and any vendor-specific settings that might allow exceptions. Ensure that changes in settings (e.g., during maintenance) are audited regularly.
   - Additional Check: If possible, schedule periodic automated scans of wireless networks to capture any deviations from approved encryption settings in near real-time.

This methodology emphasizes automated, observable evidence along with live configuration reviews and traffic captures to verify that the implemented controls not only exist on paper but are effective in practice.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements:
**4.2.1.2** Wireless networks transmitting PAN or connected to the CDE use industry best practices to implement strong cryptography for authentication and transmission.

Customized Approach Objective:
Cleartext PAN cannot be read or intercepted from wireless network transmissions.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures:
**4.2.1.2** Examine system configurations to verify that wireless networks transmitting PAN or connected to the CDE use industry best practices to implement strong cryptography for authentication and transmission.

Guidance:
Purpose: Since wireless networks do not require physical media to connect, it is important to establish controls limiting who can connect and what transmission protocols will be used. Malicious users use free and widely available tools to eavesdrop on wireless communications. Use of strong cryptography can help limit disclosure of sensitive information across wireless networks.

Wireless networks present unique risks to an organization; therefore, they must be identified and protected according to industry requirements. Strong cryptography for authentication and transmission of PAN is required to prevent malicious users from gaining access to the wireless network or utilizing wireless networks to access other internal networks or data.
