Good Practice: Wireless networks should not permit fallback or downgrade to an insecure protocol or lower encryption strength that does not meet the intent of strong cryptography.

Further Information: Review the vendor's specific documentation for more details on the choice of protocols, configurations, and settings related to cryptography.

title: Maintain an inventory of trusted keys and certificates used to protect PAN during transmission.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visibly maintained inventory: Look for a centralized register (spreadsheet, database, or IT management system) listing all keys and certificates. This inventory should include key details (algorithm, key strength, roles, expiration, custodian) and for certificates, the issuing CA and expiration date.
   • Regular updates: Evidence of periodic reviews, automated alerts, or change logs that monitor certificate renewals or key replacements, ensuring that the inventory stays current.
   • Technical integration: Artifacts from systems (like configuration files, logs, or key management system outputs) that reference and use the trusted keys and certificates to protect PAN during transmission.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Static documentation: Organizations might produce a one-off document that lists keys and certificates without linking it to actual system configurations or logs showing ongoing monitoring.
   • Incomplete or outdated inventory: A register that rarely gets updated, missing recently renewed or replaced keys and certificates, or only includes high-level information without technical details.
   • Manual paper-based lists: Lists that are not integrated with automated systems, making them prone to staleness and human error.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Inventory Existence and Completeness
   WHY: An up-to-date, detailed inventory confirms that all keys and certificates protecting PAN in transit are identified and tracked.
   HOW: Review the inventory and verify entries include key attributes (algorithm, strength, expiration, custodian) and certificate details (issuing CA, expiration). Cross-check with actual system configurations where these keys/certificates are deployed.
   FAIL: The inventory is missing technical details, is incomplete, or does not reflect the current state of the environment.

   CHECK: Evidence of Regular Updates and Monitoring
   WHY: Automated updates and logs demonstrate that the inventory is actively maintained, reducing the risk of outdated trusted assets.
   HOW: Look for change logs, update notifications, or automated integration from the key management system. Verify if alerts or scheduled reviews for expiring keys/certificates are in place.
   FAIL: Lack of automation, missing change logs or alerts, or an inventory that hasn’t been updated in a significant period.

   CHECK: Alignment with Actual Operational Configurations
   WHY: Matching the inventory against system configurations ensures the documented keys and certificates are actually in use during PAN transmission.
   HOW: Sample a subset of keys/certificates from the inventory. Check corresponding system configurations, such as SSL/TLS settings or key management console outputs, to confirm the keys are correctly deployed and actively used.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between the inventory and system configurations, or evidence that some entries in the inventory are not deployed in practice.

   CHECK: Verification of Trust and Security Parameters
   WHY: Confirming that keys and certificates are not only listed but also meet security standards (trusted sources, valid issuers, proper expiration management) is crucial for protecting PAN.
   HOW: Validate that each key/certificate in the inventory has been vetted for trust (e.g., approved algorithm, recognized certificate authority) and check for policies regarding their lifecycle management. Use automated tools or reports if available.
   FAIL: Presence of keys/certificates from untrusted sources, deprecated algorithms, or expired credentials in the inventory.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Quick cross-reference tip: Use an automated script to pull live certificate details from the endpoints handling PAN transmission and compare them to the inventory. Discrepancies usually reveal gaps in manual record-keeping.
   • Indicator of poor implementation: An inventory that looks like a static snapshot rather than a dynamic, continuously updated log. This often suggests that periodic controls are ignored.
   • Focus on details: Pay special attention to expiration dates and renewal practices. Critical vulnerabilities often arise when expired or near-expiry keys/certificates remain active in the environment.
   • Integration is key: The more integrated your inventory is with automated monitoring tools, the harder it is for organizations to fake compliance with outdated data.
content:
4.2.1.1 An inventory of the entity’s trusted keys and certificates used to protect PAN during transmission is maintained.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
4.2.1.1.a Examine documented policies and procedures to verify that processes for maintaining the inventory are defined.
4.2.1.1.b Examine the inventory to verify it is kept up to date.

#### Customized Approach Objective
All keys and certificates used to protect PAN during transmission are identified and confirmed as trusted.

#### Applicability Notes
This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required.

#### Guidance
The inventory helps track algorithms, protocols, key strength, custodians, and expiry dates; for certificates, include the issuing CA and expiration date.

title: Implement strong cryptography and security protocols to safeguard PAN during transmission.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying that PAN (Primary Account Number) is protected during transmission using strong cryptography and secure protocols, in line with the defined requirement 4.2.1.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Logs and network traces that show data transmissions are encrypted (e.g. TLS connections). Evidence of certificate validation, such as accepted and rejected certificate logs, indicates only trusted keys are used.
   - Technical Artifacts: Configuration files for servers, load balancers, and applications that enforce secure TLS versions; certificate management system reports listing valid certificate chains (non-expired, not revoked). Network diagrams annotated with encryption endpoints and secure protocols are also evidential.
   - Operational Patterns: Routine operational monitoring that alerts on use of deprecated or insecure protocol downgrade attempts; periodic review and update of crypto libraries and security parameters.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Fake Compliance: Organizations might show policy documents and training evidence without actually enforcing protocol settings in systems (e.g. configuration that accepts deprecated protocols).
   - Surface-level Implementations: Relying solely on documentation without verifying active encryption in live traffic; using expired or self-signed certificates that are manually validated rather than being automatically checked.
   - Technical Gaps: Not configuring systems to reject insecure cipher suites or certificate revocation codes; only encrypting PAN end-to-end while ignoring intermediate transmission segments.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify TLS/SSL Configuration on Devices
   WHY: Ensures that only secure protocol versions (e.g. TLS 1.2/1.3) and appropriate cipher suites are enabled, preventing fallback to insecure algorithms.
   HOW: Use automated scanning tools (e.g. SSL Labs, Nmap’s ssl-enum-ciphers) to inspect server configurations and compare with documented policies. Additionally, manually examine configuration files for allowed protocols and ciphers.
   FAIL: Detection of deprecated protocols (e.g. SSL, TLS 1.0/1.1), insecure ciphers, or settings that permit protocol downgrades.

   CHECK: Review Certificate Management Logs and Configurations
   WHY: Validates that only trusted, valid (non-expired, not revoked) certificates are accepted during communications.
   HOW: Inspect log files from encryption endpoints (e.g. load balancers, firewalls) for certificate handshake details, and check certificate validity parameters and revocation status via certificate management systems.
   FAIL: Finding expired, self-signed, or revoked certificates that are actively accepted; lack of validation logs or evidence of certificate checking.

   CHECK: Capture and Analyze Network Traffic for PAN Transmissions
   WHY: Confirms that PAN data is encrypted during transit over public and open networks.
   HOW: Use network capture tools like Wireshark/Zeek to capture sample traffic, then examine if PAN data elements are appearing in plaintext. Validate presence of an encrypted session (e.g. TLS handshake and encrypted payload) when PAN is being transmitted.
   FAIL: Identification of PAN data in unencrypted (clear-text) segments; absence of a proper handshake sequence indicating no encryption.

   CHECK: Test Rejection of Untrusted Keys/Certificates
   WHY: Ensures that system configurations are enforcing trusted certificate validation, rejecting unverified or rogue certificates during transmission.
   HOW: Attempt controlled tests by injecting an untrusted certificate into a test environment and verifying the system’s behavior. Record automatic rejection events in the system logs.
   FAIL: Acceptance of untrusted or invalid certificates or lack of corresponding error/alert logs on certificate rejection.

   CHECK: Cross-check Operational Monitoring and Alerting Logs
   WHY: Continuous monitoring is critical for operational effectiveness, ensuring that any deviations or insecure transmissions are immediately flagged.
   HOW: Review intrusion detection system (IDS) or security information and event management (SIEM) dashboards for alerts related to encryption anomalies or downgrades, and verify that incidents are being correctly logged and remediated.
   FAIL: Lack of alerts in the presence of known insecure transmission attempts; outdated monitoring rules that don’t capture anomalies in crypto protocols.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Leverage automated scanning tools to quickly assess TLS configurations and certificate validity rather than manually parsing configuration files.
   - Critical Indicator: Pay special attention to logs showing certificate handshakes; repeated fallback attempts signal configuration weaknesses.
   - Details Matter: Verify that all endpoints, including internal systems interfacing externally, implement these controls rather than assuming only perimeter devices are in scope. Ensure that testing covers edge cases (e.g. load balancers, proxies).
   - Experience Note: In many cases, gaps appear not from flawed policies, but from overlooked platform updates; always check that systems are running current crypto libraries which support secure configurations.

This methodology focuses on providing automated and operation-level evidence (rather than mere documentation) and emphasizes real-world operational effectiveness and configuration implementation that cannot be easily faked.
content:
4.2 PAN is protected with strong cryptography during transmission.

#### Defined Approach Requirements
4.2.1 Strong cryptography and security protocols are implemented as follows to safeguard PAN during transmission over open, public networks:
• Only trusted keys and certificates are accepted.
• Certificates used are confirmed as valid and not expired or revoked.
• The protocol in use supports only secure versions/configurations and does not allow fallback to insecure algorithms.
• The encryption strength is appropriate for the methodology in use.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
4.2.1.a Examine documented policies and procedures and interview personnel to verify the processes.
4.2.1.b Examine system configurations to verify implementation of strong cryptography.
4.2.1.c Examine cardholder data transmissions to confirm all PAN is encrypted.
4.2.1.d Verify that untrusted keys/certificates are rejected.

#### Purpose
Sensitive PAN must be encrypted during transmission over public networks to prevent interception.

#### Good Practice
Consider encrypting PAN on internal networks as well and use network diagrams to identify all transmission points.

(Appearing across PDF pages 117 to 119, additional guidance, applicability notes, and examples are provided.)

title: Document and assign roles and responsibilities for activities in Requirement 4.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes formal documents or artifacts (e.g., organizational charts, RACI matrices) that list designated personnel responsible for activities under Requirement 4. Such artifacts should be accessible to the audit team and include role assignments, expected activities, and approval signatures or acknowledgment forms.
• Technical artifacts might include workflow management or ticketing systems where assigned roles are logged alongside activity completion data (e.g., automated notifications when a role handles a task). System logs that tag responsibilities to specific user IDs help validate the active assignment of roles.
• Operational patterns include regular review cycles (e.g., scheduled meetings or audits where roles are re-validated) and observable adherence to assigned duties as demonstrated in operational logs, task completions, and follow-up interviews confirming role awareness.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply create a document that lists roles without ensuring that responsibilities are actively communicated or enforced. This superficial documentation may not reflect real-world understanding.
• Some may assign generic roles that overlap in a way that responsibilities remain ambiguous or not tied to specific operational activities.
• Often, individual acknowledgments are recorded solely as a formality without periodic updates or evidence of refresher training, leading to outdated roles. Automated systems that simulate responsibility assignments without audit logs or concrete ties to activities are also a risk.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify existence of a formal document (e.g., RACI matrix or similar) that details roles and responsibilities for Requirement 4.
   WHY: It confirms formal assignment and documentation of accountability.
   HOW: Review the latest approved version of the document, confirm inclusion of key activities, and validate signatures or approval stamps.
   FAIL: Absence of a structured document or presence of outdated/unsigned files.

2. CHECK: Interview staff who are named in the document to verify understanding and active assignment of responsibilities.
   WHY: Verbal confirmation from personnel demonstrates that assignments are operational and understood.
   HOW: Conduct spot interviews with a sampling of individuals responsible for different activities, asking them to describe their role and responsibilities in Requirement 4.
   FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly articulate their responsibilities or discrepancies between the documentation and actual practice.

3. CHECK: Examine operational tools (e.g., ticketing systems, monitoring dashboards) for evidence that tasks associated with Requirement 4 are being logged against specific roles.
   WHY: It provides observable, automated evidence that roles are actively executing their responsibilities.
   HOW: Review logs and correlate them with the documented roles; check if alerts or task completions are attributed to responsible individuals.
   FAIL: Lack of mapping between logged activities and assigned roles, or evidence that tasks are handled by unauthorized users.

4. CHECK: Check for periodic training or acknowledgment records related to these roles to ensure continued awareness and updates to responsibilities.
   WHY: Regular updates indicate that roles are not static and that personnel remain informed of their responsibilities.
   HOW: Examine training records, meeting minutes, or periodic acknowledgments that reference Requirement 4 roles and responsibilities; check if roles have been reviewed in alignment with organizational changes.
   FAIL: No evidence of role reviews or training sessions, suggesting a risk that roles are misunderstood or outdated.

EXPERT TIPS
• As a shortcut, focus on the integration between the formal documentation (e.g., RACI matrix) and real-time operational logs. If the mapping between roles and logged activities is clear, it’s a strong indicator of practical implementation.
• Pay special attention to recent changes in the document: sign-offs and acknowledged updates are critical to ensure that the implementation keeps pace with organizational or process changes.
• Look for evidence of corrective actions when roles are found to be ambiguous or poorly implemented. A robust remediation record is a good indicator of genuine compliance management rather than just paper compliance.
• Check for consistency between the documented roles and actual IT/operational account assignments – mismatches here are a red flag indicating a potential bypass of effective responsibility assignment.

These checks collectively help validate that roles and responsibilities for activities under Requirement 4 are not only documented, but actively understood and operationalized within the organization.
content:
4.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 4 are documented, assigned, and understood.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
4.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 4 are documented and assigned.
4.1.2.b Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 4 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as documented and are understood.

#### Purpose
If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, critical activities may not be carried out.

#### Good Practice
Consider using a responsibility assignment matrix (RACI) and obtaining personnel acknowledgment of their roles.

title: Manage security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 4.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes access-controlled repositories (intranet, document management systems) where the current version of security policies and operational procedures (as identified in Requirement 4) are stored.
• Technical artifacts include version control logs, timestamps, and distribution lists which prove that the documents are kept up to date.
• Operational patterns such as periodic training records, meeting minutes, and internal audit reports indicate that the policies are not just documented, but actively used and known to all affected parties.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim compliance by producing outdated policy documents that list a review date, but without evidence of implementation or proper version control.
• Sometimes policies are well-documented on paper or in PDFs but are not integrated into day-to-day operations or properly communicated to the staff.
• Superficial training sessions or one-time email circulation can be mistakenly used as evidence, rather than proving ongoing familiarity and integration of the policies.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that the latest version of security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 4 is accessible and maintained in an official, controlled repository.
   WHY: This confirms the documents are current, under version control, and managed as per the requirements.
   HOW: Access the document repository, review version control logs (timestamps, change history), and compare with the documented review cycle schedule.
   FAIL: If you find outdated documents, missing version control comments, or no evidence of regular updates.

2. CHECK: Confirm that all affected parties have been made aware of these policies and procedures, and that usage is integrated into operational practices.
   WHY: It verifies that the policies are not just in place but are effectively communicated and employed across relevant departments.
   HOW: Interview a cross-section of staff and management; review training records, internal communications, and employee acknowledgements of receipt of policy updates.
   FAIL: If employees are unaware of the procedures, training records are missing, or there is a lack of evidence of distribution and acknowledgment.

3. CHECK: Examine the evidence of periodic reviews or audits that attest to the policies’ operational effectiveness and update process.
   WHY: Regular reviews ensure that the policies remain relevant and effective, reinforcing their active use over time.
   HOW: Look for scheduled review meeting minutes, audit reports, or inspection records; validate that the review cycle coincides with documented update schedules.
   FAIL: If there is no review evidence, or documentation shows that policies are simply re-circulated without critical updates based on risk assessments.

4. CHECK: Validate integration of policies in operational procedures through incident response or activity logs.
   WHY: This ensures that the policies are practically applied in operational contexts and not just theoretical documents.
   HOW: Review incident management records, change management logs, and any exercises (such as simulations or drills) that reference the documented policies.
   FAIL: If operational logs do not reference or follow the documented policies, indicating a mere paper compliance.

EXPERT TIPS
• A quick shortcut is to check the document repository’s metadata (last modified, version history) as it often reveals if regular reviews are taking place.
• Interviewing a few key personnel in various departments can quickly spotlight if the control is a paper exercise or an active part of the operational culture.
• Critical details include ensuring that training is an ongoing process and that there is a clear, documented feedback loop to update and enhance security policies based on real-world incidents.
• Pay attention to cross-departmental consistency – if policies are not uniformly implemented, even a solid document repository won’t suffice for effective compliance.
content:
4.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 4 are:
• Documented.
• Kept up to date.
• In use.
• Known to all affected parties.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
4.1.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 4 are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

#### Customized Approach Objective
Expectations, controls, and oversight for Requirement 4 activities are clearly defined and adhered to.

title: Provide key management guidance for service providers sharing cryptographic keys.
methodology:
CHECK: Review the key management guidance documentation provided to customers.
WHY: The document should clearly detail the secure transmission, storage, and updating of shared cryptographic keys, demonstrating that the service provider offers practical steps to manage keys according to PCI-DSS requirements 3.7.1 through 3.7.8.
HOW: Retrieve and review the guidance document from the customer portal or service provider's website. Verify its contents against the specific elements of secure key transmission (e.g., use of encrypted channels), secure storage (e.g., encrypted databases/HSMs), and periodic key updates.
FAIL: The document is either absent, overly generic, or does not address key management specifics as required.

CHECK: Verify distribution records or notices confirming the guidance has been disseminated to customers.
WHY: Evidence of distribution (e.g., emails, customer acknowledgements, support ticket logs) shows that the guidance isn’t merely theoretical but is actively provided to those using shared keys.
HOW: Examine distribution logs, email notices, or customer support communications that include the key management guidance. Look for timestamps and customer acknowledgments indicating receipt and review.
FAIL: No distribution records exist or the records show that only a generic security policy was provided instead of the detailed key management guidance.

CHECK: Interview relevant personnel to confirm operational use of the provided guidance.
WHY: Direct discussion with customer support or security operations personnel can confirm that customers are informed and trained on the guidance and that it is an active part of the service provider's key management regime.
HOW: Conduct interviews with customer support teams, security administrators, or compliance officers. Ask for examples of situations where the guidance was used in advising customers during key transmission or update procedures.
FAIL: Personnel are unaware of detailed guidance on key management, or they provide vague answers that suggest the guidance is not integrated in daily operations.

CHECK: Analyze evidence of periodic reviews or updates to the key management guidance.
WHY: Regularly updating the guidance ensures that it remains aligned with evolving security practices and compliance requirements.
HOW: Review version history logs and revision notes attached to the guidance document. Ensure these indicate regular reviews and incorporate updates as dictated by new cryptographic best practices or regulatory changes.
FAIL: The guidance document lacks version control or is outdated, indicating that the service provider does not actively maintain it.

EXPERT TIPS: 
• Look for specific technical instructions such as encryption protocol details (TLS configurations, HSM usage) within the documentation as these cannot be easily faked.
• Confirm that the guidance covers all three areas—transmission, storage, and update—rather than omitting one area, which is a common gap in superficial implementations.
• Quick validation can be achieved by cross-checking the guidance details against known industry best practices for cryptographic key management. Absence of such specifics can indicate poor implementation.
• Always check for customer acknowledgment of receipt and periodic reminders, reinforcing that the guidance is actively communicated and not merely archived documentation.
content:
3.7.9 Additional requirement for service providers only: Where a service provider shares cryptographic keys with its customers for transmission or storage of account data, guidance on secure transmission, storage and updating of such keys is documented and distributed to the service provider's customers.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
3.7.9 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: If the service provider shares cryptographic keys with its customers for transmission or storage of account data, examine the documentation provided to verify it includes guidance on how to securely transmit, store, and update customers' keys in accordance with all elements specified in Requirements 3.7.1 through 3.7.8.

#### Customized Approach Objective
Customers are provided with appropriate key management guidance whenever they receive shared cryptographic keys.

#### Applicability Notes
This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.

#### Purpose
Providing key management guidance helps prevent mismanagement or unauthorized disclosure of cryptographic keys.

title: Ensure key custodians formally acknowledge their key-custodian responsibilities.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence: Signed (written or electronic) acknowledgment forms stored in a centralized Key Management System (KMS) or HR system; documented procedures that include a section on key custodian responsibilities.
   • Technical artifacts: Audit logs showing electronic signatures, timestamped acknowledgments; configuration files or e-mail trails indicating that key custodians have been informed and have formally accepted their responsibilities.
   • Operational patterns: Regularly updated compliance reports; periodic recertification processes in place ensuring continued confirmation of responsibilities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking compliance: Simply including a generic statement in policy documents without actual signed or timestamped acknowledgment records.
   • Surface-level implementation: Relying solely on initial training sessions or policy read-throughs without ongoing verification; maintaining acknowledgments in unstructured records that are not easily auditable.
   • Technical gaps: Failure to integrate acknowledgment capture into operational systems, resulting in inconsistent or unsystematic record keeping.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify existence of documented key management policies/procedures that explicitly require key custodians’ formal acknowledgments.
   WHY: This ensures that there is an established process and requirement built into the organization's practices.
   HOW: Review policy documentation and verify inclusion of specific language on formal acknowledgment; cross-check with prior audit findings or policy update logs.
   FAIL: Absence of any reference to formal acknowledgments or outdated version of policies lacking this requirement.

   CHECK: Evaluate the repository where key custodian acknowledgments are stored (electronic database, HR system, or KMS) and cross-check with current key personnel lists.
   WHY: Demonstrates that acknowledgments are formally collected and maintained rather than being a one-time policy statement.
   HOW: Request a sample set of acknowledgment records, verify time stamps, signatures/electronic logs, and compare with active key custodian personnel.
   FAIL: If records are missing, incomplete, or do not align with the current list of custodians.

   CHECK: Confirm that key custodians have been trained on their responsibilities and have access to ongoing guidance/support.
   WHY: Being knowledgeable and updated on cryptographic operations is vital for operational security and effective key management.
   HOW: Interview a sample of key custodians and inspect training logs or help desk support documentation tied to key management roles.
   FAIL: Evidence indicating that key custodians are unclear about responsibilities or that training/support records are rarely updated.

   CHECK: Review audit trails or logs for periodic revalidations of key custodian acknowledgments.
   WHY: Demonstrates that the process isn’t static and is periodically revalidated to reflect any personnel changes or evolving responsibilities.
   HOW: Analyze log data or certificate renewal processes that require re-acknowledgment; correlate with changes found in HR systems or key management databases.
   FAIL: Absence of a recurring validation process, indicating a potential lapse in continuous compliance.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus on the electronic log system – if it’s well-maintained with automated alerts for missing or outdated acknowledgments, it’s a strong indicator of implementation effectiveness.
   • Critical indicator: Look for tightly coupled audit logs showing acknowledgment timestamps and linkage to key IDs; loose or manual records usually point to poor operational controls.
   • Details: Ensure that the acknowledgment mechanism is tied into onboarding/offboarding processes and integrates with identity management systems to avoid orphaned records.
   • Best Practice: Confirm that there are escalation procedures in case of missing acknowledgments, such as automated reminders or re-training events, which are strong signs of an operationally active control.
content:
3.7.8 Key management policies and procedures are implemented to include that cryptographic key custodians formally acknowledge (in writing or electronically) that they understand and accept their key-custodian responsibilities.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
3.7.8.a Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data and verify that they define acknowledgments for key custodians in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.
3.7.8.b Examine documentation or other evidence showing that key custodians have provided acknowledgments in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

#### Customized Approach Objective
Key custodians are knowledgeable about their responsibilities in relation to cryptographic operations and can access assistance and guidance when required.

#### Guidance
This process ensures that individuals in the key-custodian role commit to their responsibilities.

title: Prevent unauthorized substitution of cryptographic keys.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Evidence that the cryptographic system’s key management configuration enforces strict controls; logs showing key modification or substitution attempts with clear audit trails. 
• Operational patterns where only designated, authorized accounts can execute key substitution or key update actions, backed by role-based controls.
• Visible inclusion of anti-key substitution controls (e.g., digital signatures, certificate pinning) in system configurations.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Relying solely on documented policies without technical enforcement, such that the actual key management system does not enforce proper checks.
• Overlooked logging or inadequate audit trails that allow unauthorized substitutions to go undetected.
• Weak RBAC configurations where generic or privileged accounts can substitute keys without triggering controls.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Validate cryptographic system configuration for key management controls
WHY: Ensures that the key management system is set to reject any attempt of key substitution unless performed by a properly authenticated and authorized process.
HOW: Access system configuration settings or use a configuration query tool to confirm that key substitution operations are restricted. Check that all key update functions require multi-factor authentication and follow a documented process with role-based restrictions.
FAIL: The configuration allows key substitution without multi-layer verification, or settings are absent, permitting potential unauthorized changes.

CHECK: Review audit logs for key management events
WHY: Audit logs provide tangible evidence showing that the system enforces controls, by logging all key-related operations including attempts to substitute keys.
HOW: Examine logs from the cryptographic system or key management server over a designated review period. Look for events related to key updates or substitutions and verify that they include details about the authorized individual and timestamp. Automated log analysis scripts can help highlight anomalies.
FAIL: Gaps in logging practices or entries that lack evidence of proper authorization for key changes.

CHECK: Interview key management and security personnel
WHY: Direct discussion with personnel clarifies if practices align with documented controls and if processes are correctly followed in practice rather than existing only on paper.
HOW: Ask personnel to describe their process for updating keys and how unauthorized substitutions are prevented. Verify that their responses align with policy and that they can demonstrate using the controls in their day-to-day operations.
FAIL: Inconsistencies between interview responses and documented procedures, or personnel unable to detail the secure process for key updates.

CHECK: Conduct a controlled simulation of unauthorized key update attempt
WHY: A test simulating an unauthorized key substitution reveals implementation gaps in real-world scenarios.
HOW: In a controlled test environment, attempt to initiate a key substitution from an account that is not authorized, and observe if the system rejects the attempt. Use an automated test script if available.
FAIL: If the system accepts or processes unauthorized key substitution attempts without triggering security measures.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verify that audit logs are not only enabled but are also regularly reviewed by a dedicated monitoring team. Automated alerts should be configured for abnormal key management events.
• Look for evidence of multi-phase approval for key substitution actions; this is a strong indicator of robust implementation.
• Pay attention to the remediation process; a proper incident response plan should be documented and tested regularly.
• Check if key management solutions are integrated with centralized security monitoring to immediately detect any policy deviations.

These validation steps focus on observable, technical evidence of implementation rather than relying solely on policy documents, ensuring that controls preventing unauthorized substitution of cryptographic keys are both configured correctly and actively enforced.
content:
3.7.7 Key management policies and procedures are implemented to include the prevention of unauthorized substitution of cryptographic keys.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
3.7.7.a Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data and verify that they define prevention of unauthorized substitution of cryptographic keys.
3.7.7.b Interview personnel and/or observe processes to verify that unauthorized substitution of keys is prevented.

### Customized Approach Objective
Cryptographic keys cannot be substituted by unauthorized personnel.

### Purpose
If an attacker is able to substitute an entity's key with a key the attacker knows, the attacker will be able to decrypt all data encrypted with that key.

### Good Practice
The encryption solution should not allow for or accept substitution of keys from unauthorized sources.

title: Manage manual cleartext key-management operations using split knowledge and dual control.
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying that manual cleartext key-management operations are performed using split knowledge and dual control in accordance with req-3.7.6.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Look for physical or digital evidence that two or more personnel are required to complete key management tasks. Check for secure storage (e.g., split key components housed in separate secure containers or systems) and pairing logs that require dual participation.
   - Technical Artifacts: Validate that key-management policies explicitly define the process of splitting keys and mandate dual control. System audit logs, video evidence of key-handling sessions, and HSM configuration settings that enforce such controls can serve as artifacts.
   - Operational Patterns: Observe routine processes during which key shares are individually handled and then combined only in the presence of more than one authorized operator. Look for periodic reconciliation of key logs ensuring no single individual ever has full access.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faking Compliance: Organizations might produce extensive documentation stating that dual control is in place while in practice only one individual is involved in operations or logs are not independently reviewed.
   - Superficial Implementations: Merely splitting a key in two parts without ensuring that no individual ever sees the entire key (or using weak random functions for key generation) does not meet the control.
   - Technical Gaps: Process logs that are not tamper-proof, poorly segregated duties, or use of manual processes that can be easily manipulated without triggering dual control checks.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review the documented key-management policies and procedures to verify they require both split knowledge and dual control.
   WHY: This ensures that the organization has defined the correct approach, including roles that guarantee no single individual can reconstruct the cleartext key on their own.
   HOW: Select a sample policy document, trace a key-management operation flow in the documentation, and confirm that two-person involvement is required at all stages.
   FAIL: Absence of explicit requirements for split knowledge and dual control, or vague language that does not mandate distinct roles.

   CHECK: Interview key-management personnel and observe live or recorded key management sessions.
   WHY: Direct observation and discussion reveal whether dual control is truly enacted and whether personnel understand and follow the process.
   HOW: Ask at least two personnel to describe their roles; observe a live or recorded process where key components are handled separately and then combined, ensuring no one person has full access to the key.
   FAIL: Evidence that a single person is allowed to manage or view complete keys, or frequent deviations from the documented process.

   CHECK: Audit system and physical logs that capture dual control events and cross-check with access control records.
   WHY: Logs and verifiable evidence of participation ensure that dual control is enforced in practice, reducing the risk of collusion or breach.
   HOW: Locate automation logs or physical sign-in sheets that require the presence (or digital acceptance) of two separate individuals, then verify against timestamps and user IDs for consistency with the key management events.
   FAIL: Inconsistent, incomplete, or missing logs, or evidence that logging is easily circumvented.

   CHECK: Validate that cryptographic key generation (even if later split for manual operations) originates from approved secure systems (e.g., HSMs) or approved random number generators.
   WHY: Ensuring the secure generation of keys is critical, as splitting a weak or uncontrolled key is not acceptable even with dual control.
   HOW: Review system configurations or obtain evidence from HSM logs verifying that keys are generated in a controlled environment. Confirm that downstream key-splitting procedures do not expose the cleartext key to any single individual.
   FAIL: Evidence that keys are generated outside a secure cryptographic module or lack of proper logging on key generation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcuts: Identify any audit trails with dual-person signatures or dual confirmation fields. Look for technology-specific features (e.g., HSM audit logs) that automatically enforce and record dual control events.
   - Critical Indicators: Observe if any individual can override the dual control by having overlapping roles; this is a strong indicator of deficient implementation.
   - Details That Matter: Ensure that key shares are stored separately and that even if components are accessed individually, they are never combined unless under the controlled dual participation session. Validate that training for the dual control process is current and evidenced by periodic recertification.

Overall, the approach must validate that the process is not merely documented in policies but actively demonstrated by operational evidence and technical artifacts that attest to effective split knowledge and dual control.
content:
3.7.6 Where manual cleartext cryptographic key-management operations are performed by personnel, key-management policies and procedures are implemented, including managing these operations using split knowledge and dual control.

### Customized Approach Objective
Cleartext secret or private keys cannot be known by anyone. Operations involving cleartext keys cannot be carried out by a single person.

### Applicability Notes
This control is applicable for manual key-management operations. A cryptographic key that is simply split into two parts does not meet this requirement. Secret or private keys stored as key components or key shares must be generated via an approved random number generator within a secure cryptographic device (e.g., HSM) or in accordance with ISO 19592 or equivalent standards.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
3.7.6.a Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data and verify that they define using split knowledge and dual control.
3.7.6.b Interview personnel and/or observe processes to verify that manual cleartext keys are managed with split knowledge and dual control.

### Purpose
Split knowledge and dual control of keys are used to eliminate the possibility of a single person having complete access to a key.

title: Implement procedures for the retirement, replacement, or destruction of keys.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Documented key management policies and procedures that explicitly define how keys are retired, replaced, or destroyed (including roles, responsibilities, and triggers such as cryptoperiod expiry, key compromise, or personnel changes).
• System logs and audit trails from the key management system showing actual events for key retirement, replacement, or destruction. For example, records confirming that a key, once retired, is no longer referenced in encryption operations.
• Evidence from automated monitoring or alerts configured in key management solutions that flag keys reaching the end of their useful life or suspected of being compromised.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply present polished documentation without showing any real-time evidence; policies may exist on paper while the operational process is manual or inconsistent.
• Faking compliance by backdating log entries or creating dummy records in the key management system without actual enforcement of retiring keys.
• Implementing procedures that only swap key identifiers without verifying that retired keys are effectively disabled from encryption operations, and neglecting secure archival if keys are retained.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Review Key Management Policy and Procedure Documentation
WHY: Validated policies that detail the retirement, replacement, or destruction of keys ensure that there is an established, standardized process that meets PCI-DSS requirements.
HOW: Inspect the documented policies to verify they include triggers such as end-of-cryptoperiod, suspected compromise, or personnel changes. Confirm provisions for secure archival if keys are retained.
FAIL: Absence of specific procedures for handling keys that are retired or compromised, or vague references that do not align with the PCI-DSS requirement.

CHECK: Inspect Key Management System Logs for Retirement Events
WHY: System logs showing key retirement events provide technical evidence that keys are being actively decommissioned once conditions are met.
HOW: Query audit trails or logs for evidence of key retirement, replacement, or destruction. Verify that retired keys are no longer used for encryption processes and check for timestamps that align with defined cryptoperiods or suspected compromises.
FAIL: Missing log entries for key retirement events, or evidence that retired keys are still participating in encryption operations.

CHECK: Interview Key Custodians and Technical Staff
WHY: Direct interviews help verify that the established process is understood and followed in day-to-day operations, beyond just the existence of documentation.
HOW: Ask staff to describe and demonstrate recent instances where a key was retired, replaced, or destroyed. Inquire about integration between the key management process and encryption platforms, and how exceptions are handled.
FAIL: Inconsistent or vague answers from personnel, or inability to provide recent examples supporting the documented processes.

CHECK: Verify Integration and Automated Controls within the Key Management System
WHY: Automation minimizes human error and ensures that compliance is enforced in real time rather than on an ad hoc basis.
HOW: Check system configurations for automated alerts or triggers that retire keys upon reaching their cryptoperiod or when anomalies are detected. Validate configurations to ensure retired keys are removed from active encryption use.
FAIL: Absence of automation or configuration settings that allow manual override without proper justification, leading to potential continued use of retired keys.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Always cross-check automation settings with manual logs to confirm that system-generated events are genuine and not backdated or fabricated.
• Pay attention to audit logs for gaps or inconsistencies which might indicate that keys are not properly decommissioned.
• Critical details include verifying that retired keys are inaccessible for encryption and that any archived keys are securely stored (using methods like key-encryption keys) if retention for compliance reasons is required.
• Use integrated dashboards that correlate key lifecycle events with cryptographic operations to quickly identify any misconfiguration or process lapse.
• Look for process reviews or periodic audits that validate the end-to-end workflow from key generation to destruction; lack of such reviews is a red flag for possible process breakdowns.
content:
3.7.5 Key management policies and procedures are implemented to include the retirement, replacement, or destruction of keys used to protect stored account data, as deemed necessary when:
- The key has reached the end of its defined cryptoperiod.
- The integrity of the key has been weakened, including when personnel with knowledge of a cleartext key component leaves the company, or the role for which the key component was known.
- The key is suspected of or known to be compromised.

Retired or replaced keys are not used for encryption operations.

### Customized Approach Objective
Keys are removed from active use when it is suspected or known that the integrity of the key is weakened.

### Applicability Notes
If retired or replaced cryptographic keys need to be retained, these keys must be securely archived (for example, by using a key-encryption key).

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
3.7.5.a Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data and verify that they define retirement, replacement, or destruction of keys in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.
3.7.5.b Interview personnel to verify that processes are implemented in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

### Purpose
Keys that are no longer required, weakened, or compromised should be archived, revoked, and/or destroyed to prevent misuse.

title: Implement key management procedures for cryptographic key changes at the end of their cryptoperiod.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-3.7.4:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for updated cryptographic key inventories that include expiration dates (cryptoperiods) and a timeline for key replacement. Check system logs or audit trails that document key generation and key change events.
   • Technical Artifacts: Artifacts include automated key rotation and archival logs from a centralized key management system (Hardware Security Module, Key Management Service) showing key retirements and new key introductions. Evidence of scheduling or triggers for key replacement that align with defined cryptoperiods is crucial.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular, automated key rotation events. Clear correlation between documented cryptoperiod lengths and observed key change events. Periodic reviews of key management processes, including reports on keys nearing expiration.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance: Organizations may present static documentation or policies that state compliant procedures without showing real-time and historical key rotation evidence.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Relying solely on manual processes documented in policy without correlating them with system logs and automated alerts. Overlooking cryptoperiod triggers in automated systems.
   • Technical Gaps: Misalignment between key inventory and logged cryptoperiods; absence of evidence for decommissioning expired keys; missing integration between key management solutions and audit logs.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the organization maintains an automated log of key generation and key expiration events.
   WHY: Automated logs provide concrete evidence that keys are changed at the end of their cryptoperiod, minimizing human error or policy-only implementations.
   HOW: Access and review logs from the key management system, filter for key retirement and new key generation events, and correlate with defined cryptoperiod dates.
   FAIL: Absence of detailed logs or mismatch between cryptoperiod deadlines in the logs and the defined policy indicating keys are not being updated in a timely manner.

   CHECK: Examine the key management system configuration for automated triggers that initiate key change procedures once the key nears expiration.
   WHY: Automated triggers ensure that key changes avoid manual delays and enforce consistent adherence to cryptoperiod limits.
   HOW: Review configuration settings in the key management system, verify scheduled tasks or scripts, and inspect change history for periodic execution.
   FAIL: Manual or ad hoc key change events, or configuration settings lacking automated scheduling for key expiration.

   CHECK: Validate that key inventories include documented cryptoperiods and corresponding management policies.
   WHY: Accurate inventory documentation links each cryptographic key with a lifespan, which is essential for managing timely key changes.
   HOW: Cross-reference key inventory records with policy documents and operational logs, ensuring each key entry has an associated expiration date and renewal event.
   FAIL: Missing cryptoperiod information on key inventories, mismatched entries between inventory and system logs, or outdated policy documentation.

   CHECK: Interview key management and operational personnel to confirm their understanding and execution of key change procedures at the cryptoperiod end.
   WHY: Personnel interviews provide insight into how well policies are enforced and whether there is an operational culture of adhering to cryptoperiod standards.
   HOW: Conduct interviews asking how key changes are initiated, what monitoring is in place, and review training records on key management practices.
   FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly articulate the key change process, or evidence that the process is inconsistent with documented procedures.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Focus on the integration between the key management system and automated alerting/monitoring systems. This integration is a strong indicator of process maturity.
   • Critical Indicator: Look for a documented, scheduled review process (e.g., quarterly) that confirms all cryptoperiod endpoints have been met and keys have been updated. Automated tracking systems that alert for expired keys are especially telling.
   • Practical Detail: Ensure that logs and key inventories are time-synchronized to avoid discrepancies in event timestamps. Even minor misalignments can hide non-compliance.
   • Final Note: Even with documented policies, always correlate evidence with real operational data. A snapshot of a key inventory alone is insufficient if the automated operational logs are missing or incomplete.

This comprehensive methodology ensures that the key management procedures for cryptographic key changes at the end of their cryptoperiod are not just documented but actively and effectively implemented.
content:
3.7.4 Key management policies and procedures are implemented for cryptographic key changes for keys that have reached the end of their cryptoperiod, as defined by the associated application vendor or key owner, and based on industry best practices and guidelines, including the following:
• A defined cryptoperiod for each key type in use.
• A process for key changes at the end of the defined cryptoperiod.

### Customized Approach Objective
Cryptographic keys are not used beyond their defined cryptoperiod.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures
3.7.4.a Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data to verify that they define changes to cryptographic keys that have reached the end of their cryptoperiod and include all elements specified in this requirement.
3.7.4.b Interview personnel, examine documentation, and observe key storage locations to verify that keys are changed at the end of the defined cryptoperiod.

### Purpose
Changing encryption keys when they reach the end of their cryptoperiod is imperative to minimize the risk of unauthorized decryption of data.

### Definitions
A cryptoperiod is the time span during which a cryptographic key can be used for its defined purpose.

title: Implement key-management policies for secure key storage.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that cryptographic keys are stored using a key-encrypting key (KEK) mechanism, often within a dedicated Hardware Security Module (HSM) or equivalent secure key management system.
  WHY: The use of an HSM or KEK indicates that keys are not stored in plaintext and that a secure, segregated process is followed.
  HOW: Review system architecture diagrams, HSM inventory, and configuration files that detail key storage practices, and inspect key rotation logs.
  FAIL: Evidence that keys are found in configuration files or source code, or that there is no documentation or audit trail of HSM usage.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply encrypt keys using a hard-coded, static symmetric key that is stored in an insecure location like configuration files or source code.
• Documentation may claim usage of secure key management without actual proof (e.g., fake HSM inventory records) or only implementing administrative policies without technical enforcement.
• Surface-level compliance is met by having a documented procedure without verification of physical or logical protections (e.g., keys accessible by system administrators without two-factor authentication).

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Confirm HSM or secure key vault usage by tracing key storage locations in system configurations.
   WHY: Direct evidence of secure storage mechanisms reinforces that keys are not being stored in less secure environments.
   HOW: Execute system queries against key management endpoints, review API calls to the HSM, and inspect physical/logical access controls of the key store.
   FAIL: If keys are extracted from local file systems, environment variables, or documented as being stored in server config files without HSM mention.

2. CHECK: Review key management logs for key generation, rotation, and access events.
   WHY: Operational logs provide real-time evidence that key management procedures are enforced and regularly audited.
   HOW: Analyze log files, looking for timestamped records of key unlock events, rotations, and HSM access; use automated log analysis tools where available.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete logs, or logs that show irregularities such as infrequent rotations or unauthorized key access.

3. CHECK: Inspect the cryptographic material handling within applications to ensure keys are never embedded in source code.
   WHY: Embedding keys in source code poses an extreme risk as it can easily be reverse-engineered or accessed by unauthorized individuals.
   HOW: Perform a source code audit (using automated scanning tools and manual checks) for hard-coded keys, test for clearance of keys in build artifacts.
   FAIL: Discovery of any cryptographic keys, secret passphrases, or similar sensitive parameters in the code repositories or build scripts.

4. CHECK: Validate the key management policy documentation against operational evidence to ensure the written procedures align with deployed practices.
   WHY: Alignment between policy and implementation is critical to ensure that security controls are operationally effective.
   HOW: Interview security and system administrators, review internal audit reports, and compare policy documents with observed configurations and procedures.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between documented key management policies and the actual implemented controls, such as missing key rotation or weak access control implementations.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use centralized logging and monitoring to correlate key management events with potential anomalies; unauthorized key usage may be detected quickly this way.
• Check for automated alerting in the event of failed access or unauthorized key retrieval attempts. This is a strong indicator of operational effectiveness.
• Look closely at system and network segmentation that protects the HSM environment from general administrative access.
• Prioritize direct evidence from key management systems (such as HSM audit trails) over solely relying on policy documentation. 
• Real-world testing, such as a controlled attempt to access key stores outside of normal applications, can expose misconfigurations and weaknesses.

This comprehensive methodology ensures that key-management policies for secure key storage are not just written policies, but are actively enforced through technical controls and regular audits.
content:
3.7.3 Key-management policies and procedures are implemented to include secure storage of cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.

Purpose
Storing keys without proper protection could provide access to attackers, resulting in the decryption and exposure of account data.

Good Practice
Data encryption keys can be protected by encrypting them with a key-encrypting key. Keys can be stored in a Hardware Security Module (HSM). Secret or private keys that can decrypt data should never be present in source code.

Customized Approach Objective
Cryptographic keys are secured when stored.

title: Cryptographic keys are secured during distribution.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Review key management policies and procedures that explicitly detail secure distribution methods for cryptographic keys.
  WHY: This demonstrates that the organization has formally defined how keys should be transmitted and that the process adheres to security practices such as encryption during transmission and controlled access.
  HOW: Look for documented procedures mentioning use of secure channels (e.g., encrypted transport protocols), authorized key custodians, and methods such as key splitting or hardware security modules (HSMs) for key handling. Verify that these documents are current and reference practical implementation steps.
  FAIL: Absence of key distribution controls in the policy, or overly generic documentation that fails to address secure channels, is an indicator of non-compliance.

• CHECK: Inspect technical artifacts like transmission logs, secure key exchange records, and configuration settings in key management systems used during distribution.
  WHY: Observable, automated evidence such as audit logs and system configuration snapshots provide undisputed proof that secure processes are active in real-time.
  HOW: Query system logs to confirm that keys are sent through encrypted channels (look for TLS, SSH, or VPN usage) and review logs for authentication against authorized custodians. Check secure configuration files or HSM audit trails for key distribution events.
  FAIL: If logs show unencrypted transmission, or if there are no logs verifying secure distribution events, the control may be ineffectively implemented.

• CHECK: Conduct live observation or technical testing of the key distribution process to verify that distribution always uses secure methods.
  WHY: Direct observation validates that procedures are followed in practice and are not merely documented procedures that are overlooked during daily operations.
  HOW: Arrange a demonstration or simulation where a new cryptographic key is distributed. Validate that the distribution process uses a secure channel and that only authorized personnel receive the key through mechanisms like one-time tokens or multifactor verification.
  FAIL: If the demonstration reveals that keys are sent via insecure channels (e.g., email without encryption, plain FTP) or are accessible to unauthorized personnel, this indicates a failure of the control.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might produce well-crafted documentation, but the actual distribution processes may be poorly implemented or utilize insecure channels.
• Failing checks might occur when key distribution methods are superficially secured (like using standard email with password protection) that do not meet strong encryption standards, or when administrative accounts are misconfigured to give broader access than permitted.
• Technical gaps include not logging distribution events or not having automated checks against distribution channels, leading to a false sense of security.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK the key-management policy document for explicit instructions on using encrypted, authenticated channels for key distribution. Confirm that the document references secure methodologies and authorized parties.
2. CHECK system and audit logs to verify that key distribution events are logged and that the logs show evidence of secure protocols (such as TLS or SSH) being used. Automate log queries if possible.
3. CHECK configuration settings in key management systems (or associated HSMs) to ensure that keys are transmitted securely, including verifying that channels enforce encryption and access is restricted to authorized users.
4. CHEK live distribution observation or simulate a key distribution process to directly verify that the process uses secure methods. Document the evidence (screenshots, CLI outputs) of secure transmissions.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Ask for automated monitoring or alerts on key distribution breaches and review any recent alerts as this is often more reliable than periodic manual reviews.
• A critical indicator of poor implementation is a lack of correlation between documented procedures and technical logs; always require evidence that policies are enforced at the operational level.
• Focus on whether distribution channels are configured to automatically enforce encryption and access controls, rather than simply relying on manual processes.
• Details matter: Ensure that any temporary keys or one-time secrets used in the process are also subject to the same secure distribution practices.

This methodology provides both document-based and technical evidence of proper implementation, emphasizing automated, observable processes to ensure that cryptographic keys are securely distributed in practice.
content:
#### Defined Approach Requirements

**3.7.2** Key-management policies and procedures are implemented to include secure distribution of cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.

#### Customized Approach Objective

Cryptographic keys are secured during distribution.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**3.7.2.a** Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data to verify that they define secure distribution of cryptographic keys.

**3.7.2.b** Observe the method for distributing keys to verify that keys are distributed securely.

**Purpose**

Secure distribution or conveyance of secret or private cryptographic keys means that keys are distributed only to authorized custodians, as identified in Requirement 3.6.1.2, and are never distributed insecurely.

title: Strong cryptographic keys are generated.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence includes documented key-management policies that specify use of contemporary, strong cryptographic algorithms (e.g., AES-256, RSA 2048+) and detailed procedures for key generation.
   - Technical artifacts such as configuration files, cryptographic library settings, generation logs, and audit trails demonstrate that key generation routines invoke secure random number generators and adhere to high entropy standards.
   - Operational patterns include routine logging of key generation events, use of automated cryptographic modules with secure defaults, and periodic internal reviews of generated key quality.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations might claim compliance by providing outdated documentation that merely lists cryptographic options rather than demonstrating implementation.
   - Surface-level implementations include using default random generators without proper seeding, employing weak key algorithms, or reusing keys inappropriately.
   - Technical gaps often seen include missing evidence of runtime behavior, lack of logs detailing key generation, or inadequate periodic review of the effectiveness of cryptographic key generation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify documented key-management policies and procedures mention the use of strong cryptographic algorithms and secure key generation methods.
   WHY: Ensures that the organization’s official guidelines mandate secure key generation practices.
   HOW: Review the key-management policy document and procedures to confirm they specify the use of strong cryptographic methods (such as AES-256, RSA 2048+, or ECC perimeter recommendations) and secure random sources.
   FAIL: Absence of specific references to both strong cryptographic algorithms and secure key-generation procedures.

   CHECK: Inspect system configurations and parameter settings for key generation functions in cryptographic modules.
   WHY: Configuration settings indicate that the cryptographic libraries are correctly configured to generate strong keys.
   HOW: Examine configuration files or use automated tools to review cryptographic library settings, ensuring that the defined algorithms and key lengths meet current security standards.
   FAIL: Discovering configurations that rely on legacy algorithms or insufficient key lengths (e.g., AES-128 when stronger options are required).

   CHECK: Analyze logs and audit trails for key generation events.
   WHY: Operational logs provide observable evidence that keys are generated using strong cryptographic processes in practice.
   HOW: Use log analysis tools to verify that key generation events are recorded, check for metadata that confirms use of secure random number generators and appropriate key lengths during generation.
   FAIL: Lack of logs or audit trails, or logs that exhibit aberrant patterns (e.g., repeated identical key properties, insufficient entropy, or infrequent generation events).

   CHECK: Test a sample of generated keys for compliance with strength requirements (key length, algorithm used, entropy levels).
   WHY: Direct analysis of key properties confirms that the generated keys meet strong cryptographic standards beyond theoretical compliance.
   HOW: Retrieve a sample of keys via the key management interface, then use cryptographic analysis tools to assess key length, algorithm, and entropy metrics.
   FAIL: Identification of keys that do not meet the minimum strength requirements, such as insufficient key length or low estimated entropy.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Focus on runtime logs and configuration scans rather than solely reviewing policy documents. Automated scans provide real-time assurance of secure configuration.
   - Critical Indicator: Look for discrepancies between documented procedures and actual system configurations; mismatches often signify superficial compliance.
   - Detail to Note: Ensure that cryptographic libraries and hardware security modules (if used) are updated and configured according to vendor best practices. The use of secure generators and consistent key lengths across all systems is vital.
   - Always validate that key generation practices are part of regular audits and that incident response/correction mechanisms exist if weak keys are detected.
content:
# Requirements and Testing Procedures

## Guidance

### 3.7 Where cryptography is used to protect stored account data, key management processes and procedures covering all aspects of the key lifecycle are defined and implemented.

#### Defined Approach Requirements

**3.7.1** Key-management policies and procedures are implemented to include generation of strong cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.

#### Customized Approach Objective

Strong cryptographic keys are generated.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**3.7.1.a** Examine the documented key-management policies and procedures for keys used for protection of stored account data to verify that they define generation of strong cryptographic keys.

**3.7.1.b** Observe the method for generating keys to verify that strong keys are generated.

**Purpose**

Use of strong cryptographic keys significantly increases the level of security of encrypted account data.

**Further Information**

See the sources referenced at Cryptographic Key Generation in Appendix G.

title: Cryptographic keys are stored in the fewest possible locations.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes centralized key management consoles, audited logs of key access, and detailed inventory reports that list only the authorized storage locations for cryptographic keys.
• Technical artifacts include configuration files for key management systems, scripts or queries showing key locations from centralized servers (e.g., HSMs), and automated monitoring alerts when keys are accessed or stored elsewhere.
• Operational patterns include consistent use of a verified key management system across environments, regular audits that confirm no unauthorized locations, and automated reconciliations between documented and actual key stores.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes rely solely on policy documentation that states keys are stored in limited locations, while in practice keys may also be present in application code, configuration files, or backup systems.
• Surface-level implementations often involve using multiple key storage venues inadvertently (e.g., one for active use and another in overlooked staging environments).
• Technical gaps frequently missed include keys embedded in hard-coded scripts, old unused key copies on decommissioned systems, or legacy backup media that still contain keys.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Centralized Key Repository Verification
   WHY: Confirming that keys are managed by a dedicated key management system (KMS) or Hardware Security Module (HSM) reduces the risk of multiple, uncontrolled key locations.
   HOW: Query and review configurations of the key management system to ensure that only the designated systems hold keys, and cross-check with the documented key inventory.
   FAIL: If keys are found stored in additional locations such as application config files, source code, or undocumented servers, the check fails.

2. CHECK: Key Inventory Reconciliation
   WHY: A complete and accurate inventory is critical to verifying that cryptographic keys are only in pre-approved storage locations.
   HOW: Compare automated key inventory lists from the key management system with manual or system-generated asset inventories. Review logs for any unauthorized additions or modifications.
   FAIL: Discrepancies or evidence of keys existing outside the centralized system indicate non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Backup and Archive Storage Analysis
   WHY: Backups or archives can inadvertently store old cryptographic keys, which increases exposure risk if not properly managed.
   HOW: Inspect backup storage configurations and audit logs to verify that backup repositories do not contain unauthorized copies of cryptographic keys. Confirm deletion of keys from non-controlled locations after backup.
   FAIL: Finding keys in backup systems or legacy storage means that keys are not being limited to the fewest locations.

4. CHECK: Source Code and Application Configuration Review
   WHY: Sometimes keys are hard-coded in source code or stored in application configuration files, bypassing central key management.
   HOW: Perform automated scans of source code repositories and configuration management systems for embedded keys; verify that keys are referenced via secure key management APIs.
   FAIL: Discovery of hard-coded keys or keys stored in configuration files outside the centralized key management system indicates non-compliance.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include using automated scanners and configuration management tools to map all instances of key usage; cross-reference these against the authorized key management system.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include sporadic issuance of keys across multiple environments, lack of updated inventory reports, and inability to trace key lifecycle events.
• In practice, focus on verifying operational effectiveness—check live systems and logs rather than solely relying on policies or static documentation. Detailed log reviews and real-time querying of key repositories provide the best assurance that keys are stored in the fewest possible locations.
content:
| Requirements and Testing Procedures   | Guidance                                                                                                       |
|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Defined Approach Requirements         |                                                                                                               |
| 3.6.1.4 Cryptographic keys are stored in the fewest possible locations. |                                                                                                               |
| Customized Approach Objective         | Cryptographic keys are retained only where necessary.                                                          |
| Defined Approach Testing Procedures    |                                                                                                               |
| 3.6.1.4 Examine key storage locations and observe processes to verify that keys are stored in the fewest possible locations. |                                                                                                               |
| Purpose                               | Storing any cryptographic keys in the fewest locations helps an organization track and monitor all key locations and minimizes the potential for keys to be exposed to unauthorized parties. |

title: Access to cleartext cryptographic key components is restricted.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• The key management system or secure cryptographic module configuration clearly lists the authorized key custodians with granular access controls. 
• Audit logs or system access logs that show only designated personnel have accessed cleartext cryptographic key components. 
• Operational records indicating routine reviews of access rights with confirmation that only necessary personnel are maintained as key custodians.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might claim compliance on paper (using outdated or infrequently reviewed access lists) while actual system configurations allow broader access. 
• Relying solely on administrative processes without automated enforcement can lead to manual errors where non-custodians inadvertently gain access. 
• Using shared generic or privileged accounts that are not individually assigned to specific key custodians, effectively bypassing strict control.

VALIDATION STEPS
