• Remember: Operational effectiveness is confirmed by dynamic validation, not merely by policy existence – always verify runtime behaviors through multiple angles.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

3.3.1.3 The personal identification number (PIN) and the PIN block are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.

Customized Approach Objective

This requirement is not eligible for the customized approach.

Applicability Notes

PIN blocks are encrypted during the natural course of transaction processes, but even if an entity encrypts the PIN block again, it is still not allowed to be stored after the completion of the authorization process.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.3.1.3 Examine data sources, to verify that PINs and PIN blocks are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.

Guidance

Purpose

PIN and PIN blocks should be known only to the card owner or entity that issued the card. If this data is stolen, malicious individuals can execute fraudulent PIN-based transactions (for example, in-store purchases and ATM withdrawals). Not storing this data reduces the probability of it being compromised.

Examples

Data sources to review to ensure that PIN and PIN blocks are not retained upon completion of the authorization process include, but are not limited to:

- Incoming transaction data.
- All logs (for example, transaction, history, debugging, error).
- History files.
- Trace files.
- Database schemas.
- Contents of databases, and on-premise and cloud data stores.
- Any existing memory/crash dump files.

title: Track data is not stored after authorization.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes application logs, database schema reviews, and runtime memory dumps that show only permitted elements (PAN, cardholder name, expiry, service code) are present post-authorization.
• Look for technical artifacts such as audit logs or automated scripts that immediately scrub or mask unused track data after the authorization process.
• Operational patterns include periodic audits, automated deletion routines in transaction processing, and secure log configurations that continuously verify sensitive data handling.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may fake compliance by claiming that data is deleted without verifying that backup systems, crash dumps, or debug logs do not inadvertently store full track data.
• Surface-level implementations might mask data only during transmission or only in some logging tables, while leaving full track data in less-obvious data stores like temporary caches or debug logs.
• Often, technical gaps include incomplete removal from all systems (e.g., cloud storage backups or memory dumps) which might be overlooked during routine checks.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Review Transaction and Logging Data
WHY: Ensures that no database or log file, including error logs and debug traces, retains full track data after authorization.
HOW: Query and inspect relevant databases, log files, and file system directories for any occurrences of actual magnetic stripe data beyond the allowed elements.
FAIL: Finding full track data traces (e.g., Track 1 or Track 2 data) in any logs or transactional records post-authorization indicates non-compliance.

CHECK: Evaluate Application Memory and Crash Dumps
WHY: Ensures that sensitive track data is not inadvertently placed in crash dumps or temporary memory storage.
HOW: Obtain and analyze memory dumps and crash logs from application servers, employing forensic tools to search for complete track data patterns.
FAIL: Discovery of unmasked, full track data in memory dumps or crash report files indicates a breach in control implementation.

CHECK: Verify Data Handling Code/Configuration Reviews
WHY: Code reviews or configuration files may provide evidence that the application logic enforces data scrubbing rules.
HOW: Inspect application source code, configuration management scripts, or automated processes responsible for truncating and deleting full track data after an authorization event.
FAIL: Absence of clear data scrubbing routines or configurations that override deletion implies that full track data may inadvertently be stored.

CHECK: Automated Integrity and Security Scans
WHY: Regular scans help ensure that the operational environment continuously complies with the control requirements.
HOW: Use automated tools to scan databases and file systems for patterns identified with full track data. Verify that scheduled scans report no positive findings.
FAIL: Detection of full track data during scans suggests gaps in control enforcement.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Prioritize reviewing the systems with the highest transaction volume and check integrations with third-party systems. They are most prone to unintentional data capture.
• Critical Indicators: Ensure that all potential storage locations (e.g., temporary files, backup archives, cloud storage) are included in your review.
• Details That Matter: Focus on non-obvious areas such as debugging tools, error logs, and memory crash dumps which are commonly overlooked yet crucial in proving implementation effectiveness.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

3.3.1.1 The full contents of any track are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.3.1.1 Examine data sources to verify that the full contents of any track are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.

Customized Approach Objective

This requirement is not eligible for the customized approach.

Applicability Notes

In the normal course of business, the following data elements from the track may need to be retained:

- Cardholder name.
- Primary account number (PAN).
- Expiration date.
- Service code.

To minimize risk, store securely only these data elements as needed for business.

Purpose

If full contents of any track (from the magnetic stripe on the back of a card if present, equivalent data contained on a chip, or elsewhere) is stored, malicious individuals who obtain that data can use it to reproduce payment cards and complete fraudulent transactions.

Definitions

Full track data is alternatively called full track, track, track 1, track 2, and magnetic-stripe data. Each track contains a number of data elements, and this requirement specifies only those that may be retained post-authorization.

Examples

Data sources to review to ensure that the full contents of any track are not retained upon completion of the authorization process include, but are not limited to:

- Incoming transaction data.
- All logs (for example, transaction, history, debugging, error).
- History files.
- Trace files.
- Database schemas.
- Contents of databases, and on-premise and cloud data stores.
- Any existing memory/crash dump files.

title: Sensitive authentication data is not stored after authorization.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Systems that process payment transactions log detailed transaction flows, including actions that explicitly delete sensitive authentication data (SAD) immediately after authorization. 
• Technical artifacts such as transaction processing code reviews, database configurations, and memory handling logs illustrate that SAD is only temporarily present (in volatile memory, if at all) and never written to persistent storage.
• Operational patterns include automated data purging routines triggered by the completion of an authorization response, alongside mechanisms that verify deletion processes (e.g., secure memory management controls).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might claim compliance by relying solely on documented policies without ensuring that systems enforce these policies at runtime.
• Superficial implementations, such as logging deletion events in a non-secure or non-monitored log file, may be used to mask the fact that SAD is sometimes stored or left in memory longer than necessary.
• Improperly configured systems might temporarily write SAD to disk-based swap files or persistent log storage that are not immediately audited, even though the front-end application code indicates a deletion process.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify In-Memory-only Handling of SAD
WHY: True compliance requires that SAD is retained exclusively in non-persistent memory, ensuring it is not recoverable post-authorization.
HOW: Review system architecture diagrams and application code paths for transaction processing. Confirm that any use of memory is tied to volatile storage (e.g., RAM). Check runtime memory snapshots during a test transaction to ensure no fallback to persistent storage (like swap or temporary files) occurs.
FAIL: If transaction flow analysis or memory snapshots indicate that SAD appears in persistent storage (disk-based swap, temp files, database tables), then this control has likely failed.

CHECK: Observe Automated, Immediate Data Deletion Processes
WHY: Automation reduces the risk of human error. The immediate and irreversible deletion of SAD after authorization is key to controlling its exposure.
HOW: Initiate a controlled test transaction and monitor system logs and deletion routines. Validate that logs capture a clear event of data deletion immediately after the authorization response is sent. Interview system operators to confirm that these automated processes run at every transaction.
FAIL: Absence of deletion events in system logs or prolonged presence of SAD in test monitoring suggests a failure to securely purge SAD.

CHECK: Confirm Secure Runtime Environment Configurations
WHY: Ensuring the system’s runtime environment is configured to use strictly non-persistent memory prevents accidental data retention.
HOW: Review configuration settings of the operating environment (including RAM management and swap file policies) with system administrators. Validate that there are explicit controls preventing writing sensitive data to disk. Use vulnerability scanning tools to detect potential misconfigurations.
FAIL: If system configurations allow volatile data to be written to persistent storage (for example, via swap files or misconfigured temporary directories), this is a sign of non-compliance.

CHECK: Validate Transaction Flow Through End-to-End Testing
WHY: Real transaction flow testing confirms that the deletion process is not only documented but functionally effective in live order scenarios.
HOW: Execute a series of test transactions that include SAD. Monitor, via secure logs and memory analysis, the lifecycle of SAD from input through authorization to deletion. Ensure there is a documented, repeatable pattern that aligns with the control objectives.
FAIL: Detection of retained SAD at any point beyond the authorization response indicates that the control is either misconfigured or bypassed.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcuts: Directly request and review memory dump analysis for high-risk components. Look for any signs of disk-based caching or swap file usage related to SAD.
• Critical indicators: Unexplained gaps in deletion logs or inconsistent deletion events across different transaction types are red flags.
• Practical details: Focus closely on edge cases where errors during the authorization process might inadvertently lead to persistent storage of SAD. Look for code review notes that explain how errors and exceptions are handled in relation to data deletion.

By following the above methodology, you can confirm that sensitive authentication data is effectively rendered unrecoverable post-authorization, ensuring adherence to PCI DSS Requirement 3.3.1.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

3.3.1 SAD is not stored after authorization, even if encrypted. All sensitive authentication data received is rendered unrecoverable upon completion of the authorization process.

Customized Approach Objective

This requirement is not eligible for the customized approach.

Applicability Notes

Issuers and companies that support issuing services, where there is a legitimate and documented business need to store SAD, are not required to meet this requirement. A legitimate business need is one that is necessary for the performance of the function being provided by or for the issuer. Refer to Requirement 3.3.3 for additional requirements specifically for these entities.  

Sensitive authentication data includes the data cited in Requirements 3.3.1.1 through 3.3.1.3.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.3.1.a If SAD is received, examine documented policies, procedures, and system configurations to verify the data is not stored after authorization.

3.3.1.b If SAD is received, examine the documented procedures and observe the secure data deletion processes to verify the data is rendered unrecoverable upon completion of the authorization process.

Purpose

SAD is very valuable to malicious individuals as it allows them to generate counterfeit payment cards and create fraudulent transactions. Therefore, the storage of SAD upon completion of the authorization process is prohibited.

Good Practice

It may be acceptable for an entity to store SAD in non-persistent memory for a short time after authorization is complete, if following conditions are met:
- There is a legitimate business need to access SAD in memory after authorization is complete.
- SAD is only ever stored in non-persistent memory (for example, RAM, volatile memory).
- Controls are in place to ensure that memory maintains a non-persistent state.
- SAD is removed as soon as the business purpose is complete.
- It is not permissible to store SAD in persistent memory.

Definitions

The authorization process completes when a merchant receives a transaction response (for example, an approval or deny).

Refer to Appendix G for the definition of “authorization.”

Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures, v4.0.1
©2006 - 2024 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All Rights Reserved.

title: Account data storage is minimized using retention and secure deletion policies.
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that a current data retention and secure deletion policy exists and covers all storage locations (e.g., production systems, backups, archives, removable media, paper records, etc.).
  WHY: Visible documentation combined with system inventories proves the organization has a formal approach to minimize account data storage.
  HOW: Review the policy documentation; check system inventories and configurations where account data is stored; interview personnel responsible for data management to confirm they follow documented procedures.
  FAIL: If the policy is outdated, incomplete or does not mention all data repositories (backup systems, removable media, etc.), this is a red flag.

• CHECK: Assess evidence of defined retention periods and documented business/legal justification for each type of account data.
  WHY: This proves that data is only retained as long as necessary and is aligned with legal, regulatory, and business requirements.
  HOW: Examine retention tables/spreadsheets included in policy documents and verify with a sample of system data entries that retention dates are correctly applied. Match these against business justifications or regulatory requirements defined in the policy.
  FAIL: If retention periods are vague or misaligned with operational data records or if no business justification is provided, compliance is questionable.

• CHECK: Verify that secure deletion or data destruction processes are technically and operationally implemented to render account data unrecoverable when retention limits are exceeded.
  WHY: This demonstrates real-world operational enforcement of the secure deletion processes instead of just a policy statement.
  HOW: Review logs and automated job schedules from secure deletion tools, observe the secure deletion methods in test environments, and validate that the mechanism (e.g., cryptographic erasure or physical media destruction procedures) prevents data recovery.
  FAIL: If deletion logs are missing, deletion methods are not verifiable (e.g., simple file removal), or data remnants can be recovered through forensic analysis, then the control is not operating effectively.

• CHECK: Confirm periodic review and verification processes (at least quarterly) that ensure data exceeding retention limits are purged or rendered unrecoverable.
  WHY: Regular evidence of verification is crucial to ensure the control remains active and effective over time.
  HOW: Examine audit logs, change management records, or system reports that show quarterly reviews; interview relevant staff to document the review process and sample recent review reports.
  FAIL: Absence of records or evidence of regular reviews suggests the control might not be enforced as stated.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may rely solely on having documented policies without showing operational evidence (e.g., no logs or automated tools evidence of enforcement).
• Some entities might perform deletion manually or infrequently, leading to a gap between the policy and actual operational effectiveness.
• Data stored in non-traditional locations (e.g., shadow backups or legacy systems) is sometimes overlooked, rendering the complete scope of the control ineffective.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Validate against automated logs rather than relying exclusively on policy documents—automated deletion logs are tough to fake.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include absence of quarterly verification logs and evidence of account data residing in unexpected storage locations.
• Ensure interviews include technical staff and data owners, as compliance often fails on backups or archived media which are less frequently audited.
• Check for encrypted data remnants that have not been securely deleted, as these can indicate merely a superficial implementation rather than true data destruction.

By following these steps, you can confidently verify that account data is stored only as long as necessary and is securely deleted or rendered unrecoverable when it is no longer needed.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

3.2.1 Account data storage is kept to a minimum through implementation of data retention and disposal policies, procedures, and processes that include at least the following:

- Coverage for all locations of stored account data.
- Coverage for any sensitive authentication data (SAD) stored prior to completion of authorization. This bullet is a best practice until its effective date; refer to Applicability Notes below for details.
- Limiting data storage amount and retention time to that which is required for legal or regulatory, and/or business requirements.
- Specific retention requirements for stored account data that defines length of retention period and includes a documented business justification.
- Processes for secure deletion or rendering account data unrecoverable when no longer needed per the retention policy.
- A process for verifying, at least once every three months, that stored account data exceeding the defined retention period has been securely deleted or rendered unrecoverable.

Customized Approach Objective

Account data is retained only where necessary and for the least amount of time needed and is securely deleted or rendered unrecoverable when no longer needed.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.2.1.a Examine the data retention and disposal policies, procedures, and processes and interview personnel to verify processes are defined to include all elements specified in this requirement.

3.2.1.b Examine files and system records on system components where account data is stored to verify that the data storage amount and retention time does not exceed the requirements defined in the data retention policy.

3.2.1.c Observe the mechanisms used to render account data unrecoverable to verify data cannot be recovered.

Purpose

A formal data retention policy identifies what data needs to be retained, for how long, and where that data resides so it can be securely destroyed or deleted as soon as it is no longer needed. The only account data that may be stored after authorization is the primary account number or PAN (rendered unreadable), expiration date, cardholder name, and service code. The storage of SAD data prior to the completion of the authorization process is also included in the data retention and disposal policy so that storage of this sensitive data is kept to minimum, and only retained for the defined amount of time.

Good Practice

When identifying locations of stored account data, consider all processes and personnel with access to the data, as data could have been moved and stored in different locations than originally defined. Storage locations that are often overlooked include backup and archive systems, removable data storage devices, paper-based media, and audio recordings. To define appropriate retention requirements, an entity first needs to understand its own business needs as well as any legal or regulatory obligations that apply to its industry or to the type of data being retained. Implementing an automated process to ensure data is automatically and securely deleted upon its defined retention limit can help ensure that account data is not retained beyond what is necessary for business, legal, or regulatory purposes.

title: Roles and responsibilities for Requirement 3 activities are documented and assigned.
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documentation exists (e.g., policies, procedures, or RACI matrices) that clearly list roles and responsibilities for activities under Requirement 3. These documents are current and reflect the actual structure of duties.
• Technical artifacts such as access control systems, role management interfaces, and HR systems tie accounts or assignments to the documented roles, showing that employees are actually designated.
• Operational patterns like regular training sessions, periodic role review meetings, and evidence of role acknowledgment (e.g., signed forms or electronic attestations) indicate that responsibilities are communicated and understood.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might produce static documentation (a dated RACI matrix or policies) without ensuring that these roles are actually used or that personnel are aware of them.
• Relying solely on documentation stored in a central repository without operational evidence (such as employee interviews or training records) can be an evasion.
• Technical gaps are often missed when roles listed in documents do not align with system-level role assignments or when changes in personnel are not promptly reflected in the assignments.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify the existence of up-to-date documentation (e.g., a RACI matrix or process flow chart) that delineates roles and responsibilities for Requirement 3 activities.
WHY: Current documentation demonstrates that there is an established process with clearly assigned roles.
HOW: Ask for the latest version of policies, procedures, and role assignment sheets that include Requirement 3; check timestamps and revision histories.
FAIL: If documentation is out-of-date, missing, or does not mention Requirement 3 explicitly, the control likely has not been properly implemented.

CHECK: Interview designated personnel responsible for Requirement 3 activities.
WHY: Interviews provide direct evidence that personnel understand and accept their roles beyond what is documented.
HOW: Randomly select a sample of employees whose roles relate to Requirement 3 and ask them to describe their responsibilities, how they perform them, and how they coordinate with other teams.
FAIL: If personnel are unable to clearly articulate their responsibilities or indicate that their roles differ from documentation, it suggests insufficient communication or updates.

CHECK: Review evidence of role acknowledgment and periodic training or review sessions.
WHY: Regular training and attestation records prove that the assignment of roles is active and regularly reinforced among staff.
HOW: Inspect training logs, meeting minutes, or signed acknowledgments where employees confirm understanding of their tasks under Requirement 3.
FAIL: Absence of training records or role acknowledgment documents indicates that the control might exist only on paper.

CHECK: Correlate system-level role configurations with documented assignments.
WHY: Matching technical configurations (such as access control lists, internal role management system settings) to documented roles confirms that the organization enforces responsibilities operationally.
HOW: Access administrative settings or ask for oversight logs that show assigned responsibilities; verify that personnel credentials align with their documented roles.
FAIL: Mismatches or lack of a technical correlation means that the documented roles are not actively enforced by systems.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Instead of solely reviewing documentation, pick one or two critical roles and trace the lifecycle—document inception, role acknowledgment, operational implementation—to assess real-world adherence.
• Critical Indicator: Discrepancies between HR records, training logs, and the documented RACI matrix can be a red flag; ensure all three align consistently.
• Key Detail: Check for a process that updates roles upon staff turnover or when organizational changes occur. The control is only as effective as its ability to remain dynamically accurate. Regular reviews and automated alerts for out-of-date profiles are practical indicators of strong control.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

3.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 3 are documented, assigned, and understood.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.1.2.a Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities performing activities in Requirement 3 are documented and assigned.

3.1.2.b Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 3 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as documented and are understood.

Customized Approach Objective

Day-to-day responsibilities for performing all the activities in Requirement 3 are allocated. Personnel are accountable for successful, continuous operation of these requirements.

Guidance

Purpose
If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, personnel may not be aware of their day-to-day responsibilities, and critical activities may not occur.

Good Practice
Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents.
As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities.

Examples
A method to document roles and responsibilities is a responsibility assignment matrix that includes who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed (also called a RACI matrix).

Defined Approach Requirements

3.1.2 The card verification code is not stored upon completion of the authorization process.

Customized Approach Objective

This requirement is not eligible for the customized approach.

Applicability Notes

The card verification code is the three- or four-digit number printed on the front or back of a payment card used to verify card-not-present transactions.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.1.2 Examine data sources, to verify that the card verification code is not stored upon completion of the authorization process.

Guidance

Purpose

If card verification code data is stolen, malicious individuals can execute fraudulent Internet and mail-order/telephone-order (MO/TO) transactions. Not storing this data reduces the probability of it being compromised.

Examples

If card verification codes are stored on paper media prior to completion of authorization, a method of erasing or covering the codes should prevent them from being read after authorization is complete. Example methods of rendering the codes unreadable include removing the code with scissors and applying a suitably opaque and un-removable marker over the code.

Data sources to review to ensure that the card verification code is not retained upon completion of the authorization process include, but are not limited to:
• Incoming transaction data.
• All logs (for example, transaction, history, debugging, error).
• History files.
• Trace files.
• Database schemas.
• Contents of databases, and on-premise and cloud data stores.
• Any existing memory/crash dump files.

©2006 - 2024 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All Rights Reserved.

title: Security policies and operational procedures for Requirement 3 are defined and maintained.
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for verifying that security policies and operational procedures for Requirement 3 are defined and maintained:

CHECK: Documentation Presence and Currency
WHY: Ensures that policies and procedures are not only documented but also updated regularly to reflect current operational needs and improvements. This creates a verifiable baseline that the control exists.
HOW: • Locate the central document management repository or policy portal where the relevant security policies and procedures (as specified in Requirement 3) are stored.
     • Review revision histories, change logs, and timestamps to confirm recent updates that reflect current processes.
     • Cross-check document versions with the official change management records.
FAIL: Missing or outdated documents, lack of revision history, or policies that have not been updated since significant process changes.

CHECK: Evidence of Active Use and Dissemination
WHY: A documented policy is only effective if it is actively communicated and used by all affected parties. Visible operational patterns, such as training records and internal communications, support this check.
HOW: • Inspect training records, internal newsletters, and meeting minutes that reflect the distribution of the policies to staff.
     • Utilize search queries in collaboration tools (e.g., intranet posts or email chains) for policy update notifications.
     • Interview a sample of personnel from affected departments to assess awareness and correct understanding of the policies.
FAIL: Lack of training records or notification logs, or when interviews reveal that employees are unaware or misinformed about current procedures.

CHECK: Verification of Policy Update and Maintenance Process
WHY: A structured change management process demonstrates that policies and procedures are actively maintained. This is a sign of operational effectiveness beyond a static document repository.
HOW: • Review change management records and incident logs for entries that indicate periodic reviews or urgent updates to security policies.
     • Check the consistency between change management tickets and the actual updates in the documentation.
     • Validate that any process or technology change triggering an update has resulted in documented revisions and communicated updates.
FAIL: Absence of change logs or the presence of inconsistencies between change records and documentation; infrequent or ad-hoc updates that do not correlate to known changes.

CHECK: Direct Personnel Interviews to Validate Awareness
WHY: Interviewing affected personnel ensures that security policies and procedures are not just present, but are effectively known and integrated into day-to-day activities. This is critical evidence that the control is truly operative.
HOW: • Conduct structured interviews or surveys with a representative sample of staff, focusing on those whose roles are directly impacted by Requirement 3.
     • Ask specific questions regarding the existence, contents, and update frequency of the policies, as well as procedures on how changes are communicated.
     • Verify if there is a feedback mechanism in place for personnel to question or suggest updates to the policies.
FAIL: General lack of awareness among employees, inconsistent answers that do not align with the documented procedures, or absence of dissemination efforts.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use automated document management system searches to quickly retrieve evidence of recent changes and avoid relying solely on manual inspection.
• Pay close attention to the timestamp and revision history; a lack of recent updates is a strong indicator of poor operational maintenance.
• Correlate training and communication records with documented change logs to detect when updates might have been missed.
• Ensure that the sample for personnel interviews covers diverse roles to avoid bias and accurately represent the affected population.

This methodology focuses on obtaining operational and observable evidence rather than just reviewing policy documents, ensuring that the security policies and operational procedures in Requirement 3 are not only defined but effectively maintained and enforced.
content:
Defined Approach Requirements

3.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 3 are:
- Documented.
- Kept up to date.
- In use.
- Known to all affected parties.

Defined Approach Testing Procedures

3.1.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 3 are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

Purpose

Requirement 3.1.1 is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout Requirement 3. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in Requirement 3, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated.

Good Practice

It is important to update policies and procedures as needed to address changes in processes, technologies, and business objectives. For this reason, consider updating these documents as soon as possible after a change occurs and not only on a periodic cycle.

Definitions

Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard: Requirements and Testing Procedures, v4.0.1
©2006 - 2024 PCI Security Standards Council, LLC. All Rights Reserved.

title: Change wireless encryption keys when personnel changes or compromise is suspected
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-2.3.2 (changing wireless encryption keys when personnel changes or compromise is suspected):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Active maintenance of key management logs in wireless network controllers (or management platforms) showing documented key change dates and actions triggered by personnel status changes or compromise incidents.
   • Technical Artifacts: Configurations in wireless access points and controllers that record key update timestamps, automated reports/logs matching personnel changes (e.g. joiners-movers-leavers process records), and incident response details demonstrating prompt key updates after a compromise.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular, periodic reviews of wireless encryption settings; evidence of annual or event-driven key rotations; documented interviews and training for personnel handling key management.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations may show only periodic key changes on paper without correlating these changes to actual personnel events or documented compromise events.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on static policies (or outdated key lists) without real-time logging of key updates on wireless network devices can easily mask non-operational controls.
   • Technical Gaps: Failure to link key changes with documented joiners-movers-leavers events or compromised incidents, and omission of automated logging of changes within network devices.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of a documented joiners-movers-leavers (JML) process that explicitly requires wireless encryption key changes when personnel with key knowledge leave or change roles.
   WHY: This confirms that the organization has a defined operational process linking personnel changes to wireless key rotations.
   HOW: Examine HR and IT cross-functional policies and interview responsible personnel. Look for version-controlled procedures detailing when and how keys must be changed.
   FAIL: No such process documented or evidence that the process is applied when personnel leave.

   CHECK: Inspect network device logs and wireless controller configurations for timestamps of key changes that correlate with personnel change or incident response events.
   WHY: Automated evidence from devices is far less likely to be faked and shows actual operational enforcement of key changes.
   HOW: Use network management tools or CLI/API access to review historical configuration changes in wireless controllers, confirming that key changes are performed after personnel departures or reported key compromise events.
   FAIL: Lack of correlating log entries or configuration histories indicating outdated encryption keys beyond event triggers.

   CHECK: Review incident and compromise management reports to see if any reported compromise of wireless encryption keys triggered a key change event.
   WHY: This aligns with the requirement that suspected or known compromises require prompt key changes, demonstrating responsiveness to security incidents.
   HOW: Compare incident response records with network logs. Interview incident response team members and check if the evidence shows follow-up changes after a compromise event.
   FAIL: No incident record showing follow-up key replacement when a key compromise was suspected or confirmed.

   CHECK: Validate the change management logs or automated alerts for periodic manual review of key settings on the wireless environment.
   WHY: Periodic manual or automated reviews are critical to ensure no unauthorized or outdated keys remain deployed inadvertently.
   HOW: Request recent audit reports or change management logs that include wireless encryption key reviews. Verify the evidence with system screenshots showing the current key set along with change dates.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies between documented reviews and actual configurations, or absence of periodic review controls.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Cross-reference HR records with network logs – if the schedule or dates don’t sync, the control has likely been faked.
   • Critical Indicator: The absence of automated key-change logs on wireless controllers is a red flag; logs maintained solely on paper are easier to manipulate.
   • Remember: In practice, evidence from network management tools (e.g. CLI/API logs) is paramount. Even if policies exist in documentation, they must be corroborated by configuration and audit logs that cannot be easily backdated or faked.
   • Tip: Validate that key change actions are directly tied to specific events (personnel changes/incident reports) and not just periodic changes. Look for incident report cross-references.

Following these checks will help auditors confirm both the existence and operational effectiveness of key management processes in wireless environments as required by PCI-DSS req-2.3.2.
content:
## Requirements and Testing Procedures

### Defined Approach Requirements

2.3.2 For wireless environments connected to the CDE or transmitting account data, wireless encryption keys are changed as follows:
- Whenever personnel with knowledge of the key leave the company or the role for which the knowledge was necessary.
- Whenever a key is suspected of or known to be compromised.

### Customized Approach Objective

Knowledge of wireless encryption keys cannot allow unauthorized access to wireless networks.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

2.3.2 Interview responsible personnel and examine key-management documentation to verify that wireless encryption keys are changed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

### Guidance

**Purpose**  
Changing wireless encryption keys whenever someone with knowledge of the key leaves the organization or moves to a role that no longer requires knowledge of the key, helps keep knowledge of keys limited to only those with a business need to know.

Also, changing wireless encryption keys whenever a key is suspected or known to be compromised makes a wireless network more resistant to compromise.  
**Good Practice**  
This goal can be accomplished in multiple ways, including periodic changes of keys, changing keys via a defined "joiners-movers-leavers" (JML) process, implementing additional technical controls, and not using fixed pre-shared keys.

In addition, any keys that are known to be, or suspected of being, compromised should be managed in accordance with the entity's incident response plan at Requirement 12.10.1.

title: Change wireless vendor defaults upon installation or confirm their security
methodology:
Below is the complete validation methodology for verifying that wireless vendor defaults have been changed or confirmed secure, following the intent of PCI DSS req-2.3.1.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Screen captures or live demonstrations of the wireless device’s configuration interface showing non-default settings for encryption keys, passwords, SNMP community strings, or other security-related defaults. Evidence of secure configuration applied during initial setup.
   • Technical Artifacts: Configuration files or output from command-line interfaces (CLI) that list the current settings; change logs from wireless network management systems indicating when and by whom changes were made; firmware or system change reports.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular audits and configuration backup processes, change management logs documenting the alteration of default settings, and observed secure login attempts during administrative sessions.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance via Documentation: Organizations might prepare and circulate policies stating that defaults are changed without demonstrating the actual technical configuration modifications.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Simply confirming out-of-date installation checklists or having settings changed on paper while the devices remain on vendor defaults in production.
   • Technical Gaps: Overlooking changes in all applicable areas, e.g., SNMP defaults might be left unchanged while passwords are modified; partial configuration change lists only cover a subset of security-related defaults.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Confirm wireless access point configuration settings (password, encryption keys, SNMP strings) are not on vendor defaults.
   WHY: Direct evidence from the management interface or CLI output indicates that vendor defaults have been replaced with secure custom settings.
   HOW: Log into the wireless device; use configuration commands (or GUI screens) to display the current settings; compare these values to the documented vendor defaults; verify that all default values have been replaced.
   FAIL: If the device’s current settings match the known vendor defaults or if the change histories are not recorded, this indicates a compliance failure.

   CHECK: Review configuration change logs for wireless devices to verify that the modification of defaults occurred upon installation.
   WHY: Audit logs provide a traceable record that changes were made and by authorized personnel, reducing risk of unchanged defaults in production.
   HOW: Access system or change management logs associated with wireless device configurations; look for timestamped entries specifically indicating updates to passwords, encryption keys, and SNMP settings; interview administrators if necessary.
   FAIL: Absence of audit trails or evidence that changes were recorded shortly after installation suggests that defaults may not have been effectively changed.

   CHECK: Use network scanning tools to attempt connection using known vendor defaults for SNMP, admin passwords, and encryption keys (in a controlled audit environment).
   WHY: An active test that fails to log into devices using known defaults confirms that the actual configurations have been hardened.
   HOW: Perform controlled penetration testing or vulnerability scanning on wireless devices; try using vendor default credentials; document the failure of these authentication attempts.
   FAIL: Successful login or detection of default SNMP community strings indicates a breach of this control.

   CHECK: Examine configuration backup files or exported configuration reports for wireless devices.
   WHY: These files provide an unalterable record of configuration at a point in time and can be cross-referenced with known defaults.
   HOW: Retrieve backup configuration files from the management system; review the settings for encryption keys, passwords, and SNMP configurations; verify modifications relative to vendor defaults.
   FAIL: Backup files reflecting vendor default values or unmodified configurations indicate non-compliance.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: When possible, use automated configuration compliance tools that can compare current settings against a secure baseline and known vendor defaults.
   • Critical Details: Ensure that every wireless access point and related communication component (including SNMP and remote management interfaces) is covered. Often, only the primary user-facing settings are verified, leaving management interfaces as potential weak points.
   • Practical Indicator: During a live demonstration, observe an administrator's session. Any hesitance in providing live screenshots or unusual time stamps in configuration logs might indicate a superficial or delayed implementation.
   • Note: Look for evidence that default credentials were not simply changed to other known weak values; ensure that strong, random passwords and encryption keys are in use.

This methodology emphasizes checks that rely on direct, observable evidence of configuration settings and robust change management logs over mere policy documentation, thus providing assurance that the control is effectively implemented.
content:
## 2.3 Wireless environments are configured and managed securely.

### Defined Approach Requirements

**2.3.1** For wireless environments connected to the CDE or transmitting account data, all wireless vendor defaults are changed at installation or are confirmed to be secure, including but not limited to:  
- Default wireless encryption keys.  
- Passwords on wireless access points.  
- SNMP defaults.  
- Any other security-related wireless vendor defaults.

### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**2.3.1.a** Examine policies and procedures and interview responsible personnel to verify that processes are defined for wireless vendor defaults to: either change them upon installation or to confirm them to be secure in accordance with all elements of this requirement.

**2.3.1.b** Examine vendor documentation and observe a system administrator logging into wireless devices to verify:  
- SNMP defaults are not used.  
- Default passwords/passphrases on wireless access points are not used.

**2.3.1.c** Examine vendor documentation and wireless configuration settings to verify other security-related wireless vendor defaults were changed, if applicable.

### Customized Approach Objective

Wireless networks cannot be accessed using vendor default passwords or default configurations.

### Applicability Notes

This includes, but is not limited to, default wireless encryption keys, passwords on wireless access points, SNMP defaults, and any other security-related wireless vendor defaults.

title: Encrypt all non-console administrative access using strong cryptography
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Administrators are observed using encrypted remote tools (e.g., SSH for Linux, RDP with proper TLS configurations for Windows, or secure management interfaces for out-of-band devices) with strong ciphers verified by configuration reviews.
• System configuration files or management console screenshots show that non-console administrative interfaces are set to disable insecure protocols (like telnet or unencrypted HTTP).
• Network traffic captures on administrative sessions yield evidence of strong cryptographic protocols in use, indicated by negotiated cipher suites and protocol versions.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply enable encryption without validating that only strong protocols/ciphers are used – for example, allowing fallback to weak algorithms or deprecated protocol versions.
• Misconfigurations where administrative tools still allow connections in cleartext as backup options on certain interfaces.
• Relying solely on policy documentation or vendor default settings without validating the actual runtime configurations or monitoring logs indicating non-compliant sessions.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Configuration of Remote Access Tools
WHY: Direct review of system/service configuration files (e.g., sshd_config for SSH, RDP settings, or out-of-band management interfaces) confirms that strong encryption is enforced.
HOW: Access configuration files, inspect for required settings (e.g., disabling weak ciphers, ensuring protocol versions are set to secure levels such as TLS 1.2 or above) and compare against industry best practices.
FAIL: Presence of settings that allow weak ciphers (e.g., CBC mode, TLS 1.0/1.1) or fallback options indicating non-enforcement of encryption.

CHECK: Interrogate Network Traffic During Administrative Sessions
WHY: Live network capture showing an encrypted session using strong cryptography validates that administrative traffic isn’t exposed to eavesdroppers.
HOW: Use a network sniffer (e.g., Wireshark) during a remote administrative session, filter for the specific protocols, and confirm that the cryptographic handshake indicates usage of strong ciphers and protocol versions.
FAIL: Detected plain-text exchanges or negotiation of weak or deprecated cipher suites during remote administration sessions.

CHECK: Review Log Files for Administrative Access Events
WHY: Logs that record the type of connection and details about the protocol and cipher used serve as operational evidence of secure connections.
HOW: Query system or device logs that record remote login attempts and session parameters; verify that events detail use of encrypted channels and check for any anomalies referencing insecure sessions.
FAIL: Absence of detailed logging on session encryption parameters or evidence of fallback to unencrypted methods.

CHECK: Validate Disablement of Insecure Protocols
WHY: Assurance that protocols such as telnet, HTTP-based admin interfaces, or unencrypted management channels are completely disabled reinforces compliance.
HOW: Inspect both configuration files and management settings to confirm that insecure protocols are not allowed; attempt a test connection using an insecure protocol and observe if it is blocked.
FAIL: Insecure protocols remain enabled or accessible, even if rarely used, which indicates non-compliance with the strong cryptography requirement.

EXPERT TIPS
• Always compare configuration settings with current industry standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-52 for TLS usage) to ensure that cipher suites cannot be easily downgraded.
• Check firmware and hardware management interfaces separately, as these are often overlooked and may harbor insecure default configurations.
• Utilize automated scanning tools to verify encryption settings, but supplement these with manual verification (e.g., reviewing logs and traffic captures) to rule out misconfigurations.
• The most critical detail is ensuring fallback options are disabled entirely – even if strong configuration exists, any fallback path can be exploited.

Following these practical steps ensures the control is not only documented but operationally secure against eavesdropping on non-console administrative access.
content:
### Requirements and Testing Procedures

#### Defined Approach Requirements

**2.2.7** All non-console administrative access is encrypted using strong cryptography.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

**2.2.7.a** Examine system configuration standards to verify they include encrypting all non-console administrative access using strong cryptography.  
**2.2.7.b** Observe an administrator log on to system components and examine system configurations to verify that non-console administrative access is managed in accordance with this requirement.  
**2.2.7.c** Examine settings for system components and authentication services to verify that insecure remote login services are not available for non-console administrative access.  
**2.2.7.d** Examine vendor documentation and interview personnel to verify that strong cryptography for the technology in use is implemented according to industry best practices and/or vendor recommendations.

### Guidance

**Purpose**  
If non-console (including remote) administration does not use encrypted communications, administrative authorization factors (such as IDs and passwords) can be revealed to an eavesdropper. A malicious individual could use this information to access the network, become administrator, and steal data.

**Good Practice**  
Whichever security protocol is used, it should be configured to use only secure versions and configurations to prevent use of an insecure connection—for example, by using only trusted certificates, supporting only strong encryption, and not supporting fallback to weaker, insecure protocols or methods.

**Examples**  
Cleartext protocols (such as HTTP, telnet, etc.) do not encrypt traffic or logon details, making it easy for an eavesdropper to intercept this information. Non-console access may be facilitated by technologies that provide alternative access to systems, including but not limited to, out-of-band (OOB), lights-out management (LOM), Intelligent Platform Management Interface (IPMI), and keyboard, video, mouse (KVM) switches with remote capabilities. These and other non-console access technologies and methods must be secured with strong cryptography.

**Further Information**  
Refer to industry standards and best practices such as NIST SP 800-52 and SP 800-57.

title: Configure system security parameters to prevent misuse
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for PCI-DSS req-2.2.6 “Configure system security parameters to prevent misuse”.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: System configuration documents and live configurations show hardening parameters (e.g., secure default settings, restricted ports, hardened protocols) that can’t simply be turned off.
• Technical Artifacts: Automated configuration management output (such as scripts from Puppet, Chef, or Ansible) or system configuration baselines in tools like CIS-CAT and STIG checklists; live configuration files (sshd_config, registry settings, security parameter configuration files).
• Operational Patterns: Regular configuration reviews, logging of configuration changes, and periodic automated scans that report on deviations from the approved baseline.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Relying solely on documentation or screenshots rather than demonstrating live, operating configurations. Simply having policies that require secure settings while system defaults remain vulnerable.
• Surface Implementations: A system that shows some security parameter settings in documentation yet lacks ongoing monitoring, alerting, or automated enforcement is a common pitfall.
• Technical Gaps: Incomplete parameter testing on all system components, especially for custom or cloud-access systems, and misalignment between configuration standards and applied settings.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Validate that system configuration standards explicitly list all pertinent security parameters (e.g., parameter hardening for network protocols, user sessions, file access controls).
  WHY: Ensures that a written, authoritative reference exists to guide secure configuration.
  HOW: Examine official system hardening guidelines and configuration standards from the organization; correlate these with supported vendor recommendations.
  FAIL: Missing or vague guidelines and no connection to vendor-hardening best practices.

• CHECK: Interview system administrators and security managers on specific security parameter settings (what is set, why, frequency of review).
  WHY: Confirms that operational staff are knowledgeable about the secure configurations and understand the implications.
  HOW: Conduct structured interviews; ask for details of common parameter settings and incident remediations in the context of misconfigurations.
  FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly articulate the settings or reliance solely on rote memorization without practical insight.

• CHECK: Inspect live system configurations for compliance with approved security parameters (e.g., verify settings in configuration files or system registries that prevent misuse).
  WHY: Directly demonstrates that the systems in operation are configured to prevent misuse.
  HOW: Utilize configuration management tools (such as Ansible, CIS-CAT, or a custom script) to extract current configuration values and compare them against the approved baseline.
  FAIL: Configurations are not aligned with the documented baseline or default/weak settings are observed.

• CHECK: Verify that periodic automated scans and configuration change management logs are in place and actively monitored.
  WHY: Ongoing operational monitoring ensures that configurations remain secure over time and deviations are quickly remediated.
  HOW: Review change logs, system audit trails, and automated scanning reports; confirm that alerts are being generated for non-compliant settings.
  FAIL: Lack of regular scans, absent change logs, or missing trend analysis reflecting reapplication of outdated or non-compliant settings.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated configuration scanning tools to pull current parameter settings and flag non-compliance immediately.
• Key Indicator: If administrators cannot swiftly show consistent and current configuration details from automated tools, it often indicates reliance on outdated, static screenshots or documentation.
• Don’t Overlook: Specific system components (like cloud interfaces) might require tailored parameters; ensure that these are included in the standard and are actively verified.
• Critical Detail: Ensure that configuration settings are not only documented but are enforced by technical controls (such as automated remediation scripts) – manual checks can be bypassed.

This methodology focuses on observability of the actual applied settings and operational evidence, minimizing reliance on mere documentation.
content:
### Requirements and Testing Procedures

#### Defined Approach Requirements

2.2.6 System security parameters are configured to prevent misuse.

#### Defined Approach Testing Procedures

2.2.6.a Examine system configuration standards to verify they include configuring system security parameters to prevent misuse.

2.2.6.b Interview system administrators and/or security managers to verify they have knowledge of common security parameter settings for system components.

2.2.6.c Examine system configurations to verify that common security parameters are set appropriately and in accordance with the system configuration standards.

#### Guidance

**Purpose**  
Correctly configuring security parameters provided in system components takes advantage of the capabilities of the system component to defeat malicious attacks.

**Good Practice**  
System configuration standards and related processes should specifically address security settings and parameters that have known security implications for each type of system in use.  
For systems to be configured securely, personnel responsible for configuration and/or administering systems should be knowledgeable in the specific security parameters and settings that apply to the system. Considerations should also include secure settings for parameters used to access cloud portals.

**Further Information**  
Refer to vendor documentation and industry references noted in Requirement 2.2.1 for information about applicable security parameters for each type of system.

title: Mitigate risks associated with insecure services, protocols, or daemons
methodology:
Below is a detailed practical validation methodology for control req-2.2.5 – Mitigate risks associated with insecure services, protocols, or daemons:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence: A curated list or inventory of running services/daemons that are known to be insecure, with annotations or documentation showing business justification for each instance.
   - Technical artifacts: Configuration files, system inventories (e.g., automated scans listing enabled services), and additional security settings (such as encrypted communication settings, access controls, or applied patches) that mitigate inherent risks.
   - Operational patterns: Regularly updated system configuration baselines and documented exception processes for services that require insecure protocols, coupled with audit logs of configuration changes and security assessments.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faked compliance: Organizations might produce static documentation or a list of approved service exceptions while actual system configurations remain unchanged or still expose insecure protocols.
   - Superficial implementations: Simply disabling warnings in monitoring tools or creating an alternative firewall rule that only superficially hides the insecure service rather than securing it.
   - Technical gaps: Not verifying that documented additional security controls (e.g., encryption, access restrictions, logging) are actually implemented, or that justification for the service is current and still valid.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Insecure Service Inventory and Justifications
   WHY: The presence of an updated and approved inventory with documented business justifications shows that insecure services or protocols have been deliberately reviewed and exceptions have been granted.
   HOW: 1. Retrieve the list of running services/daemons from system configuration (e.g., check using commands like 'netstat', 'ss', or querying service management tools).
         2. Cross-reference against documentation and configuration standards that specify which services are considered insecure.
         3. Interview stakeholders to confirm business justification and review timestamps of last approval.
   FAIL: Missing inventory records, outdated justifications, or discrepancies between running services and documented exceptions.

   CHECK: Examine Configuration Settings for Additional Security Features
   WHY: Ensuring additional security controls (e.g., encryption, strong authentication, access restrictions) are implemented reduces the inherent risk of insecure protocols.
   HOW: 1. Access configuration files (system and application level) to verify security features are enabled or patched.
         2. Validate settings by comparing against vendor recommendations and industry standards (e.g., NIST guidelines).
         3. Use automated configuration management or vulnerability scanning tools to identify if insecure settings persist.
   FAIL: Absence or misconfiguration of security features or differences between documented controls and actual configurations.

   CHECK: Confirm Regular Reviews and Updates to the Exception Process
   WHY: Continuous validation and updates ensure any changes in the threat landscape are addressed promptly, preventing outdated justifications from being applied to insecure setups.
   HOW: 1. Review audit trails or logs that record changes to service configurations and exception approvals.
         2. Interview IT operations staff regarding frequency and process for reviews.
         3. Confirm that change management records include secure countermeasures for identified insecure services.
   FAIL: Lack of audit trails, infrequent reviews, or missing change management records linking security feature adjustments.

   CHECK: Evaluate Automated Monitoring and Alerting
   WHY: Automated systems provide continuous oversight, ensuring that insecure protocols or misconfigurations are quickly identified and remediated.
   HOW: 1. Check logs or dashboard outputs from security monitoring tools that flag insecure services or protocol anomalies.
         2. Validate that alerts trigger incident response procedures.
         3. Confirm periodic examples of incidents being addressed.
   FAIL: No evidence of automated monitoring, infrequent alerts, or gaps between detection and remediation actions.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut Verification: Use automated tools (e.g., configuration management systems and vulnerability scanners) to quickly generate reports on running services and compare them with the approved exception list.
   - Critical Signals: Pay attention to services that historically pose higher risks (e.g., telnet, FTP) and verify if they are subject to strong mitigating controls; inconsistencies often indicate a lapse in enforcement.
   - Detail Focus: Look at the granularity of configuration files. Often, insecure daemons are hidden under generic service listings; verifying the raw configuration files provides unfiltered evidence.
   - Interview multiple roles to avoid sole reliance on overseen documentation; cross-check with records from the change management system and active monitoring logs.
   - Maintain skepticism on documented justifications without corroborating operational evidence; observe timestamps and verify the consistency across different artifacts.

This thorough methodology prioritizes technical and automatable evidences over policy alone, ensuring that the control is both implemented and effective in reducing the risks associated with insecure services, protocols, or daemons.
content:
### Requirements and Testing Procedures

| **Defined Approach Requirements** | **Defined Approach Testing Procedures** | **Guidance** |
| ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 2.2.5 If any insecure services, protocols, or daemons are present:  
• Business justification is documented.  
• Additional security features are documented and implemented that reduce the risk of using insecure services, protocols, or daemons. | 2.2.5.a If any insecure services, protocols, or daemons are present, examine system configuration standards and interview personnel to verify they are managed and implemented in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.  
2.2.5.b If any insecure services, protocols, or daemons are present, examine configuration settings to verify that additional security features are implemented to reduce the risk of using insecure services, daemons, and protocols. | **Purpose**  
Ensuring that all insecure services, protocols, and daemons are adequately secured with appropriate security features makes it more difficult for malicious individuals to exploit common points of compromise within a network.  
**Good Practice**  
Enabling security features before new system components are deployed will prevent insecure configurations from being introduced into the environment. Some vendor solutions may provide additional security functions to assist with securing an insecure process.  
**Further Information**  
For guidance on services, protocols, or daemons considered to be insecure, refer to industry standards and guidance (for example, as published by NIST, ENISA, and OWASP).  

### Customized Approach Objective

System components cannot be compromised by exploiting insecure services, protocols, or daemons.

title: Ensure only necessary functionality is enabled while unnecessary functions are removed or disabled
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible Evidence: Active inventory lists of running services, protocols, daemons, and functions that have been approved. Screenshots or logs from configuration management systems noting removal or disabling of unnecessary functionalities.
• Technical Artifacts: System configuration files (e.g., /etc/services, systemd unit definitions), baseline configuration scripts, automation tool reports (e.g., Ansible, Chef) that enforce the approved service baseline, and change management records showing removal or disabling of unnecessary components.
• Operational Patterns: Regular automated scans or periodic manual reviews that compare active services against a documented list; logs evidencing that extraneous services are not started at boot time; monitoring alerts for unauthorized services.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Document-Only Compliance: Organizations might maintain an updated service approval list but fail to enforce it on all systems. A configuration file in the document may not reflect the actual runtime state.
• Surface-Level Disabling: Some functionalities may be technically disabled in configuration, but residual daemons or background processes may remain active.
• Incomplete Inventories: Failing to account for all service vectors, such as web servers bundled with applications or legacy protocols that are rarely used but still enabled.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Inventory to Active Process Verification
   WHY: Matching a documented service inventory against currently running services ensures only necessary functionality is active.
   HOW: Use automated discovery tools (e.g., automated scripts or configuration management queries) to list services and processes, then cross-check with the approved baseline configuration. Manually verify on sample systems if needed.
   FAIL: Any extra service or daemon running that is not documented in the approved configuration suggests non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Configuration File and Management System Inspection
   WHY: Verifying that configuration files and automation scripts explicitly disable or remove unnecessary functionalities proves that the change has been implemented at the source level.
   HOW: Review configuration files (e.g., systemd unit files, /etc/services, configuration scripts) and examine automation tool outputs or reports showing applied changes. Look for parameters (e.g., disable flags) confirming services are disabled or removed.
   FAIL: Presence of enabled configurations for services that should be disabled, or absence of automation reports confirming removal, indicate failure.

3. CHECK: Baseline and Vulnerability Scan Comparison
   WHY: A vulnerability scan often detects leftover or unauthorized active services, providing independent validation of the control being active.
   HOW: Run an automated vulnerability scanner or a CIS benchmark assessment on a sample subset of systems and verify that only approved services are detectable. Validate that there are no unexpected active network ports or processes.
   FAIL: Identification of unnecessary services, open ports, or detected vulnerabilities linked to extra functionalities signals non-compliance.

4. CHECK: Change Management and Audit Log Review
   WHY: Consistent monitoring through logs and change management systems reinforces that the control is actively maintained through documented and verified changes.
   HOW: Inspect audit logs and change management records to confirm that removal or disabling actions for unapproved functionalities are tracked and there is evidence of routine reviews.
   FAIL: Incomplete, outdated, or absent logs related to disabling/removal actions suggest a lax enforcement of the control.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcuts: Use endpoint security tools that can regularly report installed and running services versus a known-good baseline. Utilize real-time monitoring dashboards to flag deviations immediately.
• Critical Indicators: Focus on high-risk services such as legacy protocols (e.g., FTP, Telnet), unused web servers, or non-essential interfaces like USB if not required. Residual processes that don’t align with documented configurations are critical red flags.
• Details That Matter: Do not merely rely on configuration standards – always verify the running state. Ensure that automated scans, configuration management reports, and manual reviews align and that any discrepancies trigger further investigation.

By following these steps, auditors can practically validate that only necessary functionality is enabled and that all unnecessary services are effectively removed or disabled, thereby maintaining a minimized attack surface in accordance with PCI-DSS requirement 2.2.4.
content:
Requirements and Testing Procedures
Defined Approach Requirements
2.2.4 Only necessary services, protocols, daemons, and functions are enabled, and all unnecessary functionality is removed or disabled.  

Defined Approach Testing Procedures
2.2.4.a Examine system configuration standards to verify necessary services, protocols, daemons, and functions are identified and documented.  
2.2.4.b Examine system configurations to verify the following:  
• All unnecessary functionality is removed or disabled.  
• Only required functionality, as documented in the configuration standards, is enabled.  

Customized Approach Objective

System components cannot be compromised by exploiting unnecessary functionality present in the system component.  

Guidance

Purpose

Unnecessary services and functions can provide additional opportunities for malicious individuals to gain access to a system. By removing or disabling all unnecessary services, protocols, daemons, and functions, organizations can focus on securing the functions that are required and reduce the risk that unknown or unnecessary functions will be exploited.  

Good Practice

There are many protocols that could be enabled by default that are commonly used by malicious individuals to compromise a network. Disabling or removing all services, functions, and protocols that are not used minimizes the potential attack surface—for example, by removing or disabling an unused FTP or web server.

Examples

Unnecessary functionality may include, but is not limited to scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, interfaces (USB and Bluetooth), and unnecessary web servers.

title: Manage primary functions with differing security levels on system components
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• System configuration standards explicitly detail management of primary functions with different security levels. These standards will reference the choice between single-function deployment, isolation methods, or hardened configurations for multiple functions.
• Technical artifacts such as configuration files, virtualization management consoles, network segmentation diagrams, and logs that demonstrate separation of duties or isolated environments when multiple functions share a component.
• Operational patterns like discrete network zones, clearly separated virtual instances/containers with unique security profiles, and real-time monitoring alerts indicating that lower-security functions do not interact with higher-security ones.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply document that functions are separated without validating that multiple functions aren’t deployed on the same physical device, essentially faking compliance through policy-only evidence.
• Misconfigured virtualization or container settings may be declared as isolation, but in practice, shared resources or misapplied access controls can allow cross-function interference.
• Surface-level evidence such as a listing of services running without verification of enforced isolation boundaries, leaving gaps that tests could exploit.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that system configuration standards (and associated configuration files) mandate a solution for handling primary functions with differing security levels.
WHY: This ensures that the organization has formally recognized and planned for the isolation/separation requirements.
HOW: - Review configuration standards documents and compare them against the requirement.
     - Locate sections addressing service separation or virtualization isolation controls.
FAIL: Lack of documented standards or a generic statement without provisions for differing security levels.

CHECK: Inspect system components with multi-function capabilities to determine if only one primary function is enabled, or if multiple functions coexist, whether they are configured to be isolated (physically, logically, or via virtualization controls).
WHY: Directly verifying if systems abide by a single or properly isolated model shows real-world operational compliance rather than paper compliance.
HOW: - Enumerate running services or installed virtual instances/containers.
     - Identify if services with differing security requirements are co-located on the same hardware.
     - Check for isolation mechanisms (e.g., separate VMs, containers with dedicated memory/network segmentation).
FAIL: Discovery of multiple functions without any isolation controls or evidence of mutual influence (lack of virtualization containment, shared accounts, or overlapping network paths).

CHECK: Evaluate virtualization infrastructure (if applicable) to confirm that virtual machines or containers housing primary functions adhere to one of the accepted models: no co-existence, robust isolation, or uniform high-security configuration.
WHY: Virtual environments are high-risk for function bleed between isolated zones. Proper configuration is critical to maintain separation.
HOW: - Examine virtualization management consoles, access control lists, and storage configurations.
     - Validate that virtual network interfaces and resource allocations are properly segmented.
     - Interview administrators or review change logs to confirm segregation policies in practice.
FAIL: Evidence of mixed trust workloads on a single virtual host without clear isolation measures or uniform security posture adjustments.

CHECK: Monitor and assess operational logs or network segmentation tests to ensure that lower-security functions cannot affect resources or data belonging to higher-security functions on the same physical component.
WHY: Real-time behavior and segregation can only be confirmed by observing traffic or access patterns rather than relying solely on configuration settings.
HOW: - Review network flow logs, firewall logs, or intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts that indicate cross-communication between functions.
     - Execute controlled tests, if permitted, to verify that lower-security functions cannot access higher-security areas.
FAIL: Logs showing anomalous or unauthorized communication between separate function zones or evidence that isolation controls are breached during live operations.

EXPERT TIPS
• Quick Shortcut: Use configuration management tools (e.g., script-based audits) to automatically list running services and verify their assigned security profiles. This reduces the risk of overlooking misconfigurations.
